There is no branch that surfaces result.vcsError, so a real revert failure (e.g. abandoned change — see packages/vcs/tests/jj-real-repo.test.js:131-152 which proves revertToJjPointer returns success:false with a useful error) would be silently swallowed and the user is told only that the run was forked. Combined with the orphaned write path, --restore-vcs gives the user no signal in either failure mode.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Entire src/ide/ subtree is orphaned dead code (zero importers, zero tests, no docs)
file: packages/smithers/src/ide/SmithersIdeService.js (433 lines), packages/smithers/src/ide/tools.js (95 lines), packages/smithers/src/ide/index.js, and 13 SmithersIde*.ts type files
evidence: grep across the whole repo for createSmithersIdeService / createSmithersIdeCli / detectSmithersIdeAvailabilityEffect / SmithersIdeService / SMITHERS_IDE_TOOL_NAMES / isSmithersIdeAvailable / createAvailableSmithersIdeCli / openWebview / showOverlay / runTerminal returns ZERO hits outside packages/smithers/src/ide/ itself. No package.json export lists ./ide (only reachable via the ./* wildcard). apps/cli/src/ide does not exist; apps/cli/src/mcp/semantic-tools.js never registers the IDE CLI. The only repo reference to 'smithers-ctl' in product code is a comment in .smithers/scripts/ask-user.ts (line 14), not an import. The files have been untouched since the JS migration (git log shows only the @smithers/* -> @smithers-orchestrator/* rename and the JS-migration commit). It is a complete, real Effect service + MCP namespace CLI (smithers_ide_open_file/open_diff/show_overlay/run_terminal/ask_user/open_webview) with no consumer.
recommendation: Either wire src/ide/ into a real consumer (the desktop smithers-ctl MCP server it was clearly built for) and add unit tests + a docs entry, or delete the subtree. As-is it is ~500 lines of untested, unreachable surface that the ./* export silently publishes.
vnote: Independently verified all material claims. (1) Zero importers: precise grep for import/require/from of ./ide, smithers-orchestrator/ide, SmithersIdeService, createSmithersIde*, smithers_ide_* across the whole repo (packages, apps, .smithers, scripts, docs, skills) returns nothing outside packages/smithers/src/ide/. The one apps/cli hit (published-deps-declared.test.js:43) lists 'src/ide' in skipSubdirs — it DELIBERATELY EXCLUDES the subtree from a dep-declaration check, not a consumer. examples/bisect-guide.jsx and .smithers/specs/package-restructure-plan.md hits are path-substring/plan-doc false positives; the 4 'test files referencing ide' are all substring false positives (confirmed empty when filtered to SmithersIde/src/ide). (2) Not exported: src/index.js has zero ide refs; package.json exports lists no ./ide (only ./* wildcard); no ide/index.d.ts exists (dir has only .ts type sources + .js). (3) Zero tests: no test references the subsystem. (4) Zero docs: docs/ and skills/ have no SmithersIde/smithers ide/smithers-ctl refs. (5) smithers-ctl (DEFAULT_COMMAND in SmithersIdeService.js:23) is the binary shelled to and does NOT exist as a CLI in this repo; only refs are a COMMENT in .smithers/scripts/ask-user.ts:14 (confirmed it's a comment, not an import) and a description string in tools.js:21. (6) apps/cli/src/mcp/semantic-tools.js never registers the IDE CLI (matches were 'decidedBy'/'idempotentHint' substring false positives). (7) files:['src/'] + ./* wildcard means the entire subtree IS published as smithers-orchestrator/ide/* with no consumer. It is a complete Effect service (Context.Tag SmithersIdeService, layer, availability detection) plus MCP namespace CLI (SMITHERS_IDE_TOOL_NAMES, createSmithersIdeCli) exporting openFile/openDiff/showOverlay/runTerminal/askUser/openWebview. Only trivial inaccuracies: line counts are 432/94/44 (finding said 433/95), and the 13 .ts files are 12 result/config types + SmithersIdeServiceApi.ts — neither affects the substance. P1 severity is appropriate for ~570 LOC of untested, unreachable, silently-published surface.

title: createSmithersPostgres and findFreePgPort have no test coverage inside packages/smithers
evidence: createSmithersPostgres (lines 481-564) and findFreePgPort (lines 568-580) are exercised only by packages/engine/tests/create-smithers-postgres.test.jsx (which imports ../../smithers/src/create.js), and that suite is gated behind a real Postgres/PGlite environment. The error-teardown path (the catch at lines 547-554 that drains the teardown stack on a post-boot failure, e.g. socket-server leak prevention) is never asserted (grep for 'teardown'/'catch'/'reject' in that test file = 0). findFreePgPort has no test referencing it at all. create-unit.test.js only covers the synchronous bun:sqlite createSmithers path.
recommendation: Add a packages/smithers test for createSmithersPostgres with provider:'pglite' (no external dependency) asserting: tables/outputs/schemaRegistry parity with createSmithers, the returned close() drains teardown, and the catch-block teardown fires when boot fails (e.g. inject a bad connectionString). Cover findFreePgPort returning a usable port.

title: bin/smithers.js local-CLI delegation logic is untested in this package
evidence: The bin contains non-trivial, correctness-critical logic — getExplicitWorkflowPath (positional + WORKFLOW_PATH_COMMANDS scanning), resolveLocalSmithersBinJs (reads node_modules/smithers-orchestrator/package.json bin, with try/catch JSON parse and string-vs-object bin handling), findNearestWorkflowLocalCli (upward walk), and delegateToLocalCliIfPresent (self-path realpath guard, signal/exit-code propagation). None of these helpers are exported or covered by any test in packages/smithers/tests. The 'two React copies -> null useContext' trap this guards against is exactly the kind of regression a unit test should pin.
recommendation: Export the pure helpers (getExplicitWorkflowPath, resolveLocalSmithersBinJs, findNearestWorkflowLocalCli) or factor them into a tested module, and add unit tests for: workflow-path detection across each WORKFLOW_PATH_COMMAND, malformed package.json (catch branch), bin-as-string vs bin.smithers, and the self-path no-delegate guard.

title: Several create.js branches uncovered: anchor-based default dbPath, journalMode option, input ALTER catch
evidence: create-unit.test.js always passes an explicit opts.dbPath, so the findSmithersAnchorDir-driven default dbPath resolution (lines 372-374: anchorDir ? join(anchorDir,'smithers.db') : './smithers.db') is never exercised. The journalMode option (line 392, opts?.journalMode ?? 'WAL') has 0 references in the test (grep journalMode = 0). The catch branch around the input-table payload ALTER (lines 427-436, swallowing older-SQLite/permission errors) is not directly forced — the existing 'adds the default input payload column' test hits the success path only.
recommendation: Add cases: (a) createSmithers with no opts.dbPath under a temp HOME containing a .smithers/ dir, asserting the DB lands at the anchor; (b) journalMode:'DELETE' (or 'MEMORY') asserting PRAGMA journal_mode applied; (c) the ALTER catch path can be left if hard to trigger, but the anchor/journalMode paths are cheap and real.

title: findSmithersAnchorDir fsRoot guard and HOME-unset branch only covered indirectly
evidence: findSmithersAnchorDir is tested only transitively via apps/cli/tests/resolve-root.test.js (through resolveLaunchRootDir). That suite covers anchor-found, nested-anchor, anchor-not-found-fallback, and the home-boundary rejection, but never exercises the `dir === fsRoot` guard (line 29, only reachable when HOME is unset) nor the `home === undefined` path (line 19, process.env.HOME absent). There is no direct unit test for findSmithersAnchorDir in packages/smithers.
recommendation: Add a small packages/smithers unit test that deletes process.env.HOME and verifies the upward walk terminates at the filesystem root returning undefined, plus the symlink/false-positive note in the JSDoc (e.g. a sibling /home2-style path).

title: Stale/misleading JSDoc on the create.js Task wrapper
evidence: The Task wrapper's JSDoc claims it 'resolves ZodObject output references against the schema registry by reference equality, injecting the outputSchema.' The implementation only spreads props and adds smithersContext: RuntimeSmithersContext (lines 256-259). It does not resolve any output reference or inject outputSchema — that resolution happens downstream in BaseTask / the engine. The comment describes behavior the function does not perform.
recommendation: Update the comment to reflect what the wrapper actually does (binds the runtime SmithersContext so BaseTask can resolve outputs against the registry), or remove the inaccurate claim.

title: prepareOutputSchemas duplicated and divergent between create.js and external/create-external-smithers.js
evidence: Both files define a private prepareOutputSchemas that counts duplicate Zod schema identities and clones ambiguous ones into zodToKeyName/ambiguousZodSchemas. The create.js version additionally builds an `outputs` map keyed by the cloned aliases (so api.outputs.foo resolves by identity), while the external version omits `outputs` entirely. The de-dup/clone logic is copy-pasted; a future fix to the ambiguity handling must be applied in two places. Both are independently tested, masking the drift.
recommendation: Extract a shared prepareOutputSchemas helper (e.g. into a small internal module) that both create.js and external import, with create.js layering the `outputs` alias map on top. Eliminates the duplicated identity-clone logic.

title: Public mdxPlugin (and createExternalSmithers) lack package-level coverage / in-repo consumers
evidence: mdxPlugin is a real public export consumed by the .smithers pack (.smithers/preload.ts, .smithers/gateway.ts, fixtures, scripts) and re-implemented identically in apps/cli/src/mdx-plugin.js, but has no unit test (trivial 3-line wrapper, low risk). createExternalSmithers is a documented public 'multi-language host' API yet has no in-repo product consumer (grep shows only its own package's tests and re-exports) — it is well unit-tested but effectively only validated against itself.
recommendation: Low priority: optionally collapse the duplicated mdx-plugin into a single shared export, and consider whether createExternalSmithers warrants an e2e (real engine run from a HostNodeJson tree) given it has no other in-repo exercise; otherwise document it as an external-integration API explicitly.

title: 11 of 19 WorkflowSessionService methods have zero unit tests
evidence: Counting `.<method>(` calls across packages/scheduler/tests/*.js: approvalTimedOut=0, eventReceived=0, signalReceived=0, timerFired=0, heartbeatTimedOut=0, cacheResolved=0, cacheMissed=0, recoverOrphanedTasks=0, cancelRequested=0, getTaskStates=0, getSchedule=0, getCurrentGraph=0. These methods (makeWorkflowSession.js lines 711-861) contain real branching: correlation matching in eventReceived/signalReceived (lines 729-776), timer resume math (timerFired 777-792, timerResumeAtMs 113-127), orphan-recovery counting + OrphanRecovery wait (835-848), cancel state transitions (849-857). A grep of engine/driver/e2e tests for these methods returns nothing — they are untested across the entire repo.
recommendation: Add scheduler-level unit tests for each session method, covering: eventReceived/signalReceived name+correlation match/no-match, timerFired waiting-timer transition and the !waiting-timer early-return, approvalTimedOut on a non-waiting task, heartbeatTimedOut retry vs fail, cacheResolved/cacheMissed, recoverOrphanedTasks (count>0 vs count==0 OrphanRecovery wait), cancelRequested terminal-state preservation, getTaskStates/getSchedule/getCurrentGraph.
vnote: Substance partly confirmed but counts are wrong and 'untested across the entire repo' overstates it. The WorkflowSessionService interface has exactly 17 methods, not 19 (verified: grep -c 'readonly .*:' WorkflowSessionService.ts = 17; makeWorkflowSession returns 17). The title says '11' but the bullet list enumerates 12. It IS true that zero scheduler-package tests (packages/scheduler/tests/*.js, 102 passing tests) call approvalTimedOut/eventReceived/signalReceived/timerFired/heartbeatTimedOut/cacheResolved/cacheMissed/recoverOrphanedTasks/cancelRequested/getTaskStates/getSchedule/getCurrentGraph. However, several of these ARE exercised at the engine integration level: timerFired (engine.js:4989), getTaskStates (engine.js:4974), cancelRequested (WorkflowDriver.js:643), and the orphan/budget/ralph reactions via real-engine jsx tests. hotReloaded is in fact covered by workflowSession-prune-approvals-retrycounts.test.js. So the genuine gap is scheduler-UNIT coverage, and the strict 'untested anywhere' wording is false for ~6 of the listed methods.

title: decide() ralph-advancement, ContinueAsNew, RALPH_MAX, and budget branches are untested
evidence: makeWorkflowSession.js decide() lines 572-628 implement the readyRalphs advance loop: iteration increment, RALPH_MAX_REACHED failure (595-599), onMaxReached='return-last' done (601), continueAsNewEvery loop-threshold ContinueAsNew (605-622), the hasInProgress/pendingExists deferral guard (#267, 609-611), and ReRender after advance (625-627). grep for ralph/ContinueAsNew/RALPH_MAX/initialRalphState in tests/workflowSession*.js returns nothing — only scheduleTasks' readyRalphs *detection* is tested (state-plan-boundaries.test.js), never the session's *reaction*. Likewise the Aspect-budget branches (533-554: skip-remaining/warn/fail -> ASPECT_BUDGET_EXCEEDED) have no scheduler test; the engine only tests the standalone evaluateAspectBudget helper, not the session's response.
recommendation: Add decide()-level tests using makeWorkflowSession({ initialRalphState, evaluateAspectBudget }) that drive a ralph to: a normal next iteration (ReRender), maxIterations with fail and return-last, and continueAsNewEvery (ContinueAsNew loop-threshold incl. the in-progress deferral). Add tests for the three Aspect-budget onExceeded outcomes.
vnote: Largely refuted at the 'untested' level, true only at the scheduler-unit level. The Aspects-budget branches (decide() 533-554, all three onExceeded outcomes) ARE covered end-to-end and assert the session's response in packages/engine/tests/aspects-budget.test.jsx (fail->ASPECT_BUDGET_EXCEEDED, warn->task still runs, skip-remaining->task skipped) via the real engine that builds the session with makeWorkflowSession — so 'the engine only tests the standalone evaluateAspectBudget helper, not the session's response' is false. The readyRalphs advancement + #267 in-progress deferral guard + ReRender-after-advance (decide() 572-627) are covered by packages/engine/tests/parallel-loop-advancement.test.jsx (non-legacy new-engine test at line 17 asserts iterations land while a sibling is in flight) and loop-until-reeval/nested-loop-runtime. continueAsNewEvery/ContinueAsNew is covered by packages/components/tests/continue-as-new.test.jsx (Loop continueAsNewEvery={5}). The genuinely uncovered path is RALPH_MAX_REACHED: grep for 'RALPH_MAX_REACHED' / 'reached maxIterations' returns no assertion anywhere in the repo, and no runtime test drives onMaxReached='fail' to the cap. Scheduler-unit coverage of decide() reactions is indeed absent (state-plan-boundaries.test.js only imports scheduleTasks, not makeWorkflowSession).

title: Scheduler/WorkflowSession Effect Tags and SchedulerLive are dead provisioning (never consumed)
evidence: SchedulerLive + WorkflowSessionLive are merged into SmithersCoreLayer in apps/cli/src/smithersRuntime.js:8 and packages/server/src/smithersRuntime.js:7, but nothing ever resolves the Tags: grep for `yield* Scheduler`, `.schedule(`, or `yield* WorkflowSession` across engine/server/driver/cli returns no hits. The engine calls the bare `scheduleTasks(...)` function directly (engine.js:6789) and constructs sessions via `makeWorkflowSession(...)` (engine.js:5557, 5836), never via the Context Tag. So Scheduler.js (the Context.Tag class), SchedulerLive.js (Layer.succeed wrapping scheduleTasks), and the SchedulerService typedef are unused machinery; WorkflowSessionLive/WorkflowSession Tag are likewise provided-but-unconsumed.
recommendation: Either delete the Scheduler/WorkflowSession Effect Tags + Live layers (and stop merging them into the runtime), or migrate the engine to actually consume them via the Tag so the layering is real. Right now they add public-API and runtime-wiring surface with no consumer.
vnote: Confirmed. SchedulerLive + WorkflowSessionLive are merged into SmithersCoreLayer (apps/cli/src/smithersRuntime.js:8, packages/server/src/smithersRuntime.js:7) but no code resolves either Tag: grep for 'yield* Scheduler' / 'yield* WorkflowSession' / 'Scheduler.schedule' returns nothing across packages/apps/e2e. The engine imports the bare function scheduleTasks from ./scheduler.js (engine.js:19) and calls it directly (engine.js:6789), and builds sessions via makeWorkflowSession (engine.js:5557, 5836) — never via the Context Tag. SchedulerService typedef is only referenced inside Scheduler.js itself (and generated index.d.ts). The Scheduler/SchedulerLive Tags are even re-exported through packages/engine/src/scheduler.js:20 -> index.js, widening dead public surface. P2 is appropriate.

title: ~9 session methods are dead in production; the package ships a much larger API than is used
evidence: Across engine+driver+server the only session methods ever invoked are submitGraph, taskCompleted, taskFailed, approvalResolved, getTaskStates (driver/engine), cancelRequested (WorkflowDriver.js:643), and timerFired (engine.js:4989). grep across all consumers finds zero callers of eventReceived, signalReceived, hotReloaded, heartbeatTimedOut, cacheResolved, cacheMissed, recoverOrphanedTasks, approvalTimedOut, getSchedule, getCurrentGraph — the engine reimplements event/signal/cache/orphan/heartbeat/hot-reload handling itself. Additionally the driver's session contract supports getNextDecision?() (WorkflowDriver.js:554,611) but makeWorkflowSession does NOT implement getNextDecision, and signalReceived's __signalName branch (makeWorkflowSession.js:756-760) matches a meta key (__signalName) that the graph extractor never sets (only __eventName/__waitForEvent exist in packages/graph/src/extract.js:539).
recommendation: Trim the WorkflowSessionService surface to what is actually used, or document these as a deliberate extension contract. At minimum drop the dead __signalName branch in signalReceived (extractor never sets it) and reconcile getNextDecision between driver expectations and the scheduler implementation.
vnote: Confirmed. Across engine/driver/server/cli the only session methods invoked are submitGraph, taskCompleted, taskFailed, approvalResolved (engine.js:4910), getTaskStates (engine.js:4974), timerFired (engine.js:4989), cancelRequested (WorkflowDriver.js:643) = 7 used. Dead in production (no consumer, verified incl. dynamic/string refs): approvalTimedOut, eventReceived, signalReceived, hotReloaded, heartbeatTimedOut, cacheResolved, cacheMissed, recoverOrphanedTasks, getSchedule, getCurrentGraph = 10 (the engine reimplements heartbeat at engine.js:2854, orphan at 5023, events/signals/cache itself). getSchedule/getCurrentGraph have ZERO callers anywhere including tests. The __signalName branch (makeWorkflowSession.js:756-757) is confirmed dead: __signalName appears ONLY in makeWorkflowSession.js; the extractor (packages/graph/src/extract.js:539-541) sets only __waitForEvent/__eventName/__correlationId, so the branch always falls through to __eventName. getNextDecision is genuinely expected by WorkflowDriver.js:554-555 and 611-612 but makeWorkflowSession does NOT implement it, and the engine passes its makeWorkflowSession as session: workflowSession (engine.js:5660), so those driver branches are always-false dead paths. Note: the driver's WorkflowSession type is packages/driver/src/WorkflowSession.ts (a separate duck-typed contract), not the scheduler Tag. P2 appropriate.

title: eventReceived and signalReceived are near-duplicate ~24-line handlers
evidence: makeWorkflowSession.js:729-751 (eventReceived) and 752-776 (signalReceived) differ only in the `expected` name resolution (eventReceived uses __eventName; signalReceived prefers __signalName then __eventName) and the name compared against. The waiting-event match, correlation check, finished transition, and output-set blocks are byte-identical. Both are also production-dead (see prior finding).
recommendation: Extract a shared `resolveWaitingEvent(matchName, payload, correlationId, nameKeys)` helper used by both, or collapse to one method, to remove the copy-paste and the divergence risk.

title: Scheduler unit tests exercise a configuration the engine never uses
evidence: Every makeWorkflowSession call in tests passes only `{ nowMs: () => 1_000 }` (grep of tests shows the sole option object is `{ nowMs: () => 1_000 }`). But the engine ALWAYS constructs sessions with requireStableFinish: true and requireRerenderOnOutputChange: true (engine.js:5560-5561 and 5839). Consequently the production branches — decideAfterOutputChange's ReRender path (makeWorkflowSession.js:371-373), the requireStableFinish ReRender-before-finish (669-674) and ReRender-before-deadlock (648-653) — are never hit by tests (they take the false-branch decide() path instead). grep of tests for requireStableFinish/requireRerenderOnOutputChange/ReRender returns nothing.
recommendation: Mirror the engine's real options in the session test suite (requireStableFinish + requireRerenderOnOutputChange true) and add assertions on the ReRender decisions, so the tests validate the code path that actually ships.
vnote: Confirmed at the literal level but P1 is too high. Verified: every makeWorkflowSession(...) call in packages/scheduler/tests passes only { nowMs: () => 1_000 } (12 occurrences in workflowSession-retry.test.js and workflowSession-prune-approvals-retrycounts.test.js). grep for requireStableFinish/requireRerenderOnOutputChange/ReRender in scheduler tests returns nothing, while the engine always sets both true (engine.js:5560-5561, 5839). So the decideAfterOutputChange ReRender path (371-373), requireStableFinish ReRender-before-finish (669-674) and ReRender-before-deadlock (648-653) are not covered by scheduler-unit tests. However these production paths ARE exercised end-to-end by the real engine jsx test suites (the new engine is the default path and uses these options), so the risk is lower than P1 implies — it is a unit-coverage fidelity gap, not an entirely-untested ship path. P2.

title: DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK / describeDeadlock diagnostic has no scheduler-level test
evidence: makeWorkflowSession.js:207-242 (describeDeadlock) builds a detailed operator-facing diagnostic (missing-task hints, deps/needs guidance) and decide() returns DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK at 655-658. grep of packages/scheduler/tests for DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK/describeDeadlock/deadlock returns nothing. It is only covered indirectly by packages/engine/tests/deps-deadlock.test.jsx, not by the package that owns the string-building logic.
recommendation: Add a unit test that constructs a pending task with a dependsOn pointing at a missing node and asserts the Failed/DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK decision and the key phrases of the message (the 'no such task' branch and the deps/needs hint).

title: Approval 'continue' path stores resolution as output without usage; cache/output shape inconsistency
evidence: applyApprovalResolution (makeWorkflowSession.js:389-396) for approvalOnDeny==='continue' writes outputs.set(key, { nodeId, iteration, output: resolution }) with no `usage` field, whereas other completions go through markTaskFinished / cacheResolved which normalize usage (e.g. cacheResolved sets usage: output.usage ?? null at 822). finishedResult() reads `[...outputs.values()].at(-1)?.output` (295), so the final run output for a denied-but-continued gate is the ApprovalResolution object — likely intended, but the inconsistent TaskOutput shape (missing usage/text) is a latent inconsistency and is untested (only approvalResolved approved=true is tested, 1 call total).
recommendation: Route the 'continue' branch through a single output-writing helper that produces a consistent TaskOutput shape, and add tests for approvalOnDeny 'skip' / 'continue' / default-fail and approvalTimedOut.

title: decide() depth>10 guard silently swallows the decision into a Wait
evidence: decide(depth) at makeWorkflowSession.js:464-467 returns { _tag: 'Wait', reason: { _tag: 'ExternalTrigger' } } when depth>10. Only line 566 recurses with depth+1 (the `changed` re-evaluation loop); all other internal callers reset depth to 0. If a graph ever produced >10 successive state-mutating-but-non-dispatching passes, the run would silently suspend on ExternalTrigger with no diagnostic, rather than failing or logging. There is no test for this guard and no observability when it trips.
recommendation: Either prove the loop is bounded by construction and add a comment, or emit a diagnostic/Failed when the cap is hit so an infinite re-evaluation can't masquerade as a benign external-trigger wait. Add a regression test if the bound is intentional.

title: index.d.ts is a committed generated artifact serving as the type entry for ALL subpath exports
evidence: package.json maps every subpath export's `types` to the single ./src/index.d.ts (e.g. './TaskState', './Scheduler', and the catch-all './*'), and tsup.config.ts generates it via `pnpm build` (dts only). It is checked into git (git ls-files confirms; not gitignored) and dated 14:54 vs other src files 12:20 — i.e. it can drift from source if a contributor edits a .ts type but forgets to rebuild. There is no CI/test asserting index.d.ts is in sync with src.
recommendation: Add a check (or a generated-file guard like the repo's check-docs/check-llms gates) that fails CI when `tsup --dts-only` output differs from the committed index.d.ts, so the published types can't silently go stale.

title: inspect()/decide() JSDoc signatures omit the options/depth parameters
evidence: scheduleTasks.js inspect() is documented as `@param {PlanNode} node` (lines 124-127) but the implementation is inspect(node, options = {}) and threads options.includeContinuedFailures through every branch. Same pattern for makeWorkflowSession.js decide() documented `@returns {EngineDecision}` (461-463) while implemented as decide(depth = 0). The JSDoc (the only type annotation in these .js files) understates the real signature.
recommendation: Add the missing @param entries for options (inspect/collectFailureKeys) and depth (decide) so the JSDoc matches the implementation and downstream type extraction is accurate.

title: WorkflowSessionLive builds a single shared session — a latent correctness bug if ever consumed
evidence: packages/scheduler/src/WorkflowSessionLive.js:6 is Layer.sync(WorkflowSession, makeWorkflowSession), which constructs ONE WorkflowSession instance for the whole layer/runtime. makeWorkflowSession holds per-run mutable state (state.runId, state.states, state.descriptors, etc. in makeWorkflowSession.js:260-277). If anything ever resolved the WorkflowSession Tag, every run would share one stateful session — cross-run state corruption. It is currently harmless only because the Tag is never consumed (see confirmed dead-provisioning finding), but the layer encodes an incorrect singleton-session assumption. Pairs with the dead-code finding: this is dead AND wrong.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: signalReceived is byte-for-byte duplicate of eventReceived except the dead __signalName lookup
evidence: makeWorkflowSession.js eventReceived (729-751) and signalReceived (752-776) are identical except signalReceived first checks descriptor.meta?.__signalName (756-760) before falling back to __eventName. Since __signalName is never set by the extractor (packages/graph/src/extract.js:539-541 sets only __waitForEvent/__eventName/__correlationId) and __signalName appears nowhere else in the repo, signalReceived is functionally a copy of eventReceived. Both methods are also never called by any consumer. This is duplicated dead logic that should be collapsed or removed.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: #267 out-of-order completion concurrency machinery has zero tests anywhere
evidence: These four methods implement the subtle #267 fix: tasks run without blocking the loop, settle into `settledTasks`, and are reported one-at-a-time so a slow task never blocks scheduling work that became ready elsewhere. grep of all *.test.js/*.test.ts across packages for `startInflightTask|nextCompletionDecision|drainInflight|settledTasks|inflightTasks|267` returns ZERO matches. The driver package tests (tests/ctx-utils.test.js) only exercise `run` once with an empty-task graph; the deduplication (`inflightTasks.has(key)` early return at line 477-479), the deadline-elapsed re-submit of `lastGraph` (line 532-535), the `cancelled`-kind branch (line 537-539), the `taskFailed`-without-EngineDecision fallthrough to `getNextDecision` (line 551-557), and `drainInflight` (drain-before-failure/continue-as-new at lines 350/364) are never executed.
recommendation: Add driver-package unit tests with a fake session that returns Execute for two tasks then Wait, asserting: (a) a fast task's completion is reported before a slow sibling finishes; (b) duplicate Execute of an in-flight key is a no-op; (c) deadline elapse with no completion re-submits lastGraph; (d) drainInflight awaits all in-flight before a Failed/ContinueAsNew exit. These are correctness-critical and currently unguarded against regression.
vnote: PARTLY REFUTED on scope, CONFIRMED on the core gap. The auditor's grep for the named symbols (startInflightTask/nextCompletionDecision/drainInflight/settledTasks) is technically true (zero matches), but the claim 'these methods are never executed' and 'the only driver test is ctx-utils.test.js which only exercises run with an empty-task graph' is FALSE. The auditor missed packages/components/tests/driver.test.js, whose test 'drives render, submit, execute, re-render, and finish' returns an Execute decision with a real single task (staticPayload 'done'), which flows through the INHERITED WorkflowDriver.executeTasks -> startInflightTask -> defaultTaskExecutor -> settledTasks -> nextCompletionDecision -> session.taskCompleted -> getNextDecision fallthrough (WorkflowDriver.js:540-545,551-556). ReactWorkflowDriver (packages/react-reconciler/src/driver.js:8) only overrides the constructor, so all this machinery is the very code under audit. So lines 449-465, 475-497 (happy path, single task), 528,540-556 ARE covered. However the CONCURRENCY-SPECIFIC behaviors that are the actual point of #267 remain genuinely untested: (a) out-of-order fast-before-slow reporting (only single-task Execute exists), (b) the dedup early-return inflightTasks.has(key) at 477-479, (c) the deadline-elapsed re-submit of lastGraph at 532-535, (d) the cancelled-kind branches at 486-488 and 537-539 (the only abort test in components/driver.test.js aborts BEFORE submit at run() line 309, never entering executeTasks), (e) drainInflight at 350/354/364, and (f) the taskFailed-without-EngineDecision fallthrough at 546-556. Because the basic execution path IS covered and only the harder edge cases are not, P1 'zero tests anywhere' overstates it; adjusting to P2 as a real but narrower edge-case test gap.

title: Deferred-deps deadlock detection (DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK) is completely untested
evidence: When a task with `deps` defers forever and the run reaches a Finished decision, the driver converts it into `{status:'failed', error: SmithersError('DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK', ...)}` instead of silently skipping (WorkflowDriver.js:353-360). SmithersCtx.recordDeferredDep feeds ctx._deferredDeps, read at WorkflowDriver.js:435. grep across all test files for `describeDeferredDeadlock|DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK|lastDeferredDeps` returns no driver/engine test (only an unrelated 'never ran' string in packages/server/tests/getNodeDiff.test.js). This documented anti-silent-skip safety net has no regression guard.
recommendation: Add a test where the workflow build() calls ctx.recordDeferredDep(...) and the fake session returns Finished, asserting the run result is status:'failed' with the DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK message listing the never-run node. Also cover the empty-waitingOn vs non-empty-waitingOn message branches in describeDeferredDeadlock.
vnote: CONFIRMED. Grep across all *.test.js/*.test.ts in the repo for DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK, describeDeferredDeadlock, lastDeferredDeps, recordDeferredDep, and _deferredDeps returns ZERO matches. The driver-level path (WorkflowDriver.js:352-360: lastDeferredDeps.length>0 at a Finished decision -> drainInflight -> status:'failed' with SmithersError('DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK', describeDeferredDeadlock(...))) is unexercised. describeDeferredDeadlock (WorkflowDriver.js:182-196) and both its message branches (waitingOn.length===0 vs non-empty) are untested. SmithersCtx.recordDeferredDep (SmithersCtx.js:231-234) and ctx._deferredDeps -> WorkflowDriver.js:435 capture are untested. Note: there is a SECOND, distinct deadlock path in packages/scheduler/src/makeWorkflowSession.js:657 (describeDeadlock) which is ALSO untested (no scheduler test references it) - the finding only named the driver path but the gap is real and slightly broader. The non-test occurrences of DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK are all docs/llms bundles plus the two source files, never a test.

title: WorkflowDriver run-loop branches (Wait/ReRender/ContinueAsNew/Failed, abort, cancelRun, renderAndSubmit) have no direct driver-package tests
evidence: tests/ctx-utils.test.js exercises only: run->Finished, initializeSession (direct/effect/loaded), and unknown-decision->failed. The Execute/ReRender/Wait/ContinueAsNew/Failed branches, signal.aborted->cancelRun (lines 309-311, 328-330, 458-460, 525-527), handleWait's 7 WaitReason cases incl. RetryBackoff sleep path, continueAsNew with/without handler, and cancelRun's session.cancelRequested mapping are not covered here. Partial indirect coverage exists in packages/components/tests/driver.test.js (wait reasons, Failed, already-aborted cancel) but the driver package — the unit under test — barely touches its own flagship class, and mid-run abort (abort after work starts) and the renderAndSubmit worktree/deferred-deps/output-snapshot bookkeeping are untested anywhere.
recommendation: Mirror the ReactWorkflowDriver coverage from packages/components down into the driver package against the base WorkflowDriver, and add the missing mid-run-abort and renderAndSubmit (outputTablesByNodeId population, worktreePathsById, mergeOutputSnapshots) assertions.

title: Public RunOptions type omits initialOutputs/initialIteration/initialIterations that run() actually reads
evidence: WorkflowDriver.run() reads options.initialOutputs (line 307), options.outputs (307), options.initialIterations/iterations/ralphIterations (312-314), and options.initialIteration/iteration (317-320). The engine passes exactly these (packages/engine/src/engine.js:5747-5749). None of initialOutputs, initialIteration, initialIterations, outputs, iterations, ralphIterations, iteration appear in the exported RunOptions type (RunOptions.ts) which is shipped as a public subpath (@smithers-orchestrator/driver/RunOptions, 6 external importers). Anyone authoring a WorkflowDriver.run() call against the published type cannot discover these supported resume/seed fields.
recommendation: Add the runtime-read seed fields to RunOptions (or split a documented DriverRunOptions extends RunOptions) so the public type matches what run() consumes. Today the type silently undersells the API.

title: loadCreateSession has an unreachable createSession branch and a dead relative-path fallback
evidence: loadCreateSession() tries `mod.createSession` then `mod.makeWorkflowSession`. grep across packages for any `export function/const createSession` or `export { createSession }` returns nothing — the scheduler exports only makeWorkflowSession (packages/scheduler/src/index.js:48), so the createSession branch (line 166-167) is never taken. LOCAL_SCHEDULER_SPECIFIER='../../scheduler/src/index.js' (line 23) is a relative import that, from node_modules/@smithers-orchestrator/driver/src/WorkflowDriver.js, resolves to node_modules/@smithers-orchestrator/driver/scheduler/src/index.js (does not exist); the bare '@smithers-orchestrator/scheduler' specifier already wins first in the loop. Additionally the sole production instantiation (engine.js:5657) passes `session` directly, so initializeSession/loadCreateSession is only reachable from tests at all.
recommendation: Drop the createSession alias and the LOCAL_SCHEDULER_SPECIFIER relative fallback (or convert to a documented test-only seam). At minimum the createSession check should be removed since no module provides it.
vnote: CONFIRMED on substance, one reasoning detail corrected (severity unchanged at P2). The mod.createSession branch (WorkflowDriver.js:166-167) is genuinely unreachable: grep across all packages shows NO module exports createSession; scheduler/src/index.js exports only makeWorkflowSession (line 48), and the ctx-utils.test 'initializeSession can load the default scheduler session factory' (line 494-497) exercises only the makeWorkflowSession branch (168-169). The LOCAL_SCHEDULER_SPECIFIER='../../scheduler/src/index.js' fallback (line 23, 158) is also dead because '@smithers-orchestrator/scheduler' is a workspace:* dependency of driver (package.json:73) and is tried first in the loop, so it always resolves and returns before the second specifier is reached. CORRECTION to the auditor's reasoning: from packages/driver/src/WorkflowDriver.js the relative path actually resolves to packages/scheduler/src/index.js (which EXISTS), not to a nonexistent node_modules path as the auditor stated; the deadness comes from the first specifier always winning, not from the file being missing. Also confirmed: initializeSession/loadCreateSession is only reachable from tests in practice - the sole production instantiation (engine.js:5657) passes session: workflowSession directly, so run() at WorkflowDriver.js:308 short-circuits initializeSession. P2 dead-code severity is appropriate.

title: Cross-loop-boundary resolveRow fallback (unscoped-producer match) is untested
evidence: resolveRow has a deliberately narrow relaxation: when key.iteration is undefined and no exact-iteration row matched, it returns the first matching row whose nodeId carries no loop scope (`!row.nodeId.includes('@@')`) so an in-loop task can depend on an out-of-loop producer. The tests in tests/ctx-utils.test.js that pass key.iteration undefined ('resolveRow defaults missing key iteration to current iteration') always have an exact match at the current iteration, so the `matching.find(... !includes('@@'))` fallback line is never reached. This is the exact regression the comment warns about (dependent task resolving undefined and unmounting forever).
recommendation: Add a test: ctx with iteration>=1, outputs containing only an unscoped producer row at iteration 0, calling ctx.output('rows', {nodeId}) with no iteration, and assert it resolves the unscoped row; plus a negative test that an in-loop (scoped) producer at a not-yet-produced iteration still defers (returns undefined).

title: onSchedulerWait fires on every completion with a near-zero duration and empty tasks
evidence: nextCompletionDecision always invokes `this.onSchedulerWait?.(performance.now() - waitStart, {runId, tasks: []})` in the finally block, even when settledTasks already had an entry and no Promise.race wait actually occurred (the `if (this.settledTasks.length===0 && inflightTasks.size>0)` guard skips the await but the finally still runs). In production onSchedulerWait records the schedulerWaitDuration metric (engine.js:5666), so every out-of-order drain records a spurious ~0ms sample. The `tasks: []` is also always empty despite SchedulerWaitHandler's context typing a non-empty readonly TaskDescriptor[].
recommendation: Only call onSchedulerWait when an actual wait happened (a Promise.race was awaited), or document that the metric intentionally records every drain tick. Consider passing the real waiting tasks instead of always [].

title: Scheduler-level deadlock detection (makeWorkflowSession describeDeadlock) is also untested
evidence: While verifying the driver-level deadlock finding I found a second, independent DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK path inside the scheduler at makeWorkflowSession.js:657 using a local describeDeadlock(state). grep for 'describeDeadlock' and 'DEPENDENCY_DEADLOCK' across packages/scheduler/tests/ returns zero matches, so this scheduler-emitted deadlock failure (a distinct code path from the driver's describeDeferredDeadlock) has no regression guard either.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: TokenLimiter and Summarizer are no-op placeholders shipped as working public API and documented as functional
evidence: TokenLimiter.processEffect(_store) ignores the store entirely and only logs 'configured for N tokens ... operates at thread level' (line 15-22); charBudget is computed but never used to trim anything. Summarizer(_agent) never references the agent and processEffect(_store) only logs 'configured — operates at thread level'. Both return real MemoryProcessor objects. docs/concepts/memory.mdx:70-78 presents `TokenLimiter(4000) // keep history under token budget` and `Summarizer(myAgent) // compress old messages with an LLM` and a mermaid flow (TL trims history, SM compresses), and skills/smithers/llms-full.txt:7961-7976 repeats this. The processors.test.js only asserts `.name` and that `process()` 'does not throw' — i.e. tests assert the no-op.
recommendation: Either implement thread-scoped trimming/summarization (the processors receive a store but the code admits it needs a thread context they never get), or mark them clearly as unimplemented and fix docs/concepts/memory.mdx + the llms bundles to stop claiming they trim/compress. Drop the unused `_agent`/`charBudget` until implemented.
vnote: Verified packages/memory/src/TokenLimiter.js:15-23 — processEffect(_store) ignores the store and only logs 'TokenLimiter: configured for N tokens (charBudget chars) — operates at thread level'; charBudget is computed but never used to trim. Summarizer.js:8-26 — _agent is never referenced and processEffect(_store) only logs 'Summarizer: configured — operates at thread level'. Contrast with TtlGarbageCollector.js:12-16 which actually calls store.deleteExpiredFactsEffect(). docs/concepts/memory.mdx:73-86 presents TokenLimiter(4000) // keep history under token budget, Summarizer(myAgent) // compress old messages with an LLM, and a mermaid flow (TL 'trim history to budget', SM 'compress old messages with an LLM'). skills/smithers/llms-full.txt:7961-7976 repeats the same. processors.test.js:40-71 asserts only .name and that process() does not throw — i.e. tests assert the no-op. Confirmed exactly as stated.

title: MemoryService / createMemoryLayer Effect layer and <Task memory> recall/remember are never wired into any runtime
evidence: Grep across the whole repo (excluding packages/memory and the packages/smithers re-export at index.js:233) finds zero consumers of createMemoryLayer or MemoryService. The graph extracts `memoryConfig` from `<Task memory={...}>` (packages/graph/src/extract.js:671, dom/extract.js:824) but the only read of `memoryConfig` anywhere is a components test assertion (packages/components/tests/components.test.js:175) — the engine/scheduler runtime (packages/engine/src/effect/single-runner.js, builder.js) never reads memoryConfig and never provides the MemoryService layer. The CLI `smithers memory list` only uses createMemoryStore + parseNamespace + listFacts (apps/cli/src/index.js:2240-2247). Generated workflow packs emit `memory={{ recall, remember }}` (apps/cli/src/workflow-pack.js:3634-3789) that does nothing at execution.
recommendation: Confirm intent (ticket .smithers/tickets/smithers/0028 treats recall as a dormant future feature). If recall/remember is not executed, the Effect service layer is unexercised infrastructure — either consume it in the runtime or trim it to what's actually used (the store) and stop emitting non-functional `memory={{recall,remember}}` in generated packs / docs that imply it works.
vnote: Partly confirmed, but the 'dead-code / unexercised infrastructure' framing is wrong: createMemoryLayer + MemoryService ARE exercised by packages/memory/tests/service.test.js (6 tests at lines 14-128 exercise the Effect layer end-to-end, including one that explicitly asserts recall is NOT on the API surface). So the Effect service is tested public API, not dead/unexercised code. The ACCURATE and important part stands: no PRODUCTION runtime consumes them. Grep of packages/engine, packages/scheduler, apps/cli/src finds zero reads of memoryConfig and zero provides of MemoryService. memoryConfig is extracted by graph/src/extract.js:671 and dom/extract.js:824 but read nowhere except components/tests/components.test.js:175. apps/cli/src/index.js:2240-2247 only uses createMemoryStore+parseNamespace+listFacts. workflow-pack.js:3633-3636 emits memory={{ recall: {...}, remember: {...} }} into generated mission packs that does nothing at execution. Ticket .smithers/tickets/smithers/0028 confirms recall/vectors are a dormant future feature. Net: not dead code, but the generated packs + docs imply recall/remember works when the runtime never executes it. Downgraded to P2 because it is a documentation/misleading-emission issue over tested-but-unwired API, not an unexercised dead layer.

title: Error paths untested for 9 of 11 store operations
evidence: Only getFact (read failure) and setFact (write failure) error-wrapping are tested via fake throwing dbs. deleteFactEffect, listFactsEffect, createThreadEffect, getThreadEffect, deleteThreadEffect (two writes), saveMessageEffect, listMessagesEffect, countMessagesEffect, deleteExpiredFactsEffect (all in MemoryStoreLive.js:139-276) have no failure-path test — their toSmithersError mapping (DB_QUERY_FAILED vs DB_WRITE_FAILED, operation label) is unverified. deleteThreadEffect deletes messages then thread; a failure between the two (partial delete, no transaction) is untested.
recommendation: Add failure-injection tests (throwing fake db like the existing ones) for each remaining op, asserting the correct code/label. Consider wrapping deleteThread's two deletes in a transaction and testing partial-failure behavior.
vnote: Verified store.test.js:85-103 only tests getFact (read failure via throwing select()) and setFact (write failure via throwing insert().values()). The remaining ops in MemoryStoreLive.js — deleteFactEffect (139-144), listFactsEffect (149-164), createThreadEffect (171-184), getThreadEffect (189-195), deleteThreadEffect (200-211, two writes), saveMessageEffect (217-230), listMessagesEffect (236-256), countMessagesEffect (261-266), deleteExpiredFactsEffect (271-276) — have no failure-path test. Grep for 'boom|rejects|throw|DB_QUERY_FAILED|DB_WRITE_FAILED|toThrow' in e2e.test.js, service.test.js, types.test.js, store-concurrent.test.js returns nothing. Their toSmithersError mapping (DB_QUERY_FAILED vs DB_WRITE_FAILED, operation label) is unverified. Confirmed.

title: Boundary/branch coverage gaps: ttlMs=0, undefined value, listMessages limit=0, deleteExpiredFacts driver fallback, exposed store handle
evidence: setFact stores `ttlMs ?? null` so ttlMs=0 persists as 0 (deleteExpiredFacts then expires it immediately since updatedAtMs+0<now) — never tested, and arguably wrong vs 'no TTL'. setFact does `JSON.stringify(value)`; value=undefined yields valueJson=undefined (not a string) → NOT NULL insert failure / later JSON.parse('undefined') throw — no guard, no test. listMessagesEffect uses `if (limit)` (line 243) so limit=0 returns ALL messages instead of none — untested boundary. deleteExpiredFactsEffect uses `result?.changes ?? result?.rowsAffected ?? 0` (line 275) but only the bun-sqlite .changes path is exercised; rowsAffected and the 0 fallback are dead-untested. countMessagesEffect `rows[0]?.count ?? 0` fallback untested. service.test.js:119-128 only asserts `memory.store != null`, never calls a method through the exposed store.
recommendation: Add tests for ttlMs=0 semantics (and decide intended behavior), undefined/non-serializable value handling, listMessages(threadId,0), and at least one call through memory.store.*Effect. Decide whether ttlMs=0 should mean 'no TTL' and guard JSON.stringify against undefined.

title: react-types.ts is dead code (zero references anywhere)
evidence: Full-repo grep for 'react-types' returns nothing outside the file itself. It re-exports TaskMemoryConfig from @smithers-orchestrator/graph/types but is not an entry in tsup.config.ts (entry is only src/index.js), not imported by any .js/.ts, and not in package.json exports beyond the catch-all ./* mapping. The package's own TaskMemoryConfig comes from src/TaskMemoryConfig.ts, not this file.
recommendation: Delete src/react-types.ts.
vnote: Verified packages/memory/src/react-types.ts contains only 'export type { TaskMemoryConfig } from "@smithers-orchestrator/graph/types";'. Full-repo grep for 'react-types' (excluding node_modules/dist) returns nothing outside the file. It is a .ts file, so the package.json './*' export (which maps to ./src/*.js) cannot even surface it; tsup.config.ts entry is only src/index.js. The package's own TaskMemoryConfig comes from src/TaskMemoryConfig.ts (index.js:14 imports './TaskMemoryConfig.ts'), not react-types.ts. Truly unused. Confirmed.

title: TaskMemoryConfig defined three times with a diverging shape
evidence: TaskMemoryConfig exists in (a) packages/memory/src/TaskMemoryConfig.ts with an extra top-level `namespace?: string | MemoryNamespace` field, (b) packages/graph/src/types.ts:106 (the canonical one used by GraphNode.memoryConfig at types.ts:195) which has NO top-level namespace, and (c) packages/memory/src/react-types.ts re-exporting (b). The memory package exports (a) (index.d.ts:25-37 includes the namespace field) and packages/components/src/components/TaskProps.ts:8 imports it via @smithers-orchestrator/memory/types → src/types.js → index.d.ts. So the type the component prop advertises (with top-level `namespace`) diverges from the type the graph extractor actually parses (`recall`/`remember`/`threadId` only). The top-level `namespace` field is undocumented (docs/concepts/memory.mdx shows only recall/remember/threadId) and unused.
recommendation: Make memory re-export the single canonical TaskMemoryConfig from @smithers-orchestrator/graph (drop the divergent src/TaskMemoryConfig.ts and its stray top-level `namespace`), so the component prop type and the graph parser agree.

title: Four exported config types have no consumers (speculative public API)
evidence: Repo-wide grep (excluding packages/memory and the packages/smithers index re-export) finds zero consumers of WorkingMemoryConfig, MessageHistoryConfig, MemoryProcessorConfig, SemanticRecallConfig. They appear only in their own source + the generated index.d.ts export list. WorkingMemoryConfig.schema (a zod object) is never read or validated by any code; SemanticRecallConfig is explicitly called a 'type stub ... never implemented' in .smithers/tickets/smithers/0028. MemoryProcessorConfig.processors (string[]) is never consumed by any processor registry.
recommendation: Either delete these until their features exist, or keep them only if 0028 is actively being implemented — but stop exporting them as if they were live API. At minimum document them as forward-looking stubs.
vnote: Verified WorkingMemoryConfig.ts, MessageHistoryConfig.ts, MemoryProcessorConfig.ts, SemanticRecallConfig.ts. Repo-wide grep (excluding their own files, packages/memory/src/index.d.ts, packages/smithers re-exports) finds only typedef re-declarations in packages/memory/src/types.js:8,11,12,16 and index.js:8,12,13,17 (pure re-export plumbing), plus a comment in service.test.js:78. No code annotates a real parameter/function or validates a value with any of them. WorkingMemoryConfig's schema?: T (generic ZodObject) is never read/validated. SemanticRecallConfig is called a 'type stub ... never implemented' in ticket 0028:42-44. MemoryProcessorConfig.processors (string[]) is consumed by no processor registry. Confirmed as forward-looking stubs exported as live API.

title: No test for percent-encoding round-trip of namespace ids containing % or : for enumerated kinds
evidence: encodeNamespaceId escapes % then : (`%`→`%25`, `:`→`%3A`); decodeNamespaceId reverses in the correct order. But tests only cover round-trips for ids WITHOUT % (types.test.js, store-concurrent.test.js:132-144). store-concurrent.test.js:156 even asserts the decode does NOT run for unknown kinds. No test feeds an enumerated-kind namespace whose id contains a literal '%' or ':' (e.g. {kind:'workflow', id:'100%:done'}) through namespaceToString→parseNamespace to lock the escape ordering. The decode branch (decodeNamespaceId for enumerated kinds with escaped chars) is therefore unexercised for the case it exists to handle.
recommendation: Add a round-trip test for enumerated-kind ids containing '%', ':' and combinations to pin the escape/unescape ordering against regression.

title: deleteThread is non-transactional across two writes (partial-delete risk)
evidence: deleteThreadEffect runs `delete messages` then `delete thread` as two separate writeEffect calls inside Effect.gen with no transaction. If the second delete fails, the messages are gone but the thread row remains (orphaned thread / partial state). No transaction wrapper and no test covers a mid-operation failure.
recommendation: Wrap the two deletes in a single SQLite transaction (drizzle db.transaction) so deleteThread is atomic, and add a partial-failure test.
vnote: Verified MemoryStoreLive.js:200-211. deleteThreadEffect runs writeEffect('memory deleteThreadMessages', delete messages) then writeEffect('memory deleteThread', delete thread) as two sequential writes inside Effect.gen with no transaction. If the second delete fails, messages are gone but the thread row remains (orphaned empty thread). Grep confirms no '.transaction(' anywhere in packages/memory/src, and no mid-operation failure test. P2/medium is appropriate: real consistency gap but low practical likelihood (second delete failing immediately after the first succeeds), and the worst case is an orphaned empty thread row, not data corruption.

title: Committed generated index.d.ts is stale — public KnownSmithersErrorCode/SmithersErrorCode types are missing 6 real error codes
evidence: smithersErrorDefinitions.js defines 92 codes; the checked-in index.d.ts namespace declares only 86. comm -23 shows 6 codes present in the JS runtime catalog but absent from the d.ts: AGENT_CONFIG_INVALID, TASK_FORK_CYCLE, TASK_FORK_SESSION_UNAVAILABLE, TASK_FORK_SOURCE_NOT_COMPLETE, TASK_FORK_SOURCE_NOT_FOUND, TASK_HIJACK_UNSUPPORTED. These codes ARE emitted by real code (packages/graph/src/validateForkSources.js, packages/scheduler/src/makeWorkflowSession.js, packages/agents/src/*Agent.js) and ARE listed in docs/reference/types.mdx's KnownSmithersErrorCode union. Because KnownSmithersErrorCode = keyof typeof smithersErrorDefinitions and SmithersErrorCode derives from it, every external type consumer (the package's only published .d.ts) sees a union that disagrees with both the runtime catalog and the docs. The d.ts is a build artifact (tsup --dts-only) that was not regenerated after these codes were added.
recommendation: Regenerate index.d.ts (pnpm -C packages/errors build) and commit, or add a CI check that fails when the committed d.ts diverges from the source catalog. Consider not committing the generated d.ts at all if a per-file declaration strategy can be used.
vnote: IMPACT CONFIRMED (P1), ROOT CAUSE REFUTED. Verified exactly 6 codes (AGENT_CONFIG_INVALID, TASK_FORK_CYCLE, TASK_FORK_SESSION_UNAVAILABLE, TASK_FORK_SOURCE_NOT_COMPLETE, TASK_FORK_SOURCE_NOT_FOUND, TASK_HIJACK_UNSUPPORTED) are in the 92-key smithersErrorDefinitions.js runtime catalog and in CI-enforced docs (check-docs.mjs validates errors.mdx + types.mdx KnownSmithersErrorCode against the JS catalog) but absent from the 86-key `declare namespace smithersErrorDefinitions` in committed src/index.d.ts (package.json types -> ./src/index.d.ts). End-to-end proof: `npx tsc` rejects `const f: KnownSmithersErrorCode = "TASK_HIJACK_UNSUPPORTED"` (TS2322, union has 86 members) while "RUN_NOT_FOUND" is accepted. All 6 codes are emitted by real product code (graph/validateForkSources.js, scheduler/makeWorkflowSession.js, engine/engine.js, apps/cli). HOWEVER the auditor's premise that the d.ts is 'stale / not regenerated' is WRONG: I ran `pnpm -C packages/errors build` (tsup --dts-only) and the regenerated index.d.ts is BYTE-IDENTICAL (git status clean, still 86 codes). Real root cause: tsup's bundled-declaration generator silently drops the consecutive TASK_HIJACK_UNSUPPORTED + 4 TASK_FORK_* block (src lines 51-75) plus AGENT_CONFIG_INVALID when merging the namespace — plain `tsc --emitDeclarationOnly` on the same file emits all 92. So the recommendation 'regenerate and commit' would NOT fix it.

title: isSmithersError returns true for EngineError, so toSmithersError(engineError) returns a non-SmithersError and violates its @returns {SmithersError} contract
evidence: isSmithersError matches any object with a string `code` that is a known code plus a `message`. EngineError (Data.TaggedError) has code:'SCHEDULER_ERROR' and message, so isSmithersError(engineError) === true (verified at runtime). In toSmithersError, isSmithersErrorLike(cause) is therefore true for an EngineError; with no label/options the early-return at lines 40-45 fires and returns the raw EngineError instance unchanged. Verified: toSmithersError(new EngineError(...)) returns the same instance, w instanceof SmithersError === false, and w.summary === undefined. Callers relying on the documented SmithersError return shape (.summary/.docsUrl) get a non-SmithersError. The dedicated `normalizedCause instanceof EngineError ? normalizedCause.code` branch at toSmithersError.js:49 is also dead because the smithersCause branch already captures EngineError.
recommendation: Either make isSmithersError exclude tagged errors (e.g. reject values whose _tag is a known tagged tag, or require a SmithersError brand) or have toSmithersError treat EngineError before the isSmithersErrorLike check. Add a regression test asserting toSmithersError(engineError) returns an instanceof SmithersError with a populated summary.
vnote: RUNTIME BEHAVIOR CONFIRMED, SEVERITY LOWERED to P2. Verified at runtime: isSmithersError(new EngineError({code:'SCHEDULER_ERROR',message:'boom'})) === true; toSmithersError(engineError) with no label/code/details hits the early-return at toSmithersError.js:40-45 and returns the SAME EngineError instance (w === e), w instanceof SmithersError === false, w.summary === undefined — a real @returns {SmithersError} violation. BUT only reachable through EngineError, which is NEVER constructed in product code (grep `new EngineError`/`_tag:'EngineError'` = 0 outside packages/errors; no deserialization path) and no other product Data.TaggedError carries a `code` that is a known SmithersErrorCode. So latent (dead-code-gated), not active P1. Secondary 'line 49 is dead' claim is IMPRECISE: line 49 IS reachable for an EngineError carrying an UNKNOWN code — verified toSmithersError(new EngineError({code:'MADE_UP_CODE'})) yields w.code==='MADE_UP_CODE'; inert only because EngineError is never built. No regression test covers the label-less path.

title: EngineError class and EngineErrorCode type are dead across the whole repo
evidence: Repo-wide grep (packages, apps, .smithers, scripts, docs) for `new EngineError`, `EngineError(` and imports of EngineError outside packages/errors returns ZERO hits. EngineErrorCode type also has zero external usages. EngineError is not re-exported from the public barrel (packages/smithers/src/index.js exposes errorToJson, getSmithersErrorDefinition, etc. but not EngineError) and is not in the documented error surface (docs/reference/errors.mdx). It is referenced only internally by toSmithersError.js and the also-dead tagged.js. The dedicated EngineError handling branches in toSmithersError.js (lines 12-14, 49, 54) and causeSummary cannot be exercised by any real EngineError because none is ever constructed.
recommendation: Remove EngineError.js, EngineErrorCode.ts, and the EngineError branches in toSmithersError.js (and the EngineError import/re-export in tagged.js), or, if it is intended public API, construct it somewhere and re-export it from the smithers barrel + document it.
vnote: CONFIRMED with minor imprecision (P2 stands). EngineError is never constructed in product code, never deserialized, not documented (errors.mdx/types.mdx), and NOT re-exported from the smithers meta-barrel (packages/smithers/src/index.js curates errorToJson/SmithersError/etc. but omits EngineError). EngineErrorCode type has zero external usages. CORRECTIONS: (1) EngineError IS re-exported from @smithers-orchestrator/errors public surface — packages/errors/src/index.js:18 and the published d.ts export list (index.d.ts:901 exports EngineError + type EngineErrorCode), so it is reachable external API, not literally unreferenced; (2) it is constructed by the test errors-core.test.js:360, so removal breaks that test. 'Dead across the whole repo' overstates — accurate: exported public API never used internally, never constructed in product code, undocumented.

title: tagged.js is an orphan barrel — never imported anywhere
evidence: tagged.js re-exports a subset of the package (tagged errors, fromTaggedError, etc.) and is reachable only via the package.json `./*` subpath export (`@smithers-orchestrator/errors/tagged`). Repo-wide grep for `errors/tagged` as a module specifier finds only a stale planning note (.smithers/specs/package-restructure-plan.md mentioning src/errors/tagged.ts); no source file imports it. It is not referenced by index.js either. It has zero test coverage.
recommendation: Delete tagged.js, or if it is a deliberate public entrypoint, import it somewhere and cover it with a test.
vnote: CONFIRMED (P2). grep across packages/apps/.smithers/scripts/docs for any import of tagged.js returns ZERO real imports; the only 'errors/tagged' hit is a stale restructure note (.smithers/specs/package-restructure-plan.md:204) referencing a different old path src/errors/tagged.ts, not an import. tagged.js is reachable only via the package.json `./*` glob subpath export and is not referenced by index.js; no test imports it (tests/ has only errors-core.test.js). Nuance: every src/*.js is exposed via the same `./*` glob, so tagged.js is not a uniquely-declared entrypoint, just an unused convenience barrel.

title: errorToJson loses code/context for EngineError (and any tagged error fromTaggedError can't map)
evidence: fromTaggedError only maps the 8 SmithersTaggedError tags; for any other _tag (including EngineError, whose _tag is 'EngineError') it hits `default: return undefined`. errorToJson then treats EngineError as a plain Error and emits only {name,message,stack} — verified output: {"name":"EngineError","message":"boom","stack":...} with no code or context. Currently inert because EngineError is never constructed, but if any tagged error outside the known 8 reaches errorToJson its structured fields are silently dropped.
recommendation: If EngineError stays, give errorToJson an explicit EngineError branch (serialize code/context) or have fromTaggedError handle it; otherwise removing EngineError (see other finding) makes this moot. Either way add a test for errorToJson on a tagged/engine error.
vnote: RUNTIME BEHAVIOR CONFIRMED (P2). Verified errorToJson(new EngineError({code:'SCHEDULER_ERROR',message:'boom',context:{...}})) returns {name:'EngineError',message:'boom',stack:...} — code/context dropped because fromTaggedError has no 'EngineError' case (default: undefined) and it falls to the plain-Error branch. Inert in practice (EngineError never constructed). Refinement: the generic tagged-error case IS already tested — errors-core.test.js:465 asserts errorToJson(new RunNotFound(...)) yields {code:'RUN_NOT_FOUND', details:{runId}}. Only the EngineError-specific path (and any future non-8 tagged _tag) is untested, so the gap is narrower than 'no test for errorToJson on a tagged/engine error.'

title: Untested functions, branches, and error paths vs the ~100% bar
evidence: No test covers: (1) tagged.js barrel (0 tests, 0 imports); (2) isSmithersError(engineError) false-positive (the surprising true result is unasserted); (3) toSmithersError(engineError) with NO label — only the labeled EngineError path is tested at line 359, so the early-return-returns-EngineError bug is uncaught; (4) fromTaggedError's default/unknown-tag branch (lines 60-62) and EngineError tag; (5) errorToJson(engineError) code/context loss; (6) errorToJson asserting the `cause` field (json.cause is never asserted) and the default name 'SmithersError' (only the custom-name path at line 183 is checked); (7) SmithersError constructed with an options object that has only `name` and no cause (verified: cause becomes undefined) — the isOptionsObject hasOwnProperty('name') branch is only tested alongside a cause; (8) WorkflowFailed/TaskAborted payload round-trip when status/name/details are omitted (e.g. TaskAborted with no name emits name:'TaskAborted').
recommendation: Add targeted tests for tagged.js exports, EngineError interaction (isSmithersError/toSmithersError/errorToJson), fromTaggedError unknown-tag default, errorToJson cause + default name, options-object-with-only-name, and omitted-field payload round-trips.

title: Five tagged-error classes are never constructed anywhere in the repo
evidence: grep for `new RunNotFound(`, `new InvalidInput(`, `new DbWriteFailed(`, `new AgentCliError(`, `new WorkflowFailed(` across packages/apps/.smithers (excluding packages/errors) returns 0 for all five; only TaskAborted, TaskTimeout, TaskHeartbeatTimeout are constructed (in engine/scheduler). The five exist solely to support fromTaggedErrorPayload/toTaggedErrorPayload round-tripping at the worker boundary (packages/engine/src/effect/single-runner.js).
recommendation: Lower priority and lower confidence: they are part of the closed SmithersTaggedErrorPayload serialization contract, so keeping them is defensible. If the durable payload union is meant to be exactly these 8, document that intent; otherwise prune the never-emitted tags. Verify with engine maintainers before removing.
vnote: ADJUSTED for accuracy; P2 stands. Title 'never constructed anywhere' is FALSE: RunNotFound, InvalidInput, DbWriteFailed, AgentCliError, WorkflowFailed ARE all constructed in packages/errors/src/fromTaggedErrorPayload.js:42-66 (the auditor's grep excluded 'packages/errors' which contains that file). Accurate statement (which the finding's body itself makes): they exist only for the symmetric fromTaggedErrorPayload/toTaggedErrorPayload round-trip, and are never constructed in product code OUTSIDE that closed round-trip, so the 5 tags are inert because no product code ever produces an instance to serialize. Defensible closed-union contract — keep but document intent; verify with engine maintainers before pruning.

title: tsup bundled-declaration generation silently drops error codes; published types diverge from runtime catalog with no CI guard
evidence: The committed src/index.d.ts is regenerated identically by `tsup --dts-only` yet drops 6 of 92 codes from `declare namespace smithersErrorDefinitions` (regenerated file byte-identical, git status clean, still 86 codes). Plain `tsc --emitDeclarationOnly` on src/smithersErrorDefinitions.js emits all 92. The dropped set is the consecutive block TASK_HIJACK_UNSUPPORTED + TASK_FORK_SOURCE_NOT_FOUND + TASK_FORK_SOURCE_NOT_COMPLETE + TASK_FORK_SESSION_UNAVAILABLE + TASK_FORK_CYCLE (src lines 51-75) plus AGENT_CONFIG_INVALID (line 293). check-docs.mjs validates docs/reference/errors.mdx and types.mdx KnownSmithersErrorCode against smithersErrorDefinitions.js (docs stay at 92) but NOTHING validates the published src/index.d.ts against the runtime catalog, so the only .d.ts shipped to TS consumers silently disagrees with both the runtime and the CI-gated docs. This is the real mechanism behind audit finding #1, and it means the proposed 'regenerate the d.ts' fix is ineffective. Suggested fix: emit per-file declarations with tsc (or stop committing a bundled d.ts) and add a check asserting the published KnownSmithersErrorCode union equals Object.keys(smithersErrorDefinitions).
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: No regression test for the label-less toSmithersError early-return on a non-SmithersError tagged cause
evidence: The only EngineError test (line 359) always passes a label ('schedule'), exercising the normalize/wrap path and never the early-return at toSmithersError.js:40-45. No test asserts toSmithersError(engineError) with no label returns an instanceof SmithersError with a populated summary (the path that currently returns a raw non-SmithersError). Line 49 (`normalizedCause instanceof EngineError ? normalizedCause.code`), reachable only via an unknown-code EngineError, has zero test coverage. errorToJson(engineError) (fromTaggedError -> undefined -> plain-Error branch dropping code/context) is also untested. Suggested fix: add tests for these three paths (or they become moot if EngineError is removed as dead code).
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: runs.rerun uses backend-specific raw SQL with a hardcoded `payload` column
evidence: `const client = (resolved.workflow.db.session?.client ?? resolved.workflow.db.$client); const row = client?.query?.("SELECT payload FROM input WHERE run_id = ? LIMIT 1").get(runId);`. (1) `.query(...).get()` is the bun:sqlite API; engine/src/effect/builder.js:777 shows the codebase also targets Postgres (`client.query({ text })`), where there is no `.get()` — the optional chaining silently yields `input = {}` (reruns with empty input) instead of erroring. (2) The default input table has a `payload` column, but packages/smithers/src/create.js:188 builds the `input` table from a user-supplied zod schema via `zodToTable("input", schemas.input, { isInput: true })`, which may have no `payload` column at all — the query then throws/returns nothing. create.js comment (lines 180-182) explicitly says these tables are 'never to issue SQLite queries' and must work 'across every backend'. A proper helper, loadInput() (packages/db/src/loadInputEffect.js), already exists and resolves the runId column generically.
recommendation: Replace the raw `SELECT payload FROM input` with the existing `loadInput(db, inputTable, runId)` helper (resolve the input table via resolved.workflow.inputTable / drizzleSchema.input) so rerun works on custom input schemas and non-SQLite backends, then reconstruct the rerun input from the loaded row.
vnote: Verified gateway.js:4542-4546 issues raw `client?.query?.("SELECT payload FROM input WHERE run_id = ? LIMIT 1").get(runId)`. Two real defects confirmed: (1) `.query(...).get()` is the bun:sqlite API; engine/src/effect/builder.js:773-777 builds a Postgres adapter whose client uses `client.query({ text })` with no `.get()`, so the optional chaining silently yields no row -> rerun launches with `{}` input instead of erroring. (2) packages/db/src/zodToTable.js:22-58 shows that when schemas.input is supplied (create.js:188-189 `zodToTable("input", schemas.input, { isInput: true })`), the input table has `runId` + one named column per zod field and NO `payload` column, so `SELECT payload` is wrong for custom schemas. The generic helper loadInput(db, inputTable, runId) exists (packages/db/src/snapshot.js:152, originally loadInputEffect.js) and is the canonical pattern used in 15+ engine/cli sites (engine.js:1427,1622,5123,...; hijack-session.js:73; cli/index.js:4932), resolving the input table via resolveSchema(db) (engine.js:2020). The workflow object returned by resolveRun carries db/tables so the fix is feasible. Real P1 bug.

title: Several gateway RPC methods are untested (runs.rerun, signals.send/submitSignal, cronRun/cron.trigger, hijackRun)
evidence: Grep across packages/server/tests for these method strings: `hijackRun`, `runs.rerun`/`rerun`, `submitSignal`/`signals.send`, and `cronRun`/manual `cron.trigger` return no matches in the routeRequest test path. signalName hits in gateway-webhooks.test.jsx come from webhook config, not the signals.send RPC. The webhooks test exercises signalRun() indirectly but never the gateway's signals.send/submitSignal dispatch (param normalization: correlationKey→signalName, data/payload fallback, resumeRunIfNeeded).
recommendation: Add routeRequest-level tests for signals.send/submitSignal (correlationKey aliasing + resume), cron.trigger/cronRun (manual trigger updates cron run time and starts a run), hijackRun (returns sessionId for a real run, RunNotFound otherwise), and runs.rerun (after fixing the SQL bug) against the real Gateway+SQLite stack already used elsewhere.
vnote: Partly correct but the cron claim is wrong. CONFIRMED untested through the real Gateway routeRequest: runs.rerun (no test anywhere), hijackRun (no routeRequest test; CLI 'hijack' refs are the separate hijack-session.js flow), and signals.send/submitSignal. The submitSignal test in e2e/faults/case14-gateway-rpc-roundtrip.test.ts:695 is NOT real e2e against gateway.js -- startGateway (line 382) is a hand-rolled WebSocketServer that reimplements RPC via handleRpc (line 269 for submitSignal); the file even has a test.skip at line 853 noting the real packages/server Gateway can't boot in e2e. The webhooks test (gateway-webhooks.test.jsx) exercises the webhook->signalRun HTTP path, not the signals.send RPC dispatch. REFUTED: cronRun/cron.trigger IS tested via the real Gateway in gateway.test.jsx:705 (full cron.add/list/trigger/remove lifecycle asserting a real run reaches finished). Severity P1 remains appropriate for the three confirmed gaps.

title: HTTP server (index.js) signal route and key error branches are untested
evidence: server.test.js (658 LOC) covers POST/GET runs, cancel, resume, frames, approve/deny, SSE events, list, approvals, 404/500. But grep for 'signal' in server.test.js finds only the SSE abort controller — the `/v1/runs/:runId/signals/:signalName` and legacy `/signal/:runId/:signalName` POST handler (signalRun + delivered response) has no test. Also untested: 409 RUN_ALREADY_EXISTS on duplicate non-resume create, RUN_ID_REQUIRED when resume true without runId, the resume short-circuit when an existing run's heartbeat is fresh, the waiting-timer cancel branch that cancels timer attempts and emits TimerCancelled, and GET /metrics.
recommendation: Extend server.test.js with cases for the signal route (happy path + 404 unknown run), duplicate-run 409, resume-without-runId 400, resume-of-fresh-run short circuit, waiting-timer cancel (asserting TimerCancelled event + cancelled attempt), and GET /metrics returning Prometheus text.
vnote: server.test.js (658 LOC) tests startServer from index.js but its only 'signal' reference is the SSE AbortController (line 476). Grep across all packages/server tests found NO RUN_ALREADY_EXISTS, RUN_ID_REQUIRED, or TimerCancelled, and no test hitting /v1/runs/:runId/signals/:signalName or /signal/:runId/:signalName. Verified the real, uncovered branches: signal route index.js:1117-1137 (signalRun + delivered + 404), 409 RUN_ALREADY_EXISTS index.js:724-726, 400 RUN_ID_REQUIRED index.js:715-716, resume fresh-heartbeat short-circuit index.js:721-723, waiting-timer cancel/TimerCancelled index.js:871-910, and GET /metrics index.js:673. NOTE: serve.test.js DOES have a GET /metrics suite (lines 509-618) but it tests createServeApp from serve.js -- a DIFFERENT HTTP server -- so index.js:673 remains untested. Test list in server.test.js confirms only create/get/cancel(active)/resume/frames/approve/deny/SSE/list/approvals/404/500 are covered.

title: Connection-limit (503) WS upgrade rejection path is untested
evidence: The `server.on('upgrade')` handler writes a raw `HTTP/1.1 503 Service Unavailable` and destroys the socket when `this.connections.size >= this.maxConnections`. Grep for `maxConnections`/`503`/`Service Unavailable`/`connection limit` across tests finds only streamDevTools.test.tsx line 362 ('rejects cross-run requests with Unauthorized when connection limits runs') which is about run-subscription scoping, not the connection-count cap. The 503 branch has no coverage.
recommendation: Add a test constructing a Gateway with maxConnections: 1, opening one WS, then asserting a second upgrade attempt receives HTTP 503 with the X-Smithers-API-Version header and is closed.

title: Built-in operator UI (1.4k-line stringified browser app) has zero behavioral coverage
evidence: `defaultOperatorUiClient()` is a ~1,400-line browser function (token storage, render, refresh, RPC calls, setInterval(refresh,5000)) serialized via `(${fn.toString()})();` into DEFAULT_OPERATOR_UI_CLIENT_JS. gateway-ui.test.jsx only fetches the HTML shell + client.js asset over HTTP and asserts boot-config strings and the auth gate; it never executes the client logic. None of the in-browser behavior (auth flow, polling, error handling) is exercised.
recommendation: Either add a jsdom/Playwright test that boots the stringified client against a real Gateway and asserts it renders runs and reacts to token entry, or split the non-trivial logic into importable, unit-testable helpers so it isn't a single untested string blob.

title: getNodeDiffRoute documents and destructures parameters it never uses (getCurrentPointerImpl, restorePointerImpl)
evidence: The JSDoc opts type still lists `getCurrentPointerImpl?` and `restorePointerImpl?`, and the function destructures them as `getCurrentPointerImpl: _getCurrentPointerImpl` and `restorePointerImpl: _restorePointerImpl` (underscore = intentionally unused). The read-only diff rewrite (it now resolves both endpoints to commit IDs and never restores the working copy) made them obsolete. getNodeDiff.test.js passes these in ~10 places (lines 80-528) but the impl ignores the values, so the tests assert a contract that no longer exists.
recommendation: Drop both parameters from the JSDoc and the destructure, and remove them from the getNodeDiff.test.js call sites so the documented/tested surface matches the implementation.
vnote: Verified getNodeDiff.js: JSDoc lists getCurrentPointerImpl (line 260) and restorePointerImpl (line 262); the function destructures them as `_getCurrentPointerImpl` (276) and `_restorePointerImpl` (278) with underscore = intentionally unused, and grep confirms those names appear ONLY at the destructure -- never referenced in the body. getNodeDiff.test.js still passes them (20 occurrences, e.g. lines 80,82,112,114,129,136,194,196,356,358...) so the tests assert a contract the read-only diff impl no longer honors. Minor dead type/param surface; P2 is correct.

title: ConnectRequest declares a `{ password: string }` auth variant that is never implemented
evidence: ConnectRequest.auth is typed `{ token: string } | { password: string }`, but Gateway.authenticate (gateway.js:3049-3051) reads only `'token' in (request.auth ?? {}) ? request.auth.token : null` and `grep -n password src/gateway.js` returns no hits. A client sending `auth: { password }` is silently treated as anonymous. The password variant is dead type surface.
