recommendation: Remove the `{ password: string }` variant from ConnectRequest (and the index.d.ts mirror), or implement a password auth mode if it is actually intended.
vnote: ConnectRequest.ts:13-15 types auth as `{ token: string } | { password: string }`. gateway.js authenticate at line 3050 reads only `"token" in (request.auth ?? {}) ? request.auth.token : null`; `grep password src/gateway.js` returns zero hits, so a client sending auth:{password} is treated as anonymous. The password variant is mirrored in src/index.d.ts:218 as well. Dead type surface; P2 is correct.

title: ConnectionState typedef field `subscribe?: Set<string>` is stale (runtime uses `subscribedRuns`)
evidence: The ConnectionState JSDoc typedef declares `subscribe?: Set<string>`, but every runtime read/write uses `connection.subscribedRuns` (gateway.js:1250,1253,1660,1663,2739-2742,3020,3187 etc.) — `grep -n 'connection\.subscribe\b'` excluding subscribedRuns returns nothing. The typedef documents a field that does not exist on the actual connection object.
recommendation: Rename the typedef field from `subscribe` to `subscribedRuns: Set<string> | null` to match the implementation.

title: mapEvent's ~30-case SmithersEvent→wire mapping has minimal direct coverage
evidence: mapEvent translates every SmithersEvent type into a broadcast event/payload (NodePending, NodeFailed.error, NodeOutput→task.output with text/stream, Approval*, TaskHeartbeat, etc.). Tests reach it only via the live event stream in gateway.test.jsx, where grep shows only 'NodeStarted' is asserted as an event type. The per-type payload shaping (e.g. NodeOutput mapping `text`→`output` and `stream`, ApprovalGranted/Denied→approval.decided with approved bool, NodeRetrying attempt) is not directly verified, and the default/unmapped-event branch is untested.
recommendation: Add a focused unit test calling gateway.mapEvent(...) for each event type (and an unknown type) asserting the produced {event, payload} shape, including the null-return path for unmapped events.

title: asStringRecord is a redundant one-line alias of asObject
evidence: `function asStringRecord(value) { return asObject(value); }` — it merely forwards to asObject. Only used once (line 909, decodeBase64UrlJson). It adds an indirection with no added behavior or narrowing.
recommendation: Inline asObject at the single call site and delete asStringRecord (or, if the JWT-payload narrowing intent matters, give it a real distinct implementation/name).

title: case14 gateway-rpc-roundtrip e2e is not real e2e: it reimplements the RPC handlers instead of booting the real Gateway
evidence: e2e/faults/case14-gateway-rpc-roundtrip.test.ts startGateway (line 382) is a hand-rolled `new WebSocketServer(...)` whose message handler calls a local `handleRpc(db, grant, method, params)` (line 451) that re-implements connect/approveNode/submitSignal/cancelRun/resumeRun/getRun/etc directly against SQLite (e.g. submitSignal logic at lines 269-300). The real packages/server gateway.js routeRequest is never exercised. The file's own test.skip at line 853 admits the real Gateway 'fails on effect / @smithers-orchestrator/devtools resolution' in the e2e package. Per the repo's 'no mocks / real backends only' e2e bar, this case fabricates the gateway-under-test and so does not validate the shipping RPC dispatch despite its name.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Published TaskDescriptor type is missing forkSource (type drift the build does not catch)
evidence: types.ts declares `forkSource?: string` and src/extract.js:636 sets it on Task descriptors; scheduleTasks.js:8 imports TaskDescriptor from @smithers-orchestrator/graph and reads descriptor.forkSource at line 54; engine.js:3148 reads desc.forkSource. But the generated/committed index.d.ts TaskDescriptor$1 (the actual published type, exports map points types→./src/index.d.ts) has NO forkSource. I regenerated with `tsup --dts-only` and the output is identical (so it is not merely stale — the dts bundler genuinely drops the field). A probe file `const t={} as TaskDescriptor; t.forkSource` inside packages/scheduler typechecks to `TS2339: Property 'forkSource' does not exist on type 'TaskDescriptor$1'`. It is masked today only because consumers are .js without checkJs.
recommendation: Fix the dts pipeline so forkSource (and the full types.ts TaskDescriptor) is emitted into index.d.ts, and add a CI/type test (e.g. a .ts assertion importing TaskDescriptor and reading forkSource) that fails when published types drift from types.ts. Any TS consumer reading forkSource is currently broken.
vnote: Reproduced fully. types.ts:154 has `forkSource?: string`; index.d.ts TaskDescriptor$1 (124-172) lacks it. Field-by-field diff of the two TaskDescriptor definitions shows EXACTLY one missing field: forkSource (aspects, which also has a leading JSDoc comment, survives). I regenerated src/index.d.ts via `tsup --dts-only` (the package's `build` script; tsup.config.ts: dts:{only:true,resolve:false}, outDir src) and the output is byte-identical to the committed file with still NO forkSource — so it is genuinely dropped by the dts bundler, not stale (git confirms both files were last touched in the same Aspects commit 26c04f01, which correctly added the aspects field). extract.js:636 sets forkSource on descriptors; scheduleTasks.js:8 imports TaskDescriptor from @smithers-orchestrator/graph and reads descriptor.forkSource (54); engine.js:3148 and validateForkSources.js read it. ADJUSTMENT TO THE EVIDENCE WORDING ONLY: there are currently ZERO .ts/.tsx consumers reading forkSource (all consumers are .js shielded by checkJs being off), so the headline 'Any TS consumer reading forkSource is currently broken' is latent rather than active — no consumer breaks today, but the published type is objectively wrong and the build does not detect it. Severity P1 is justified for a published-API type defect that the build pipeline silently drops.

title: JS logic files are not type-checked (checkJs off) — JSDoc types are decorative only
evidence: All logic lives in .js files (extract.js, dom/extract.js, worktree-path.js, validateForkSources.js, utils/*.js). tsconfig has allowJs but not checkJs, and emitDeclarationOnly. I appended a deliberate type error to worktree-path.js (assign number to a `@returns {string}` function) and `pnpm typecheck` still exited 0 with no diagnostics. This is the root cause that let forkSource drift out of index.d.ts undetected; the entire package's JSDoc annotations are unenforced.
recommendation: Enable checkJs in packages/graph (and ideally repo-wide for .js logic packages) so JSDoc types are actually validated; fix the resulting errors. This is the structural guard that would have caught the forkSource bug.
vnote: `checkJs` appears nowhere in the repo (grepped packages/, apps/cli, all tsconfig*.json; root tsconfig has allowJs:true and no checkJs; graph/scheduler/engine tsconfigs are standalone with allowJs and no extends, so they cannot inherit it). Empirically verified with the repo's own tsc (node_modules/.pnpm/typescript@5.9.3/.../bin/tsc): a probe .js with `@returns {string}` returning a number exits 0 with no diagnostics under --allowJs, and errors `TS2322: Type 'number' is not assignable to type 'string'` only when --checkJs is added. Confirmed graph `tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit` passes (exit 0) today, so the forkSource drift is uncaught precisely because JSDoc in .js logic files (extract.js, dom/extract.js, worktree-path.js, validateForkSources.js, utils/*.js) is unenforced. Root cause for finding #1.

title: Two divergent extractors with a colliding name; legacy src/dom/extract.js is dead in product but still tested
file: packages/graph/src/dom/extract.js (whole file; see TODO at lines 17-25)
evidence: Production resolves extraction via react-reconciler core-peer.js→extractGraph (src/extract.js). I grepped the whole repo: src/dom/extract.js's extractFromHost is imported ONLY by packages/engine/tests/reconciler-graph-roundtrip.test.jsx:14 and packages/react-reconciler/tests/external-host-node.test.js:3 — never in apps/cli, scripts, workflows, or any product src. Its own TODO(migration) header says it has not reached parity. The root package exports its OWN extractFromHost = extractGraph (src/extract.js:702), so `import { extractFromHost } from '@smithers-orchestrator/graph'` and `from '@smithers-orchestrator/graph/dom/extract'` return DIFFERENT functions (verified at runtime: identity check false). dom/extract.js also lacks fork (forkSource) and aspects (__aspects) handling that production extract.js has, so the roundtrip/external-host-node tests assert behavior that production code does not run.
recommendation: Either delete src/dom/extract.js and its dependents (utils/tree-ids.js — see separate finding) and repoint the two tests at the production extractGraph, or finish the migration in the TODO. Same-named, divergent extractFromHost across two import paths is a real footgun; the 'roundtrip' tests give false confidence about the production pipeline.
vnote: Production resolves extraction in react-reconciler/core-peer.js:20-33 via importing the root `@smithers-orchestrator/graph` module and reading mod.extractGraph (= src/extract.js extractGraph). src/dom/extract.js's extractFromHost is imported ONLY by 3 test files: packages/graph/tests/dom-extract.test.jsx:3 (the auditor missed this dedicated test, but it does not change the conclusion), packages/engine/tests/reconciler-graph-roundtrip.test.jsx:14, packages/react-reconciler/tests/external-host-node.test.js:3. Never in any product src, apps/cli, scripts, workflows, or .smithers. Its TODO(migration) header (lines 17-25) explicitly states non-parity. dom/extract.js has ZERO references to fork/forkSource/__aspects/aspects (grep empty), whereas production extract.js handles forkSource (636) and aspects (252,261,674). Root export sets extractFromHost = extractGraph (extract.js:702) while dom/extract exports a different function — two same-named functions across import paths `@smithers-orchestrator/graph` vs `@smithers-orchestrator/graph/dom/extract` (package.json `./*` subpath export resolves the latter to src/dom/extract.js). The roundtrip perf test (line 150) and the external-host-node tests assert on the dead extractor while the roundtrip file's header claims to test 'the public surface that workflow authors actually use' — false confidence confirmed. (Note: the other roundtrip tests use runWorkflow, the real path; only the perf test at line 150 uses the dead extractor in that file.)

title: src/utils/tree-ids.js is dead in production (only the legacy dom/extract.js uses it)
evidence: stablePathId/resolveStableId are exported here, but repo-wide grep shows the only importer is src/dom/extract.js:6 (itself test-only). Production src/extract.js inlines its own stablePathId (line 37) and resolveStableId (line 48) rather than importing this module. So tree-ids.js (and its tests tree-ids.test.js/utils-tree-ids.test.js) only cover code that no product path executes.
recommendation: If dom/extract.js is removed, delete utils/tree-ids.js too; otherwise dedupe by having src/extract.js import from utils/tree-ids.js so there is a single id implementation.
vnote: Repo-wide grep for tree-ids/stablePathId/resolveStableId: the only non-test src importer of utils/tree-ids.js is src/dom/extract.js:6 (itself test-only dead code per finding #3). Production src/extract.js inlines its own stablePathId (37) and resolveStableId (48); scheduler/buildPlanTree.js inlines its own copies too (12,23). The two dedicated tests (tree-ids.test.js, utils-tree-ids.test.js) only cover code no product path executes. P2 appropriate.

title: src/extract.js re-declares shared constants instead of importing constants.js (drift risk)
evidence: extract.js hardcodes `const DEFAULT_MERGE_QUEUE_CONCURRENCY = 1` and `const WORKTREE_EMPTY_PATH_ERROR = "<Worktree> requires a non-empty path prop"` locally, even though src/constants.js exports both with a comment stating they are 'Centralized to keep component and extractor error messages in sync'. worktree-path.js and dom/extract.js correctly import from constants.js; production extract.js does not, so the centralization is defeated for the file that matters most.
recommendation: Import DEFAULT_MERGE_QUEUE_CONCURRENCY and WORKTREE_EMPTY_PATH_ERROR from ./constants.js in extract.js to prevent silent drift.

title: Dead duplicate type exports: Scorer/ScorerBinding/SamplingConfig/ScorerFn/ScorerInput/ScoreResult/AgentLike/RetryPolicy/etc.
file: packages/graph/src/types.ts:63-95,47-61,32-35 (+ Scorer.ts, ScorerBinding.ts, SamplingConfig.ts, ScorerFn.ts, ScorerInput.ts, ScoreResult.ts, AgentLike.ts, RetryPolicy.ts, MemoryNamespaceKind.ts, ExtractResult.ts)
evidence: Repo-wide grep for external importers of these graph types (both `@smithers-orchestrator/graph` and `/Subpath` forms) returns zero for Scorer, ScorerBinding, SamplingConfig, ScorerFn, ScorerInput, ScoreResult, AgentLike, ApprovalOption, RetryPolicy, MemoryNamespaceKind, CachePolicy, ExtractResult; and they are not referenced inside graph either (TaskDescriptor uses ScorersMap=Record<string,unknown>, not Scorer). Canonical definitions already live elsewhere: Scorer/ScorerBinding/SamplingConfig/ScorerFn/ScorerInput/ScoreResult in packages/scorers/src/types.ts, AgentLike in packages/agents/src/AgentLike.ts, RetryPolicy in packages/scheduler/src/RetryPolicy.ts. The graph copies are unused parallel duplicates that can silently drift.
recommendation: Drop the unused duplicated type exports from graph (or re-export the canonical ones) so there is a single source of truth. Keep only the types graph actually owns/uses (TaskDescriptor, WorkflowGraph, Host*/Xml*, ExtractOptions/ExtractGraph, GraphSnapshot, TaskAspects, TaskMemoryConfig, MemoryNamespace, ScorersMap).
vnote: The dead-PUBLIC-EXPORT observation is correct and zero external importers confirmed: the main `smithers-orchestrator` package (packages/smithers/src/index.d.ts) imports from graph ONLY XmlNode/TaskDescriptor/GraphSnapshot (lines 3,6,42) and sources Scorer-chain from @smithers-orchestrator/scorers and AgentLike from @smithers-orchestrator/agents/AgentLike; CachePolicy/RetryPolicy are sourced from @smithers-orchestrator/scheduler (components/scheduler import @smithers-orchestrator/scheduler/CachePolicy, not graph), and RetryPolicy/CachePolicy are not even re-exported in smithers' public surface. Canonical defs exist in scorers/agents/scheduler as claimed. HOWEVER the finding's scope/recommendation OVERREACHES: AgentLike(types.ts:187), ApprovalOption(169), RetryPolicy(180), CachePolicy(184), and MemoryNamespaceKind(102 via MemoryNamespace) are STRUCTURALLY USED by graph's own TaskDescriptor/MemoryNamespace and therefore cannot be 'dropped' — only their redundant standalone re-export wrappers (the per-type .ts files + index.js typedefs) are unused. Only the Scorer chain (Scorer, ScorerBinding, SamplingConfig, ScorerFn, ScorerInput, ScoreResult) and ExtractResult are CLEANLY dead: the chain hangs off ScorerBinding which nothing in the used API references (TaskDescriptor.scorers uses ScorersMap=Record<string,unknown>), and ExtractResult is referenced only by the dead dom/extract.js. P2 dead-code stands; the framing that all 12 are droppable duplicates is partly wrong.

title: worktree-path.js has no dedicated unit tests; branches only hit indirectly
evidence: No test file imports resolveWorktreePath in packages/graph/tests (grep found none). Its branches — absolute path → resolve() (line 17-18), relative + opts.baseRootDir (line 20-22), relative + process.cwd() fallback (line 23), and empty path → WORKTREE_EMPTY_PATH throw (line 14-15) — are only exercised indirectly via extract.test.jsx worktree cases (which pass baseRootDir) and driver tests in another package. The cwd-fallback branch and absolute-path branch are not directly asserted here.
recommendation: Add a worktree-path.test.js covering absolute paths, relative+baseRootDir, relative+cwd fallback, and empty/whitespace path throwing WORKTREE_EMPTY_PATH, since this is a published export (re-exported by packages/smithers and used by driver SmithersCtx).

title: Misleading test titles: parseXmlJson tests named 'returns null' but assert toThrow()
evidence: Test 'returns null for invalid JSON' asserts `expect(() => parseXmlJson("not-json")).toThrow()` and 'returns null for empty string' asserts `expect(() => parseXmlJson("")).toThrow()`. parseXmlJson (utils/xml.js:40-42) is just `return JSON.parse(json)`, which throws rather than returning null, so the titles directly contradict the asserted behavior and the function name implies a safe parser it is not.
recommendation: Rename the tests to reflect that invalid/empty input throws (or make parseXmlJson return null on parse failure if a safe parser was the intent). Clarify the contract in the JSDoc.

title: TaskAspects missing from index.js @smithers-type-exports block (export-marker inconsistency)
evidence: The codegen-managed `@smithers-type-exports-begin/end` typedef block lists 25 types but omits TaskAspects, even though TaskAspects is a real exported type (index.d.ts exports it; engine/aspects/evaluateAspectBudget.js imports `@smithers-orchestrator/graph/TaskAspects`). It only reaches the .d.ts transitively via TaskDescriptor. Comparing the marker block to index.d.ts exports shows TaskAspects present in d.ts but absent from the marker list.
recommendation: Add TaskAspects to the @smithers-type-exports block (or re-run the generator) so the documented type-export surface matches the actual exports and stays stable across codegen.

title: Low-value/duplicated test files inflate the suite without adding coverage
file: packages/graph/tests/constants-extended.test.js (also tree-ids.test.js vs utils-tree-ids.test.js, xml-utils.test.js vs utils-xml-extended.test.js)
evidence: constants-extended.test.js only asserts that the module is an object, values are primitives, and there is >0 key — tautological and fully subsumed by constants.test.js which asserts the actual values. tree-ids and xml have near-identical 'extended' vs base pairs covering the same functions. This is duplication/bloat rather than added boundary coverage.
recommendation: Merge each duplicated pair into one focused file and drop the tautological assertions; spend the budget on real gaps (worktree-path.js, aspects skip-remaining/latency-warn branches, WaitForEvent onTimeout='continue' and default 'fail' branches, envHeartbeatTimeoutMs invalid/zero override).

title: Untested branches in src/extract.js aspects and wait-for-event/heartbeat env logic
evidence: No test sets SMITHERS_TASK_HEARTBEAT_MS, so envHeartbeatTimeoutMs's invalid/empty/<=0/non-finite branches (lines 23-28) and the override floor are uncovered. aspects() covers tokenBudget onExceeded='warn' and latencySlo onExceeded='fail' only — token onExceeded='skip-remaining', latency onExceeded='warn', latency perTask, array __aspects, and tokenBudget-without-numeric-max rejection are untested. WaitForEvent continueOnFail derivation (onTimeout==='continue' || 'skip') is only tested for 'skip'; the 'continue' branch and default 'fail'→continueOnFail=false branch are not separately asserted.
recommendation: Add boundary tests for the heartbeat env override, the remaining aspects onExceeded/perTask permutations + malformed __aspects, and WaitForEvent onTimeout values 'continue' and default 'fail'.

title: additionalProperties as a SchemaObject/RefObject is silently dropped (typed extra props not validated)
evidence: buildObject only handles `s.additionalProperties === true || s.additionalProperties === undefined`. The SchemaObject.ts type declares `additionalProperties?: boolean | SchemaObject | RefObject`, so the schema-object form is part of the contract but unhandled. Verified at runtime: `jsonSchemaToZod({type:'object', additionalProperties:{type:'number'}, properties:{a:{type:'string'}}})` parses `{a:'x', b:5}` to `{a:'x'}` and `{a:'x', b:'str'}` to `{a:'x'}` — extra props are stripped, never validated, and the additionalProperties:false case (verified to also strip) is untested.
recommendation: Handle additionalProperties when it is a SchemaObject/RefObject by adding `obj.catchall(jsonSchemaToZod(s.additionalProperties, spec, visited))`, and treat `false` explicitly (strict object). Add tests for true/false/schema-object cases.
vnote: Reproduced at runtime. buildObject (jsonSchemaToZod.js:142-147) only catches `additionalProperties === true || undefined`. `jsonSchemaToZod({type:'object', additionalProperties:{type:'number'}, properties:{a:{type:'string'}}})` parsed `{a:'x',b:5}` -> `{"a":"x"}` and `{a:'x',b:'str'}` -> `{"a":"x"}`: the typed extra prop is stripped, never validated. additionalProperties:false also strips silently (`{a:'x',b:5}` -> `{"a":"x"}`) rather than rejecting. SchemaObject.ts:16 declares the schema-object/ref form, so it is in the contract. No test in packages/openapi/tests/ references additionalProperties or catchall. P1 stands.

title: enum is ignored for numbers/integers and for typeless schemas
evidence: buildString is the only builder that honors `enum`. Verified: `jsonSchemaToZod({type:'integer', enum:[1,2,3]})` accepts `5` (buildNumber ignores enum). `jsonSchemaToZod({enum:['a','b']})` with no `type` falls through to the `z.any().describe('untyped schema')` fallback and accepts `'zzz'`. JSON Schema enums are commonly written without a `type`, so this silently drops a real constraint. Also untested: a string `enum` containing mixed non-string values (z.enum silently drops numeric members).
recommendation: Promote enum handling above the type switch (build z.enum / z.literal-union for any enum regardless of declared type), or at minimum honor numeric enums in buildNumber and typeless enums in the fallback. Add tests.
vnote: Reproduced. buildString is the only builder honoring enum. `jsonSchemaToZod({type:'integer', enum:[1,2,3]}).parse(5)` returned 5 (buildNumber, jsonSchemaToZod.js:115-124, ignores enum). `jsonSchemaToZod({enum:['a','b']}).parse('zzz')` returned 'zzz' (typeless falls to the z.any() fallback at lines 78-80). Also confirmed: `z.enum(['a',5,'b'])` drops the numeric member — the resulting set is only ['a','b'] and parsing 5 throws, so a string enum with mixed members silently loses entries. No test covers numeric or typeless enums. P1 stands.

title: Non-JSON request bodies (multipart/form-data, x-www-form-urlencoded) are silently dropped
evidence: buildOperationSchema only reads `requestBody.content['application/json']`; executeRequest only serializes `args.body` as JSON with Content-Type application/json. Verified: a `required:true` requestBody whose only content type is multipart/form-data yields an empty operation schema (`Object.keys(shape) === []`) and the body is never sent — silently, with no warning. No test covers any non-JSON content type. Docs (docs/concepts/openapi-tools.mdx) do not state this limitation.
recommendation: Either support common alternate content types or, at minimum, detect a body with no application/json content and surface a clear error/warning. Document the JSON-only limitation. Add a test fixture with multipart content.
vnote: Reproduced. buildOperationSchema (buildOperationSchema.js:44-58) only reads content['application/json']; a required multipart/form-data-only requestBody produced an operation schema with shape keys `[]` — the body is absent from the input contract and is never sent (executeRequest at _helpers.js:120-125 only serializes args.body as JSON). grep confirms docs/concepts/openapi-tools.mdx and docs/llms-openapi.txt never mention application/json, content-type, multipart, or a JSON-only limitation. No test exercises a non-JSON content type. P1 stands.

title: Pre-loaded object specs without an `openapi` key (e.g. Swagger 2.0) throw a misleading parse error
evidence: The object short-circuit checks only `'openapi' in input`. An object spec using `swagger:'2.0'` falls through to `readFileSync(<object>)` -> catch -> `parseSpecText(<object>)` -> `JSON.parse(<object>)`, which throws 'Failed to parse OpenAPI spec as JSON or YAML' (verified at runtime). Inconsistent with parseSpecText, which *accepts* text-based specs that have `swagger` but not `openapi` (validation at _specHelpers.js:31 allows `'swagger' in parsed`). So Swagger-as-text is accepted by the parser but Swagger-as-object is rejected with a wrong message.
recommendation: Detect plain objects more robustly (e.g. `typeof input === 'object' && !Array.isArray(input)` returns it as-is, or check `'openapi' in input || 'swagger' in input || 'paths' in input`). If Swagger 2.0 is genuinely unsupported downstream (extractOperations assumes OpenAPI 3 shape), reject it explicitly with a clear message rather than via a JSON.parse failure.
vnote: Reproduced. `loadSpecSync({swagger:'2.0', info:..., paths:{}})` threw 'Failed to parse OpenAPI spec as JSON or YAML'. Object short-circuit (loadSpecSync.js:16, loadSpecEffect.js:18) checks only `'openapi' in input`, so a swagger-object falls through to readFileSync(object) -> catch -> parseSpecText(object) -> JSON.parse(object). Confirmed inconsistency: parseSpecText's validation (_specHelpers.js:31) allows `'swagger' in parsed`, so swagger-as-TEXT is accepted but swagger-as-OBJECT is rejected with a wrong message. P2 is appropriate (edge case, but a real wrong-message bug).

title: Repeated path parameters only substitute the first occurrence
evidence: `url = url.replace(`{${key}}`, encodeURIComponent(value))` uses String.replace which replaces only the first match. Verified: buildUrl('https://api.example.com', '/a/{id}/b/{id}', {id:'X'}, {}, {}) => 'https://api.example.com/a/X/b/%7Bid%7D' — the second {id} is left literal and percent-encoded into the path.
recommendation: Use `url.replaceAll(`{${key}}`, encodeURIComponent(value))` (or a global regex). Add a test for a path with a repeated parameter.
vnote: Reproduced. `buildUrl('https://api.example.com','/a/{id}/b/{id}',{id:'X'},{},{})` returned 'https://api.example.com/a/X/b/%7Bid%7D' — String.replace (_helpers.js:58) only replaces the first `{id}`; the second is left literal and percent-encoded into the path, producing a broken URL. No test covers a repeated path parameter. P2 is appropriate.

title: A parameter literally named `body` is silently overwritten by the request body
evidence: The request body is always assigned to the hardcoded key `props.body`. Verified: a required query parameter named `body` plus a JSON requestBody collapse to a single `body` key in the resulting object schema (the requestBody wins). There is no collision detection. Dup-named path+query params also collide into one key (later param wins) — verified shape has a single `id`.
recommendation: Detect collisions (param name === 'body', or two params sharing a name across different `in` locations) and either namespace them or error clearly. Add tests.
vnote: Reproduced. A required query param named 'body' plus a JSON requestBody collapsed to a single 'body' shape key (the requestBody assignment at buildOperationSchema.js:51 overwrites props.body). Dup-named path+query params (both 'id') also collapsed to a single 'id' key (later param wins, since props is keyed by param.name). No collision detection, no test. P2 is appropriate.

title: Path-level parameters and mergeParameters precedence are entirely untested
evidence: Every fixture in tests/fixtures.js declares only operation-level `parameters`; none uses pathItem-level parameters, none uses `$ref` parameters (components.parameters), and no test exercises a path+operation name collision. This leaves the documented 'operation-level takes precedence' behavior, the path-level `.map(p => deref(spec,p))` arrow (the source of extractOperations' 66.67% function coverage), and mergeParameters' key-collision filtering unverified.
recommendation: Add a fixture with path-level parameters (including a $ref parameter and a name+in collision with an operation-level param) and assert the merged/deduped result.
vnote: Verified by reading tests/fixtures.js and all *.test.js: every fixture declares only operation-level `parameters`; none uses pathItem-level `parameters`, none uses a `$ref` parameter (components.parameters), and no test exercises a path+operation name+in collision. So extractOperations.js:24 (the pathItem `.map(p => deref(spec,p))` path) and mergeParameters' collision-filtering (_specHelpers.js:45-48) are exercised only with empty path-level arrays. The behavior is genuinely unverified. P1 stands as a real coverage gap for a documented merge rule.

title: Metric increments are never asserted; duration is not recorded on error
evidence: No test reads any of openApiToolCallsTotal / openApiToolCallErrorsTotal / openApiToolDuration (grep of tests/ for Metric/openApiTool returns nothing). Additionally, in executeToolEffect the `nowMs() - started` and `Metric.update(openApiToolDuration, ...)` lines sit after the awaited tryPromise, so on a failed request they are never reached — the duration histogram captures only successful latencies while the error counter is bumped via tapError. The 100% coverage of metrics.js is meaningless (it is a pure re-export).
recommendation: Add a test that captures metric state before/after a success and a failure (Effect Metric.value), and move duration measurement so it is recorded for failures too (e.g. ensure/finalizer) if failed-call latency is meant to be observed.

title: No real-backend e2e: e2e.test.js mocks globalThis.fetch
evidence: tests/e2e.test.js overrides globalThis.fetch with bun `mock()` returning fabricated Response objects; there is no Bun.serve/createServer anywhere in tests/ (grep returned nothing). The package's whole purpose is issuing real HTTP requests, yet no test does a real round-trip against a local server, so real content-type parsing, real header rejection, and real URL routing are unverified. The execution-escaping header-injection test even restores real fetch and points at the real domain api.example.com (relies on fetch throwing before any network I/O).
recommendation: Add a real e2e using `Bun.serve` on an ephemeral port: load a spec whose server URL is the local server, call a generated tool, and assert the server received the expected method/path/query/headers/body. This is the no-mocks-compliant way and would catch the path/body/header bugs above.

title: `deprecated` is parsed but never used; OpenApiToolCalled event is typed/formatted but never emitted
evidence: extractOperations computes `deprecated` onto every ParsedOperation but nothing consumes it (no tool metadata, no listOperations output, no description annotation). Separately, OpenApiToolCalled is in the engine eventType union and has a formatting case in apps/cli/src/format.js:280 and a category in event-categories.js:68, but nothing in packages/openapi ever emits it — the docs explicitly call it 'typed/categorized for future event-bus integration', so the CLI formatting case is currently unreachable.
recommendation: Either surface `deprecated` (e.g. annotate the tool description / drop or flag deprecated ops) or remove the field. Decide whether to emit OpenApiToolCalled from executeToolEffect (it already has the operationId/method/path annotations) or remove the dead CLI handler until the producer exists.
vnote: Split verdict. The `deprecated` half is CONFIRMED dead: extractOperations.js:45 computes `deprecated` onto every ParsedOperation, and grep across packages/apps/.smithers found NO consumer of a ParsedOperation .deprecated (the other .deprecated hits are unrelated agent-detection code). The OpenApiToolCalled half is REFUTED as 'dead/unreachable': it is genuinely never EMITTED from packages/openapi, but the CLI handler at apps/cli/src/format.js:280 and categorizer event-categories.js:68 are NOT unreachable dead code — they are covered by unit tests (apps/cli/tests/format-unit.test.js:53 and :125 assert the formatted output 'GET /v1 (200)') and the observability tracker (trackEvent.js:592) is tested at effect-metrics-track.test.js:314,324. Docs across docs/ and llms bundles explicitly document it as a typed/categorized forward-compatibility variant. So this is intentional, tested forward-compat plumbing, not dead code. Downgrading: the only real defect here is the unused `deprecated` field.

title: Package limitations are undocumented (cookie params, JSON-only bodies, no parameter styles, no Swagger object)
evidence: buildOperationSchema silently skips `in: 'cookie'` params (buildOperationSchema.js:29-30), supports only application/json bodies, ignores OpenAPI parameter serialization style/explode (arrays become String() comma-joined in executeRequest.js:96), and effectively assumes OpenAPI 3. docs/concepts/openapi-tools.mdx has no Limitations section covering any of these; grep for limitation/unsupported/cookie/multipart found nothing.
recommendation: Add a short Limitations section to the concept doc enumerating: cookie params dropped, JSON-only request bodies, no style/explode array serialization, OpenAPI 3 assumed.

title: include+exclude combined behavior and resolveBaseUrl localhost fallback are untested
evidence: Docs claim 'If both are set, exclude wins' but no test sets both include and exclude together (tests/tool-factory.test.js and e2e.test.js test them only individually). resolveBaseUrl's fallback to 'http://localhost' (no options.baseUrl and no spec.servers / empty servers array) is verified to fire at runtime but has no test (noOperationIdSpec has no servers but is never run through tool execution).
recommendation: Add a test with include+exclude overlapping, and a test asserting tools created from a server-less spec default to http://localhost.

title: Generated index.d.ts is committed and can drift; `./*` and `./metrics` subpaths point types at the full bundle
evidence: index.d.ts is a tsup `--dts-only` artifact (tsup.config.ts writes into src/) but is git-tracked. Running `bun run build` currently produces no diff (in sync), so drift is latent, not present — but any future JSDoc/type edit that skips the build would silently desync the published types. Additionally package.json maps both `./metrics` and the `./*` wildcard `types` to `./src/index.d.ts`, so importing `@smithers-orchestrator/openapi/metrics` or `/tool-factory` (used by apps/cli/src/index.js:2494) surfaces the entire package's type bundle rather than the subpath's actual exports.
recommendation: Add a CI check that `bun run build` leaves index.d.ts unchanged (mirror the repo's check-docs/check-llms gates), or generate per-entry .d.ts so subpath `types` are accurate.

title: smithers tree --watch crashes on first delta: CLI passes DevToolsNode to applyDelta (needs DevToolsSnapshotV1)
evidence: applyDelta's signature is `applyDelta(snapshot: DevToolsSnapshotV1, delta): DevToolsSnapshotV1` (packages/devtools/src/applyDelta.js:49, index.d.ts:522) and it reads `snapshot.seq` (line 53) and `snapshot.root` (line 61). But apps/cli/src/tree.js:240 calls `applyDelta(snapshot.root, event.delta)` passing `snapshot.root` (a DevToolsNode, per protocol DevToolsSnapshot = {version,runId,frameNo,seq,root:DevToolsNode}). I reproduced the exact call: it throws `InvalidDeltaError: Delta base seq 1 does not match snapshot seq undefined` because a node has no `.seq`. tree.js:244-246 then writes a stderr error and returns EXIT_SERVER_ERROR. The server emits `kind: "delta"` events in normal operation (streamDevTools.js:186/412/485), so the watch path reliably hits this. checkJs is NOT enabled in apps/cli/tsconfig.json, so TS never validates the JSDoc signature against this call; and tree.test.js's only runTreeWatch test (line 198) aborts immediately before any delta is applied.
recommendation: Fix the CLI call to `applyDelta(snapshot, event.delta)` and assign the returned snapshot (it already returns a full DevToolsSnapshotV1). Add a runTreeWatch test that drives at least one delta event end-to-end. Consider enabling checkJs in apps/cli, and/or hardening applyDelta to throw a clearer InvalidDeltaError when the first arg lacks `seq`/`root` so misuse fails loudly with an actionable message.
vnote: Fully reproduced. tree.js:240 calls applyDelta(snapshot.root, event.delta), passing a DevToolsNode where applyDelta (applyDelta.js:49, index.d.ts:522) expects a DevToolsSnapshotV1. A node has no .seq, so the line-53 guard throws 'InvalidDeltaError: Delta base seq 1 does not match snapshot seq undefined' — I ran the exact call and got that error. tree.js:244-246 then writes stderr and returns EXIT_SERVER_ERROR, so --watch reliably dies on the first delta. The server emits real delta events with numeric baseSeq (streamDevTools.js:184-193; diffSnapshots at 175-183), so the watch path always hits a delta. apps/cli/tsconfig.json has allowJs:true but NO checkJs, so the JSDoc signature is never enforced against the JS call. No test covers it: tree.test.js's only runTreeWatch test (line 182, 'SIGINT...') aborts immediately via queueMicrotask(()=>abort.abort()), and watch-mode-read-commands.test.js covers ps/inspect/events/node --watch but NOT tree --watch. I also confirmed the proposed fix applyDelta(snapshot, event.delta) works and returns a full snapshot (DevToolsSnapshotV1 and protocol DevToolsSnapshot are structurally identical: {version,runId,frameNo,seq,root}). P1 is correct.

title: applyDelta validation is asymmetric: addNode/updateProps/updateTask accept malformed payloads and corrupt the tree
evidence: The docstring says 'Throws InvalidDeltaError for malformed ops' (applyDelta.js:43). replaceRoot validates its node: `if (!op.node || typeof op.node !== "object") throw new InvalidDeltaError(...)` (lines 65-67). But addNode does NOT validate `op.node` (lines 82-89): I confirmed an addNode with `node: undefined` does not throw — it splices `null/undefined` into children (result children = [null, 2]). Likewise updateProps with `props: "not-an-object"` does not throw — it sets `node.props = "not-an-object"` (a string), violating the Record<string,unknown> contract. updateTask similarly clones whatever is passed.
recommendation: Validate op.node in addNode (same guard as replaceRoot) and validate that updateProps.props / updateTask.task are objects (or undefined for updateTask), throwing InvalidDeltaError otherwise, so the documented contract holds and a bad delta can't silently corrupt the snapshot tree.
vnote: Reproduced all three. addNode with node:undefined does NOT throw and splices null into children ([null, {...}]). updateProps with props:'not-an-object' does NOT throw and sets node.props to a string, violating Record<string,unknown>. updateTask with task:'weird' does NOT throw and sets node.task to a string. Only replaceRoot validates its node (applyDelta.js:65-67), while the docstring at line 43 says 'Throws InvalidDeltaError for malformed ops'. Test gap confirmed: applyDelta-edge-cases.test.ts covers unknown ids, removing root, op-order, baseSeq mismatch (lines 41-108), but none feed malformed node/props/task payloads. P2 is correct — these are produced internally by diffSnapshots so practical risk is low, but the documented contract is violated.

title: snapshotSerializer maxEntries does not bound flat arrays/objects of scalars
evidence: maxEntries is intended as a size guard (default SNAPSHOT_SERIALIZER_DEFAULT_MAX_ENTRIES = 100_000, snapshotSerializer.js:7). But `state.traversed` is only incremented for object/array values, AFTER the scalar fast-paths return (lines 56-75 return before line 76's check). I confirmed `snapshotSerialize([1,2,3], { maxEntries: 1 })` returns `[1,2,3]` with NO MaxEntries warning and no truncation. An array (or object) of 1,000,000 scalars only counts as 1 entry, so the guard provides no protection against the most common large-payload shape. The existing test (snapshotSerializer.test.ts:111-121) only uses arrays of objects, masking this.
recommendation: Either count every serialized value (including scalars) toward `traversed`, or document explicitly that maxEntries counts only container nodes. Add a test for a large flat scalar array to lock in whichever semantics are intended.
vnote: Reproduced. snapshotSerialize([1,2,3], {maxEntries:1}) returns [1,2,3] with no warning; an array of 1000 scalars with maxEntries:1 returns all 1000 unbounded. Scalar fast-paths (snapshotSerializer.js:53-75) return before the state.traversed>=maxEntries check (line 76) and the traversed++ (line 80), so only container nodes count. Contrast: array of 3 objects with maxEntries:1 correctly yields ['[MaxEntries]',...]. Both existing maxEntries tests (snapshotSerializer.test.ts:111-121 and 125) use arrays of objects, masking the scalar gap. P2 is correct.

title: Five package exports have no consumers anywhere in the repo (effectively dead public API)
evidence: index.js re-exports countNodes, buildSnapshot, SMITHERS_NODE_ICONS, findNodeById, collectTasks (lines 18-23). Repo-wide grep (excluding packages/devtools and dist) for imports of each: countNodes — none; buildSnapshot — only internal (SmithersDevToolsCore.js) + its own test; SMITHERS_NODE_ICONS — none; findNodeById — only internal (SmithersDevToolsCore.js) + own test; collectTasks — only internal + own test. `gateway.js buildSnapshot` and `defaultOperatorUi.js findNodeById` are unrelated local definitions, not imports of these. The only externally consumed exports are SmithersDevToolsCore, printTree, snapshotSerialize, diffSnapshots, applyDelta, and the type SmithersNodeType.
recommendation: Either keep them as a deliberately-stable utility surface (and say so), or stop re-exporting the ones used only internally (buildSnapshot/findNodeById/collectTasks are internal helpers of SmithersDevToolsCore) and drop SMITHERS_NODE_ICONS/countNodes if no consumer is planned. At minimum add a README documenting which exports are intended public API vs internal.
vnote: Grep accuracy confirmed: countNodes, buildSnapshot, SMITHERS_NODE_ICONS, findNodeById, collectTasks (index.js:18-23) have ZERO external runtime importers across packages/apps/.smithers/scripts/docs (the gateway.js countNodesByState/buildSnapshot and defaultOperatorUi.js findNodeById hits are unrelated local definitions, not imports). Internally: countNodes used by buildSnapshot.js; buildSnapshot/findNodeById/collectTasks used by SmithersDevToolsCore.js; SMITHERS_NODE_ICONS used by printTree.js (and has not even an internal test). Externally consumed exports are exactly SmithersDevToolsCore, printTree, snapshotSerialize, diffSnapshots, applyDelta + types. However this is a PUBLISHED library (@smithers-orchestrator/devtools 0.24.1, a 'debugging utilities' package) whose package.json also exposes a wildcard './*' subpath export, so a stable public utility surface is a defensible intentional design, not strictly 'dead'. The auditor acknowledges this. Adjusting to P2/none-ish: factually accurate but 'effectively dead public API' overstates it for a published lib; best treated as a documentation/intent gap (mark which exports are public vs internal) rather than dead code. adjustedSeverity P2.

title: DevToolsRunStore: verbose logging, unknown-event recording, orphan ToolCallFinished, and getTaskState-miss branches are untested
evidence: grep of tests/ shows zero references to `verbose`, so the three verbose console.log branches (DevToolsRunStore.js:119-121, 129-131, 140-142) are never exercised. No test verifies the documented open-tail behavior that an unknown `type` event is pushed to run.events but ignored by the reducer (I confirmed run.events.length===1, status stays 'running'). ToolCallFinished with no matching started call (lines 185-187, the `if (tc)` false branch) is untested. getTaskState on a missing run (lines 68-69) and getTaskState without iteration where nodeId doesn't match (lines 73-77 fallthrough to undefined) are untested.
recommendation: Add tests: a store constructed with { verbose: true } asserting the start/finish/fail log lines fire; an unknown engine event asserting it lands in run.events and status is unchanged; an orphan ToolCallFinished asserting no throw and empty toolCalls; getTaskState on a missing run and on a present run with an unknown nodeId both returning undefined.

title: snapshotSerializer: top-level non-plain values and anonymous-class instances are untested boundary cases
evidence: Existing tests only place Maps/class-ish values as nested object properties (snapshotSerializer.test.ts:91-95). I confirmed top-level serialization differs by path: snapshotSerialize(new Map(...)) -> '[Map]', a named class instance -> '[Foo]', but an anonymous class instance -> {"y":5} and a custom-prototype object -> {"a":1} (because the guard is `ctorName && ctorName !== "Object"`, snapshotSerializer.js:91-94, and ctorName==='' is falsy). None of these top-level paths nor the anonymous-class fallthrough are covered.
recommendation: Add tests for top-level Map/class/Date/function/symbol values and for an anonymous-class / null-prototype instance to lock in the (intentional or not) 'treated as plain object' behavior, since it changes how non-plain objects surface in snapshots.

title: diffSnapshots p95 timing assertion is a CI-flaky unit test
evidence: diffSnapshots.test.ts:236-253 asserts the p95 of a 500-node diff over 30 trials is < 25ms (DIFF_SNAPSHOTS_500_NODE_P95_BUDGET_MS). Wall-clock perf assertions are non-deterministic on shared CI runners (the repo's own MEMORY notes CI runs on clean Ubuntu boxes), and a slow/loaded runner can red this with no real regression.
recommendation: Move the perf budget to a dedicated, opt-in/benchmark suite (or gate it behind an env flag) so functional CI does not depend on absolute timing; keep the functional roundtrip assertions in the unit suite.

title: SMITHERS_NODE_ICONS uses the same '⚡' glyph for both 'task' and 'parallel'
evidence: packages/devtools/src/SMITHERS_NODE_ICONS.js:6 sets task: '⚡' and line 8 sets parallel: '⚡'. In printTree output, parallel container nodes are visually indistinguishable from task nodes, which undermines the icon's purpose in the tree printer.
recommendation: Give 'parallel' a distinct icon (e.g. a fork/branch glyph) so the printed tree visually distinguishes parallel containers from tasks.

title: Server snapshotFromFrameRow constructs a SmithersDevToolsCore + captureSnapshot whose result is discarded
evidence: packages/server/src/gatewayRoutes/getDevToolsSnapshot.js:334-342 does `const core = new SmithersDevToolsCore(); core.captureSnapshot(root);` then returns a hand-built object {version,runId,frameNo,seq,root} that never reads `core`. The comment says 'Keep parity with existing devtools snapshot capture semantics', but captureSnapshot only sets _lastSnapshot on a core that is immediately GC'd — it has no effect on the output. This points at a confusing devtools API (captureSnapshot is purely side-effecting on the instance).
recommendation: Drop the dead core/captureSnapshot call in the server route. On the devtools side, consider documenting that captureSnapshot mutates instance state and returns the snapshot, so consumers don't cargo-cult an instance they then discard.
vnote: Confirmed at getDevToolsSnapshot.js:334-335: 'const core = new SmithersDevToolsCore(); core.captureSnapshot(root);' then returns a hand-built {version,runId,frameNo,seq,root} that never reads core. captureSnapshot (SmithersDevToolsCore.js:33-37) only does buildSnapshot(tree) + sets this._lastSnapshot and returns the snapshot; both the return value and the instance are discarded, so the call is a pure no-op (instance immediately GC'd). The 'Keep parity with existing devtools snapshot capture semantics' comment is misleading — it has no effect on output. Genuinely dead code + a sign of a confusing instance-mutating API. P2 / medium confidence is appropriate (harmless wasted allocation, not a functional bug).

title: printTree prints props.name/props.id without type-narrowing (Record<string,unknown> values)
evidence: printTree.js:24-29 does `else if (node.props.name) line += ` "${node.props.name}"`` and `else if (node.props.id) line += ` [${node.props.id}]``. props is Record<string,unknown>, so a non-string name/id (object/number/array) is interpolated via String coercion (e.g. '[object Object]'). Tests only cover string values (treeUtilities.test.ts:262-286).
recommendation: Guard with `typeof node.props.name === 'string'` (and same for id) before interpolating, and add a test with a non-string props.name to confirm graceful handling.

title: applyDelta replaceRoot/addNode/removeNode/updateProps reject only by side effects, but updateProps with no props key sets node.props = undefined
evidence: Extends finding #2: updateProps unconditionally does node.props = cloneValue(op.props). With op.props omitted (undefined), node.props becomes undefined, breaking the DevToolsNode invariant props: Record<string,unknown> (DevToolsNode.ts). Same throwaway-validation gap as the asymmetric-validation finding; noted as concrete corroborating evidence for the recommended addNode/updateProps/updateTask guards.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: useGatewayExtensionStream never clears `error` after a successful reconnect
evidence: On a transient disconnect the catch block calls `setError(...)` (line 87). The reconnect loop then waits + resubscribes, and on the next yielded frame only resets `attempt` and pushes frames (lines 72-81). It never calls `setError(undefined)`. So after a successful reconnect the hook returns `{ streaming: true, error: <old disconnect error>, ... }` — inconsistent state where a consumer rendering the error banner keeps showing it even though the stream is healthy again. Contrast useGatewayRpc/useGatewayExtensionResource which `setError(undefined)` before each attempt.
recommendation: Clear the error when the stream resumes — e.g. call `setError(undefined)` at the top of the for-await body (when the first frame of a new subscription arrives) or right before resubscribing in the loop.
vnote: CONFIRMED as a real bug. Read packages/gateway-react/src/useGatewayExtensionStream.ts: the catch block calls setError(...) on line 87, and the only places error could be reset are the effect re-init (line 63, runs when deps change, not on reconnect) — the reconnect loop (lines 67-96) and the for-await frame handler (lines 72-81) only reset `attempt` and push frames, never setError(undefined). So after a transient drop + successful resubscribe, the hook keeps returning the stale error. The contrast claim is accurate: useGatewayRpc (line 29) and useGatewayExtensionResource (line 38) both call setError(undefined) before each attempt, and the sync useGatewayRunEvents derives error from live.isError (self-clearing). However I downgrade P1->P2: there is NO in-repo runtime consumer of this hook (grep found only docs/specs/llms-full.txt and its own tests — no app, skill, or .smithers workflow imports it; apps/smithers is out of scope and the product UI lives in a separate repo), and the symptom is a transient stale display state after recovery, not a crash or data loss. Real correctness bug in published SDK surface, but moderate blast radius.

title: Exported hooks useGatewayMutation and useGatewayRunStream have zero tests
evidence: grep of tests/ for `useGatewayMutation` and `useGatewayRunStream` returns no matches. Both are public exports (src/index.ts:49-50) and are part of the documented SDK surface (docs/guides/custom-workflow-ui.mdx, docs/integrations/gateway.mdx). useGatewayRunStream's runId->key mapping and the disabled/idle-key branch (`['gateway:streamRunEvents:idle']`, enabled=false) are entirely unexercised; useGatewayMutation's runner wiring to registry.rpc is only covered indirectly via useSyncMutation's rollback test.
recommendation: Add direct tests: useGatewayRunStream subscribing a real fake-transport stream for a given runId and the runId=undefined disabled case; useGatewayMutation firing a method through the registry and surfacing success.
vnote: CONFIRMED. grep of packages/gateway-react/tests/ (incl tests/sync/sync.test.ts) for `useGatewayMutation` and `useGatewayRunStream` returns zero matches. sync.test.ts directly tests useSyncQuery, useGatewayQuery, useSyncMutation, useSyncSubscription, useGatewayRuns, useGatewayRun, useGatewayApprovals, useGatewayWorkflows, useGatewayRunEvents, useGatewayRunTree, useGatewayConnectionStatus — but never the two named thin-wrapper shortcuts. Both are public exports (src/index.ts:49-50) and documented SDK surface (docs/guides/custom-workflow-ui.mdx:127, docs/integrations/gateway.mdx:75). useGatewayRunStream's runId->key mapping (gatewayKeys.runEvents vs the ['gateway:streamRunEvents:idle'] idle key) and the enabled=false/runId=undefined branch are entirely unexercised directly; useGatewayMutation's registry.rpc runner wiring is only exercised indirectly via useSyncMutation's rollback test (which builds its own runner, not via useGatewayMutation). Note: useGatewayMutation IS used in apps/smithers (GatewayWorkflowsSection.tsx, GatewayRunInspector.tsx) but that app is out of scope and is not a test.

title: useGatewayExtensionStream reconnect path and error surfacing are untested
evidence: The two stream tests only cover (a) backoff-key stability across re-renders and (b) the ring buffer cap. The catch/setError branch, the backoff-wait + resubscribe loop, the `streaming` toggles, and the disabled/undefined-namespace early return (lines 56-59) are never exercised. This is the exact path that contains the stale-error bug above, and it ships as public SDK surface with no in-repo consumer (no app/docs imports it), so unit tests are the only verification.
recommendation: Add a test where the fake streamExtension throws once (transient drop) then yields frames on the retry: assert error is surfaced on drop, then cleared and frames flow after reconnect; plus a test that `enabled:false`/undefined namespace yields streaming=false and no subscription.
vnote: CONFIRMED. Read packages/gateway-react/tests/extension-hooks.test.ts lines 160-227: the two useGatewayExtensionStream tests cover only (a) backoff-key stability across re-renders (no throw, infinite-block iterator) and (b) the ring-buffer cap (iterator that just ends). Neither makes streamExtension throw, so the catch/setError branch (line 87), the backoff-wait+resubscribe loop (lines 92-95), the streaming toggles (lines 64/99), and the disabled/undefined-namespace early return (lines 56-59) are never exercised. This is exactly the path containing the stale-error bug in finding #1, and the hook has no in-repo consumer, so unit tests are the only verification path. Confirmed P1 as a test-gap on the most fragile/least-covered code in the package.

title: useSyncMutation success-path branches (invalidate, onSuccess, reset, mutateSafe, success status) untested
evidence: The only useSyncMutation test exercises onMutate+onError rollback on rejection. The success path is uncovered: `onSuccess` callback (line 93), the `invalidate` loop over keys (lines 94-96), the `status:'success'` store write, `mutateSafe` swallowing errors (lines 109-118), and `reset()` (lines 120-122) are all untested. `mutateSafe` and `reset` are part of UseSyncMutationResult's public contract.
recommendation: Add a success-path test asserting status transitions idle->loading->success, onSuccess fires, listed keys are invalidated, mutateSafe returns undefined on failure, and reset() returns to idle.

title: Connection observer offline/connecting transitions and reconnectingSince never asserted
evidence: useGatewayConnectionStatus tests only cover online, unauthorized, idle (after reset), and connect()/listRuns. The `markOffline` branch (status 'offline' with `reconnectingSince` set), the `markConnecting` (idle->'connecting') transition, and the offline->online clearing of `reconnectingSince` are never exercised. A non-auth transport error path (the `else` at createGatewayCollections.ts:173 and :202) is therefore untested.
recommendation: Add a test where a non-auth rpc/stream error flips status to 'offline' with a numeric reconnectingSince, and a subsequent success flips back to 'online' clearing reconnectingSince.

title: invalidate() re-pull of pollable list collections via the pulser is untested
evidence: The whole invalidate() mechanism — the per-fingerprint pulser bus (createPulser), the INVALIDATE_SCOPE pseudo-stream wired into pollable collections (knownCollection lines 263-271), and `invalidate(prefix)` matching keys via syncKeyMatches — has no direct test. The refetch() helpers in useGatewayRuns/useGatewayApprovals/useGatewayWorkflows all route through `registry.invalidate(...)`, so a regression in the pulser would silently break live re-pull after a mutation, yet only useSyncQuery.refetch (a different code path) is tested.
recommendation: Add a test that mounts useGatewayRuns over a fake transport whose listRuns return value changes, calls refetch()/invalidate(gatewayKeys.runs()), and asserts the row set updates.

title: isAuthError 401/403 status and code-based branches untested
evidence: Only the message-regex path (`'UNAUTHORIZED: missing token'`) is exercised (sync.test.ts:582). The `status === 401`, `status === 403`, FORBIDDEN/Forbidden message regex, and the `code`-based `/^(Unauthorized|Forbidden)$/i` branch are all unverified. These determine whether onAuthError fires and whether status flips to 'unauthorized' vs 'offline' — a subtle classification with multiple OR branches.
recommendation: Add table-driven cases for `{status:401}`, `{status:403}`, `{code:'Unauthorized'}`, and a 'Forbidden ...' message, asserting markUnauthorized + onAuthError; plus a generic Error asserting markOffline (no onAuthError).

title: useGatewayExtensionAction error path and double-call generation fence untested
evidence: The single action test only covers the happy path. The catch block (sets error+pending=false then rethrows, lines 33-39) and the generation guard that prevents a stale slow call from clobbering a fast newer one (the `if (generation === generationRef.current)` checks) are untested — even though the doc comment and index.d.ts explicitly promise 'a fast double-click cannot resolve out of order'.
recommendation: Add a test where extensionRpc rejects (assert error set, pending false, promise rejects) and one where a slow first call resolves after a fast second call (assert the stale result is dropped).

title: useGatewayRunEvents afterSeq filter and error state untested
evidence: The runEvents test covers heartbeat filtering + maxEvents cap, but not the `afterSeq` option (line 45 filters `row.seq > afterSeq`) nor the error branch (`live.isError` -> `new Error('Run event stream failed.')` and `streaming:false`, lines 58-59). Both are part of the hook's documented return contract (index.d.ts:74-82).
recommendation: Add a test passing `afterSeq` and asserting earlier-seq frames are excluded, and one forcing the collection into an error state asserting error is set and streaming false.

title: createGatewayReactRoot success path (mount + dual-provider wiring) untested
evidence: Only the missing-root throw is tested. The happy path — finding `#root` (or a custom rootId), constructing SmithersGatewayClient, building collections via createGatewayCollections(createSmithersGatewayTransport(client)), and rendering BOTH SmithersGatewayProvider and SyncProvider — is the package's primary entry point for embedded custom UIs and is entirely uncovered. The default rootId fallback ('root') branch is also unverified.
recommendation: Under happy-dom, add a real root div and assert createGatewayReactRoot mounts the element, returns a SmithersGatewayClient, and that a child can read both useSmithersGateway and useSyncClient.

title: Committed src/index.d.ts is a redundant build artifact not referenced by package exports
evidence: package.json `exports['.'].types` resolves to `./src/index.ts` (the source), and `./*` -> `./src/*.ts`; nothing resolves `index.d.ts`. grep across packages/apps/docs for `gateway-react/src/index.d` returns nothing. The file is only produced/cleaned by tsup (tsup.config.ts: `clean:['index.d.ts']`, dts-only) but consumers never read it. It can silently drift from src/index.ts (it is hand-checked-in and 453 lines) yet is never the published type entrypoint.
recommendation: Either stop committing src/index.d.ts (gitignore it) since types are published from source .ts, or point exports.types at the generated .d.ts and gate it in CI. As-is it is a maintenance hazard with no consumer.
vnote: REFUTED. The premise that this is an anomalous, unconsumed, never-gated maintenance hazard is wrong on the facts. (1) It is a deliberate REPO-WIDE convention: `git ls-files 'packages/*/src/index.d.ts'` shows 26 packages commit src/index.d.ts, all with the same exports.types -> ./src/index.ts pattern and `files: ['src/']`. (2) It IS gated against drift: scripts/publish.mjs lines 137-156 runs `pnpm -r build` (which regenerates every package's src/*.d.ts via tsup --dts-only) then checks git status and FAILS the release if any committed .d.ts drifted — the comment explicitly cites the 0.24.0 stale packages/smithers/src/index.d.ts incident as the reason the gate exists. (3) It IS a consumer artifact: it ships in the published tarball (files: src/) as the bundled declaration and is the type entrypoint referenced for at least one root subpath (root package.json `./sandbox` -> types: ./packages/sandbox/src/index.d.ts). The exports.types pointing at ./src/index.ts is the source-types-for-workspace path; the committed .d.ts is the published-bundle declaration. This is not dead code and the suggested fix (gitignore it) would re-introduce the exact drift bug the publish gate was built to prevent.

title: useGatewayRunTree casts node status to NodeStatus despite the source type being plain `string`
evidence: `const status = (root?.status ?? 'queued') as NodeStatus;` — but GatewayRunNode.status is typed `status: string` (packages/gateway-client/src/sync/GatewayRunNode.ts:6). The `as NodeStatus` assertion is unsound: any status string the server emits outside the five tones would be reported to consumers as a NodeStatus, defeating the union's purpose. The producer snapshotToGatewayRunNode constrains it today, so this is type-safety debt rather than an active bug.
recommendation: Either narrow GatewayRunNode.status to the NodeStatus union at its source, or runtime-validate/clamp the value here instead of asserting.

title: buildRunTree recursion is unguarded against cyclic childIds
evidence: build() recursively follows `node.childIds` with no visited-set; a cycle (or a node listing itself as a child) in the flat rows would infinite-recurse and stack-overflow the render. Devtools data is server-generated so a cycle is unlikely, but the flat collection is reconciled per-frame from untrusted-shaped payloads and there is no defensive bound.
recommendation: Track a visited Set in build() (skip already-visited ids) so a malformed/cyclic frame degrades gracefully instead of crashing the UI.
vnote: PARTIALLY confirmed; severity/scope overstated but kept at P2. CONFIRMED: packages/gateway-react/src/sync/useGatewayRunTree.ts build() (lines 29-35) follows node.childIds recursively with no visited-set, so a self-referential or cyclic childId would infinite-recurse. However the evidence's framing as 'untrusted-shaped payloads' overstates the risk: childIds are not arbitrary — they are produced by packages/gateway-client/src/sync/flattenGatewayRunNode.ts (line 8: childIds = children.map(c=>c.id)) from a genuine nested tree (snapshotToGatewayRunNode output, derived from the run's devtools graph), which is structurally a tree/DAG and cannot naturally contain a cycle. So a cycle would require server-side corruption, not normal data. It is a legitimate defensive-hardening gap (the original auditor correctly self-rated it 'low' confidence and P2), but it is not a realistic crash path in normal operation. P2 stands.

title: useSyncClient missing-provider throw and SyncContext default are untested
evidence: grep of tests/ for `useSyncClient` and `SyncContext` returns zero. useSmithersGateway's analogous throw IS tested (gateway-react.test.ts:103-114) but useSyncClient's 'missing <SyncProvider>' error path — the deliberate fail-fast for the entire sync hook family — has no test. SyncContext's null default (the thing that makes the throw reachable) is similarly unasserted.
recommendation: Add a test rendering a sync hook (or useSyncClient directly) outside SyncProvider and asserting it throws the documented 'missing <SyncProvider>' message.

title: No README for the package
evidence: `ls README*` in packages/gateway-react returns no matches. The package is a published SDK (@smithers-orchestrator/gateway-react, listed in docs/integrations/gateway.mdx export tables) but ships with no package-level README; consumers must rely on the central docs guide. Other published packages in this monorepo conventionally carry one.
recommendation: Add a short README pointing at docs/guides/custom-workflow-ui.mdx and listing the provider + hook entrypoints, or confirm the central-docs-only convention is intentional.

title: useGatewayExtensionStream `streaming` is initialized to `enabled` and a same-tick early-return path can briefly report streaming=true with no subscription
evidence: useState(enabled) seeds `streaming` true on first render when namespace+key are set, then the effect either keeps it true (subscribed) or, in the disabled branch, sets it false (lines 56-58). For the enabled path streaming stays true until the iterator ends. This is mostly fine, but combined with the never-cleared error (finding #1) the {streaming,error} pair can be internally inconsistent (streaming=true while error holds a stale disconnect Error). Minor: there is no separate 'connected vs reconnecting' signal, so a consumer cannot distinguish a live stream from one in backoff — the docstring (lines 29-32) promises surfacing BackpressureDisconnect 'as an error and the backoff loop will retry' but provides no way to know the retry succeeded other than new frames arriving.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: flattenGatewayRunNode visit() is also unguarded against cycles (same class as buildRunTree)
evidence: While verifying finding #5 I read flattenGatewayRunNode: its recursive visit() (lines 6-18) likewise has no visited-set. This is the producer side of the same childIds data; if its input tree ever contained a shared/cyclic child node it would infinite-recurse before buildRunTree ever runs. Same low-probability defensive gap, noted for completeness since the two functions are the producer/consumer pair for the same flat-node representation. Note: this file is in gateway-client, adjacent to the assigned gateway-react area.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: streamRunEventsResilient replay detection reads frame.payload.event, but real gap_resync frames have no payload.event
evidence: Line 383: `const isReplay = isObject(frame.payload) && frame.payload.event === "run.gap_resync";`. The server (packages/server/src/gateway.js:1791-1801 sendRunGapResync) emits gap_resync with outer frame.event="run.gap_resync" and payload `{ apiVersion, type:"GapResync", streamId, runId, fromSeq, toSeq, snapshot }` — there is NO `event` field on the payload. So against a real server isReplay is always false, defeating the entire flap-protection logic the long JSDoc (lines 339-361) describes. The streamRunEvents filter on line 323 correctly uses the OUTER `frame.event === "run.gap_resync"`, proving the inconsistency. The reconnect test passes only because tests/gatewayReconnectResume.test.ts:105-113 sendGapResync injects `event:"run.gap_resync"` INTO the payload, which the real server never does.
recommendation: Detect replay via the outer frame field: `frame.event === "run.gap_resync"`. Update the test fixture's sendGapResync to mirror the real server payload (no payload.event; fromSeq/toSeq instead) so the test actually guards the production shape.
vnote: Independently confirmed and grounded against THREE sources. (1) SmithersGatewayClient.ts:383 reads `frame.payload.event === "run.gap_resync"`. (2) The real server's sendRunGapResync (packages/server/src/gateway.js:1791-1801) emits sendEvent(connection, "run.gap_resync", { apiVersion, type:"GapResync", streamId, runId, fromSeq, toSeq, snapshot }); sendEvent (gateway.js:3293-3306) wraps it so the OUTER frame.event="run.gap_resync" but the payload has NO `event` key. (3) The server contract test packages/server/tests/gateway-v1-contract.test.jsx:269-275 asserts gap.payload via objectContaining({ type:"GapResync", fromSeq, toSeq }) with no `event` field. Therefore isReplay is ALWAYS false against a real server, defeating the flap-protection the JSDoc (lines 339-361) describes: line 387 resets backoff on the first gap_resync frame, re-enabling the base-delay busy-loop the design intends to prevent. By contrast the streamRunEvents filter (line 322-323) correctly uses the OUTER frame.event. The reconnect test passes only because tests/gatewayReconnectResume.test.ts:105-113 sendGapResync injects event:"run.gap_resync" INTO the payload (line 111), which the real server never does — so the flap-protection test (line 274-336) does not guard the production shape. Note: the real gap_resync payload also lacks payload.seq (it has fromSeq/toSeq), so lastSeq is not advanced from a gap frame either; that is a secondary inconsistency in the same region. P1 confirmed.

title: createSmithersGatewayTransport.stream's streamRunEvents branch is entirely untested
evidence: Coverage report: file is 56.25% lines, uncovered 31,41,46-70. The only test (tests/sync/createSmithersGatewayTransport.test.ts) exercises the streamDevTools branch. The streamRunEvents branch (lines 45-71) — including asRunId validation, the seq-extraction fallback `frame.payload.seq ?? frame.seq ?? undefined`, and the SyncStreamFrame mapping — is the primary production read path (it wires the resilient generator into the sync cache) and has zero coverage. The rpc() method (line 41) and the unknown-scope throw (line 92) are also untested.
recommendation: Add tests for the streamRunEvents scope (asserting runId validation, afterSeq passthrough, and seq extraction from both payload.seq and outer seq), the rpc() passthrough, and the unknown-scope error.
vnote: Confirmed. Ran `bun test --coverage`: src/sync/createSmithersGatewayTransport.ts = 83.33% funcs / 56.25% lines, uncovered 31,41,46-70 — exactly the rpc() body (41) and the entire streamRunEvents branch (46-70: asRunId validation, the resilient-generator wiring, the seq fallback `frame.payload.seq ?? frame.seq ?? undefined`, and the SyncStreamFrame mapping). The only test (tests/sync/createSmithersGatewayTransport.test.ts) exercises ONLY the streamDevTools branch. This is the primary production read path (wires the resilient generator into the sync cache) and has zero coverage. Minor correction: the unknown-scope throw at line 92 is NOT flagged uncovered by the coverage report (so the auditor's claim that line 92 is untested is slightly off), and the test uses a hand-built fake client object rather than the real client (unit-level, acceptable). Core test-gap (streamRunEvents branch + rpc passthrough) fully confirmed; P1 stands given this is the live read path.

title: gatewayCollectionDefs is largely untested (27% lines) — most collection defs and frame mappers have no coverage
evidence: Coverage report: 27.36% lines; uncovered 13-15,19,23,27-29,45-51,55-66,70-76,80-84,90-95,97-102,104-114,137-142,144-152. Only the `nodes` def + snapshotRows path is tested (tests/sync/gatewayCollectionDefs.test.ts). Untested: the `workflows`, `runs`, `run`, `approvals`, `runEvents` factories; `eventRows` (its `typeof frame.seq !== 'number'` heartbeat-skip branch); `runStatusFromFrame` (all branches: run.completed/started/resumed/paused, failed/cancelled normalization); and `runRowsFromFrame` (the live status-merge that drives the `run` collection's stream).
recommendation: Add unit tests for each collection def's key/method/getKey/rows wiring, eventRows seq-skip, runStatusFromFrame's branch matrix, and runRowsFromFrame merging a status onto an existing run row.
vnote: Confirmed via coverage run: src/sync/gatewayCollectionDefs.ts = 18.75% funcs / 27.36% lines, uncovered 13-15,19,23,27-29,45-51,55-66,70-76,80-84,90-95,97-102,104-114,137-142,144-152 — matching the auditor's range. The only test (tests/sync/gatewayCollectionDefs.test.ts) exercises the `nodes` def + snapshotRows. Untested: workflows/runs/run/approvals/runEvents factories; eventRows (incl the `typeof frame.seq !== 'number'` heartbeat-skip at line 46); runStatusFromFrame (all branches: run.completed state/status + failed/cancelled normalization at 60-64, run.started/run.resumed at 65, run.paused at 66); and runRowsFromFrame (status-merge driving the `run` collection stream, 80-87). Read all of gatewayCollectionDefs.ts and the test to verify the gap. P1 stands — runStatusFromFrame/runRowsFromFrame is the live status-merge feeding the run collection.

title: Stale empty src/index.d.ts is checked in and shipped
evidence: The file contains only `\nexport {  }`. package.json `types` points to `./src/index.ts` (the TS source), so index.d.ts is never referenced, yet it ships via `files: ["src/"]`. git log shows it was last touched at v0.18.0 (c3682dca) and is empty/stale; the sibling gateway-react/src/index.d.ts is a real 23KB generated file. tsup.config.ts uses `outDir: src` so a real `pnpm build` would overwrite it, meaning the committed empty version is a stale artifact.
recommendation: Delete the committed src/index.d.ts (and gitignore generated *.d.ts in src), or regenerate it via the build. Either way the empty stub should not be tracked.
vnote: Confirmed. packages/gateway-client/src/index.d.ts is a tracked 13-byte file containing only `\nexport {  }\n`. The sibling packages/gateway-react/src/index.d.ts is a real 23KB generated file. exports["."].types points to ./src/index.ts (the TS source), so the empty .d.ts is never referenced by consumers, yet it ships via files:["src/"]. git log shows it was last touched at v0.18.0 (c3682dca). tsup.config.ts uses dts:{only:true} + outDir:"src", so a real build would regenerate/overwrite it — confirming the committed empty stub is a stale artifact. Minor correction: there is no top-level `types` field in package.json (the auditor said `types` points to ./src/index.ts); it is exports["."].types — substance unchanged. P2 confirmed.

title: Exported GatewayRequestFrame type is never imported or used anywhere
evidence: grep across the whole repo: GatewayRequestFrame is only its own definition and the re-export in index.ts:7. It is not used internally — SmithersGatewayConnection.requestRaw (line 110) builds the request frame inline as an untyped object literal `{ type: "req", id, method, params }` rather than typing it as GatewayRequestFrame. No external package references it (the `parseGatewayRequestFrame` in packages/server is an unrelated server symbol).
recommendation: Either delete GatewayRequestFrame.ts and its export, or actually use it to type the frame built in SmithersGatewayConnection.requestRaw.
vnote: Confirmed. Repo-wide grep (excluding node_modules/dist/index.d.ts): GatewayRequestFrame appears only at its own definition (src/GatewayRequestFrame.ts:1) and the re-export in src/index.ts:7. No internal or external consumer (gateway-react, apps/cli, .smithers all clean). The server's parseGatewayRequestFrame is an unrelated symbol. SmithersGatewayConnection.requestRaw (line 110) builds the request frame inline as an untyped literal `{ type: "req", id, method, params }` rather than typing it as GatewayRequestFrame — so the one place it would describe doesn't use it. It is an exported public-API type; arguably intentional surface, but it is genuinely unused internally. P2 dead-code confirmed.

title: Three exported gatewayKeys factories (cronList, nodeOutput, nodeDiff) are unused
evidence: Repo-wide grep: gatewayKeys.cronList and gatewayKeys.nodeOutput have zero references anywhere (incl tests and gateway-react). gatewayKeys.nodeDiff appears only in a self-referential assertion (tests/sync/createGatewayCollection.test.ts:254) that just checks the factory output shape. gatewayKeys.devtools is used only in tests. The actual node-output hook (packages/gateway-react/src/useGatewayNodeOutput.ts:9) uses the raw string "getNodeOutput" via useGatewayRpc, not gatewayKeys.nodeOutput. There are no cron/nodeOutput/nodeDiff collection defs that would consume these keys.
recommendation: Remove the unused factories, or document them as an intentional reserved public-API surface. If kept, add real consumers (or at least cover the iteration-default branches at lines 16-17/20-21).
vnote: Confirmed. Precise grep for `gatewayKeys.cronList` / `gatewayKeys.nodeOutput` / `gatewayKeys.nodeDiff` across the repo: cronList and nodeOutput have ZERO references; nodeDiff appears only at tests/sync/createGatewayCollection.test.ts:254 in a self-referential shape assertion (the same test at lines 252-253 asserts gatewayCollectionDefs does NOT contain nodeOutput/nodeDiff, i.e. no collection def consumes these keys). The real node-output hook packages/gateway-react/src/useGatewayNodeOutput.ts:8-9 uses the raw string "getNodeOutput" via useGatewayRpc, not gatewayKeys.nodeOutput. (Note: client.cronList() the RPC method at SmithersGatewayClient.ts:512 IS used and is a distinct symbol from the gatewayKeys.cronList cache-key factory.) These are exported as part of a public `gatewayKeys` convenience object so they may be intentional reserved surface; medium confidence / P2 is appropriate. Confirmed.

title: Duplicated helper functions copy-pasted across files (isObject x3, isGatewayResponseFrame x2 identical, withoutVirtualFields x3, asRecord x2)
evidence: isGatewayResponseFrame is byte-identical (verified via diff) in SmithersGatewayClient.ts:169-182 and SmithersGatewayConnection.ts:50-63. isObject is defined three times (SmithersGatewayClient.ts:117, SmithersGatewayConnection.ts:37, sync/createSmithersGatewayTransport.ts:25). withoutVirtualFields is defined three times (createGatewayCollection.ts:101, gatewayCollectionDefs.ts:70, reconcileSnapshotNodes.ts:4) with the same `$`-prefix/undefined-stripping logic. asRecord twice (snapshotToGatewayRunNode.ts:23, gatewayCollectionDefs.ts:13).
recommendation: Extract these into a shared internal module (e.g. src/internal/frames.ts and src/sync/rowUtil.ts) and import. Keeps the wire-frame guard logic single-sourced so a future change can't drift between the HTTP and WS validators.

title: getDevToolsSnapshot is used by the nodes collection but is missing from the client's typed RPC surface
evidence: gatewayCollectionDefs.nodes uses `method: "getDevToolsSnapshot"` (line 118) and gatewayKeys.devtoolsSnapshot exists, but "getDevToolsSnapshot" is NOT in GatewayRpcMethod (packages/gateway/src/rpc/index.ts:72-91) nor in GatewayRpcTypeMap (no entry), and SmithersGatewayClient has no getDevToolsSnapshot() convenience method. It is a real server route (packages/server/src/gateway.js:3998). It only works because createGatewayCollection routes through the untyped client.rpc->rpcRaw(string) path. A typed `client.rpc("getDevToolsSnapshot", ...)` would fail to compile.
recommendation: Add getDevToolsSnapshot to GatewayRpcRequestMap/ResponseMap (and the gateway/rpc method union if appropriate) plus a convenience method, so the nodes collection's method name is type-checked rather than a magic string.

title: Run-row status vocabulary diverges from node status vocabulary (raw 'finished' vs normalized 'ok')
