evidence: runStatusFromFrame for run.completed returns `asString(innerPayload.state) ?? asString(innerPayload.status)` (line 61). The real server completed payload is `{ runId, status: "finished" | "failed" | "cancelled" }` (packages/server/src/gateway.js:3760-3784), so a finished run yields run-row status = "finished". But snapshotToGatewayRunNode.toRunStatus (snapshotToGatewayRunNode.ts:75-98) normalizes "finished"->"ok". The `run` collection therefore stores raw "finished" while the `nodes` collection stores normalized "ok" for the same lifecycle, and this path is entirely untested.
recommendation: Decide on one status vocabulary and normalize consistently (reuse toRunStatus, or document that the run collection passes through raw server status by design). Add a test pinning the chosen behavior.

title: Untested error/auth/reconnect branches in createGatewayCollection
evidence: Coverage uncovered lines include: isAuthError status==401/403 path (75-76 only the message path is tested via 'UNAUTHORIZED: missing token'); defaultRows single-object and empty branches (75-76); the insert-key-already-present-becomes-update branch (182-185); refetchMode:'upsert' path (257); the 'client has no stream implementation' throw (277); and the entire openStreamLoop catch/auth-return/onError/sleep+reconnect block (292-308). The reconnect-on-graceful-end and stream-failure-then-resume behaviors are only exercised at the SmithersGatewayClient level, not through createGatewayCollection's own backoff loop.
recommendation: Add tests: stream impl missing -> throws; stream throws non-auth -> onError + reconnect with backoff via the controllableStreamTransport.fail(); stream throws auth error -> onAuthError + loop stops; isAuthError via status 401/403 and via code 'Unauthorized'; refetchMode 'upsert'; insert-on-existing-key coalesces to update.

title: snapshotToGatewayRunNode nodeKind/nodeName/nodeStatus branches partially untested
evidence: Coverage report: 85.57% lines, uncovered 53-60,77,84-87. The nodeKind switch arms for Signal/WaitForEvent->signal, Human/HumanTask->human, Loop/ForEach->loop (lines 53-60) are not exercised (tests only cover Task->agent, Approval->approval, and the default compute). nodeName's props.label/props.name fallbacks and toRunStatus's failed/waiting families (lines 77,84-87) are also uncovered.
recommendation: Add a snapshot fixture covering Signal/WaitForEvent, Human/HumanTask, Loop/ForEach node types and props.label/props.name fallbacks, plus a failed and a waiting-event runState to cover toRunStatus's failed/waiting arms.

title: streamExtension and extensionRpc lack reconnect/error-frame and abort coverage
evidence: tests/extension.test.ts covers the happy path (initial + filtered live frames) and an extensionRpc error frame, but not: the GATEWAY_EXTENSION_STREAM_ERROR frame that throws GatewayRpcError (lines 588-596), the invalidGatewayResponse when the subscribe reply omits/mis-types streamId (lines 574-576), or response.initial===undefined skip (line 577). extensionStreamMethodName/extensionMethodName helpers are covered, but the stream-error throwing path is a documented contract (JSDoc lines 551-553) with no test.
recommendation: Add tests for an ext.stream.error frame matching the streamId (asserts GatewayRpcError with the wire code/message), a malformed subscribe response (throws INVALID_GATEWAY_RESPONSE), and a response without `initial`.

title: connect() open-failure path leaves the socket unclosed (inconsistent with abort path)
evidence: On WebSocket 'error' before open, onError (lines 272-274) rejects but does not call ws.close(), whereas onAbort (lines 276-280) does close the socket. A socket that errored is usually already closing, but the asymmetry is a latent resource-leak risk for WebSocket implementations that fire 'error' without auto-closing.
recommendation: Call ws.close() in onError for symmetry, or document why it is unnecessary. Add a test asserting closeCalls after an open-failure error.

title: Five network probe adapters have zero unit tests despite the spec mandating fixture tests for each
evidence: packages/usage/tests/usage.test.js imports neither claudeOauthUsage, codexWhamUsage, anthropicHeaderUsage, openaiHeaderUsage, nor googleUsage. Each has multiple untested branches: e.g. anthropicHeaderUsage.js lines 34-47 (401 -> none, 429 -> headers+error with/without retry-after, plain headers, catch); openaiHeaderUsage.js lines 39-55 (401, 429, `!res.ok && windows.length===0` -> none, catch); claudeOauthUsage.js lines 38-50 (401/429/!ok/catch); codexWhamUsage.js lines 33-43 (401/!ok/catch, ChatGPT-Account-Id header only when accountId present); googleUsage.js always returns none (trivial but untested). The spec .smithers/specs/usage-and-limits.md:445-447 explicitly requires 'Each adapter against recorded JSON/header fixtures: a healthy Claude report, an exhausted weekly window, a Codex pro snapshot, OpenAI headers including a 429 retry-after, a Google 429 RetryInfo, a Kimi source:none.'
recommendation: Add unit tests that inject a fetch seam (or use a real local HTTP fixture server per the no-mocks rule) to exercise each adapter's 200/401/429/non-ok/throw paths and assert the resulting UsageProbe. At minimum, googleUsage and the header-error formatting (retry-after present/absent) are pure enough to test without network.
vnote: Verified by reading tests/usage.test.js lines 5-20: the import list includes only parsers/formatters/builders/getters and readUsageCache — none of claudeOauthUsage, codexWhamUsage, anthropicHeaderUsage, openaiHeaderUsage, googleUsage. All five are public exports (index.js lines 16-20). The only test exercising getAccountUsage/getUsageForAccounts uses provider 'kimi', which hits the inline case 'kimi' branch in getAccountUsage.js:45-46 and never invokes any adapter (confirmed by reading probeFor). Spec .smithers/specs/usage-and-limits.md:445-447 verbatim requires per-adapter fixture tests (healthy Claude, exhausted weekly, Codex pro, OpenAI 429 retry-after, Google 429 RetryInfo, Kimi source:none). Cited branch ranges are accurate (anthropicHeaderUsage 34-47, openaiHeaderUsage 39-55, claudeOauthUsage 38-50, codexWhamUsage 33-43; googleUsage always returns none). Nuance worth noting: the adapters call the module-global fetch directly with no DI seam, so the parsing logic IS already isolated and tested (parseClaudeOauthUsage/parseCodexUsage/parseAnthropicRateLimitHeaders/parseOpenAiRateLimitHeaders all have tests); what is untested is the HTTP status-dispatch and header-extraction code in the adapters, which today cannot be tested without adding a fetch seam or a real local HTTP fixture server. The recommendation already acknowledges this. P1 stands given the explicit spec mandate.

title: Credential readers (readClaudeCredentials, readCodexCredentials) have zero tests, including the JWT account-id fallback
evidence: Neither readClaudeCredentials nor readCodexCredentials is imported by usage.test.js. Untested branches include: readClaudeCredentials.js configDir .credentials.json present/parseable/empty/missing-accessToken (lines 20-25, 57-68) and the darwin Keychain spawnSync path (lines 27-37) — note this function only DI-seams `platform`, not the spawn/readFile, so the Keychain branch is untestable without a process seam the spec asked for ('Use a DI seam for the spawn/read', spec line 449-450). readCodexCredentials.js: missing configDir, missing/unparseable auth.json, accessToken empty, account_id from tokens vs the decodeJwtClaims(id_token)['https://api.openai.com/auth'].chatgpt_account_id fallback (lines 19-40) — none exercised.
recommendation: Add temp-configDir fixture tests for both readers covering present/absent/malformed credential files and the id_token JWT account-id fallback. Add a spawn/read DI seam to readClaudeCredentials so the macOS Keychain branch is testable on non-macOS CI (per spec §11).
vnote: Verified: neither readClaudeCredentials nor readCodexCredentials appears in usage.test.js imports (lines 5-20), and both are public exports (index.js:14-15). readClaudeCredentials.js only DI-seams platform (param at line 19); spawnSync (line 28) and readFileSync (via readFileSafe, line 23/46) are hardcoded — so the configDir file path IS testable with a temp dir but the darwin Keychain branch (lines 27-37) needs a spawn seam to test on non-macOS CI, exactly as the evidence and spec §11 (lines 448-450, 'Use a DI seam for the spawn/read') state. readCodexCredentials.js (lines 18-41) reads only files (no spawn), so all its branches — missing configDir, missing/unparseable auth.json, empty accessToken, account_id from tokens.account_id vs the decodeJwtClaims(id_token)['https://api.openai.com/auth'].chatgpt_account_id fallback at lines 32-39 — are testable with temp dirs and none are exercised. Confirmed accurate.

title: No e2e coverage of the `smithers usage` command
evidence: grep across e2e/ for 'usage', getUsageForAccounts, formatUsageReports returns no matches. apps/cli/src/index.js:5874-5894 implements the `usage` command (with --account/--provider/--fresh filters, table to stderr, JSON envelope to stdout via c.ok({reports})) but no e2e exercises it. The package's only two externally-consumed exports are getUsageForAccounts and formatUsageReports (apps/cli/src/index.js:45).
recommendation: Add an e2e (or CLI integration) test seeding a fake accounts.json under SMITHERS_HOME with an unsupported provider (e.g. kimi) so it needs no network, and assert the table + JSON envelope render and the cache file is written. The existing getUsageForAccounts caching test (usage.test.js:215) is the right pattern to extend at the CLI level.

title: Pure parser/formatter branches and boundaries untested
evidence: Verified by running the code: humanizeDurationShort.js:10 returns 'now' for NaN and for exactly 0 (untested; tests only cover -5, 42, 185, 9660, 442800). formatUsageReports.js usedCell: the `estimated` unit branch (lines 16-20, renders '~50/1000 (est)'), and the count-only-remaining / used+limit fallbacks (lines 22-25) are never rendered by any test. parseCodexUsage labelForMinutes boundaries — minutes<=60 -> hourly, <1440 -> 5h, >=20160 -> monthly (parseCodexUsage.js:13-17) — only the ~300 and ~10080 cases are tested; hourly/monthly/undefined-minutes fallback are untested. parseClaudeOauthUsage seven_day_sonnet branch (parseClaudeOauthUsage.js:44) is only exercised with null. credits without balance (parseCodexUsage.js:73) untested.
recommendation: Add table-driven cases for these pure functions — they are deterministic and trivially testable, so reaching ~100% here is cheap.

title: Declared dependency @smithers-orchestrator/errors is unused
evidence: package.json:29 lists "@smithers-orchestrator/errors": "workspace:*" as a dependency, but grep -rn 'errors' over packages/usage/ (excluding package.json) returns no matches — no source, test, or .d.ts file imports it. Only @smithers-orchestrator/accounts is actually imported (buildUsageReport.js:1 API_KEY_PROVIDERS, usageCache.js:3 accountsRoot).
recommendation: Remove the unused @smithers-orchestrator/errors dependency from packages/usage/package.json, or use it (the adapters currently return ad-hoc error strings rather than typed errors).
vnote: Verified: package.json:29 declares '@smithers-orchestrator/errors':'workspace:*'. grep -rn 'errors' over src/ and tests/ (all .js/.ts/.d.ts) returns zero matches; grep for '@smithers-orchestrator/errors' across the whole package matches only package.json:29. The errors package is real (packages/errors/ exists), so this is a genuinely unused declared workspace dependency, not a phantom. Only @smithers-orchestrator/accounts is actually imported. Confirmed.

title: readUsageCache does not validate cache version and accepts array `entries`
evidence: Verified by running readUsageCache against crafted files: a file with {version:2, entries:{...}} is silently coerced to {version:1, entries:{...}} (usageCache.js:31-33 reads parsed.entries but always returns version:1, ignoring the on-disk version), so the version field is effectively unenforced — a future incompatible v2 format would be consumed as v1. Separately, the guard `typeof parsed.entries === 'object'` accepts arrays: a file with entries:[1,2,3] is returned verbatim as entries, contradicting the docstring's promise to 'return an empty cache when the file is missing or malformed'.
recommendation: Reject (return an empty cache) when parsed.version !== 1, and tighten the entries guard to reject arrays (e.g. check !Array.isArray(parsed.entries)). Add tests for both malformed shapes.
vnote: Verified at runtime against crafted temp files. A file {version:2, entries:{a:{report:{}}}} is returned as {version:1, entries:{a:{report:{}}}} — the on-disk version 2 is silently coerced to 1 (usageCache.js:31-32 reads parsed.entries but hard-returns version:1). A file {version:1, entries:[1,2,3]} is returned verbatim as entries:[1,2,3] because the guard at line 31 only checks typeof parsed.entries==='object' (arrays pass), contradicting the docstring 'return an empty cache when the file is missing or malformed' (lines 20-21). Forward-compat severity is mild (no v2 format exists today and writeUsageCache always writes version:1; an array entries degrades gracefully since downstream cache.entries[label] string access on an array returns undefined). P2 appropriate.

title: Anthropic/OpenAI count windows can report a negative `used` when remaining > limit
evidence: Verified at runtime: parseAnthropicRateLimitHeaders with requests-limit=100, requests-remaining=150 yields {limit:100, remaining:150, used:-50} (parseAnthropicRateLimitHeaders.js:25 `used = limit - remaining` with no clamp; same in parseOpenAiRateLimitHeaders.js:28). Providers can briefly report remaining > limit (burst/credit), which would surface a negative 'used' in the UI.
recommendation: Clamp used to a minimum of 0 (e.g. Math.max(0, limit - remaining)) in both countWindow helpers, and add a boundary test.
vnote: Verified at runtime: parseAnthropicRateLimitHeaders with limit=100, remaining=150 returns {limit:100, remaining:150, used:-50}; parseOpenAiRateLimitHeaders with the same yields used:-50. Both compute used = limit - remaining with no clamp (parseAnthropicRateLimitHeaders.js:25, parseOpenAiRateLimitHeaders.js:28 — cited lines accurate). Real-world trigger (provider briefly reporting remaining>limit on burst/credit) is uncommon, so medium confidence / P2 is right; Math.max(0, limit-remaining) is the trivial fix.

title: parseDurationSeconds silently parses leading garbage (lenient regex)
evidence: Verified: parseDurationSeconds('abc5s') returns 5 and parseDurationSeconds('6m0sXYZ') returns 360 — the global regex (parseDurationSeconds.js:20) matches duration tokens anywhere in the string, so non-duration prefixes/suffixes are ignored rather than rejected. The docstring says it 'Returns undefined for input it cannot parse, so callers can omit a reset time rather than render a wrong one', but malformed-with-embedded-duration input yields a (possibly wrong) number instead of undefined. The test only covers fully-valid and fully-invalid ('nope') inputs.
recommendation: Either anchor the parse (validate the whole trimmed string is a sequence of duration tokens before summing) or document the lenient behavior. Add tests for embedded-garbage inputs to lock in the chosen behavior.

title: readClaudeCredentials reads token expiresAt but the adapter never checks it before sending
evidence: readClaudeCredentials.js:64-65 parses and returns expiresAt, but claudeOauthUsage.js sends the bearer token (lines 29-37) without consulting expiresAt, relying solely on the endpoint's 401 (claudeOauthUsage.js:38). The expiresAt field is therefore returned but never used by any caller (grep shows no read of `.expiresAt` outside readClaudeCredentials.js). The spec (lines 403-405) discusses preferring CLI refresh over sending an expired token. Functionally harmless (401 path degrades cleanly) but the field is dead.
recommendation: Either drop expiresAt from the return type or use it to short-circuit to a clearer 'token expired; run `claude` to refresh' error before spending a network round-trip.

title: getUsageForAccounts has untested branches: --fresh bypass, the claude-code hard 180s floor, and the cache-write-failure catch
evidence: tests/usage.test.js:215-227 is the only test of getUsageForAccounts and exercises just the default-options cache-hit/staleness path with provider 'kimi' (grep confirms no test uses fresh: or a claude-code provider through getUsageForAccounts). Untested branches in getUsageForAccounts.js: the fresh=true bypass interacting with refreshIntervalMs (lines 50-53), the hardFloorMs('claude-code')=180_000 floor that must override --fresh (lines 29-31, 51), the NaN/Infinity stale-age path when fetchedAt is missing/non-string (lines 47-49), and the write-failure catch at lines 67-73 that must not break the command. These are pure-enough to test with a seeded temp SMITHERS_HOME cache file and the kimi provider (no network).
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Approval detection ignores state-string normalization, so /smithers-approve and the active-run prompt miss waiting nodes
evidence: collectNodeStates() captures node.props.state RAW (extension.ts:231: `state: typeof node.props?.state === "string" ? node.props.state : "unknown"`). The smithers-approve command (extension.ts:502 `.filter((node) => node.state === "waiting-approval")`) and buildSmithersPiSystemPrompt (line 107 `node.state === "waiting-approval" || node.state === "waiting-timer"`) then compare against the exact literal. But the canonical store/RunTree code normalizes states via normalizeState() (DevToolsStore.ts:103-105, lowercases + collapses `_`/spaces) and treats `blocked`, `waitingapproval`, `waiting_approval`, camelCase as waiting-for-approval (DevToolsStore.ts:113-117, RunTree.ts:47-51). props is an opaque Record<string,unknown> (protocol DevToolsNode.props) populated from JSX, so a component emitting `blocked`/`waitingApproval`/`waiting_approval` yields zero matches here while the inspector tree correctly shows the `!` waiting marker.
recommendation: Reuse the same normalizeState helper (currently private to DevToolsStore/RunTree) for collectNodeStates and the approval/prompt filters so the extension agrees with the inspector on what 'waiting for approval' means; treat `blocked` as waiting-approval too.
vnote: Verified. collectNodeStates (extension.ts:231) stores props.state RAW. smithers-approve (extension.ts:501-502) filters on exact literal 'waiting-approval'; buildSmithersPiSystemPrompt.ts:106-108 filters on exact 'waiting-approval'||'waiting-timer'. activeRun.nodeStates is fed by collectNodeStates(active) (extension.ts:433). Meanwhile RunTree.stateIcon (RunTree.ts:28-58) normalizes via .trim().toLowerCase().replace(/[_\s]/g,'-') and treats blocked/waitingapproval/waiting-approval/waiting-timer as the '!' waiting marker, and DevToolsStore.normalizeState/runStatusForRoot (DevToolsStore.ts:103-132) treats 'blocked' as waiting-approval. protocol DevToolsNode.props is Record<string,unknown> populated by JSX via snapshotSerializer (opaque), and gateway-client confirms 'blocked' is a real waiting state string. So a component emitting blocked/waitingApproval/waiting_approval shows '!' in the tree but yields zero matches in the approve command and prompt. Zero tests cover this path (no test references collectNodeStates/smithers-approve/waiting-approval in tests/). P1 justified.

title: RunInspector subscribes to the store but never unsubscribes; dispose() is dead code
evidence: The constructor calls `store.subscribe(() => this.invalidate())` (line 64) and discards the returned unsubscribe function. `dispose()` (lines 164-166) calls `store.disconnect()` but a repo-wide grep for `.dispose(` returns no callers — the extension's openInspector (extension.ts:289-296) wires `onClose: done` to close the TUI overlay but never calls dispose(). Each `/smithers`/`/smithers-watch` open therefore leaks a listener that lives for the store's lifetime (the store is cached per-run in `runs` and reused), and the underlying devtools stream keeps running after the inspector closes.
recommendation: Store the unsubscribe handle and call it from dispose()/onClose; invoke dispose() (or at least unsubscribe) when the custom UI closes. Decide whether closing the inspector should also disconnect the stream or leave it for status-bar polling, and make that consistent.
vnote: Verified. store.subscribe returns an unsubscribe closure (DevToolsStore.ts:273-276); RunInspector constructor (RunInspector.ts:64) calls store.subscribe(() => this.invalidate()) and discards the handle. dispose() (RunInspector.ts:164-166) only calls store.disconnect() and has no callers anywhere (grep for '.dispose(' across packages/apps/.smithers/scripts returned none; only the declaration in index.d.ts:406 and RunInspector.ts:164). openInspector (extension.ts:289-296) wires onClose:done to close the TUI overlay but never calls dispose(). Stores are cached per-run in 'runs' and reused (trackRun extension.ts:256-268), so each open leaks a listener for the store's lifetime. P1 justified.

title: System prompt advertises `-u`/`-k` flag aliases that are never registered
evidence: The prompt states '`--smithers-url` / `-u`' and '`--smithers-key` / `-k`' but extension.ts only does `pi.registerFlag("smithers-url", {...})` and `pi.registerFlag("smithers-key", {...})` (lines 376-385) with no alias/short form. Users/agents told to use `-u`/`-k` will hit an unknown-flag error.
recommendation: Either register the short aliases on the flags or drop `-u`/`-k` from the prompt guidance.

title: Entire documented api/* public surface has zero direct tests
file: packages/pi-plugin/src/api/approve.ts, cancel.ts, deny.ts, getFrames.ts, getStatus.ts, listRuns.ts, resume.ts, runWorkflow.ts, streamEvents.ts
evidence: These 9 functions are re-exported from src/index.ts and are the advertised integration surface (scripts/check-docs.mjs:2323-2325 documents `import { runWorkflow, approve, streamEvents } from "@smithers-orchestrator/pi-plugin"`). Grepping tests/ shows none of them are imported or exercised (only SmithersPiHttpClient.events is tested). Each builds a specific path/body (e.g. approve.ts: `/v1/runs/${runId}/nodes/${nodeId}/approve` with body `{iteration: args.iteration ?? 0, note}`; getFrames.ts: `/v1/runs/${runId}/frames?limit=${tail ?? 20}`; listRuns.ts URLSearchParams branches). A wrong path/param would silently break with no failing test.
recommendation: Add unit tests per wrapper that stub fetch and assert URL, method, headers (auth), and JSON body for default and supplied args (including iteration?? 0, tail?? 20, listRuns limit/status branches, runWorkflow optional runId, resume's resume:true flag).
vnote: Verified. All 9 wrappers (approve/cancel/deny/getFrames/getStatus/listRuns/resume/runWorkflow/streamEvents) are re-exported from index.ts:1-9 and are the documented integration surface (docs/integrations/pi-integration.mdx:36, llms-full.txt:11097, check-docs.mjs:2324). The only test importing src/api is SmithersPiHttpClient.test.ts which tests SmithersPiHttpClient.events only. Grep hits for these names in tests are incidental (system-prompt text, ReaderSpy.cancel). Confirmed each builds a specific path/body: approve.ts uses iteration ?? 0, getFrames.ts uses ?limit=${tail ?? 20}, listRuns.ts uses URLSearchParams limit/status branches. None exercised. P1 justified.

title: DevToolsClient has no dedicated unit tests; many methods and error paths uncovered
evidence: Only a single happy-path WS integration test (DevToolsStore.test.ts:133-184) touches DevToolsClient. Untested: rpc() HTTP-error path (lines 506-512), assertOk error mapping (518-527), auditRowId() recursion across AUDIT_ROW_ID_KEYS/NESTED_AUDIT_CONTAINERS and arrays (154-180), normalizeEvent() kind/type/snake_case branches and PI_DEVTOOLS_DECODE_ERROR throws (111-140), unsupportedRpc()/performMutation fallback chain for resume() (142-152, 476-493), the SeqOutOfRange→gapResync recovery in streamDevTools (349-359), devtools.error frame handling and streamId filtering (370-385), GatewayWsConnection timeout (PI_GATEWAY_TIMEOUT, 243-255) and rejectAll on socket error, approve/deny/signal/cancel/getNodeOutput/getNodeDiff/getDevToolsSnapshot request shapes.
recommendation: Add focused tests: pure-function tests for auditRowId/normalizeEvent/unsupportedRpc/toWsUrl; WS-fixture tests for SeqOutOfRange resync, devtools.error→throw, connect.challenge timeout; an HTTP-fixture test for rpc() non-200 and ok:false frames; and performMutation method fallback for resume().
vnote: Verified. DevToolsClient is touched only by one happy-path WS integration test (DevToolsStore.test.ts:133-184) which exercises connect.challenge + streamDevTools snapshot/delta. Confirmed the cited untested code exists: rpc() HTTP-error path (505-512), assertOk (518-527), auditRowId recursion (154-180), normalizeEvent kind/type/snake_case + PI_DEVTOOLS_DECODE_ERROR throws (111-140), unsupportedRpc (142-152), performMutation fallback for resume (464-466,476-493), GatewayWsConnection timeout PI_GATEWAY_TIMEOUT (243-255) and rejectAll on socket error (287-293). approve/deny/signal/cancel/getNodeOutput/getNodeDiff request shapes (411-466) are untested. P1 justified.

title: Four view classes (RunTree, NodeInspector, Header, FrameScrubber) have no dedicated unit tests
evidence: Tests only import RunInspector (one render smoke test). No test exercises: RunTree search mode, j/k/arrow navigation, collapse/expand, auto-expansion of running/failed paths, failedDescendant bubble, ghost badge rendering; NodeInspector tab switching/scroll and output/diff/logs key resolution (firstPresent over output/row/result/value etc.) and toolCalls parsing; Header heartbeat color thresholds, snake/camel runState field parsing, dateMs ISO parsing; FrameScrubber left/right/home/end input and historical/stale rendering. These contain the bulk of the package's branch logic.
recommendation: Add per-view unit tests driving handleInput() and asserting render() output (with a passthrough theme) across selection, search, tab, scrub, and heartbeat-threshold cases.
vnote: Verified. Tests import only RunInspector (one render smoke test in DevToolsStore.test.ts:104-121 and one in the WS integration test). No test imports RunTree/NodeInspector/Header/FrameScrubber directly. Confirmed the cited branch logic exists: RunTree search mode + j/k/home/end/collapse (RunTree.ts:185-242), NodeInspector tab switching 1/2/3 + scroll (NodeInspector.ts:82-95) and firstPresent over output/row/result/value etc. (189,196,203) + toolCalls parsing (62), Header heartbeatColor thresholds (34) and snake/camel field parsing (99-116), FrameScrubber left/right/home/end (FrameScrubber.ts:16-29). These are the bulk of the package branch logic and are untested. P1 justified.

title: DevToolsStore: scrubTo, rewind, returnToLive, ghost budget/eviction, stale banner, and reconnect/backoff are untested
evidence: DevToolsStore.test.ts covers only delta apply, one gapResync sequence, ghost selection, and two render smoke tests. Untested branches include: scrubTo() boundary (targetFrame>=latestFrameNo → returnToLive), scrubTo() error path setting scrubError (lines 403-405), rewind() guards (no confirm / rewindInFlight / no runId / isRunFinished / non-historical, lines 424-442) and success path (449-464), returnToLive() requestResync, enforceGhostBudget() eviction at cap (862-871), pruneGhostNodesNowActive/pruneGhostNodesForRewind (873-888), captureGhostNodesFromDelta replaceRoot/removeNode (811-831), staleBanner timer reveal (markConnectionInterrupted/scheduleStaleBannerReveal, 706-733), ReconnectBackoff doubling+cap (160-179), consumeStream decode-error handling (557-559), and applyDeltaToLiveState baseSeq-mismatch→requestResync (620-625).
recommendation: Add deterministic unit tests for these (inject ghostNodeCap, staleBannerDelayMs, and a fake client) covering rewind guards/success, ghost eviction at cap, scrub boundary/error, baseSeq-mismatch resync, and backoff growth.
vnote: Verified. DevToolsStore.test.ts has exactly 5 tests: delta apply, one gapResync sequence, ghost selection, and two render smoke tests. Confirmed the cited untested branches exist: scrubTo error path setting scrubError (403-405), rewind() guards (424-442: !confirm/rewindInFlight/no runId/isRunFinished/non-historical) and success path (448-469), returnToLive() requestResync (409-422), ReconnectBackoff doubling+cap (160-179). None of these methods are invoked by the existing tests (which only call applyEvent/selectNode/render/disconnect). P1 justified.

title: Committed generated src/index.d.ts is unreferenced by the exports map (dead generated artifact in source)
evidence: tsup.config.ts emits the .d.ts into outDir 'src' (dts.only), and src/index.d.ts is git-tracked. But package.json exports point types to ./src/index.ts (lines 8-12), not the generated .d.ts, and no tsconfig/build references src/index.d.ts. It can drift from source (it declares a wide RunStateView$1 union for DevToolsClient and a separate Record<string,unknown> alias for the store; harmless now, but it is a duplicated hand-shadowing of the real types that must be kept in sync by hand/`build`).
recommendation: Either gitignore the generated d.ts (it's a build artifact) or stop emitting into src/ and emit to dist/, and rely on the TS source for types as the exports map already does. Avoid committing the generated declaration.
vnote: Verified. tsup.config.ts emits dts.only into outDir 'src'; src/index.d.ts is git-tracked (git ls-files confirms). package.json exports point types to ./src/index.ts (line 9), not the .d.ts; no tsconfig/build/script references index.d.ts. No .gitignore in the package. Confirmed drift risk is real: the d.ts declares both RunStateView$1 (a structured union) and a separate RunStateView=Record<string,unknown> alias, hand-shadowing. Notably pi-plugin's build script is just 'tsup --dts-only' and LACKS the 'rm -f src/index.d.ts &&' prefix that react-reconciler/server/time-travel/vcs use, making it more prone to staleness; publish.mjs:144 documents a prior incident of publishing a stale src/index.d.ts. P2 appropriate (harmless at runtime since exports resolve from .ts).

title: DevToolsClient.toWsUrl has a no-op pathname assignment
evidence: `url.pathname = url.pathname === "/" ? "/" : url.pathname;` is a tautology — both branches assign the existing value, so the line does nothing. It reads as if intended to normalize the path but has no effect.
recommendation: Remove the line, or implement the intended normalization (e.g. force the WS endpoint path) with a test.

title: DevToolsStore.retryNode and runSupportsRetry are effectively dead (no-op feature)
evidence: retryNode(_nodeId) only checks `if (!this.runSupportsRetry) return;` and otherwise does nothing — it never calls the client or mutates state. runSupportsRetry is initialized true and never reassigned anywhere in the package (grep shows only the declaration and the guard). No view or the extension calls retryNode. It is a public method on an exported class that does nothing.
recommendation: Either implement retry (wire to a gateway retry RPC) or remove retryNode/runSupportsRetry until the feature exists, to avoid an exported no-op on the public API.
vnote: Verified. retryNode(_nodeId) (DevToolsStore.ts:520-524) only does `if (!this.runSupportsRetry) return;` and otherwise nothing — never calls the client or mutates state. runSupportsRetry initialized true at line 204 and never reassigned (grep across packages/apps/.smithers/scripts/docs shows only the declaration, the guard, and the index.d.ts mirror). No view/extension calls retryNode (grep shows only the declaration and index.d.ts:311). Exported no-op on the public class. P2 appropriate.

title: DevToolsClient.signal/resume/getNodeOutput/getNodeDiff are unused within the package
evidence: Repo-wide grep shows getNodeOutput/getNodeDiff have no callers in pi-plugin (NodeInspector reads node.props directly via firstPresent over output/diff/logs keys instead of fetching from the gateway), and DevToolsClient.signal/resume have no callers in pi-plugin or elsewhere (only the unrelated api/resume.ts wrapper exists). DevToolsClient is exported, so these are reachable as public API, but they are untested and disconnected from any feature in this package — notably NodeInspector showing '(no output captured)' whenever props lack inline output, instead of calling getNodeOutput.
recommendation: Wire NodeInspector to client.getNodeOutput/getNodeDiff for the selected node (the studio app does exactly this) so the Output/Diff tabs actually populate; if signal/resume are intended for external consumers, add tests, otherwise drop them.
vnote: Verified. Repo-wide grep: getNodeOutput/getNodeDiff on DevToolsClient (411-425) have no callers in pi-plugin (the apps/cli getNodeOutputRoute/getNodeDiffRoute hits are a different server route, unrelated). DevToolsClient.signal (449-457) and resume (464-466) have no callers in pi-plugin (no '.signal('/'.resume(' matches in pi-plugin src). NodeInspector reads node.props via firstPresent over output/row/result/value (189) and diff/patches/changes (196), showing ' (no output captured)'/' (no diff captured)' instead of fetching via the client. They are reachable as public API but disconnected from any package feature and untested. Severity medium/P2 appropriate.

title: buildSmithersPiSystemPrompt activeRun branch and jsonSchemaToTypebox default branch are untested
evidence: buildSmithersPiSystemPrompt.test.ts only calls the 3-arg form; the 'Active Run Context' section (waiting nodes / recent errors slice(-3)) at lines 101-117 has no test. jsonSchemaToTypebox tests cover string/integer/boolean/array/object but not the default branch (a prop with no `type`/unknown type falls through to Type.String at extension.ts:200).
recommendation: Add a test passing an activeRun with waiting nodes and >3 errors (assert recent-3 truncation and waiting-node line), and a jsonSchemaToTypebox case for a typeless prop.

title: SmithersPiHttpClient.json (error path, headers, auth) is untested
evidence: SmithersPiHttpClient.test.ts only tests events(). json() throws SmithersError('PI_HTTP_ERROR', ...) on non-ok with status/body, conditionally sets Content-Type only when a body is present (buildHeaders withJson = opts.body !== undefined), and adds Bearer auth only when apiKey set — none of these branches are exercised.
recommendation: Add tests stubbing fetch to assert: 4xx/5xx → SmithersError with status/path metadata; Authorization header present iff apiKey; Content-Type set only on bodied requests; GET vs POST method selection.

title: getStatus/cancel/deny/resume/runWorkflow/streamEvents api wrappers untested individually (sub-scope of finding 3, but each has distinct branch logic worth separate coverage)
file: packages/pi-plugin/src/api/getStatus.ts, cancel.ts, deny.ts, resume.ts, runWorkflow.ts, streamEvents.ts
evidence: resume.ts likely sets a resume:true flag and runWorkflow.ts handles optional runId; deny.ts mirrors approve.ts with iteration ?? 0. None are imported in tests/ (only SmithersPiHttpClient.events is tested). This is the same coverage gap as finding 3 but reinforces the breadth: 9 distinct wrappers, 0 direct tests.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Deep subpath imports resolve types to index.d.ts and break for strict TS consumers (TS2305/TS2459)
evidence: package.json maps every subpath ("./*") types to "./src/index.d.ts". tsup builds index.d.ts only from symbols reachable via src/index.js (bundle/egress/execute/transport), so it omits sandboxPath and effect/* exports. Probing from a .ts consumer: importing { resolveSandboxPath, assertPathWithinRoot } from "@smithers-orchestrator/sandbox/sandboxPath" yields `error TS2305: ... has no exported member 'resolveSandboxPath'` (and 'assertPathWithinRoot'); importing from "@smithers-orchestrator/sandbox/effect/sandbox-entity" yields TS2305 for SandboxEntity/makeSandboxEntityId and TS2459 'SandboxEntityExecutor ... not exported', and BubblewrapSandboxExecutorLive TS2305 from /effect/socket-runner. Real consumers exist: packages/smithers/src/tools/utils.js imports resolveSandboxPath/assertPathWithinRoot from /sandboxPath; packages/engine/src/effect/workflow-bridge.js re-exports SandboxEntity/SandboxEntityExecutor/makeSandboxEntityId/makeSandboxTransportServiceEffect from /effect/sandbox-entity and BubblewrapSandboxExecutorLive/SandboxSocketRunner from /effect/socket-runner. These pass CI only because the importers are allowJs JSDoc .js files; any published .ts consumer breaks.
recommendation: Either generate per-subpath .d.ts (e.g. emit declarations alongside each .js and point exports "./sandboxPath", "./effect/*" at their own types), or re-export the deep-subpath symbols from src/index.js so they land in the bundled index.d.ts. Add a .ts smoke import in a test to lock the published type surface.
vnote: Reproduced empirically. tsup.config.ts emits only src/index.d.ts (entry: { index: src/index.js }); package.json maps every ./* subpath types to ./src/index.d.ts. A strict .ts probe importing from /sandboxPath, /effect/sandbox-entity, /effect/socket-runner produced exactly: TS2305 for resolveSandboxPath, assertPathWithinRoot, SandboxEntity, makeSandboxEntityId, makeSandboxTransportServiceEffect, BubblewrapSandboxExecutorLive, SandboxSocketRunner; TS2459 for SandboxEntityExecutor ('declares locally, but it is not exported'). Real consumers confirmed: packages/smithers/src/tools/utils.js (lines 7-8) and packages/engine/src/effect/workflow-bridge.js (lines 24-26). engine's own .js consumers typecheck fine because the exports 'import' condition resolves to .js source with JSDoc types; the break is types-only for .ts consumers honoring the 'types' condition. Important nuance the finding under-states: in-repo .ts consumers (e2e) are shielded by root tsconfig.json paths mapping @smithers-orchestrator/sandbox/* -> ./packages/sandbox/src/*.js (lines 194-197), so this never reddens CI; it only breaks EXTERNAL published .ts consumers that lack that paths override. The finding already notes CI passes due to allowJs importers, so its conclusion is right and P1 (published library type-surface defect) is defensible.

title: egress.js has zero direct unit tests; validation/error branches only hit incidentally
evidence: No test file imports ../src/egress.js (grep of packages/sandbox/tests returns nothing). egress is exercised only indirectly via execute.test.js (one happy-path provider egress case) and transport-runners.test.js (one bubblewrap merge case). Coverage report shows egress.js uncovered lines 16,38,54,60,63,66,79,85,99,109,178,189 — i.e. invalidEgressConfig throw, optionalString rejections (non-string / empty / >64KB / null-byte), optionalStringRecord rejections (non-object, invalid env var name, key bounds, value bounds), normalizeNoProxy non-array/non-string rejection, the caCertPem+caCertPath both-set rejection (lines 108-110), and the early-return redact branch. None of these negative cases is asserted anywhere.
recommendation: Add a dedicated egress.test.js asserting each invalidEgressConfig path (invalid object, bad env name, key/value length bounds, null bytes, noProxy non-string/array, caCertPem+caCertPath conflict) plus redactSandboxEgressConfig field-by-field and the no-egress passthrough.
vnote: No test file in packages/sandbox/tests imports egress.js or references normalizeSandboxEgressConfig/redactSandboxEgressConfig/optionalStringRecord/normalizeNoProxy. egress is exercised only indirectly: execute.test.js:350 (one happy-path provider egress case) and transport-runners.test.js:248 (one bubblewrap merge case) — both happy-path. Ran `bun test tests --coverage`: src/egress.js uncovered lines are EXACTLY 16,38,54,60,63,66,79,85,99,109,178,189 as claimed (invalidEgressConfig throw, optionalString rejections, optionalStringRecord rejections, normalizeNoProxy non-array rejection, caCertPem+caCertPath conflict at 108-110, redact early-returns). No negative case asserted anywhere.

title: Several executeSandbox error/result branches are untested
evidence: Coverage report flags execute.js uncovered lines 195,198-203 (provider returns invalid non-object result, and the provider-returns-bundlePath branch), 266-273 (diffBundle malformed / missing applier), 283 (redactSandboxConfig non-object), 349-351 (TASK_RUNTIME_UNAVAILABLE when no runtime db). Confirmed by grep: no test asserts 'returned an invalid result', 'diffBundle is malformed', 'no diff applier was provided', or 'requires a task runtime database'; no test returns { bundlePath } from a provider; no test sets config.command (so svc.execute / the codeplane execute-fail integration at execute.js:607-609 is never driven through executeSandbox).
recommendation: Add cases: provider returning a raw string/array (invalid result), provider returning { bundlePath } pointing at a prebuilt bundle, a manifest with a malformed diffBundle, a diffBundle with no applyDiffBundle option, missing runtime/parent db, and config.command driving svc.execute.

title: SandboxHttpRunner / SandboxSocketRunner are dead pass-through re-exports
file: packages/sandbox/src/effect/http-runner.js:108 (export const SandboxHttpRunner = HttpRunner); packages/sandbox/src/effect/socket-runner.js:93 (export const SandboxSocketRunner = SocketRunner)
evidence: Repo-wide grep shows the only references are the two definitions, the engine re-exports (workflow-bridge.js:25-26) and engine's index.d.ts:892/1685 declaration. No value-level consumer exists anywhere (apps, packages, e2e, .smithers). They are unmodified aliases of @effect/cluster's HttpRunner/SocketRunner adding nothing. They also leak inconsistently: SandboxHttpRunner is in engine's index.d.ts export list but SandboxSocketRunner is not.
recommendation: Remove both aliases and their engine re-exports (or, if intended as a stable public API, document and test them and fix the engine .d.ts to include SandboxSocketRunner).
vnote: http-runner.js:108 `export const SandboxHttpRunner = HttpRunner;` and socket-runner.js:93 `export const SandboxSocketRunner = SocketRunner;` are unmodified aliases of @effect/cluster namespaces (confirmed export * as HttpRunner/SocketRunner in @effect/cluster/dist/dts/index.d.ts). Repo-wide grep: only references are the two definitions plus re-export chains (engine/src/effect/http-runner.js does `export *` from sandbox, workflow-bridge.js:25-26 re-exports both, engine index.d.ts:892/1685 declares only SandboxHttpRunner). No value-level consumer (no .layer/.make/call) anywhere in packages, apps, e2e, .smithers, scripts. Inconsistency confirmed: SandboxHttpRunner is in engine index.d.ts export list (line 1685) but SandboxSocketRunner count in engine index.d.ts is 0.

title: process-runner.js exports normalizeSandboxEnv/Ports/Volumes but they are only used internally; their negative paths are untested
evidence: These three are `export`ed but no other module or test imports them (socket-runner imports only bubblewrapArgs/normalizeSandboxHandleControls/sandboxExecArgs/spawnSandboxCommand; http-runner imports dockerArgs/normalizeSandboxHandleControls/sandboxRunnerEnv/spawnSandboxCommand; tests import only bubblewrapArgs/dockerArgs/sandboxExecArgs/sandboxRunnerEnv). They are reachable only transitively via bubblewrapArgs/dockerArgs/normalizeSandboxHandleControls. Coverage report shows process-runner uncovered lines 57,71,75,78,81,100,114,118,134,148,152,171-175,187,191-201,204-207,238,244,247,267-269 — i.e. the invalid-env-name/value, bad-port, non-array ports/volumes, non-absolute volume path, resource-limit regex rejection, and workspace name/snapshotId/idleTimeoutSecs/persistence validation error branches, none of which has a direct negative test.
recommendation: Drop the `export` keyword on the three normalize* helpers (make them module-private) unless a public use is intended; add negative-case unit tests for the workspace/port/volume/env/resource-limit validators.
vnote: Repo-wide grep: normalizeSandboxEnv, normalizeSandboxPorts, normalizeSandboxVolumes are referenced ONLY within process-runner.js itself (no other module or test imports them). Tests import only bubblewrapArgs/dockerArgs/sandboxExecArgs/sandboxRunnerEnv (transport-runners.test.js:20). Coverage run confirms uncovered process-runner.js lines 57,71,75,78,81,100,114,118,134,148,152,171-175,187,191-194,197-201,204-207,238,244,247,267-269 (matches the claim; tool also showed 289-297,337). Negative tests that DO exist cover only allowNetwork=true, memoryLimit, volume-remapping, runtime-availability — not env name/value/bounds, port, volume-absolute, resource-limit regex, or workspace name/snapshotId/idleTimeoutSecs/persistence. Both the over-export and test-gap halves verified.

title: sandboxEgressEnv NO_PROXY array branch is unreachable dead code
evidence: Line 148: `env.NO_PROXY = Array.isArray(egress.noProxy) ? egress.noProxy.join(",") : egress.noProxy;`. But `egress` is produced by normalizeSandboxEgressConfig, whose normalizeNoProxy (lines 77-88) always returns a string (arrays are joined with ',') or undefined. So egress.noProxy is never an array at line 148 and the join branch can never execute.
recommendation: Simplify to `env.NO_PROXY = egress.noProxy;` (the value is already a normalized string), or drop the dead Array.isArray branch.
vnote: egress.js:148 `env.NO_PROXY = Array.isArray(egress.noProxy) ? egress.noProxy.join(',') : egress.noProxy;`. egress is produced by normalizeSandboxEgressConfig -> normalizeNoProxy (lines 77-88), which returns a string (arrays joined with ',' at line 87) or undefined — never an array. So at line 148 egress.noProxy is always a string and the Array.isArray true-branch is unreachable. Coverage shows line 148 covered (false-branch executes) but the true-branch is dead. Confirmed P2.

title: executeSandbox via the transport (non-provider) path never exercises svc.execute, and codeplane.execute is never integration-tested
evidence: execute.test.js always uses runtime 'codeplane' or providers; it drives create/ship/collect/cleanup but never sets config.command, so the `if (options.config?.command) ... svc.execute(...)` branch (execute.js:607-609) is never taken. http-runner coverage flags lines 97-100 (the codeplane execute SmithersError fail) uncovered. Only the docker/bubblewrap argv builders are unit-tested in isolation; the codeplane execute failure path is never asserted.
recommendation: Add an executeSandbox test passing config.command with a runtime whose svc.execute succeeds (docker fake bin) and one asserting codeplane execute fails with the documented message.

title: assertPathWithinRootEffect exported but only used internally
evidence: assertPathWithinRootEffect is `export`ed but repo-wide grep shows the only non-definition reference is its own wrapper assertPathWithinRoot (line 62). No external module or test imports the Effect variant; consumers (packages/smithers/src/tools/utils.js) use the Promise wrapper assertPathWithinRoot.
recommendation: Either un-export assertPathWithinRootEffect (keep it module-private) or, if it is meant to be the Effect-native entry point, surface it in the package's public type declarations and add a test.
vnote: sandboxPath.js:28 exports assertPathWithinRootEffect. Repo-wide grep: only non-definition reference is its own wrapper assertPathWithinRoot at line 62 (Effect.runPromise). No external module or test imports the Effect variant; all consumers (packages/smithers/src/tools/utils.js:7,53; tests sandbox-utils.test.js, sandbox-isolation.test.js) use the Promise wrapper assertPathWithinRoot. Coverage shows sandboxPath.js 100% lines but 88.89% funcs, consistent with the Effect entry only being reached via the wrapper. Over-export confirmed; P2 appropriate.

title: directorySize misnamed and dangling WalkResult typedef
evidence: directorySize() returns info.size for a file and 0 for anything that is not a regular file (including actual directories) — it is used only to measure README.md size, so the name implies directory measurement it never performs (test asserts directorySize(<dir>) === 0). Separately, bundle.js:19 JSDoc references @returns {Promise<WalkResult>} but no WalkResult typedef is declared anywhere in the package (grep finds only the reference), leaving a dangling type name.
recommendation: Rename directorySize to e.g. fileSizeBytes (or implement real recursive sizing if intended), and declare a WalkResult typedef or change the JSDoc to the inline shape {files:string[]; totalBytes:number}.

title: Request README writes confusing/empty runtime field on provider path
evidence: When writing the pending request README, line 420 sets `provider: selectedRuntime` (for a provider run selectedRuntime is the provider.id, e.g. 'remote-provider') and line 421 sets `runtime: provider ? options.runtime : selectedRuntime`. For provider runs options.runtime is typically undefined, so the request manifest records provider=<provider.id> and runtime=undefined; for transport runs it records provider=<runtime> and runtime=<runtime>. The 'provider'/'runtime' field semantics are inverted/overloaded between the two paths and undefined leaks into JSON.
recommendation: Use unambiguous fields (e.g. selectedRuntime + providerId) and omit undefined values so the request manifest is consistent across provider and transport paths.

title: Type-only subpath @smithers-orchestrator/sandbox/SandboxHandle also breaks external strict TS consumers (extends finding #1)
evidence: e2e/harness/stallSandbox.ts, stallSandbox.test.ts, and case02 import `import type { SandboxHandle } from "@smithers-orchestrator/sandbox/SandboxHandle"`. SandboxHandle.ts is a type-only file with NO corresponding .js, so the exports `import`/`default` conditions (./src/SandboxHandle.js) don't exist, and the `types` condition points at index.d.ts where the bare `SandboxHandle` type is NOT in the export list (grep of the index.d.ts `export {}` block returns 'NOT in export list'). In-repo these pass only because e2e extends the root tsconfig whose paths map @smithers-orchestrator/sandbox/* -> ./packages/sandbox/src/*.js (and TS falls back to the .ts via bundler resolution). An external published .ts consumer importing /SandboxHandle would get a resolution/TS2305 error. This is a concrete additional surface of the same root cause as finding #1 (single index.d.ts for all subpaths).
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: socket-runner.js darwin sandbox-exec missing-binary branch (line 68) untested
evidence: Coverage run shows socket-runner.js uncovered line 68 — the BubblewrapSandboxExecutorLive.execute darwin branch that fails with PROCESS_SPAWN_FAILED when sandbox-exec is absent. transport-runners.test.js tests the create-time darwin missing sandbox-exec path (line 396 'sandbox-exec') but not the execute-time missing-binary failure at line 68. Minor error-path gap consistent with the suite's pattern of testing create-time but not execute-time availability failures.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: context and groundTruth never reach scorers in real execution — faithfulnessScorer is silently broken on live/batch runs
evidence: ScorerInput (types.ts:18-31) declares `groundTruth` and `context`, and faithfulnessScorer/relevancyScorer build prompts from them (faithfulnessScorer.js:26-32 reads `context`). But ScorerContext (types.ts:108-117) has no `context`/`groundTruth` fields, runSingleScorerEffect only forwards `{ input, output, latencyMs, outputSchema }` to scorer.score() (run-scorers.js:65-71), and the engine's only call site (engine.js:4202-4211) supplies just runId/nodeId/iteration/attempt/input/output/latencyMs/outputSchema. So during any real run faithfulnessScorer always sees `context: undefined` and emits 'No context provided', regardless of retrieved docs. The scorers-builtins-llm.test.js 'includes context in prompt' test passes only because it calls scorer.score() directly with a hand-built input — the production path never populates it.
recommendation: Add `context?` and `groundTruth?` to ScorerContext, forward them in runSingleScorerEffect's scorer.score() call, and plumb them from the engine task descriptor. Add an e2e test through runScorersBatch (not direct scorer.score()) asserting context reaches faithfulnessScorer.
vnote: Verified end-to-end. ScorerContext (packages/scorers/src/types.ts:108-117) has no context/groundTruth fields; runSingleScorerEffect forwards only {input,output,latencyMs,outputSchema} to scorer.score() (run-scorers.js:65-71); the engine's sole call site (packages/engine/src/engine.js:4202-4211) passes only runId/nodeId/iteration/attempt/input/output/latencyMs/outputSchema. faithfulnessScorer's promptTemplate reads `context` (faithfulnessScorer.js:26-32) and emits 'No context provided' when undefined, which is the only value it ever sees in real runs. The 'includes context in prompt' test (scorers-builtins-llm.test.js:72-87) passes only via direct scorer.score() with a hand-built input; no test drives context through runScorersBatch/runScorersAsync. There is NO plumbing anywhere in engine/graph to attach context/groundTruth to a Task descriptor, even though graph/types.ts:72 declares groundTruth on ScorerInput and skills/eval-writer/SKILL.md:81 documents faithfulnessScorer() as 'grounded in source'. One evidence overstatement: relevancyScorer does NOT read context (its promptTemplate is `({ input, output })`, relevancyScorer.js:26), so only faithfulnessScorer is actually degraded — relevancy/toxicity work end-to-end since they only need input/output the engine provides. Core finding stands; P1 appropriate (documented 'grounded' scorer silently ignores its source).

title: Dead file: src/react-types.ts is never imported anywhere
evidence: The file's sole content is `export type { ScorersMap } from "@smithers-orchestrator/graph/types";`. grep across the whole repo (packages, apps, .smithers, examples, scripts) for 'react-types' returns zero hits outside the file itself; it is not referenced by index.js, index.d.ts, package.json exports, or any consumer.
recommendation: Delete src/react-types.ts. If a React-facing ScorersMap re-export is intended, wire it into the public surface; otherwise it is pure dead weight.
vnote: Grepped the entire repo (packages, apps, .smithers, workflows, skills, e2e, docs, config) for 'react-types' — zero hits outside the file itself. File's sole content re-exports ScorersMap from @smithers-orchestrator/graph/types. Not referenced by index.js or the generated index.d.ts (which re-exports ScorersMap from types.ts as ScorersMap$2, not from react-types). package.json wildcard export './*' maps to './src/*.js', and react-types is a .ts with no .js counterpart, so it isn't even reachable via the export map. tsconfig include 'src/**/*' typechecks it but nothing consumes it. Genuinely orphaned dead code; P2 correct.

title: aggregateScores builds SQL via string interpolation with a one-character escaper
evidence: Filters are concatenated as `run_id = '${escapeSql(opts.runId)}'` etc., and escapeSql only does `value.replace(/'/g, "''")` (line 107). Every other SmithersDb method uses parameterized drizzle queries; this is the only hand-rolled SQL. adapter.rawQuery (db/adapter.js:596) validates read-only (blocks drop/insert/update via RAW_QUERY_FORBIDDEN_KEYWORDS), which caps the blast radius, but a crafted runId/nodeId/scorerId (e.g. UNION SELECT or `' OR '1'='1`) can still alter SELECT semantics / read across rows since those are SELECT-only keywords. The test 'escapes single quotes' (scorers-aggregate.test.js:83-94) only proves the quote-doubling, not injection safety.
recommendation: Pass filters as bound parameters (or use the drizzle query builder with eq() against smithersScorers) instead of string interpolation; drop the bespoke escapeSql.
vnote: The security/injection core claim is REFUTED. escapeSql doubles single quotes (`'`->`''`), which is the complete, correct way to escape a string literal in SQLite (no backslash escaping by default) and in Postgres (standard_conforming_strings default). I empirically tested with bun:sqlite: both `' OR '1'='1` and `x' UNION SELECT ...` are neutralized — they become literal strings inside the quoted value and return ZERO rows. The claim that a crafted runId/nodeId/scorerId 'can still alter SELECT semantics / read across rows' is false; quote-doubling prevents breakout entirely. Additionally there is NO product caller of aggregateScores with attacker-controlled input (only callers: index re-export and packages/smithers re-export; the CLI 'scores' command uses adapter.listScorerResults). The only legitimate residual is a clean-code/consistency nit: this is the only hand-rolled string-interpolated SQL (aggregate.js:19-52,106-108) in a codebase that otherwise uses parameterized drizzle queries; bound params would be cleaner. Re-categorized from 'bug' to clean-code, severity held at P2.

title: Redundant two-layer barrel shims (create-scorer.js, builtins.js) duplicate the real implementation files
evidence: create-scorer.js only re-exports createScorer from createScorer.js and llmJudge from llmJudge.js; builtins.js only re-exports the five built-in scorers from their per-file modules. index.js imports from these shims (index.js:18,20), and tests split inconsistently between them and src/index.js / per-file imports. There are two near-identical naming conventions (create-scorer.js vs createScorer.js) for the same symbol, which is confusing.
recommendation: Collapse the indirection: either export createScorer/llmJudge directly from index.js (drop create-scorer.js) and the built-ins directly (drop builtins.js), or make these the canonical implementation files and remove the camelCase duplicates. Pick one naming convention.

title: Heavy test-file duplication inflates the suite without adding coverage
file: packages/scorers/tests/builtins.test.js vs scorers-builtins.test.js; run-scorers.test.js vs scorers-run.test.js; aggregate.test.js vs scorers-aggregate.test.js; create-scorer.test.js vs scorers-create.test.js
evidence: builtins.test.js and scorers-builtins.test.js both assert latencyScorer/schemaAdherenceScorer id/name, target/max/midpoint interpolation, and skip behavior with near-identical cases. run-scorers.test.js and scorers-run.test.js both test empty map, sampling none/all, error-returns-null, and input passthrough. aggregate.test.js (real DB) and scorers-aggregate.test.js (mock adapter) cover the same filters and mapping. create-scorer.test.js and scorers-create.test.js both re-test createScorer shape + llmJudge clamping/parse-fallback. 14 test files for 8 implementation modules is largely redundancy, not breadth.
recommendation: Consolidate each pair into one file (prefer the real-backend variant for aggregate/run), keeping only cases that add unique branch coverage. This makes the true coverage gaps visible.

title: types.test.js asserts plain JS object literals, providing no real type or behavior coverage
evidence: Every test constructs an untyped object literal and asserts a field equals what it was just set to (e.g. `const minimal = { score: 0.5 }; expect(minimal.score).toBe(0.5)`). It imports nothing from src, so it exercises no source code and validates no TypeScript types (the file is .js with no type annotations).
recommendation: Delete types.test.js. If type-level guarantees matter, add a tsd/expect-type compile-time test or rely on `tsc --noEmit`; runtime literal assertions add maintenance with zero signal.

title: parseJudgeJson treats valid top-level non-object JSON (bare number/array) as a parse failure
evidence: If a judge returns a bare number like `0.8` or an array, JSON.parse(trimmed) succeeds and returns it, but llmJudge's guard is `if (parsed && typeof parsed === 'object')`. A number fails that check and falls to the 'Failed to parse judge response as JSON' branch (score 0), even though valid JSON was returned and a reasonable score could be derived. An array passes the object check but yields Number(arr.score)=NaN→0. Neither case is tested (tests only cover well-formed objects, prose-wrapped objects, and unparseable text).
recommendation: Decide intended behavior for non-object JSON and add tests; at minimum document that only `{score,...}` objects are honored. Consider accepting a bare numeric response as the score.

title: Untested error/branch paths in run-scorers and aggregate
evidence: safeJsonStringify's `catch { return String(value) }` (run-scorers.js:145-147) is exercised for a circular object (returns '[object Object]') but the non-circular-yet-unstringifiable path (e.g. BigInt → throws TypeError) is not. runScorersAsync's DB-persistence branch (adapter !== null) is never tested — all runScorersAsync tests pass `null` adapter (scorers-run/run-scorers tests). aggregate.js computeStddev's even-length and single-element branches and computeMedian's even-length average ((sorted[mid-1]+sorted[mid])/2) are only hit via the mock-adapter test, not the real-DB e2e (aggregate.test.js p50 test uses 5 = odd values).
recommendation: Add: a runScorersAsync test with a real/in-memory adapter asserting a persisted row; a safeJsonStringify test with a BigInt; and a real-DB aggregate test with an even number of scores to cover the median-average and stddev branches.

title: smithersScorers is typed as `any` in the hand-maintained index.d.ts, erasing column types for consumers
evidence: `declare const smithersScorers: any;` — the re-exported drizzle table loses all column typing in the published .d.ts, so downstream users importing smithersScorers (e.g. for typed drizzle queries) get `any`. The runtime re-export (schema.js) is correct; only the type declaration is degraded. This is a tsup dts-resolution shortcut (`dts: { resolve: false }` in tsup.config.ts) rather than a typed re-export.
recommendation: Re-export the table type properly, e.g. `export { smithersScorers } from '@smithers-orchestrator/db/internal-schema'` in the d.ts, or enable dts resolution for that symbol so the drizzle table type is preserved.

title: package.json declares ./metrics and ./schema subpath exports that no consumer uses; types map all subpaths to index.d.ts
evidence: Exports define `./metrics`→metrics.js and `./schema`→schema.js plus a wildcard `./*`, but grep for 'scorers/metrics' and 'scorers/schema' across the repo (excluding the package) returns no importers — consumers only use the main entry and the engine uses 'scorers/run-scorers' (covered by the wildcard, not an explicit entry). Additionally every subpath's `types` field points at the single ./src/index.d.ts, so deep imports like scorers/run-scorers resolve to the full barrel's types rather than the specific module's.
recommendation: Either drop the unused explicit ./metrics and ./schema entries (the ./* wildcard already covers them) or wire per-subpath .d.ts files so deep-import types are accurate; align declared subpaths with actual consumers (run-scorers is the real deep import).

title: Public API (aggregateScores, runScorersBatch, relevancy/toxicity/faithfulness scorers) has no in-repo product consumer
evidence: Across packages/apps/.smithers (out-of-scope studio-2 excluded), only llmJudge (via .smithers rctfCompletenessScorer.ts), runScorersAsync (engine.js:36), and the type ScorersMap (ExtractPrompt.tsx) are actually consumed. aggregateScores, runScorersBatch, relevancyScorer, toxicityScorer, faithfulnessScorer, schemaAdherenceScorer, latencyScorer are only re-exported through packages/smithers and exercised by tests — no product code calls them. The CLI `scores` command (apps/cli/src/index.js:5317) uses adapter.listScorerResults, not aggregateScores.
recommendation: Legitimate for a published library to expose these, but consider either (a) demonstrating them in the .smithers init pack / examples so they have a real exercised path, or (b) marking the genuinely speculative ones. At minimum, add an e2e that drives the built-in LLM scorers through the engine's runScorersAsync path rather than only direct unit calls.
vnote: The 'dead-code' framing is wrong: this is the public API of a published library (@smithers-orchestrator/scorers), re-exported through @smithers-orchestrator/smithers and documented in skills/eval-writer and llms-full.txt — a library exposing unused-in-repo API is not dead code. Also partly inaccurate: runScorersBatch and aggregateScores ARE exercised through a REAL DB path in packages/scorers/tests/e2e.test.js (real in-memory SQLite via SmithersDb: attach scorers -> runScorersBatch -> persist -> listScorerResults -> aggregateScores). Confirmed the .smithers demo.tsx reference is only a printCode display string (not executable) and roadmapbench.tsx uses createScorer, not the built-ins. The genuine residual is a test-gap: the LLM built-in scorers (relevancy/toxicity/faithfulness) and the engine's runScorersAsync path have no real exercised product or engine-driven test — they're only covered by direct scorer.score() unit calls. Re-categorized from dead-code to test-gap, P2.

title: skills/eval-writer/SKILL.md documents scorer/llmJudge/sampling APIs that don't match the real signatures
evidence: The skill shows `faithfulnessScorer()` and `relevancyScorer()` called with NO arguments, but the real signatures require a judge agent: `faithfulnessScorer(judge)` (faithfulnessScorer.js:12), `relevancyScorer(judge)` (relevancyScorer.js:12). It shows `llmJudge({ model: claude, prompt: "Rate completeness 0-1" })`, but the real LlmJudgeConfig requires id/name/description/judge/instructions/promptTemplate (llmJudge.js:90-91, LlmJudgeConfig.ts). It shows `sampling: { kind: "ratio", ratio: 0.1 }`, but the real SamplingConfig is `{ type: "ratio", rate: number }` (types.ts:53-56; run-scorers.js shouldRun reads sampling.type and sampling.rate). Copy-pasting any of these examples fails at runtime/typecheck. The .smithers demo.tsx printCode snippet (demo.tsx:956-971) has the same `faithfulness({ source: "context" })` / `relevancy({ question })` / `llmJudge({ model, rubric })` fabrications.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Scorer execution never persists context/groundTruth even when a scorer does receive them, and inputJson is the only correlation column
evidence: Even if context/groundTruth were plumbed (see finding 1), runSingleScorerEffect's persisted row (run-scorers.js:112-129) only stores inputJson/outputJson — there is no contextJson/groundTruthJson column in ScoreRow (types.ts:72-89) or the schema (schema.js). So faithfulness/relevancy scoring against retrieved context could never be audited or reproduced from stored results. Compounds finding 1: the whole context/groundTruth dimension is declared in the type surface (types.ts:24-26, graph/types.ts:72-73) but absent from execution, persistence, and the engine descriptor.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: outputs.ts is entirely dead — exported types have zero consumers; server and db reimplement them
evidence: Exhaustive grep across packages/apps/.smithers/docs (excluding the retired smithers-studio-2 POC and protocol's own files) finds NO import of `protocol/outputs` and no name-import of OutputSchemaFieldType/OutputSchemaDescriptor/NodeOutputResponse from the protocol root. Instead packages/server/src/gatewayRoutes/NodeOutputResponse.ts defines its own identical NodeOutputResponse, getNodeOutput.js imports buildOutputSchemaDescriptor from @smithers-orchestrator/db/output-schema-descriptor, and the descriptor type lives in packages/db. docs/reference/types.mdx documents none of these (grep count 0). The only root-import consumers (pi-plugin) import only DevTools* types.
recommendation: Either delete outputs.ts and remove its re-exports from index.ts, or make server/db actually import these types from @smithers-orchestrator/protocol so there is a single source of truth for the NodeOutput RPC contract. Today it is a published-but-unused parallel definition.
vnote: Verified. Grep across packages/apps/.smithers/docs (excluding protocol's own src and the studio POC) finds NO import of OutputSchemaFieldType/OutputSchemaDescriptor/NodeOutputResponse from the protocol package, and no import of `protocol/outputs`. The only protocol-root consumers are pi-plugin's 5 files, all importing DevTools* types only. The NodeOutput contract is independently reimplemented in three places: packages/server/src/gatewayRoutes/NodeOutputResponse.ts (own inline copy), packages/db/src/output-schema-descriptor.js (own JSDoc OutputSchemaDescriptor + buildOutputSchemaDescriptor consumed by getNodeOutput.js), and packages/gateway/src/rpc/index.ts:632 (a bare "NodeOutputResponse." schema label). docs/reference/types.mdx and errors.mdx contain zero references to these types (grep empty). The published index.d.ts (lines 74-90) does inline these types, but nothing consumes them, so the published-but-unused parallel definition claim holds. P2 severity is appropriate.

title: ProtocolError type is exported but never consumed anywhere
file: packages/protocol/src/errors.ts:51 (and duplicate at src/errors/ProtocolError.ts:6, re-exported via index.ts:22)
evidence: grep -rln 'ProtocolError' across all in-scope packages/apps (excluding protocol itself and the retired studio POC) returns nothing. docs/reference/errors.mdx contains no ProtocolError reference (grep empty). It is exported from the package root types and from the /errors subpath but no code uses the shape.
recommendation: Remove ProtocolError (and the standalone errors/ProtocolError.ts) unless a consumer is intended; if it is meant to be the canonical error envelope, wire the server RPC error responses and the CLI errorMessage layer to actually type against it.
vnote: Verified. `grep -rn ProtocolError` across the whole repo (minus node_modules and studio POC) returns ONLY protocol's own files: errors.ts:51 (definition), index.ts:22 (re-export), errors/ProtocolError.ts:6 (duplicate definition), index.d.ts:100/106 (generated copy), and errors/index.js:6 (JSDoc typedef). No code in server, gateway, cli, or anywhere else types against the ProtocolError shape; the RPC error responses and the CLI errorMessage layer do not use it. docs/reference/errors.mdx has no ProtocolError reference. P2 is appropriate.

title: DEVTOOLS_PROTOCOL_VERSION is exported but never read; both producer and consumers hardcode version: 1
evidence: grep for DEVTOOLS_PROTOCOL_VERSION usage outside protocol finds only a feature-catalog string in .smithers/specs/features.ts:1349 and the package's own test. The server (streamDevTools.js, getDevToolsSnapshot.js) emits `version: 1` as a literal in every event, and pi-plugin DevToolsClient.ts/DevToolsStore.ts type and construct events with literal `version: 1` — never comparing against the shared constant. There is no version negotiation or validation anywhere.
recommendation: Either consume the constant (server stamps events with DEVTOOLS_PROTOCOL_VERSION; client validates incoming event.version against it and surfaces a typed mismatch error) so it earns its place, or drop it. As shipped it provides no protection.
vnote: Verified. Usage outside protocol is only .smithers/specs/features.ts:1349 (a feature-catalog string identifier, not code) plus the package's own self-test. Producers emit literal `version: 1` (getDevToolsSnapshot.js:337,403; streamDevTools.js:170,185,188,338,417,446,474,490) and the pi-plugin consumer types/constructs literal `version: 1` in DevToolsClient.ts (lines 36-38 type, 118/121/131/134/137 construct) — never importing or comparing the shared constant. No version negotiation or validation exists. The constant earns nothing as shipped. P2 is appropriate.

title: Quadruple-defined error contract with no drift guard between the runtime (.js) and type (.ts) copies
file: packages/protocol/src/errors/index.js (runtime arrays) vs src/errors.ts (second runtime+type copy) vs src/errors/*.ts (type unions) vs src/index.d.ts (generated)
evidence: The same four code lists exist in errors/index.js (the PUBLISHED runtime, e.g. NODE_OUTPUT_ERROR_CODES with 10 entries) and again in errors.ts as `as const` arrays + matching string-union types, and yet again as standalone unions in errors/DevToolsErrorCode.ts / NodeOutputErrorCode.ts / NodeDiffErrorCode.ts / JumpToFrameErrorCode.ts, and finally inlined into the generated index.d.ts. I confirmed they currently match (7/10/10/11) via a textual extraction, but the test only imports errors/index.js — nothing asserts errors.ts (which feeds the consumer-facing index.d.ts types) equals the runtime. Because in-monorepo type imports of `protocol/errors` resolve to errors.ts (path map `src/*.ts` wins over `src/*/index.js`) while runtime resolves to errors/index.js, a future edit to one and not the other silently splits the runtime values from the published types.
recommendation: Collapse to one source of truth: define the arrays once in errors/index.js with JSDoc `@type {const}` and derive the unions via typedef (as the JSDoc already attempts), delete errors.ts, OR add a parity test that imports both errors.ts and errors/index.js and asserts deep-equality for all four arrays so drift fails CI.
vnote: Verified all four representations exist and the dual-resolution split is real. Runtime arrays in errors/index.js (DEVTOOLS=7, NODE_OUTPUT=10, NODE_DIFF=10, JUMP_TO_FRAME=11); `as const` arrays + matching union types in errors.ts; standalone unions in errors/{DevTools,NodeOutput,NodeDiff,JumpToFrame}ErrorCode.ts; inlined again in the generated index.d.ts (lines 92-99). They currently match. Critically: in-monorepo TYPE imports of `@smithers-orchestrator/protocol/errors` (used by server/src/gatewayRoutes/NodeOutputRouteError.js:1 and server/src/index.d.ts:1207) resolve via the path map's `src/*.ts` first candidate to errors.ts, while RUNTIME imports use `protocol/errors/index.js` (cli errorMessage-coverage.test.js, protocol-contracts.test.js). I confirmed NO test imports errors.ts; both tests import only errors/index.js, so nothing asserts errors.ts == errors/index.js. A future edit to one copy silently splits runtime values from the consumer-facing/published types with CI staying green. The tsup build (entry=index.ts, which re-exports errors.ts) confirms errors.ts feeds the published index.d.ts. P1 is justified.

title: errors/*.ts type files are dead — shadowed by errors.ts in path resolution, never a consumer target
evidence: tsconfig path map `@smithers-orchestrator/protocol/*` = [src/*.ts, src/*.js, src/*/index.js]; for specifier `protocol/errors`, src/errors.ts exists and wins, so the errors/ directory's .ts files are never the resolution target. grep confirms no import of `protocol/errors/<X>` anywhere. These files are referenced only by sibling errors/ProtocolError.ts and by JSDoc @typedef lines in errors/index.js — none of which any consumer resolves (consumers get errors.ts for types, errors/index.js for runtime).
recommendation: Remove the errors/*.ts type-only files (and the JSDoc typedefs in errors/index.js that point at them), or restructure so there is one canonical type module. They are pure duplication that can drift from errors.ts.
vnote: Verified. tsconfig path map `@smithers-orchestrator/protocol/*` = [src/*.ts, src/*.js, src/*/index.js]; for `protocol/errors`, src/errors.ts is the first existing candidate and wins, so errors/DevToolsErrorCode.ts etc. are never the resolution target. Grep confirms no import of `protocol/errors/<X>` anywhere (the only `protocol/errors/` import is errors/index.js, the runtime). The errors/*.ts files are referenced only by sibling errors/ProtocolError.ts (imports the four code-type files) and by JSDoc @typedef lines in errors/index.js. Neither is a consumer target: tsup's only entry is index.ts (which imports errors.ts, NOT errors/index.js or errors/*.ts), and package.json maps `./errors` types to ./src/index.d.ts, so errors/*.ts is never compiled into any emitted d.ts. They are pure duplication that can drift. P2 is appropriate.

title: Loose test assertions: 3 of 4 error arrays are only spot-checked with toContain, not exact-asserted
evidence: DEVTOOLS_ERROR_CODES is pinned with toEqual([...]) (line 20), but NODE_OUTPUT_ERROR_CODES, NODE_DIFF_ERROR_CODES, and JUMP_TO_FRAME_ERROR_CODES are only asserted via toContain on a subset (e.g. NODE_OUTPUT only checks MalformedOutputRow/PayloadTooLarge/SchemaConversionError of its 10 codes). Accidentally deleting, renaming, or reordering an unchecked code (e.g. IterationNotFound, NodeHasNoOutput, RewindFailed, UnsupportedSandbox) would not fail this suite. The wire contract is exactly the ordered list, so order/membership should be pinned.
recommendation: Replace the toContain spot-checks with toEqual([...]) snapshots of the full ordered arrays for all four lists (matching the DEVTOOLS_ERROR_CODES style), so any change to the published contract is an intentional, reviewed test diff.
vnote: The factual core is confirmed: protocol-contracts.test.js pins DEVTOOLS_ERROR_CODES with toEqual([...]) (lines 20-28) but checks NODE_OUTPUT/NODE_DIFF/JUMP_TO_FRAME only via toContain on subsets (lines 31-48), so deleting/renaming/reordering an unchecked code (e.g. IterationNotFound, NodeHasNoOutput, RewindFailed, UnsupportedSandbox) would not fail that suite. However, the severity should be P2, not P1: there IS a real downstream guard the finding omits — apps/cli/tests/errorMessage-coverage.test.js imports all four arrays from protocol/errors/index.js and asserts every code maps to a CLI message/hint/exit-code, so a code RENAME or DELETION (the contract change that matters most) breaks that cross-package test. What remains genuinely unguarded is reordering/membership within the published wire arrays, which is lower-impact than the finding implies. Pinning all four with toEqual is still the right fix; downgrading to P2.

title: tsconfig path map for the protocol root points at a non-existent file (src/index.js)
evidence: Root tsconfig maps `@smithers-orchestrator/protocol` -> ['./packages/protocol/src/index.js'], but the package has only src/index.ts and src/index.d.ts — there is no index.js (build is `tsup --dts-only`, which never emits JS). The comparable index.ts-based package, pi-plugin, correctly maps to './packages/pi-plugin/src/index.ts' (tsconfig.json:159-161). Typecheck currently passes only because TS/bundler falls back (js->ts rewrite or node resolution to index.d.ts).
recommendation: Map the root to src/index.ts (or src/index.d.ts) for consistency with pi-plugin and to avoid relying on resolver fallback for the package's primary entry.
vnote: Verified. packages/protocol/src/index.js does NOT exist (ls fails; build is tsup --dts-only which emits no JS). Root tsconfig.json:162-164 maps `@smithers-orchestrator/protocol` -> ['./packages/protocol/src/index.js']; examples/tsconfig.json:117-119 is identical. The comparable index.ts-based package pi-plugin correctly maps to './packages/pi-plugin/src/index.ts' (tsconfig.json:156-158). I ran `tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit` in pi-plugin (which imports the protocol root) and it passed (exit 0), confirming typecheck only works via the resolver's js->ts/d.ts fallback rather than a real entry hit. The inconsistency is real; P2 (cosmetic/fragility, not a current break) is appropriate.

title: Package root has no runtime entry, so the constants re-exported from index.ts are unreachable at runtime
evidence: The '.' export only declares `types`; there is no import/default.
