Confirmed at runtime: `await import('@smithers-orchestrator/protocol')` throws 'Cannot find module' while the /errors and /devtools subpaths resolve. index.ts nonetheless `export`s runtime values (DEVTOOLS_PROTOCOL_VERSION, DEVTOOLS_ERROR_CODES and the other three arrays) which can never be imported from the root — every runtime consumer must use the verbose `@smithers-orchestrator/protocol/errors/index.js` (see apps/cli/tests/errorMessage-coverage.test.js:7).
recommendation: Either add a runtime '.' export (point import/default at a real index.js barrel) so `import { DEVTOOLS_ERROR_CODES } from '@smithers-orchestrator/protocol'` works, or drop the runtime exports from index.ts and keep it type-only to make the types-only nature of the root explicit. The current half-state is misleading.

title: Inconsistent subpath module layout between /devtools and /errors
file: packages/protocol/src/devtools.js + devtools/*.ts vs src/errors.ts + errors/index.js + errors/*.ts
evidence: devtools uses the repo convention: a single devtools.js (JSDoc typedefs -> devtools/*.ts) with no .ts umbrella, so /devtools resolves to devtools.js and the devtools/*.ts files are load-bearing for its types. errors uses BOTH an errors.ts umbrella file AND an errors/ directory (index.js + *.ts), which collide: /errors resolves to errors.ts for types and errors/index.js for runtime, leaving errors/*.ts orphaned. Two sibling contract groups, two different layouts.
recommendation: Pick one pattern for both. Mirror the devtools layout for errors (drop errors.ts, keep errors/index.js + errors/*.ts) or mirror the errors umbrella pattern for devtools — but not a hybrid that creates shadowed files.

title: DevToolsNodeType union is duplicated inline in index.d.ts instead of being a single declared type
evidence: index.d.ts line 1 inlines the full 16-member node-type union literally, duplicating the canonical union in devtools/DevToolsNodeType.ts. index.d.ts is generated by tsup so this is expected output, but it means the 16-member contract (workflow|task|...|fragment|unknown) lives in two textual places; any hand-edit to index.d.ts (which is committed, not gitignored) would diverge from source. There is no test asserting the generated index.d.ts is regeneration-stable.
recommendation: Add a CI check that `tsup --dts-only` produces no diff against the committed index.d.ts (a check-build gate), so the committed declaration can never drift from src/. I verified locally that a rebuild is currently in sync, but nothing guards it.

title: Duplicate ProtocolError definition: errors.ts and errors/ProtocolError.ts both define the identical shape independently
evidence: ProtocolError is defined twice with the same shape: errors.ts:51 inlines `code: DevToolsErrorCode | NodeOutputErrorCode | NodeDiffErrorCode | JumpToFrameErrorCode | string; message: string; hint?: string`, and errors/ProtocolError.ts:6 re-declares the same shape importing the four code types from sibling files. Neither is consumed by any non-protocol code (see ProtocolError finding). This is a second, fully redundant copy beyond the errors/*.ts code-list duplication already flagged; it can drift from errors.ts with no test catching it.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Public export `accountToProviderEnv` is dead code with false JSDoc; logic duplicated in 3 places
evidence: packages/accounts/src/accountToProviderEnv.js is re-exported from index.js:11 but a whole-repo grep (packages/apps/.smithers/scripts) finds ZERO code importers — only its own test, comments, and docs tables. Its JSDoc claims 'Used by the agent classes\' buildCommand and by smithers agent test', yet the agents set env directly (packages/agents/src/CodexAgent.js:579 `accountEnv.CODEX_HOME=...`, GeminiAgent.js:271-273, AntigravityAgent.js:292-294, KimiAgent.js:371/379) and runAgentAdd.js:43 keeps its own PROVIDER_ENV_VAR map. packages/usage/src/getAccountUsage.js:13 explicitly says its switch 'mirrors accountToProviderEnv ... so the two stay aligned' instead of importing it. So the provider->env mapping is reimplemented in accountToProviderEnv.js, getAccountUsage.js, and runAgentAdd.js/agentAddWizard.js.
recommendation: Either delete accountToProviderEnv (and trim its docs-table mention), or make it the single source of truth and have the agent adapters + usage adapters import it. At minimum fix the false JSDoc claiming it is used by buildCommand/`agent test`.
vnote: Verified. Whole-repo grep (excluding node_modules/dist) shows accountToProviderEnv has ZERO production importers: only its own definition (accountToProviderEnv.js:11), the index re-export (index.js:11), the type decl (index.d.ts:156/158), its own test (accounts.test.js), and docs/comments that merely *mention* it (usage README/index.d.ts/getAccountUsage.js comment, .smithers/specs/usage-and-limits.md). packages/agents/src/ has NO import of @smithers-orchestrator/accounts and no reference to accountToProviderEnv; each agent sets env directly from this.opts (ClaudeCodeAgent.js:469-472, CodexAgent.js:577-588, GeminiAgent.js:269-278, AntigravityAgent.js:290-299, KimiAgent.js:350-379). The JSDoc claim 'Used by the agent classes buildCommand and by smithers agent test' is verifiably FALSE: agents' buildCommand methods exist but don't call it, and `smithers agents test` (apps/cli/src/index.js:2460/2473) uses pingAccount, which has its OWN SUBSCRIPTION_DIR_ENV_VAR map (runAgentAdd.js:42-51,150). getAccountUsage.js:13 explicitly says its switch 'mirrors accountToProviderEnv ... so the two stay aligned' rather than importing it. So the provider->env mapping is reimplemented in accountToProviderEnv.js, getAccountUsage.js (via probeFor), and runAgentAdd.js (SUBSCRIPTION_DIR_ENV_VAR). Dead public export + false JSDoc + triplicated logic all confirmed. P1 stands.

title: Parser does not enforce the documented "configDir XOR apiKey, never both" invariant — secrets can leak into subscription records
evidence: Account.ts:7-8 and index.d.ts:9-12 state 'Either configDir ... or apiKey ... is set, never both.' But parseAccountsFile.js:79-92 only checks the REQUIRED field for the provider class and copies whatever is present: parseAccountsFile('{version:1,accounts:[{label:"x",provider:"claude-code",configDir:"/c",apiKey:"sk-leak"}]}') returns {label:"x",provider:"claude-code",configDir:"/c",apiKey:"sk-leak"} (verified by execution) — a plaintext API key persisted on a subscription account that is never meant to carry one. Symmetrically an openai-api account with a stray configDir is kept. addAccount.js:42-43 has the same gap (it copies configDir AND apiKey if both are passed).
recommendation: In parseAccountsFile and addAccount, reject (or strip) the non-applicable field: a subscription provider must not carry apiKey, an API provider must not carry configDir. Add tests for both cross-contamination cases.
vnote: Verified by direct execution. Account.ts:3-7 and index.d.ts:8-12 both state 'Either configDir ... or apiKey ... is set, never both. The CLI enforces this at registration time.' Running parseAccountsFile on {provider:'claude-code',configDir:'/c',apiKey:'sk-leak'} returns the account WITH apiKey:'sk-leak' retained (parseAccountsFile.js:85-92 only validates the required field then copies both). Symmetrically an openai-api account keeps a stray configDir. addAccount.js:42-43 has the same gap: executing addAccount({provider:'claude-code',configDir:'/c',apiKey:'sk-leak'}) persisted BOTH fields (confirmed via run), so 'The CLI enforces this at registration time' is false. No existing test covers the both-fields case (accounts.test.js only tests single-field and missing-required-field). The downstream impact is worse than stated: agent-detection.js renderAccountProviderLine (lines 666-667) unconditionally emits BOTH configDir and apiKey into the generated .smithers/agents.ts, and ClaudeCodeAgent.buildCommand (ClaudeCodeAgent.js:469-472) then sets BOTH CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in the spawned agent env — so a stray apiKey on a subscription account is not merely persisted but actually injected into the child process. P1 confirmed (arguably understated).

title: ACCOUNT_* error codes are not registered in the errors-package code registry that every other domain code uses
evidence: accounts throws ACCOUNT_INVALID, ACCOUNTS_FILE_INVALID, ACCOUNT_DUPLICATE_LABEL, ACCOUNT_NOT_FOUND (grep of src). None appear in packages/errors/src/smithersErrorDefinitions.js (92 codes, each with category + `when` + optional `details`; e.g. INVALID_INPUT, RUN_NOT_FOUND, TASK_TIMEOUT). Consequence: isKnownSmithersErrorCode() returns false for all four (isKnownSmithersErrorCode.js uses hasOwnProperty on the definitions map) and they get only the generic ERROR_REFERENCE_URL. SmithersErrorCode.ts:3 (`KnownSmithersErrorCode | (string & {})`) tolerates free-form codes, so nothing breaks at runtime, but these account codes are second-class versus the rest of the catalog.
recommendation: Add the four ACCOUNT_* codes (category + `when` text) to smithersErrorDefinitions.js / KnownSmithersErrorCode so they are documented and recognized like all other domain errors.

title: Untested branches/input-classes: addAccount apiKey:"" + addedAt-preservation + model paths, and accountsRoot empty-string SMITHERS_HOME
evidence: tests/accounts.test.js never calls addAccount with apiKey:"" (the env-var-only persistence branch addAccount.js:43 `if (account.apiKey !== undefined)`), never with an explicit addedAt (the preservation branch addAccount.js:40 `account.addedAt ?? new Date()...`), and never with model via addAccount (addAccount.js:44 — model only round-trips through parseAccountsFile in this suite; the addAccount+model path is only covered in apps/cli/tests/agents-ts-codegen.test.js, a different package). accountsRoot('') falling through to $HOME/.smithers (accountsRoot.js:12 truthiness of '') is verified by execution but untested/undocumented.
recommendation: Add unit tests: addAccount({provider:'openai-api',apiKey:''}) then read back apiKey===''; addAccount with explicit addedAt and assert it is preserved; addAccount({...,model:'m'}) and assert persisted model; accountsRoot({SMITHERS_HOME:'',HOME:...}) fallback.

title: defaultConfigDir defense-in-depth escape branch is unreachable and untested (only uncovered lines in the package)
evidence: Coverage run shows the only uncovered lines are defaultConfigDir.js:36-39 — the second SmithersError throw guarding against an escape after LABEL_PATTERN already passed. Because any label that passes LABEL_PATTERN (/^[A-Za-z0-9._-]+$/, excluding '.' and '..') can never resolve outside base, this throw is unreachable through the public surface; it is intentional defense-in-depth but is dead under the current regex and has no test. The escape check also hardcodes a POSIX separator (`base + "/"` at line 35), which would be wrong on Windows if it ever became reachable.
recommendation: Document that 36-39 is intentionally unreachable defense-in-depth (and exclude from coverage), or add a test that injects a regex/label that would trigger it. If it is meant to be portable, use path.sep instead of a literal '/'.

title: 0.17.0 changelog documents the accounts public API incorrectly (async + wrong provider id + wrong getAccount signature)
evidence: docs/changelogs/0.17.0.mdx:327-336 shows `await addAccount(...)`, `await listAccounts()`, `accountToProviderEnv(await getAccount("claude", "work"))`, and `provider: "claude"`. All wrong vs the real API: these functions are synchronous (addAccount.js/listAccounts.js/getAccount.js return values, not Promises); the valid provider is "claude-code" not "claude" (parseAccountsFile.js:4 VALID_PROVIDERS) so `addAccount({provider:"claude"})` throws ACCOUNT_INVALID; and getAccount's signature is (label, env) not (provider, label) — `getAccount("claude","work")` would treat "work" as the env object.
recommendation: Fix the changelog snippet (or mark it superseded): drop `await`, use provider 'claude-code', and `getAccount(label, env)`. Consider adding a current cli-agents.mdx example so the only working example isn't buried in a stale changelog.

title: package.json "./*" subpath export is unused and would serve whole-bundle types for any subpath
evidence: package.json:13-17 exposes `"./*": { types: "./src/index.d.ts", import: "./src/*.js" }`, so e.g. `@smithers-orchestrator/accounts/addAccount` would resolve runtime to addAccount.js but TYPES to the entire index.d.ts bundle (mismatched). A repo-wide grep finds no consumer importing any `@smithers-orchestrator/accounts/<subpath>` — every importer uses the root. The subpath export is dead surface and mis-typed if anyone used it.
recommendation: Drop the "./*" export (only root is consumed), or, if subpath imports are intended, point each subpath's types at the matching `./src/*.d.ts` instead of the bundle.

title: Committed generated index.d.ts has no CI sync guard (drift risk)
evidence: src/index.d.ts is a tsup-generated artifact (bundler-mangled `Account$1`, `type Account = Account$1`) committed into src/ and shipped as the package's `types`. tsup.config.ts writes it to outDir 'src'. I regenerated via `pnpm build` and diffed: currently IN SYNC, but there is no check-* gate asserting the committed .d.ts equals the freshly generated one, so editing Account.ts/AccountProvider.ts/AccountsFile.ts without rebuilding silently ships stale public types.
recommendation: Add a CI check that `tsup --dts-only` produces no diff against the committed src/index.d.ts (mirroring the repo's check-docs/check-llms pattern), or generate the .d.ts at build time and gitignore it.

title: agents.ts codegen also fails the configDir-XOR-apiKey invariant, propagating a stray API key into spawned agents
evidence: renderAccountProviderLine emits `if (account.configDir) opts.push(configDir...)` and `if (account.apiKey) opts.push(apiKey...)` with no mutual exclusion. Combined with the parseAccountsFile/addAccount gap (which can persist a claude-code account carrying apiKey:'sk-leak'), the regenerated .smithers/agents.ts gets `new SmithersClaudeCodeAgent({ configDir: '/c', apiKey: 'sk-leak', ... })`, and ClaudeCodeAgent.buildCommand (ClaudeCodeAgent.js:471-472) then sets ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-leak in the spawned `claude` env alongside CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR. This turns the storage-layer invariant violation into an actual unintended credential injected into the child agent process. The codegen layer should also drop the non-applicable field (or rely on a parser/addAccount that strips it).
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: fiberToNode in SmithersDevTools only descends one level through non-Smithers fibers, dropping deeply-wrapped nodes
evidence: When a child fiber is non-Smithers (fiberToNode returns null at line 133-134 before walking children), the else branch (143-151) manually iterates only child.child grandchildren and calls fiberToNode(grandchild). fiberToNode returns null immediately for a non-Smithers grandchild WITHOUT exploring its children. So two consecutive non-Smithers wrapper fibers (e.g. a Smithers node wrapped in two user composite components/Fragments) hide everything beneath the second wrapper. Contrast findSmithersRoot (172-185) which uses bippy's full traverseFiber — inconsistent traversal strategy. No test covers multi-level non-Smithers nesting (grep of devtools.test.js for Wrapper/grandchild/nested-component found nothing).
recommendation: Replace the hand-rolled one-level grandchild fallback with a recursive descent that, when a fiber has no nodeType, collects Smithers descendants at any depth (e.g. a helper that walks child.child recursively, or reuse traverseFiber). Add a devtools test that wraps a Task in two plain React components and asserts it still appears in the tree.
vnote: Reproduced by reading SmithersDevTools.js:131-164. fiberToNode returns null immediately for any non-host fiber (133-134) before walking children. In the else branch (143-151) it iterates only child.child grandchildren and calls fiberToNode(grandchild); a non-host grandchild yields null and its subtree is never explored. Verified via bippy/dist/core.js that isHostFiber (x=) returns true only for tags 5/26/27 or typeof type===string, so composite components (FunctionComponent tag 0) and Fragment (tag 7) are NOT host fibers. Verified composites: Workflow.js/Sequence.js return React.createElement('smithers:...') i.e. composite -> host. So a single user wrapper like <Workflow><MyWrapper><Task/></MyWrapper></Workflow> produces workflow-host -> MyWrapper(composite,non-host) -> Task(composite,non-host) -> smithers:task(host); the else branch walks MyWrapper.child=Task(non-host), fiberToNode(Task) returns null, and the smithers:task host beneath is lost. Reachable with one wrapper, not two. Contrast with findSmithersRoot (172-185) using bippy traverseFiber - inconsistent. No test covers multi-level/wrapper nesting (grepped components/tests/devtools.test.js, react-reconciler/tests/, server/tests/getDevToolsSnapshot.test.tsx for Wrapper/grandchild/nested - none). Scope note: this is a devtools/visualization-only defect (snapshot tree completeness); actual graph extraction uses extractGraph over the host node tree, not fibers, so workflow execution is unaffected. P1 is defensible given devtools accuracy is the package's stated purpose, though one could argue P2 since it is non-load-bearing for execution.

title: Dead host-config method: prepareUpdate is never called by react-reconciler 0.33
evidence: react-reconciler@0.33.0 (the pinned dep) contains zero references to prepareUpdate in both cjs/react-reconciler.development.js and cjs/react-reconciler.production.js (grep -c returned 0). The host config still defines prepareUpdate(_instance,_type,oldProps,newProps). It is vestigial from a pre-0.33 reconciler; commitUpdate now receives (instance,type,oldProps,newProps,fiber) directly (verified at react-reconciler.development.js:11211-11221).
recommendation: Remove the prepareUpdate method (and its line-181-183 uncovered branch). Optionally add a comment that 0.33 dropped the prepare/commit split.
vnote: Verified grep -c prepareUpdate on node_modules/.pnpm/react-reconciler@0.33.0_react@19.2.5/.../cjs/react-reconciler.development.js and .production.js both return 0. Confirmed commitUpdate is read from $$$config (line 17034) and called with (stateNode,type,oldProps,newProps,finishedWork) at react-reconciler.development.js:11211-11221, so the prepare/commit split is gone. reconciler.js commitUpdate(instance,...args) correctly extracts newProps from args[2], so prepareUpdate (reconciler.js:181-185) does nothing. Only reference in repo is the definition itself; no test invokes it. The other installed reconciler 0.25.1 (used by a different package) does reference prepareUpdate (3 hits), confirming it is vestigial from pre-0.33.

title: core-types.js is an orphaned re-export imported by nothing
evidence: File body is `export * from "@smithers-orchestrator/graph";`. Repo-wide grep for "core-types" finds zero imports anywhere in packages/apps/.smithers/scripts/docs — only two hits in .smithers/specs/package-restructure-plan.md describing a hypothetical future core-types.ts split, and no src file in react-reconciler imports it (grep core-types over src/ returns nothing). It is reachable only via the package.json `./*` wildcard subpath, which nobody uses. Leftover from the d843fd6f JS migration.
recommendation: Delete src/core-types.js. Consumers import @smithers-orchestrator/graph directly.
vnote: File exists (46 bytes): export * from '@smithers-orchestrator/graph'. Repo-wide grep for core-types across packages/apps/.smithers/scripts/docs finds only two hits in .smithers/specs/package-restructure-plan.md (lines 350,733) describing a hypothetical future core-types.ts split - unrelated to this existing .js. index.js exports only reconciler/driver/context, not core-types. Actual react-reconciler subpath imports used across the repo are /context, /dom/renderer, /driver, /jsx-runtime - never /core-types. Reachable only via the package.json './*' wildcard which nobody uses. Confirmed dead.

title: reconciler.js has top-level side effects but package.json declares sideEffects:false
evidence: At module scope reconciler.js runs Reconciler(hostConfig), then `if (!hookHost.__REACT_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__) installRDTHook();` (mutates globalThis, lines 401-404) and reconciler.injectIntoDevTools({...}) (405-410). package.json line 6 declares "sideEffects": false. A bundler trusting sideEffects:false may drop/reorder these effects, breaking devtools instrumentation. The behavior is load-bearing: components/tests/reconciler.test.js:16-20 asserts globalThis.__REACT_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__ is installed merely by importing SmithersRenderer.
recommendation: Either set "sideEffects": ["./src/reconciler.js", "./src/index.js"] (or true), or move the global-hook install + injectIntoDevTools into lazy initialization run from SmithersRenderer's constructor so the module is genuinely side-effect-free.
vnote: The factual claim is correct: reconciler.js runs top-level side effects (installRDTHook mutating globalThis at 402-404 and reconciler.injectIntoDevTools at 405-410), and package.json:6 declares sideEffects:false. The load-bearing assertion is confirmed: components/tests/reconciler.test.js:16-20 asserts globalThis.__REACT_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__ is installed merely by importing SmithersRenderer. However the stated breakage ('a bundler may drop/reorder these effects') does not materialize in the audited product: the package build script is 'tsup --dts-only' (tsup.config emits dts only), the files field ships raw src/, and exports point to ./src/*.js - no JS bundling occurs. apps/cli also builds --dts-only, and no package in the monorepo does real JS bundling/tree-shaking (no noExternal/bundle:true). So the only risk is to a hypothetical downstream consumer bundling the published raw-ESM package with a tree-shaker honoring sideEffects:false. Real metadata inaccuracy but no in-repo breakage and conditional impact - severity P2, not P1.

title: Container stores a single root; top-level Fragment/array silently truncated to last child
evidence: appendChildToContainer sets `container.root = child` and insertInContainerBefore ignores _beforeChild and also just sets `container.root = child`. HostContainer (HostContainer.ts) is `{ root: HostNode | null }` — single slot. If a workflow root renders a Fragment/array (multiple container-level children), each append/insert overwrites container.root, so only the last survives with no error. No test renders a top-level Fragment or array (grep of react-reconciler tests + components reconciler.test.js for Fragment/<>/array found none), and the single-root constraint is undocumented.
recommendation: Either throw/warn when more than one container child is appended, or document the single-root contract. At minimum add a test asserting current behavior so the constraint is explicit.
vnote: Verified HostContainer.ts is {root: HostNode | null} (single slot). appendChildToContainer (reconciler.js:129-131) and insertInContainerBefore (171-173, ignoring _beforeChild) both do container.root = child, overwriting. A top-level Fragment/array rendering multiple container-level children would leave only the last with no error. No test renders a top-level Fragment/array (grepped react-reconciler tests + components/tests/reconciler.test.js for Fragment/<>/array - none). Constraint is undocumented. P2 is appropriate - this is an undocumented-contract/robustness gap, not an observed user-facing crash.

title: resolveExtractGraph error/fallback paths and importCoreModule catch are untested
evidence: Repo-wide grep for "Unable to load extractGraph", "importCoreModule", and "resolveExtractGraph" across *.test.* files returns nothing. The happy default path is covered indirectly (components/tests/reconciler.test.js 'uses @smithers-orchestrator/graph extractGraph by default'), but the importCoreModule catch (line 13, returns null on import failure), the LOCAL_GRAPH_SPECIFIER fallback iteration, and the final throw (lines 31-32) are uncovered (coverage shows core-peer.js lines 13,29-32 uncovered). Note also it always awaits BOTH imports even when the first resolves (lines 21-24), which needlessly attempts the relative ../../graph path in published installs.
recommendation: Add a unit test that constructs SmithersRenderer with no extractGraph option in an environment where the graph specifier resolves, and one asserting the thrown error message when neither module exposes extractGraph (e.g. by injecting via the LOCAL specifier mock or testing resolveExtractGraph directly). Consider short-circuiting the second import when the first succeeds.

title: commitUpdate defensive branches for non-0.33 signatures are untested and likely unreachable
evidence: react-reconciler 0.33 always calls commitUpdate(instance, type, oldProps, newProps, fiber) (verified at react-reconciler.development.js:11211-11221). So args=[type(string), oldProps(obj), newProps(obj), fiber]; only the second branch (`typeof first === "string"`, lines 198-203) ever fires. The first branch (195-196: typeof second===string) and third branch (204-205: first is object) are dead for this version. Line coverage marks the function executed but never exercises those branch bodies (no branch coverage). The committed comment/code suggests defensive multi-version handling that isn't pinned by any test.
recommendation: Either drop the unreachable branches (the dep is pinned to 0.33) and keep only the (type, oldProps, newProps) extraction, or add unit tests calling the host config's commitUpdate directly with each argument shape to lock in the defensiveness.

title: SmithersDevTools verbose-logging and onCommitFiberUnmount paths are untested
evidence: Coverage (combined) leaves the verbose console.log branches (237-241 commit log, 251-254 unmount log) and the onCommitFiberUnmount/emitUnmount path unexercised — no test constructs SmithersDevTools with {verbose:true} (grep of devtools.test.js for verbose found nothing; the only verbose:true hits are in packages/agents tests) and no test triggers a Smithers-node unmount commit. The `runs` getter (line 311) is also uncovered. devtools-stop.test.js covers start/stop cleanup but not these.
recommendation: Add a devtools test with verbose:true asserting log output (spy on console), a test that renders then unmounts a Smithers node to drive onCommitFiberUnmount, and a trivial assertion on the runs getter.

title: Several react-reconciler 0.33 host-config stubs are never executed by the headless sync path
evidence: Combined coverage of reconciler.js (85% lines) leaves these uncovered everywhere: removeChildFromContainer (146), insertInContainerBefore (171), getCurrentEventPriority (254), shouldAttemptEagerTransition (260), maySuspendCommit (266), waitForCommitToBeReady (284), bindToConsole (300-302), and the suspense/scheduler stubs (350,356,362,372,380). These are required-by-0.33 host config entries that the synchronous headless render path doesn't invoke, but bindToConsole in particular contains real logic (`const fn = console[type] ?? console.log; fn(...args)`) that is wholly untested.
recommendation: Add focused unit tests that import the hostConfig (or a small harness) and call bindToConsole directly (assert it returns a thunk that calls the right console method and falls back to console.log for unknown type). The other no-op stubs are acceptable to leave, but bindToConsole's branch deserves a test.

title: commitTextUpdate (text content re-render) is not exercised by a changing-text test
evidence: commitTextUpdate sets textInstance.text = newText (line 219, marked uncovered in the react-reconciler-only coverage run). The only text test (renderer-comprehensive.test.jsx 'renders text children') renders 'Write a poem' once and never re-renders with different text, so the text-mutation path is not driven. createTextInstance is covered; the update is not.
recommendation: Add a test that renders a task with text child, re-renders with different text content, and asserts getRoot()'s text node updated (mirroring the existing keyed-move regression test style).

title: insertInContainerBefore ignores its _beforeChild argument, silently dropping ordering semantics
evidence: insertInContainerBefore(container, child, _beforeChild) does container.root = child and never consults _beforeChild. Combined with the single-slot container this means container-level insert-before ordering is a no-op that overwrites root. This is the same single-root limitation as appendChildToContainer but specifically discards the reconciler's positional intent. No test exercises insertInContainerBefore (grep of tests shows only re-render coverage in reconciler.test.js:64-71 which exercises appendChildToContainer overwrite, not the insert-before path).
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: captureWorkspaceSnapshot + withSnapshotTimeout have ZERO coverage in CI (only tested in jj-real-repo.test.js, which is skipped when jj is absent)
evidence: captureWorkspaceSnapshot appears in tests ONLY in jj-real-repo.test.js (grep across repo confirms no fake-bin test). That suite is gated by `const describeIfJj = jjAvailable ? describe : describe.skip` (line 26). CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml: ubuntu-latest, `pnpm install` + `pnpm test`, no jj install, no `fetch:jj`) has no jj and no jj-binaries package in the workspace, so resolveJjBinary falls to bare "jj" which is absent. Re-running with jj off PATH I observed: `0 pass, 12 skip`. The snapshot helper is the restore handle for durability/time-travel (used by engine/startDurability.js and time-travel) — it ships untested on every PR. withSnapshotTimeout's onTimeout sentinel (code 124) and captureWorkspaceSnapshot's intermediate null branches (logRes.code!==0, !commitId, opRes.code!==0, !operationId) are never exercised even locally.
recommendation: Add fake-bin (withFakeJj-style) tests for captureWorkspaceSnapshot covering: success (commit/change/op ids parsed), each null branch (log fails, empty commit_id, op fails, empty op id), and the snapshot-timeout path (script sleeps > 1500ms → result null). This mirrors the existing getJjPointer timeout test (jj-workspace.test.js:410) and makes the durability path green-on-CI without a real jj.
vnote: Reproduced exactly. (1) captureWorkspaceSnapshot is referenced in tests only in jj-real-repo.test.js (lines 43,307-356) plus engine/tests/restore-workspace.test.js:73 and engine/tests/snapshot-service.test.js:166 — but those engine tests are ALSO gated by `describeIfJj` (restore-workspace.test.js:61, snapshot-service.test.js:153), so they skip in CI too. (2) CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml: ubuntu-latest, `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` + `pnpm test`, no fetch:jj step) has no jj: the bundled jj-binaries packages (packages/jj-linux-x64, etc.) ship only bin/.gitkeep — the binary is gitignored ('The vendored binary is fetched at release time, never committed', packages/jj-linux-x64/.gitignore) and fetched via `pnpm fetch:jj` (scripts/fetch-jj-binaries.mjs) only at release. So resolveJjBinary falls through to bare 'jj', which is absent on the CI box, making jjAvailable false and skipping every describeIfJj suite. (3) I ran `SMITHERS_JJ_PATH=/nonexistent PATH=.../usr/bin:/bin bun test tests/jj-real-repo.test.js` and observed exactly `0 pass, 12 skip, 0 fail`. (4) Decisive corroboration: jj-workspace.test.js uses withFakeJj fake-bin tests that DO run in CI for every other jj.js export (runJj, isJjRepo, workspaceAdd, workspaceList, workspaceClose, getJjPointer including its timeout path at line 410, revertToJjPointer) — captureWorkspaceSnapshot is the ONE public function with no fake-bin test. withSnapshotTimeout is used only inside captureWorkspaceSnapshot and likewise has no fake-bin coverage; its onTimeout sentinel (code 124) and the four intermediate null branches (logRes.code!==0, !commitId, opRes.code!==0, !operationId) are never exercised in CI. This is the durability/time-travel restore-handle path shipping untested on every PR. Severity P0 is defensible given criticality, though note the code IS exercised in real-jj environments (local/release), so a case for P1 exists; I keep P0.

title: Bare require() inside an ESM module breaks workspaceAdd pre-create cleanup under Node (inconsistent with resolveJjBinary's createRequire)
evidence: jj.js is ESM (package.json `"type":"module"`, uses import). workspaceAdd does `const fs = require("node:fs"); const nodePath = require("node:path");` with no createRequire. Under Node ESM `require` is undefined — I verified: `node --input-type=module -e "await import('./packages/vcs/src/jj.js'); console.log(typeof require)"` prints `undefined`. The block is wrapped in try/catch, so under Node it throws ReferenceError, is caught, logs "pre-create cleanup failed", and SILENTLY skips removing a stale target dir and ensuring the parent dir exists — degrading workspace creation reliability. It only works today because the CLI shebang is `#!/usr/bin/env bun` (apps/cli/src/index.js:1) and Bun provides a global require. The sibling resolveJjBinary.js:5 correctly does `const require = createRequire(import.meta.url)`. The package is published as a generic library with no `engines` Bun constraint, so any Node consumer of workspaceAdd loses the cleanup.
recommendation: Either import fs/path statically at top of jj.js (preferred; they're node builtins) or add `const require = createRequire(import.meta.url)` as resolveJjBinary.js already does. Add a fake-bin test asserting a stale target dir is removed and a missing parent dir is created before `jj workspace add` runs.
vnote: Verified. jj.js is ESM (package.json type:module; uses import) and the shipped source itself (tsup builds only .d.ts via dts:{only:true}, outDir:'src'; the published `import` maps to src/jj.js directly). workspaceAdd at jj.js:197-198 does bare `require('node:fs')`/`require('node:path')` with no createRequire. I confirmed under Node ESM: `node --input-type=module -e "...require('node:fs')..."` throws `ReferenceError: require is not defined`, and `typeof require` is `undefined` after importing jj.js. The block is try/catch wrapped (jj.js:196-211): under Node it throws, is caught, logs 'pre-create cleanup failed', and silently skips removing a stale target dir + ensuring the parent dir exists. The sibling resolveJjBinary.js:5 correctly does `const require = createRequire(import.meta.url)`. It only works in-product because the CLI runs under bun (apps/cli/src/index.js:1 shebang `#!/usr/bin/env bun`, confirmed; `bun --eval typeof require` => 'function'). workspaceAdd is re-exported on the PUBLISHED public surface (packages/smithers/src/index.js:184) and consumed by engine.js:749 and time-travel resolveWorkflowAtRevisionEffect.js, so a Node consumer of the published library loses the cleanup. The existing fake-bin test that asserts the target dir is removed (jj-workspace.test.js:182-211, `[[ ! -e ... ]] || exit 9`) passes only because `bun test` runs under bun; it would fail under Node — so the require path is genuinely untested under the runtime where it breaks. Real bug; P1 is borderline-high since the product's own usage (bun) is unaffected and the failure is degraded-but-caught, but the published-API impact and the inconsistency with resolveJjBinary justify keeping P1.

title: WorkspaceSnapshot.ts is orphaned dead code with documentation that diverges from (and is richer than) the authoritative inline typedef
evidence: Grep across the whole repo shows WorkspaceSnapshot.ts is imported by nothing. The other 6 type files (JjRevertResult.ts, RunJjOptions.ts, RunJjResult.ts, WorkspaceAddOptions.ts, WorkspaceInfo.ts, WorkspaceResult.ts) are each pulled in via `@typedef {import("./X.js")...}` in jj.js:2-7, but WorkspaceSnapshot is instead redefined as an inline `@typedef {object} WorkspaceSnapshot` in jj.js:8-13. The generated public surface (src/index.d.ts:131-144) derives from that inline typedef, not the .ts file. check-docs.mjs:2438 even FORBIDS re-exporting WorkspaceSnapshot.js from index. Result: the .ts file is unreachable, and its field docs (e.g. operationId described as 'The durable restore handle... commit id ... can be garbage-collected, while the operation log is retained') are strictly more correct/detailed than the terse inline ones, so the better docs live in the file nobody reads.
recommendation: Either delete WorkspaceSnapshot.ts and fold its richer field docs into the jj.js inline typedef, or make jj.js import it like the other 6 type files and drop the inline typedef. Keep exactly one source of truth.
vnote: Verified with one wording correction that does not change the conclusion. Exhaustive grep shows nothing imports WorkspaceSnapshot.ts: no `@typedef {import("./WorkspaceSnapshot.js")}`, no `export * from "./WorkspaceSnapshot.js"` in index.js (index.js only re-exports find-root, jj, resolveGitBinary, resolveJjBinary, vcsToolingStatus), and no runtime import. The only repo reference to WorkspaceSnapshot.js is check-docs.mjs:2438. Confirmed the other 6 type files ARE imported via `@typedef {import("./X.js")}` in jj.js:2-7 (and ResolvedBinary in resolveGitBinary/vcsToolingStatus/resolveJjBinary), while WorkspaceSnapshot is instead redefined inline as `@typedef {object}` in jj.js:8-13 — making WorkspaceSnapshot.ts the only unimported type file. The generated public surface (index.d.ts:131-144) derives from the inline typedef, not the .ts file. CORRECTION to the finding's wording: check-docs.mjs:2438 is in the `forbidden` array (starts line 2420) and forbids the index re-export of ALL seven type files (ResolvedBinary, JjRevertResult, RunJjOptions, RunJjResult, WorkspaceAddOptions, WorkspaceInfo, WorkspaceResult AND WorkspaceSnapshot, lines 2431-2438) — it does NOT single out WorkspaceSnapshot. But that doesn't rescue the file: the other six are still imported via @typedef, WorkspaceSnapshot is not, so it remains genuinely orphaned. The .ts file's field docs are strictly richer (e.g. operationId: 'The durable restore handle: the commit id of an abandoned working-copy commit can be garbage-collected, while the operation log is retained...') vs jj.js's terse inline 'JJ operation id for the snapshot.' P2 is correct.

title: Two near-duplicate test files for findVcsRoot with overlapping cases and a misleading test name
evidence: Both files import the same `findVcsRoot` from src/find-root.js and re-test the same matrix: git-only, jj-only, colocated-prefers-jj, no-VCS-returns-null, deep-nested walk-up (both landed in the same commit 739cdf88). The only nominally-unique case, vcs-find-root.test.js:62 'finds VCS root when starting from root itself', passes a project subdir that contains `.git`, not the actual filesystem root — so it does NOT cover find-root.js:23-27 (the post-loop fallback that only fires when the walk reaches the true fsRoot). Neither file tests: a child with `.git` under an ancestor with `.jj` (nearest-level-wins precedence, distinct from same-dir colocated), nor a non-existent startDir.
recommendation: Merge the two files into one, drop the duplicate assertions, and add the missing branch cases (nearest-level precedence; walk reaching fsRoot with no VCS → null) so find-root.js:23-27 is actually exercised.

title: vcsToolingStatus error/negative branches untested: ok:false (both null), jj-null path, and runsVersion catch branch
evidence: Tests cover git-unusable (git==null), jj-usable-via-override, git-usable-via-override. They never assert `ok:false` with BOTH jj and git null — which is the exact condition that drives the user-facing CLI warning at apps/cli/src/index.js:2192 (`if (!vcs.ok ...)`). The `status.jj === null` path (jj resolves but `--version` fails) is also untested, and runsVersion's catch branch (spawnSync throwing) is unreachable in the current tests.
recommendation: Add a test that points both SMITHERS_JJ_PATH and SMITHERS_GIT_PATH at exit-1 fakes and asserts `{jj:null, git:null, ok:false}`, plus a jj-null-only case. These are the branches the product actually keys off of.

title: resolveGitBinary 'override file does not exist' branch is untested (resolveJjBinary has the equivalent test)
evidence: resolveGitBinary.js:17 is `if (override && existsSync(override)) return {source:'env'}`. The tests only cover override-points-at-real-file and no-override-falls-to-PATH. The branch where SMITHERS_GIT_PATH is set but the file does NOT exist (override ignored, falls to bare 'git') is untested. resolveJjBinary has exactly this case (resolve-jj-binary.test.js:41-45 'ignores SMITHERS_JJ_PATH when the file does not exist'), so the gap is an asymmetry.
recommendation: Add a resolveGitBinary test setting SMITHERS_GIT_PATH to a non-existent path and asserting `{path:'git', source:'path'}`.

title: resolveJjBinary bundled-resolution branches (bundled path, require.resolve catch, unsupported platform) are untested
evidence: The only non-override test asserts `source !== 'env'` and accepts either 'bundled' or 'path'. bundledJjPath()'s three outcomes are never deterministically covered: (a) `!pkg` → null for an unsupported `${platform}-${arch}`, (b) require.resolve throwing → null (catch at line 42-44), (c) a present binary returning source:'bundled'. Since no jj-binaries package exists in the workspace (verified: no packages/jj-binaries dir, none under node_modules/@smithers-orchestrator), the bundled branch is dead-at-rest in this repo and never green-tested.
recommendation: Inject a fake bundled package fixture (a temp @smithers-orchestrator/jj-<host>/package.json + bin/jj) on a controlled require path to cover the bundled-hit and the existsSync-miss-after-resolve branches, or at minimum a unit test of BUNDLED_PACKAGES key lookup for an unsupported platform.

title: vcsDuration metric is recorded only on the jj success path, not on failure/timeout
evidence: Metric.update(vcsDuration, performance.now()-start) is at line 53, inside the Effect.gen before the success return. Any failure/defect from Command.start or the collect fibers skips that line and is handled by the outer Effect.catchAll (line 63) which returns {code:127} without updating the metric. So failed and timed-out jj invocations (arguably the most interesting latency tail) are never measured.
recommendation: Record vcsDuration in an ensuring/finalizer (e.g. Effect.ensuring or in the catchAll) so both success and failure timings are captured.

title: Inconsistent timeout policy: isJjRepo (on the durability-startup hot path) has no timeout while getJjPointer/captureWorkspaceSnapshot do
evidence: getJjPointer (jj.js:84, JJ_POINTER_TIMEOUT_MS) and captureWorkspaceSnapshot (via withSnapshotTimeout, WORKSPACE_SNAPSHOT_TIMEOUT_MS) are explicitly bounded so 'a slow or hung jj cannot block the agent' (jj.js:100-101). But isJjRepo, which gates the entire durability path at startDurability.js:84 (`isJj = await isJjRepoFn(cwd)`), runs runJj with no timeout. A hung jj at startup would block durability init indefinitely, contradicting the stated timeout rationale applied to its siblings. revertToJjPointer/workspaceAdd/List/Close are also unbounded (more defensible since they're explicit user/engine actions, not the snapshot fast-path).
recommendation: Apply a bounded timeout to isJjRepo consistent with getJjPointer, or document why repo detection is intentionally allowed to block.

title: findVcsRoot is wrapped in Effect.sync but every consumer immediately Effect.runSync's it — pure ceremony with no Effect benefit
evidence: findVcsRoot does only synchronous existsSync walking and uses neither the error channel (E=never) nor requirements (R=never). All three engine call sites unwrap it as `Effect.runSync(findVcsRoot(rootDir))`. The Effect wrapper adds no async, no failure modeling, and no DI — it forces every caller to runSync a pure function, and the test helpers (find-vcs-root.test.js:19-21, vcs-find-root.test.js:10-12) re-wrap it again.
recommendation: Consider exposing findVcsRoot as a plain synchronous function (returning the union | null directly), or keep the Effect form only if a future async/error path is planned; otherwise it is API ceremony.

title: jj snapshot dedup/edge branches in captureWorkspaceSnapshot are only asserted under real jj, leaving the changeId-fallback and op-id parsing untested in CI
evidence: In captureWorkspaceSnapshot (packages/vcs/src/jj.js:131-147) the `changeId: changeId ?? ""` fallback (when `commit_id ++ "\n" ++ change_id` yields only one line) and the operationId-trim path are exercised only by the describeIfJj suite in jj-real-repo.test.js:307-356, which skips in CI (jj absent). A fake-bin (withFakeJj) test in jj-workspace.test.js could deterministically drive: success with both ids, log printing only commit_id (changeId falls back to ''), op log returning whitespace-only (operationId empty -> null), and the >1500ms sleep timeout path (mirroring the existing getJjPointer timeout test at jj-workspace.test.js:410). None exist today.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: `snapshots --json` and `timeline --json` emit unparseable stdout (dead command-level json option + framework CTA injection)
evidence: Both commands declare a `json: z.boolean()` option, but `--json` is intercepted by incur's GLOBAL format flag (these commands are absent from argvRequestsJsonMode, DEVTOOLS_COMMANDS, and rewriteDevtoolsJsonFlagArgv at index.js:5910/2589/2665). Verified empirically: `bun run apps/cli/src/index.js snapshots nonexistent-run --json` prints human text `No durability snapshots for run nonexistent-run` THEN a `{cta:{description:'Skills are out of date'...}}` JSON object (two documents, not parseable). `timeline nonexistent-run --json` prints `nonexistent-run` (human TUI) then a JSON payload then the same skills CTA. So `c.options.json` is always false for these commands and the option is dead. The json-stdout-contract test covers why/inspect/events/node/tree/output/diff/agents-doctor but omits snapshots and timeline (and scores/rewind), which is exactly why this ships.
recommendation: Route snapshots/timeline through the same `--json`→`-j` rewrite + json-mode detection used for tree/diff/output/rewind (add them to DEVTOOLS_COMMANDS/argvRequestsJsonMode or give them a `-j` alias), and add them to json-stdout-contract.test.js so every command advertising JSON is asserted to emit a single parseable document.
vnote: Reproduced empirically from the repo. `bun run apps/cli/src/index.js snapshots nonexistent-run --json` prints the human line `No durability snapshots for run nonexistent-run` followed by a separate `{cta:{...}}` JSON object (two documents). `timeline nonexistent-run --json` prints the human TUI line `nonexistent-run` then the JSON payload then the CTA. Verified the dispatch logic: snapshots/timeline are absent from DEVTOOLS_COMMANDS (index.js:2589), argvRequestsJsonMode (6034-6058), and rewriteDevtoolsJsonFlagArgv (2665-2672), so raw `--json` never becomes the command-scoped `-j`. snapshots.js:37 only takes its json branch when c.options.json is true, but that option stays false; the human branch always runs. Even `--format json` does not bind c.options.json (reproduced). The contract test (json-stdout-contract.test.js:151-160) covers why/inspect/events/node/tree/output/diff/agents-doctor but omits snapshots and timeline. Note: the parenthetical mention of `scores` is slightly off — scores (index.js:5303) declares NO json option and returns via c.ok() rather than console.log, so it does not have this same direct-stdout bug; and rewind IS in DEVTOOLS_COMMANDS (handled correctly, just untested in the contract). The core finding for snapshots/timeline is fully correct and P1 is justified — a shipped command-level option that is entirely non-functional and produces unparseable JSON.

title: `smithers cron` scheduler spawns `bun run src/index.js` which does not exist in user projects
evidence: processCronEffect does `spawn("bun", ["run", "src/index.js", "up", job.workflowPath, "-d"], { cwd: process.cwd(), ... })`. This assumes a `src/index.js` exists relative to the user's CWD — true only inside the smithers repo dev layout. Every other detached spawn resolves the real CLI entry: up uses `cliPath = new URL(import.meta.url).pathname` (index.js:1649) and resume-detached.js:17 uses `fileURLToPath(new URL("./index.js", import.meta.url))`. So a user running `smithers cron start` in their own project will silently fail to launch any scheduled workflow.
recommendation: Resolve the CLI entry via `fileURLToPath(new URL("./index.js", import.meta.url))` (matching resume-detached.js) and set cwd to the workflow's directory; add a unit/e2e test that asserts the spawned argv/entry path.
vnote: scheduler.js:30 does spawn("bun", ["run", "src/index.js", "up", job.workflowPath, "-d"], { cwd: process.cwd() }) — a relative `src/index.js` resolved against the user's CWD. Confirmed the published bin (packages/smithers/src/bin/smithers.js:138) loads `@smithers-orchestrator/cli` and that cron start → runScheduler() (index.js:2272) executes with process.cwd() being the user's project directory, where `src/index.js` does not exist. Confirmed every other detached spawn resolves the real entry via import.meta.url: index.js:1649 and 2902 use `new URL(import.meta.url).pathname`, resume-detached.js:17 uses `fileURLToPath(new URL("./index.js", import.meta.url))`. The scheduler is the sole outlier. No test covers the cron scheduler spawn (grep of apps/cli/tests for processCronEffect/runScheduler/cron found only docs-coverage and a packages/engine scheduler that is unrelated). Real bug, P1 confirmed.

title: Core argv-parsing functions have zero unit tests (parseMcpSurfaceArgv, findFirstPositionalIndex, rewriteBareResumeFlagArgv)
evidence: No test file imports argv-utils.js (grep across apps/cli/tests and packages/*/tests is empty). These pure functions underpin all command dispatch: findFirstPositionalIndex drives every argv rewrite in main(); rewriteBareResumeFlagArgv handles the `--resume`/`--run-id` collision; parseMcpSurfaceArgv parses `--surface semantic|raw|both` with three throw branches (missing value, invalid value, `--surface=` form). No test passes `--mcp`/`--surface` with any surface value, so the MCP raw/semantic/both selection logic is entirely unexercised.
recommendation: Add a unit test suite for argv-utils.js covering each branch: bare/`=`/missing/invalid `--surface`, BUILTIN_FLAGS_WITH_VALUES value-skipping in findFirstPositionalIndex, and rewriteBareResumeFlagArgv at end-of-argv and followed by another flag.
vnote: Full-repo grep (excluding node_modules) for argv-utils/parseMcpSurfaceArgv/findFirstPositionalIndex/rewriteBareResumeFlagArgv returns only apps/cli/src/index.js and apps/cli/src/argv-utils.js — no test file. All three are exported and central to dispatch: findFirstPositionalIndex is called 11+ times across index.js (2666, 2676, 5924, 6035, 6051, 6111, 6118, 6148, 6278, 6282, 6333, 6344, 6421); rewriteBareResumeFlagArgv at 6350; parseMcpSurfaceArgv at 6354. normalizeMcpSurface has three throw paths (missing value, invalid value, and the `--surface=` empty form which also routes through it). None of these branches, nor the BUILTIN_FLAGS_WITH_VALUES value-skipping in findFirstPositionalIndex, are exercised by any test. Confirmed P1.

title: Orphaned type files: AskOptions.ts, InitWorkflowPackOptions.ts, InitWorkflowPackResult.ts, and a shebang-only index.d.ts
evidence: The repo's typedef convention is JSDoc `import("./Foo.ts").Foo` (e.g. watch.js:8 imports WatchLoopOptions.ts). AskOptions.ts is never imported that way; ask.js:17-20 instead defines its OWN inline `@typedef AskOptions`/`AskAgentId` and `ASK_AGENT_IDS` (ask.js:24), so AskOptions.ts is a dead duplicate (it has even diverged in agent ordering). InitWorkflowPackOptions.ts / InitWorkflowPackResult.ts have zero references anywhere (grep across apps/cli + packages empty). src/index.d.ts is a single line containing only `#!/usr/bin/env bun` — an invalid declaration file; package types point at the tsup-generated dist/index.d.ts, not this one.
recommendation: Delete AskOptions.ts, InitWorkflowPackOptions.ts, InitWorkflowPackResult.ts, and src/index.d.ts.
vnote: Dead-code claims all hold. InitWorkflowPackOptions.ts and InitWorkflowPackResult.ts are referenced only by themselves (full-repo grep). src/index.d.ts contains exactly `#!/usr/bin/env bun` (19 bytes) — an invalid declaration file; package.json types points at ./dist/index.d.ts (tsup --dts-only build), and dist/index.d.ts is not the same file. AskOptions.ts is never imported: ask.js defines its own inline @typedef AskOptions (line 20), @typedef AskAgentId (line 17), and ASK_AGENT_IDS const (line 24) — confirmed it does not import AskOptions.ts. ONE sub-claim is inaccurate: the finding says AskOptions.ts 'has even diverged in agent ordering', but the orderings are IDENTICAL — AskOptions.ts has `"claude"|"codex"|"kimi"|"antigravity"|"gemini"|"pi"` and ask.js has `["claude","codex","kimi","antigravity","gemini","pi"]`. Marking adjusted (not refuted) because the central dead-code finding and P2 severity are correct; only the divergence detail is wrong.

title: Dead MCP exports: registerSemanticTools and serveSemanticMcpServer
evidence: Grep across apps/cli/src + apps/cli/tests + packages shows only `createSemanticMcpServer` is consumed (index.js:71/6359, semantic-mcp.test.js:24). registerSemanticTools and serveSemanticMcpServer are never imported outside their own file. serveSemanticMcpServer duplicates exactly what index.js:6359-6367 does inline (createSemanticMcpServer + StdioServerTransport + connect), so it is dead and redundant.
recommendation: Either delete both unused exports, or route index.js's --mcp semantic path through serveSemanticMcpServer to remove the duplicated transport-wiring and give the export a real consumer + test.
vnote: Half right. serveSemanticMcpServer (semantic-server.js:36) is genuinely dead — full-repo grep shows it is only defined, never imported/called anywhere, and the test (semantic-mcp.test.js) uses only createSemanticMcpServer. BUT registerSemanticTools is NOT dead: it is called internally at semantic-server.js:30 inside createSemanticMcpServer, which is the consumed export (index.js:71/6359 and semantic-mcp.test.js:24), so registerSemanticTools' body executes and is exercised through the test. The accurate characterization is 'exported unnecessarily' (could be un-exported), not dead code; the recommendation to 'delete both unused exports' is wrong for registerSemanticTools — deleting it would break createSemanticMcpServer. Also, the claim that serveSemanticMcpServer 'duplicates exactly what index.js:6359-6367 does inline' is imprecise: the inline path additionally calls registerRawToolsOnMcpServer(server) for the `both` surface (line 6364) before connecting, which serveSemanticMcpServer does not support, so it cannot drop-in replace the inline code. P2 is fine for the genuinely-dead serveSemanticMcpServer.

title: cron commands and scheduler tick logic have no tests
evidence: No test references cron add/list/rm/start (only docs-cli-overview-coverage.test.js mentions the group for docs parity). scheduler.js (runScheduler, schedulerTickEffect, processCronEffect, parseDurationMs-independent cron next-run computation) is never imported by any test in apps/cli/tests or packages/*/tests. Given the spawn bug above, the absence of any test that asserts what processCronEffect launches is the gap that let it through.
recommendation: Add a unit test for schedulerTickEffect/processCronEffect against a real seeded SQLite (due vs not-due jobs, next-run cron computation, error recording) and an e2e for `cron add`/`list`/`rm` round-tripping through the DB.

title: MCP revert_attempt tool handler is untested
evidence: semantic-tools-unit.test.js exercises 12 of the 14 SEMANTIC_TOOL_NAMES and ask-human-mcp.test.js covers ask_human, but revert_attempt is never invoked in any test (grep for `revert_attempt` across tests returns nothing). Its handler shapes a non-trivial response (success/error/jjPointer spreads + buildRunSummary) that goes unverified; only the name-list assertion (semantic-tools-unit.test.js:323) confirms it is registered.
recommendation: Add a revert_attempt case to semantic-tools-unit.test.js exercising success, error (result.error present), and missing-run (run==null → null) shaping via the injected adapter.

title: index.js is a 6,439-line monolith mixing parsing, ~60 command bodies, MCP wiring, and helpers
evidence: Most commands already extract their logic to dedicated modules (eval-suite, optimize-command, supervisor, watch, hijack, etc.), but large command bodies remain inline in index.js: up/executeUpCommand, monitor (runMonitorCommand 2812-2866), ui (5758-5848, ~90 lines of Gateway RPC/browser-open logic), replay/fork (each ~60 lines duplicating resume-after-fork), timeline, scores. The inline ui/replay/fork bodies are precisely the untested commands (no test imports them as functions because they are not exported), so the monolith structure directly blocks unit coverage.
recommendation: Extract ui, replay, fork, and monitor handlers into exported functions in their own modules (mirroring eval-suite.js/supervisor.js) so they can be unit-tested in isolation and index.js shrinks to dispatch+parse.

title: Detached spawn path resolution is inconsistent (`.pathname` vs fileURLToPath)
evidence: up (1649) and openMonitoredRunUi (2902) compute `cliPath = new URL(import.meta.url).pathname`, which yields a percent-encoded / leading-slash path on Windows and on paths containing spaces/non-ASCII. resume-detached.js:17 correctly uses `fileURLToPath(new URL("./index.js", import.meta.url))`. The three sites should use the same helper.
recommendation: Replace `.pathname` with `fileURLToPath(...)` at index.js:1649 and 2902 (and factor a single resolveCliEntry() helper shared with resume-detached.js).

title: runDevtoolsCommandWithTelemetry is called for snapshots/restore despite its `cmd` type being tree|diff|output|rewind
evidence: The function's JSDoc declares `@param {"tree"|"diff"|"output"|"rewind"} cmd`, but snapshots and restore pass "snapshots"/"restore". Because DEVTOOLS_COMMANDS does NOT include them, the `--json`→`-j` rewrite, validateDevtoolsArgv, and the main() devtools exit-code preservation branch (index.js:6423 `isDevtoolsCmd`) all skip these two commands — which is the underlying cause of the snapshots --json bug above. The split between 'uses the telemetry wrapper' and 'is in DEVTOOLS_COMMANDS' is silently inconsistent.
recommendation: Make the membership consistent: either add snapshots/restore to DEVTOOLS_COMMANDS (and widen the JSDoc type) or stop routing them through the devtools telemetry wrapper.

title: Contract test does not cover every command that advertises JSON output (rewind omitted despite being handled)
evidence: apps/cli/tests/json-stdout-contract.test.js:151-160 asserts the single-parseable-JSON contract for why/inspect/events/node/tree/output/diff/agents-doctor. `rewind` is in DEVTOOLS_COMMANDS (index.js:2589) and is routed through the same --json→-j rewrite, so it is wired correctly, but it is never asserted by the contract test. snapshots and timeline are also omitted (which is exactly why finding #1's regression shipped). The test should enumerate every command that advertises a JSON option so a future regression in any of them is caught.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Entire in-memory MetricsService in _coreMetrics.js is dead code (only the Tag is used)
evidence: Repo-wide grep: makeInMemoryMetricsService, MetricsServiceNoop, smithersMetricCatalogByKey/byPrometheusName from this file have no importers outside the file itself. index.js line 14 imports ONLY `MetricsService` (the Tag) from _coreMetrics.js; the live MetricsServiceLive is exported from src/MetricsServiceLive.js (adapter-based, lines 6-9), shadowing _coreMetrics.js:498. The duplicate 114-entry smithersMetricCatalog (lines 85-199), smithersMetrics, makeInMemoryMetricsService (247-496), MetricsServiceLive (498), and MetricsServiceNoop (500-510) are unreachable. metrics/metricsServiceAdapter.js:1 even has a no-op `import {} from "../_coreMetrics.js";`.
recommendation: Strip _coreMetrics.js down to just the MetricsService Tag class (and the shape typedefs). Move the Tag to its own file (e.g. MetricsService.js) and delete the dead in-memory service, duplicate catalog, and the empty `import {}` in metricsServiceAdapter.js. This removes ~400 lines and eliminates a divergent second metrics implementation.
vnote: Verified by reading _coreMetrics.js and grepping the whole repo (packages, apps, .smithers, workflows, scripts). index.js:14 imports ONLY the MetricsService Tag from _coreMetrics.js; MetricsServiceLive.js:6-9 builds the live layer from metricsServiceAdapter (NOT _coreMetrics.js:498), and index.js:27 re-exports that one. metricsServiceAdapter.js:1 indeed has `import {} from "../_coreMetrics.js";` (a no-op). makeInMemoryMetricsService, MetricsServiceNoop, and _coreMetrics.js's smithersMetricCatalogByKey/byPrometheusName/byName have ZERO importers outside the file (the canonical maps live in metrics/smithersMetricCatalog*.js and are the ones consumed). The dead catalog is also STALE/divergent: it has 113 entries vs the canonical metrics/smithersMetricCatalog.js which has ~130 and includes rewind*/snapshotsCaptured/runForksCreated/memoryFactReads/openApiToolCallsTotal/scorersStarted that _coreMetrics.js lacks. Minor inaccuracy: catalog is 113 entries, not 114. Severity P1 stands.

title: Dead in-memory recordEvent handles event types that don't exist in the SmithersEvent union
evidence: The dead makeInMemoryMetricsService.recordEvent switch handles `CacheHit`, `CacheMiss`, and `ApprovalResolved` (lines 414-433) — none of these are members of the SmithersEvent union in index.d.ts (which uses ApprovalGranted/ApprovalDenied/ApprovalAutoApproved). The live router trackEvent.js does not handle CacheHit/CacheMiss/ApprovalResolved at all. This is a stale, divergent second implementation that would mislead anyone reading it as the source of truth for event→metric mapping.
recommendation: Delete along with the rest of the dead in-memory service. If a cache-hit/miss metric path is actually desired, add CacheHit/CacheMiss cases to the live trackEvent.js with a real test.
vnote: Confirmed against SmithersEvent.ts (and index.d.ts): the union has ApprovalRequested/ApprovalGranted/ApprovalAutoApproved/ApprovalDenied but NO CacheHit, CacheMiss, or ApprovalResolved. The dead recordEvent switch in _coreMetrics.js (lines 359-479) handles CacheHit (414), CacheMiss (416), ApprovalResolved (424-433). The live router metrics/trackEvent.js handles none of these three (grep returned nothing). Cache metrics are actually incremented directly in packages/engine/src/engine.js (lines 2996-3037) via Metric.increment(cacheHits/cacheMisses), never through a CacheHit/CacheMiss event — so the dead switch's cache cases are genuinely phantom. P2 appropriate; this is a sub-case of finding #1.

title: trackEvent (608-line event→metric router) tested only for 'does not throw' — no value/label assertions
evidence: Every test calls `await runTrack(event)` with zero `expect()` calls on the resulting metric state. The suite would pass even if every case body were replaced with `return countEvent`. It therefore verifies none of the routing correctness: e.g. that RunStarted increments BOTH runsTotal and activeRuns (trackEvent.js:261-266), that TokenUsageReported applies agent/model tags and the context-window bucket (445-484), or that approvals decrement approvalPending. Additionally the AgentEvent branch (trackEvent.js:485-554, incl. agentSessions/agentErrors/agentRetries/agentTokens and hasAgentRetrySignal) and ALL Sandbox* branches (267-318) are not exercised by any test at all.
recommendation: Use the live MetricsServiceLive snapshot() (or renderPrometheus()) to assert that representative events produce the expected counter/gauge/histogram deltas and labels. At minimum cover: RunStarted/RunFinished active-run balance, TokenUsageReported tagging + bucket classification, ApprovalRequested/Granted/Denied pending balance, and the AgentEvent started/action/completed paths including error/retry detection.
vnote: Read tests/effect-metrics-track.test.js end-to-end: every test calls `await runTrack(event)` with NO expect() on metric state. `expect` is imported on line 1 but never called anywhere in the file (grep confirms). The file's own comment (lines 4-5) admits it only checks that an Effect is produced 'without throwing'. trackEvent.js is 608 lines with dozens of cases; the test has NO AgentEvent, no Sandbox* (SandboxCreated/Completed/Failed/etc.), and no ApprovalAutoApproved cases (grep count = 0 for all). The AgentEvent branch (485-554, incl. agentSessionsTotal/agentErrorsTotal/agentRetriesTotal/agentTokensTotal and hasAgentRetrySignal) and the context-window bucket logic (TokenUsageReported, line 481) are completely unexercised. P1 stands.

title: Correlation module has zero test coverage despite non-trivial normalization/merge logic
evidence: No test file imports correlation.js or anything under _coreCorrelation/ (grep returned nothing). mergeCorrelationContext drops empty strings/non-finite numbers via cleanString/cleanNumber, merges base+patch, and returns undefined when no runId results (line 60) — all untested branches. Also untested: correlationContextToLogAnnotations (only emits set fields), the deprecated in-place mutating updateCurrentCorrelationContext shim (correlation.js:31-44), runWithCorrelationContext/withCorrelationContext AsyncLocalStorage+FiberRef interplay.
recommendation: Add unit tests for mergeCorrelationContext (empty-string drop, non-finite iteration/attempt drop, no-runId→undefined, base override semantics), correlationContextToLogAnnotations (empty context→undefined), and the documented in-place mutation behavior of updateCurrentCorrelationContext.
vnote: No test file in the entire repo imports any correlation symbol (grep for mergeCorrelationContext/correlationContextToLogAnnotations/updateCurrentCorrelationContext/withCorrelationContext/runWithCorrelationContext across *.test.* returned nothing; agentTrace.test.js does not touch it). Verified the cited logic exists and is non-trivial: mergeCorrelationContext.js cleanString/cleanNumber drops empty strings & non-finite numbers (lines 8-17), normalizePatch returns undefined when empty (50), merge returns undefined when no runId (60). correlation.js:31-44 is the deprecated in-place-mutating updateCurrentCorrelationContext shim (Object.assign on the current context). correlationContextToLogAnnotations only emits set fields and returns undefined when empty (line 27). All untested. Title cites _coreCorrelation/mergeCorrelationContext.js (the real impl); correlation.js re-exports it — accurate. P1 stands.

title: Tracing layer (withSmithersSpan / inferSmithersSpanName / annotateSmithersTrace / TracingServiceLive) is untested
evidence: No test imports withSmithersSpan, annotateSmithersTrace, makeSmithersSpanAttributes, or TracingService (grep empty). inferSmithersSpanName (72-92) has 5 distinct classification branches (tool:/toolName, agent:/agent., nodeId→task, runId→run, fallthrough) and makeSmithersSpanAttributes (32-59) has the smithers.-prefix-passthrough + alias-map logic — all unverified. makeSmithersSpanAttributes is also duplicated: an inline copy in _coreTracing.js (33-51) and a standalone src/makeSmithersSpanAttributes.js using _smithersSpanAttributeAliases.js; the alias table is maintained in two places.
recommendation: Add tests asserting span-name inference per branch and attribute aliasing/prefix behavior. Deduplicate the alias map: have _coreTracing.js import _smithersSpanAttributeAliases.js (or makeSmithersSpanAttributes.js) instead of inlining a second copy.
vnote: No test anywhere references withSmithersSpan, annotateSmithersTrace, makeSmithersSpanAttributes, inferSmithersSpanName, or TracingService (grep over tests/ and *.test.* repo-wide returned nothing). Confirmed inferSmithersSpanName (_coreTracing.js:72-92) has the 5 classification branches (smithers./tool/agent/nodeId→task/runId→run/fallthrough) and the makeSmithersSpanAttributes prefix-passthrough + alias logic. Confirmed the DUPLICATION: _coreTracing.js has an inline makeSmithersSpanAttributes (32-59) with an inline alias table (33-51) used by annotateSmithersTrace/withSmithersSpan/TracingServiceLive within that file, while the package's PUBLIC makeSmithersSpanAttributes (exported from index.js:21 → src/makeSmithersSpanAttributes.js) uses the separate _smithersSpanAttributeAliases.js table. Two implementations, two identical alias tables maintained independently. P1 stands.

title: OTLP integration entry points (createSmithersOtelLayer/ObservabilityLayer/RuntimeLayer) have no tests
evidence: Grep for createSmithersOtelLayer/createSmithersObservabilityLayer/createSmithersRuntimeLayer in any test file returns nothing. These compose the actual Otlp.layerJson export, logger selection (resolveLogger json/pretty/string/logfmt at lines 19-31), min-log-level, and tracerContext span storage — the core OTLP shipping path. resolveLogger's four format branches and createSmithersOtelLayer's enabled=false→Layer.empty short-circuit (createSmithersOtelLayer.js:13-15) are untested. apps/cli/tests/observability-package.test.js only checks that the Docker-stack files are listed in package.json, not the runtime layer.
recommendation: Add a layer-construction test: build createSmithersObservabilityLayer({enabled:false}) and assert it provides MetricsService/TracingService/SmithersObservability and that Otlp is absent; build with enabled:true + a throwaway endpoint and assert the layer constructs and resolveLogger picks the right logger per format.

title: _traceEventNormalizers.js: shared/generic normalizer and provider-correlation paths largely untested
evidence: The agentTrace.test.js suite tests normalizeStructuredEvent for pi/codex/claude/gemini/fallback but never the generic genericStructuredEventMap path (tool_call_start/delta/end, message_start, response.started), the delta/thinking/message_end shared branches (normalizeSharedStructuredEvent, 306-348), nor extractProviderSessionCorrelation (384-397, codex thread_id vs session_id/sessionId/pi id). The 'antigravity' family is fully implemented (detectAgentFamily.js:15, agentTraceCapabilities.js:47-59, resolveAgentTraceCapabilities.js:40, _traceEventNormalizers.js:366) but never exercised — the detectAgentFamily test.each list and the resolveAgentTraceCapabilities loop both omit it.
recommendation: Add cases for the shared generic map and delta/message_end branches, extractProviderSessionCorrelation for codex vs others, and add antigravity to the detectAgentFamily and capability tests (incl. the cli-json-stream upgrade path it shares with gemini).

title: OTLP severity edge cases untested: truncated-json-stream WARN and session error/warning inference
evidence: inferCanonicalSeverity downgrades capture.error with payload.reason='truncated-json-stream' to WARN (62-66) — untested; agentTrace.test.js only covers capture.error→ERROR and capture.warning→WARN. inferSessionSeverity (78-92) classifies ERROR via many fields (is_error/isError/error/errorMessage/message.stopReason/message.errorMessage/rowType includes 'error') and WARN via rowType includes 'warning' — agentSessionEventToOtelLogRecord test only asserts the INFO/attribute path, so the entire error/warning inference is uncovered. shouldExportTraceEventToOtel (98-100) is also untested.
recommendation: Add direct tests for inferCanonicalSeverity (truncated-json-stream→WARN), inferSessionSeverity (each ERROR trigger field + warning rowType + default INFO), and shouldExportTraceEventToOtel (artifact.created→false).

title: renderPrometheusMetrics Frequency and Summary metric-state branches untested
evidence: renderPrometheusMetrics handles FrequencyState (172-178) and SummaryState (179-188, emitting min/quantiles/max/sum/count) but the existing prometheus tests only increment counters/histograms/gauges. These two state types and their label/quantile formatting are never rendered in any test, and the catalog-default fill path defaultPrometheusMetricLines (98-115) for defaultLabels (e.g. externalWaitAsyncPending's two default kind labels) is only indirectly touched.
recommendation: Add tests that register an effect Frequency and Summary metric and assert the rendered exposition lines, plus a test that a histogram with defaultLabels renders zero-valued default series before any updates.

title: Redaction 'secret-ish' rule carries a misleading dead replace:'' field
evidence: The secret-ish rule declares `replace: ""` (line 30) but redactValue special-cases this rule id (lines 46-50) to instead emit `match.slice(0, idx+1) + "[REDACTED_SECRET]"`, so the declared replace string is never used. A reader (or a refactor that removes the special-case) would silently change behavior to drop the key prefix and produce an empty replacement.
recommendation: Either give the rule a meaningful replace value and remove the special-case, or document that secret-ish uses bespoke handling and drop the unused replace field, to avoid the dead/contradictory config.

title: Public makeSmithersSpanAttributes and the internal _coreTracing.js copy can silently diverge — only the standalone file uses the shared alias table
evidence: The package exports makeSmithersSpanAttributes from src/makeSmithersSpanAttributes.js (index.js:21), which imports the single-source alias table from _smithersSpanAttributeAliases.js. But _coreTracing.js defines a SECOND makeSmithersSpanAttributes (lines 32-59) with the full alias map hardcoded inline (lines 33-51), and that inline copy — not the public one — is what withSmithersSpan (116), annotateSmithersTrace (98), and TracingServiceLive.annotate (136) actually call. The two alias tables are currently byte-identical, so a future edit to _smithersSpanAttributeAliases.js (e.g. adding a new alias) would update the public function and the published OTel-log path but NOT the actual span attributes emitted by withSmithersSpan/TracingServiceLive, producing inconsistent span vs log attribute keys. _coreTracing.js should import makeSmithersSpanAttributes from ./makeSmithersSpanAttributes.js (or import the alias table) instead of inlining a third copy.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: _coreMetrics.js catalog is reachable as a published deep import via the './*' subpath export, exposing a stale duplicate metric catalog
evidence: package.json declares a wildcard subpath export `"./*": { "import": "./src/*.js" }`, so external consumers can do `import { smithersMetricCatalog } from '@smithers-orchestrator/observability/_coreMetrics'` and get the STALE 113-entry catalog (missing rewind*/snapshots/memoryFactReads/openApiToolCallsTotal/scorersStarted that the canonical metrics/smithersMetricCatalog.js has). No in-repo consumer does this (grep confirms), but the wildcard export means deleting the dead in-memory service is the only way to fully eliminate the divergent second source of truth from the published surface. Reinforces dead-code finding #1: it is dead internally but technically importable, so the catalog divergence is a latent public-API hazard, not just dead code.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: bearerToken.ts is dead code — exported helper imported nowhere
evidence: `bearerToken(request)` is a fully-documented exported function, but `grep -rn 'bearerToken' --include='*.ts' --include='*.tsx'` across the entire repo (excluding its own definition) returns zero hits. Bearer extraction is done inline in authenticateProxyRequest.ts (`auth.startsWith("Bearer ")`) and handleWalkthroughs.ts instead. The function is never called and never re-exported.
recommendation: Delete src/server/bearerToken.ts, or wire it into authenticateProxyRequest/handleWalkthroughs which currently duplicate its logic.
vnote: Verified. apps/review/src/server/bearerToken.ts exports bearerToken(request) but a repo-wide grep (apps/review, packages, apps/cli, workflows, skills, docs, scripts; excluding node_modules) finds zero references to the exact symbol other than its own definition. There is no server/index.ts re-export. The two call sites that need bearer extraction inline it: authenticateProxyRequest.ts:47-48 (auth.startsWith("Bearer ")) and handleWalkthroughs.ts:30 (request.headers.get("authorization")). The packages/server/src/gateway.js `bearerTokenFromHeaders` is an unrelated, separately-named function. Genuinely dead code; P2 correct.

title: CLI --publish defaults to the non-functional review.smithers.sh domain
evidence: `const DEFAULT_PUBLISH_URL = "https://review.smithers.sh";` and loadPublishConfig() falls back to it when neither SMITHERS_REVIEW_PUBLISH_URL nor ~/.smithers-review.json provides a url. But CONTRIBUTING.md:135-136 and README.md:159 state review.smithers.sh is 'not built yet'/'blocked on credentials' and the live service is https://review.jjhub.tech. alchemy.run.ts only serves review.jjhub.tech (the smithers.sh route is gated behind REVIEW_ENABLE_SMITHERS_SH_ROUTE=1). A local user running `--publish` with only a token set (the documented minimum) hits the dead domain and gets a network/404 failure.
recommendation: Default DEFAULT_PUBLISH_URL to https://review.jjhub.tech (the live host) until the smithers.sh domain is provisioned, matching action/action.yml and runAction.ts which already default to jjhub.tech.
vnote: Verified. publishWalkthrough.ts:5 DEFAULT_PUBLISH_URL = 'https://review.smithers.sh' and line 18 falls back to it when no url is supplied. Everywhere else the live host is review.jjhub.tech: action.yml:9 default, runAction.ts:24, alchemy.run.ts:67/69 domains. The smithers.sh worker route is gated behind REVIEW_ENABLE_SMITHERS_SH_ROUTE=1 (alchemy.run.ts:51-52) and CONTRIBUTING.md:135-138 + README.md:159 say smithers.sh is 'blocked on credentials'/'not built yet'. README.md:147-148 documents the minimum --publish setup as just a token (SMITHERS_REVIEW_PUBLISH_TOKEN or ~/.smithers-review.json with only publishToken). With a token and no url, the CLI hits the dead domain. The documented happy path is broken; P1 is justified.

title: GitHub PR-posting path (src/github/) has no automated test coverage
evidence: grep of tests/ for postPullRequestReview/resolvePullRequest/listPullRequestFiles/runGh returns nothing. Only buildPullRequestReview (the pure payload composer) is tested. Untested logic includes the load-bearing 422 fallback in postPullRequestReview.ts:22-42 (folds inline comments into the body when GitHub rejects the batch — the central reliability promise in README.md:16 'a review is always posted'), the owner/repo URL parse + error throw in resolvePullRequest.ts:29-30, and the EPIPE handling in runGh.ts:18-24. This whole flow is only exercised by the production dogfood workflow, never in CI.
recommendation: Add tests injecting a fake `gh` binary (PATH shim or a runGh seam) to cover: successful post, the 422→fold-into-body fallback, the unparseable-PR-url throw, and runGh stderr propagation.
vnote: Verified. tests/ only references buildPullRequestReview (buildPullRequestReview.test.ts). grep across apps/review/tests for postPullRequestReview/resolvePullRequest/listPullRequestFiles/runGh returns nothing. The load-bearing logic exists exactly as cited: the 422 fold-into-body fallback at postPullRequestReview.ts:22-42, the unparseable-PR-url throw at resolvePullRequest.ts:30, and the EPIPE-swallow at runGh.ts:18-24. README.md:16-17 promises 'a review is always posted', which depends on the untested 422 fallback. P1 reasonable.
