title: Walkthrough publish/serve path (/api/walkthroughs + /w/<id>) untested in CI
evidence: The only test touching publishing is tests/publishService.e2e.test.ts, gated on `SMITHERS_REVIEW_E2E === '1'` (describe.skipIf), so it never runs in CI. handleWalkthroughs has no worker-level test: untested branches include the legacy REVIEW_PUBLISH_TOKEN bearer path (line 31), the session/api-key fallback auth (line 33), empty-body 400 (line 37), the 25MB 413 cap (line 38), and the R2 store + 201 response. The GET /w/<id> R2 retrieval + 404 in worker.ts:64-75 is also untested. memoryBucket() helper exists for exactly this but is only used by buildTestEnv, never asserted against.
recommendation: Add a worker.fetch test posting to /api/walkthroughs with each credential type, asserting it stores into the memoryBucket and that GET /w/<id> serves the bytes back with the immutable+noindex headers (and 404 for a missing id).
vnote: Verified. The only publish test, tests/publishService.e2e.test.ts, is gated by describe.skipIf(!enabled) where enabled requires SMITHERS_REVIEW_E2E === '1' plus live credentials, so it never runs in CI. No server test posts to /api/walkthroughs or GETs /w/<id>: grep of tests/server for walkthrough//w//_store finds only the memoryBucket helper. handleWalkthroughs.ts has the untested branches as cited (legacy token line 31, session/api-key fallback line 33-34, empty 400 line 37, 413 cap line 38, R2 put + 201 line 40-44) and the GET /w/<id> R2 retrieval + 404 is at worker.ts:64-75. memoryBucket()._store is wired into buildTestEnv.ts:18 but never asserted (the helper comment even says 'used by walkthrough tests' though no such test exists). P1 reasonable.

title: Non-streaming JSON metering and srk_ api-key proxy branches untested
evidence: handleAnthropic.test.ts covers only: outside-/v1 404, unknown-session 401, SSE metering, and spend-cap 402. The non-streaming branch (contentType not text/event-stream → parseUsageFromJson → recordUsage) is never asserted end-to-end; the api-key (srk_) proxy auth branch (authenticateProxyRequest.ts:71-75, repo=auth.repos[0], pr=0) is never hit; the upstream-fetch 502 catch (handleAnthropic.ts:116-118) and the no-body upstream branch (line 120-126) are untested. parseUsageFromJson.ts, parseUsageFromSse.ts, recordUsage.ts, and modelPrices.ts have no direct unit tests at all (modelPrices' prefix-match loop and unknown-model $0 fallback, and parseUsageFromJson's null-on-no-model path, are entirely unexercised).
recommendation: Add direct unit tests for parseUsageFromJson/parseUsageFromSse (incl. malformed/absent frames → null), modelPrices (exact, prefix, unknown), and recordUsage cost math + session decrement; add a handleAnthropic JSON-metering and srk_ api-key proxy test.

title: SSE metering silently under-bills responses larger than 1MB
evidence: teeForMetering accumulates the upstream copy with `const cap = 1<<20; ... if (acc.length >= cap) continue;` — once 1MB of SSE bytes is seen, all later chunks are skipped. But for Anthropic streaming the final `output_tokens` arrives in the LAST `event: message_delta` frame (parseUsageFromSse.ts:42-47 keeps the latest value seen); message_start carries output_tokens≈1. A review/narrate agent emitting >1MB of streamed SSE (large structured output is plausible) will have its closing message_delta dropped, so recordUsage charges ~1 output token instead of the real count — under-metering and under-decrementing the session spend cap.
recommendation: Either raise/remove the cap for the metering copy (it is text, bounded by the model's max output) or parse incrementally and retain only the last message_delta usage rather than truncating the byte buffer.
vnote: Verified. teeForMetering at handleAnthropic.ts:43-51 sets cap = 1<<20 and skips appending once acc.length >= cap (line 49 'if (acc.length >= cap) continue;'). parseUsageFromSse.ts:42-47 keeps the LATEST output_tokens from message_delta frames, and the docstring at lines 3-6 confirms message_start carries prompt-side usage while message_delta carries the running output_tokens. So for a streamed response whose SSE bytes exceed 1MB, the closing message_delta is dropped and only the message_start output_tokens (~1) is recorded — under-billing and under-decrementing the cap. No test covers the >1MB truncation case (handleAnthropic.test.ts only has a small-stream SSE metering test). Mechanism is real; the >1MB single-response trigger is plausible but not the common case, so P2 is appropriate.

title: Session spend-cap enforcement races under concurrent requests
evidence: The cap is checked synchronously before forwarding (`if (spentUsd >= spendCapUsd) return 402`) but the spend increment (recordUsage → UPDATE sessions SET spent_usd) runs in deps.waitUntil(metering) AFTER the response streams. The review workflow runs file-review agents in parallel (createReviewWorkflow.tsx:107 Parallel maxConcurrency=input.concurrency, default 8), so up to 8 concurrent proxy calls all read the same pre-increment spent_usd and pass the gate before any meter write lands. The cap is a soft 'runaway brake' (acknowledged in mintSession.ts comments) but the unbounded concurrency window is undocumented and untested.
recommendation: Document the soft-cap semantics at the check site, and/or reserve an estimated cost synchronously (atomic UPDATE ... WHERE spent_usd < cap) before forwarding so concurrent requests cannot all pass a stale read.
vnote: Mechanism confirmed, one citation wrong. The cap is checked synchronously pre-forward (handleAnthropic.ts:91-92) against spentUsd read FRESH from D1 per request (authenticateProxyRequest.ts:55 'SELECT ... spent_usd'), while recordUsage runs in deps.waitUntil(metering) AFTER streaming (handleAnthropic.ts:131-146). The workflow runs up to 8 file-review agents in parallel (createReviewWorkflow.tsx:107 Parallel maxConcurrency={input.concurrency}, default 8 per parseReviewArgs.ts:33), so concurrent proxy calls all read the same pre-increment spent_usd and pass the gate before any meter write lands. The race is real and untested. INACCURACY: the finding attributes the soft-cap/'runaway brake' comment to mintSession.ts, but mintSession.ts has no such comment — the 'runaway brake' wording actually lives in modelPrices.ts:3-4. The substance (it is a deliberate soft cap) still holds. Keeping P2; only the comment-location citation needs correcting.

title: CLI entrypoint and GitHub Action drivers have no tests
file: apps/review/src/cli/main.ts, apps/review/src/cli/parseReviewArgs.ts, apps/review/action/src/runAction.ts, runGate.ts, runReview.ts, fetchOidcToken.ts
evidence: tests/ references createSession, gateEvent, and resolveInferenceEnv from action/src, but runAction.ts (the composite-step orchestrator: re-gate → mint → mode-skip → checkout → spawn), runGate.ts, runReview.ts (spawn-with-proxy-env), and fetchOidcToken.ts are untested. On the CLI side parseReviewArgs.ts (option parsing, the `--concurrency must be a positive number` / `--timeout` validation at lines 67-68, unknown-option throw at line 64) and main.ts (PR fetch fallback, parseFindings JSON guard, publish/PR error aggregation into the exit code at line 180) have no tests despite being pure and trivially testable.
recommendation: Unit-test parseReviewArgs (all flags, value-required errors, numeric validation, unknown option). Add a fetchOidcToken test with an injected fetch (it already accepts fetchImpl) covering missing-env throw, non-ok throw, and missing-value throw. Consider a runAction skip-path test.

title: Several pure walkthrough helpers lack direct unit tests
file: apps/review/src/walkthrough/classifyChangeRole.ts, buildNarratePrompt.ts, describeChange.ts, escapeHtml.ts, src/diffs/renderFallbackDiffHtml.ts, src/workflow/normalizeReviewInput.ts, writeOpenAiSchemaFile.ts
evidence: No test file imports classifyChangeRole, buildNarratePrompt, normalizeReviewInput, escapeHtml, or renderFallbackDiffHtml. classifyChangeRole.ts has dense regex branches (test/docs/config/code classification, lines 1-17) that fallbackStory depends on but only exercises a few paths. renderFallbackDiffHtml.ts has nontrivial logic — hunk parsing, MAX_RENDERED_LINES=1500 truncation (lines 35,63-66), skippedPrefixes, preamble skipping — only reached via the binary/empty fallback assertion in renderWalkthroughHtml.test.ts, never with an actual large/edge diff. writeOpenAiSchemaFile.ts (sanitizeForOpenAI strips default/$schema/bounds, forces additionalProperties:false + required) is security/correctness-sensitive for codex output and is wholly untested.
recommendation: Add focused unit tests for classifyChangeRole (one per role + edge names like .github/, *.config.ts), renderFallbackDiffHtml (multi-hunk, truncation boundary, rename/binary headers), normalizeReviewInput (null-stripping), and writeOpenAiSchemaFile/sanitizeForOpenAI (asserts the produced JSON schema is strict).

title: verifyOidc accepts a single JWKS key when the token's kid does not match
evidence: `const match = keys.find((k) => k.kid === header.kid) ?? (keys.length === 1 ? keys[0] : undefined);` — when the header kid matches no published key but the JWKS happens to contain exactly one key, that key is used regardless of kid. handleSessions.test.ts covers wrong-key/wrong-aud/expired but not the unknown-kid-with-single-key fallback, so this lenient branch is both unverified and a slight deviation from strict kid matching against GitHub's (multi-key, rotating) JWKS.
recommendation: Either drop the single-key fallback (require an exact kid match against GitHub's JWKS) or add an explicit test documenting and pinning the intended behavior.

title: /api/admin/usage endpoint untested
evidence: handleAdmin.test.ts covers /api/admin/repos upsert+list, /api/admin/keys mint, and the 401 path, but never hits /api/admin/usage. Its daily-grouping SQL (strftime over created_at/1000), 401 auth, and 405 non-GET branches are unexercised.
recommendation: Add a handleAdminUsage test: 401 without token, 405 on POST, and a seeded usage_events row asserting the daily {day,repo,model,inputTokens,...} shape.

title: watch-and-steer.mdx documents out-of-scope product UIs (Studio 2 + PWA web app) in the For Humans guide
evidence: Lines 36-62 are a section titled 'Studio: the visual console' describing 'Studio is the Smithers operations console', its home surface, Runs surface, Cmd-K palette, and embedded terminal, with five images under docs/images/studio-2/ (home.png, runs-live.gif, command-palette.gif, workspace-terminal.png). Line 38 even tells the reader 'In this repo, `pnpm dev:studio` starts the Studio 2 dev stack' — and package.json:65 shows `dev:studio` runs `bun apps/smithers-studio-2/scripts/dev.ts`, the retired POC the project CLAUDE.md says is out of the stack. Lines 69-78 add 'A browser and remote view' describing 'The installable Smithers web app gives you that browser-native surface: open it, install it like any PWA' with image /images/0.23.0/smithers-pwa.png. The maintainer requirement is explicit: docs must NOT describe a product UI / web app / studio (the main UI is a separate repo), only custom UIs built with `smithers ui`.
recommendation: Remove the 'Studio: the visual console' and 'A browser and remote view' (PWA) sections from watch-and-steer.mdx. Reframe the page entirely around the CLI watch loop (ps, chat --follow, inspect --watch) and, if a visual surface is mentioned at all, only the in-scope `smithers ui` custom workflow UIs. Delete the leaked `pnpm dev:studio` internal dev instruction from human docs.
vnote: Factually confirmed. watch-and-steer.mdx lines 36-62 are a 'Studio: the visual console' section describing Studio as 'the Smithers operations console' with home/Runs/Cmd-K/embedded-terminal and five images under /images/studio-2/ (home.png, runs-live.gif, command-palette.gif, workspace-terminal.png all present on disk). Line 38 says 'In this repo, `pnpm dev:studio` starts the Studio 2 dev stack'; package.json:65 confirms `dev:studio` -> `bun apps/smithers-studio-2/scripts/dev.ts`. Lines 68-76 add 'A browser and remote view' marketing 'The installable Smithers web app ... install it like any PWA' with image /images/0.23.0/smithers-pwa.png (present). Project CLAUDE.md lines 31-35 explicitly: apps/smithers (chat-first PWA POC) and apps/smithers-studio-2 (studio shell POC) are 'NOT the product. Do not ... cite their tech or patterns as current'; lines 28-29: docs saying 'the UI' must mean the `smithers ui` surface 'not a product web app'. So this is a genuine docs-policy violation in published human docs. I lowered severity from P0 to P1: this is a documentation accuracy/scope issue (no build break, no runtime bug, the doc still renders), which does not meet the P0 bar reserved for breakage/correctness failures. Note: the watch-and-steer prose itself is not duplicated into llms-full/llms-core (those bundles reference studio-2 only via package/scripts tables), so blast radius is the one human-facing page.

title: watch-and-steer.mdx contradicts the docs' own 'not a GUI you click / no GUI required' thesis
evidence: README.md:48 states 'Smithers is driven by your coding agent, **not** a GUI you click', and what-is-smithers.mdx:29 says 'you don't click a GUI'. watch-and-steer.mdx:65 itself says 'no GUI required'. Yet the same page (lines 36-78) markets a clickable visual console (Studio) and an installable PWA web app as primary ways to watch/steer runs. A human reading the For Humans tab top-to-bottom gets a direct contradiction within one guide.
recommendation: Resolve by removing the Studio/PWA sections (see P0). The page's existing CLI-driven 'observe loop' and 'steering' tables are consistent with the thesis and should stand on their own.
vnote: Confirmed. README.md:48 'Smithers is driven by your coding agent, **not** a GUI you click'; docs/guide/what-is-smithers.mdx:29 'you don't click a GUI, and you don't write code'; watch-and-steer.mdx:65 itself says 'no GUI required'. Yet the same page (lines 36-78) markets a clickable Studio console and an installable PWA web app as ways to watch/steer. Real internal contradiction within the For Humans guide. (Note the auditor cited what-is-smithers.mdx:29 with the wrong path prefix - the file is at docs/guide/what-is-smithers.mdx, and the quote is exact at line 29.) This is the same lines/root-cause as Finding 1 and resolves with the same edit, but it is a valid distinct observation; P1 stands.

title: starters.mdx documents a non-existent `idea-to-prd` starter template
evidence: starters.mdx:26 says 'Pass any ID from this table to `init --template <id>`', and line 30 lists `idea-to-prd` (workflow `write-a-prd`) with command `bunx smithers-orchestrator init --template idea-to-prd`. But apps/cli/src/starter-gallery.js defines only nine starter IDs (idea-to-tickets, launch-checklist, customer-incident, nontechnical-research, requirements-interview, quality-audit, test-coverage, ship-a-change, mission-mode); `grep -rn idea-to-prd apps/cli/src` returns nothing, and there is no `write-a-prd` starter recipe. The other nine documented IDs all resolve. Running `init --template idea-to-prd` would fail to find the recipe.
recommendation: Either remove the `idea-to-prd` row from starters.mdx, or (if a PRD starter is intended) register an `idea-to-prd`/`write-a-prd` recipe in starter-gallery.js. Note the write-a-prd.tsx workflow and docs/images/workflow-ui/write-a-prd.png already exist, so the workflow is real but not wired as a starter.
vnote: Core finding confirmed and runtime-verified. starters.mdx:30 lists `idea-to-prd` (workflow `write-a-prd`) with `init --template idea-to-prd`, and line 26 promises 'Pass any ID from this table to `init --template <id>` to scaffold that starter.' starter-gallery.js defines exactly 9 IDs (idea-to-tickets, launch-checklist, customer-incident, nontechnical-research, requirements-interview, quality-audit, test-coverage, ship-a-change, mission-mode) plus aliases; `idea-to-prd` is neither an id nor an alias. I ran the module: findStarterRecipe('idea-to-prd') returns undefined while findStarterRecipe('idea-to-tickets') resolves. init-command.js:71-90 shows the consequence: an unknown template ID makes selectedTemplate undefined, so result.template is never set and the run SILENTLY ignores the requested starter (no error, no guided next steps) - so the impact is 'silently no-ops' rather than the auditor's 'would fail to find the recipe' (close enough; init itself does not crash). The bad row is also propagated into docs/llms-full.txt:579, docs/llms-core.txt:556, and apps/cli/docs/llms-full.txt:579, and the 0.22.0 changelog (docs/changelogs/0.22.0.mdx:128) lists idea-to-prd among 'Ten canonical starters' though only 9 exist. IMPORTANT CORRECTION to the auditor's recommendation note: it claims 'the write-a-prd.tsx workflow ... already exist' - that is FALSE. There is NO write-a-prd.tsx (or any *prd* workflow .tsx) anywhere in the repo; only docs/images/workflow-ui/write-a-prd.png exists. So registering an idea-to-prd recipe is not just a wiring change - the underlying workflow does not exist. Kept at P1 (doc promises a starter that does nothing).

title: README hero GIF alt text still references the out-of-scope 'Smithers Studio'
evidence: Line 14 alt text reads 'Live runs in Smithers Studio: some succeeded, some running, some paused on an approval gate...'. README.md:48 elsewhere insists Smithers is 'not a GUI you click'. Per the maintainer rule, human-facing docs should not reference a product UI / studio. The image itself (marketing/0.22.0/assets/runs-live.gif) exists, but the 'Smithers Studio' branding in the alt text names an out-of-scope/retired surface.
recommendation: Reword the alt text to describe the runs without invoking 'Studio' (e.g. 'Live Smithers runs: some succeeded, some running, some paused on an approval gate, every run resumable and rewindable'), consistent with the rest of the README's no-GUI framing.

title: tui.mdx points humans at the `gui` command, which launches a retired native app bundle
evidence: tui.mdx:20 lists 'Desktop control plane | `gui`' as a replacement for the removed TUI. The `gui` command (apps/cli/src/index.js:5721-5748) runs `open -b com.smithers.SmithersGUI <dir>`, opening the ~/gui Swift/AppKit app — which the project CLAUDE.md / audit scope describes as a retired POC not in the stack and not built/published. Pointing a human at it as a 'Desktop control plane' offers a path that won't work on a normal install (no such app bundle installed).
recommendation: Drop the 'Desktop control plane | gui' row from tui.mdx, or gate it clearly as experimental/unshipped. Direct desktop-watching users to the CLI watch commands (ps --watch, inspect --watch) that the rest of the table already covers.

title: custom-workflow-ui.mdx couples the in-scope `smithers ui` guide to out-of-scope product surfaces (apps/smithers PWA, Studio 2)
evidence: This guide is legitimately about the in-scope `smithers ui` / Gateway custom-UI system, but its prose repeatedly names out-of-scope hosts: frontmatter description (line 3) 'ship it through the Smithers Gateway, the apps/smithers PWA'; line 6 'every Smithers surface ... (the Smithers PWA, Studio 2's Runs surface, ...)'; line 8 references 'Smithers Cloud or behind Plue'; line 28 'apps/smithers mounts it as <iframe...>. Studio 2's Runs surface and the `bunx smithers-orchestrator ui` command embed the same URL.' apps/smithers and apps/smithers-studio-2 are out-of-scope per the maintainer (main UI is a separate repo; studio-2 is retired).
recommendation: Describe the custom-UI embedding story in terms of the in-repo surfaces only: the Gateway-served /workflows/<key> page and the `bunx smithers-orchestrator ui` CLI. Drop or genericize the apps/smithers PWA and 'Studio 2's Runs surface' name-drops so this guide does not advertise out-of-scope product UIs as hosts.

title: llms-full.txt, llms-core.txt, and apps/cli/docs/llms-full.txt carry the same broken `idea-to-prd` starter row
evidence: The non-existent `idea-to-prd` starter (workflow `write-a-prd`) is duplicated into the generated LLM doc bundles: docs/llms-full.txt:579, docs/llms-core.txt:556, and apps/cli/docs/llms-full.txt:579 all contain the row '| `idea-to-prd` | founders, product | `write-a-prd` | `bunx smithers-orchestrator init --template idea-to-prd` |'. So an LLM reading the shipped docs bundle (the primary consumer of these files) will recommend a starter ID that findStarterRecipe() resolves to undefined and that has no backing workflow. Regenerating the bundles after fixing starters.mdx (pnpm docs:llms) is required, not just editing the .mdx.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: 0.22.0 changelog claims 'Ten canonical starters' but only nine are defined
evidence: docs/changelogs/0.22.0.mdx:128 states 'Ten canonical starters map plain outcomes (`idea-to-prd`, `launch-checklist`, `customer-incident`, `quality-audit`, `ship-a-change`, `mission-mode`, and more) onto the underlying seeded workflows.' starter-gallery.js defines exactly nine recipes (grep 'id:' yields idea-to-tickets, launch-checklist, customer-incident, nontechnical-research, requirements-interview, quality-audit, test-coverage, ship-a-change, mission-mode) and the example list leads with `idea-to-prd`, which is the one that does not exist. The count (ten) and the lead example (idea-to-prd) are both wrong; changelogs are historical so this is lower severity, but it is the origin of the idea-to-prd documentation drift.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Stale Claude Sonnet model ID (claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929) in 7 For-Agents example pages
evidence: docs/examples/{approval-gate,claude-plugin-orchestrator,dynamic-plan,loop,multi-agent-review,tools-agent,workflow-quickstart}.mdx all hardcode model: anthropic("claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929") (15 occurrences total). This is the Sonnet 4.5 (Sep 2025) ID. The current Sonnet is claude-sonnet-4-6, and the CLI scaffolder itself emits the current ID: apps/cli/src/agent-detection.js:145 -> new SmithersClaudeCodeAgent({ model: "claude-sonnet-4-6", ... }). Every other doc (tour.mdx, components/*, integrations/*, concepts/openapi-tools.mdx, examples/hello-world.mdx) uses the current claude-fable-5 / claude-opus-4-8. These example pages are in the 'For Agents' tab navigation (docs.json line 278+ 'Examples' group), so agents copy-pasting them get a stale model.
recommendation: Replace claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929 with the current claude-sonnet-4-6 (or claude-fable-5 to match the rest of the docs) across the 7 example MDX files so example code matches the CLI scaffolder and the rest of the documentation.
file: docs/examples/approval-gate.mdx:41,47 (and dynamic-plan, loop, multi-agent-review, tools-agent, workflow-quickstart, claude-plugin-orchestrator)
vnote: Independently reproduced all 15 occurrences of anthropic("claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929") at exactly the cited line numbers across docs/examples/{approval-gate(41,47),tools-agent(36),multi-agent-review(32,38,44),dynamic-plan(34,40,45),loop(36,42),workflow-quickstart(38,45),claude-plugin-orchestrator(49,54)}.mdx. Confirmed apps/cli/src/agent-detection.js emits current IDs: line 145 claude-sonnet-4-6, line 181 claude-fable-5, line 136 claude-opus-4-8, lines 535-536 default claude-fable-5. The claude-api skill catalog confirms claude-sonnet-4-6 is the current Sonnet and claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929 is the older (legacy/active) Sonnet 4.5 ID. Verified docs.json: the Examples group (line 278) is under the "For Agents" tab (line 41), so agents do copy these. Two caveats that slightly narrow scope but don't refute: (1) the supporting claim 'every other doc uses the current model' is overstated — docs/why/background-agents.mdx:28 uses the legacy claude-opus-4-5; (2) the 7 example pages are NOT bundled into llms-full/core/integrations.txt (scripts/generate-llms.ts lists no examples/ entries and grep finds zero stale-ID leaks in the bundles), so the defect affects the rendered docs site only, not the llms.txt bundles. P1 retained: agent-facing copy-pasteable example code diverging from the scaffolder is a real correctness issue.

title: Stale Opus model ID in OpenCodeAgent JSDoc example (claude-opus-4-20250514)
evidence: packages/agents/src/OpenCodeAgent.ts:6 and packages/agents/src/index.d.ts:278 carry the JSDoc /** Model identifier (e.g., "anthropic/claude-opus-4-20250514", "openai/gpt-5.4") */. claude-opus-4-20250514 is the original Opus 4 (May 2025) ID; current Opus is claude-opus-4-8. This JSDoc surfaces in IDE hovers and any generated type docs an agent reads.
recommendation: Update the OpenCodeAgent model JSDoc example to anthropic/claude-opus-4-8 (and a current OpenAI id). Note this is a source comment, not a docs/ file, but it teaches a stale ID to agents reading the type surface.

title: reference/types.mdx SmithersWorkflow omits the zodToKeyName field present in actual WorkflowDefinition
evidence: docs/reference/types.mdx documents interface SmithersWorkflow with { readableName, description, db, build, opts, schemaRegistry }. The actual type packages/driver/src/index.d.ts:289 WorkflowDefinition$1 additionally has zodToKeyName?: Map<z.ZodObject<z.ZodRawShape>, string> and types build as => WorkflowElement (the doc says => JSX.Element). Minor drift in an otherwise hand-curated 52KB type reference.
recommendation: Add the zodToKeyName?: Map<...> field (or mark it internal) to the documented SmithersWorkflow shape to keep types.mdx a faithful mirror of the emitted .d.ts.

title: Orphan jsx stub pages not in any navigation (jsx/installation.mdx, jsx/quickstart.mdx)
evidence: docs/jsx/installation.mdx and docs/jsx/quickstart.mdx are 6-line 'folded into X' stubs not listed in docs.json (only jsx/overview is in the For Agents nav), and docs.json has redirects /jsx/installation -> /jsx/overview and /jsx/quickstart -> /jsx/overview that route away from them. They are reachable only by direct file URL. They are not included in any llms bundle manifest (scripts/generate-llms.ts only lists jsx/overview.mdx).
recommendation: Either delete the two stub files (the redirects already cover the old URLs) or leave as-is intentionally; flag is low severity because they are harmless redirect placeholders.
vnote: Confirmed both files exist as 5-6 line 'folded into X' stubs, are absent from the docs.json nav (only jsx/overview at line 74), have redirects /jsx/installation->/jsx/overview (line 448) and /jsx/quickstart->/jsx/overview (line 449), and that scripts/generate-llms.ts lists only jsx/overview.mdx (line 46) not the stubs. Adjustment: the auditor's claim that they are 'reachable only by direct file URL' is incomplete — docs/reference/package-configuration.mdx:119 contains a live internal link [JSX Installation](/jsx/installation), so the /jsx/installation path IS referenced in-product (the redirect makes that link resolve to overview). This is not pure dead code: deleting the stub without the redirect would break a 200 path target for an existing internal link. This actually reinforces the auditor's own 'leave as-is intentionally' recommendation. P2 is correct; the dead-code framing is weaker than stated because of the inbound link + redirect coverage.

title: docs/why/background-agents.mdx uses non-current model claude-opus-4-5
evidence: docs/why/background-agents.mdx:28 has `const analyst = new AnthropicAgent({ model: "claude-opus-4-5" });`. claude-opus-4-5 is a legacy (still active) Opus ID, not the current claude-opus-4-8 used elsewhere in the docs (tour.mdx, agents/claude-code.mdx, integrations/*). Surfaced while verifying finding 1's claim that 'every other doc uses the current model' — that claim is slightly inaccurate. Minor: this is a non-changelog narrative page, low blast radius, and the model is not retired.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: 13 canonical init workflows ship with no custom UI (violates the 'every built-in workflow has a UI' bar)
file: apps/cli/src/workflow-pack.js:1651 (UI_WORKFLOWS) vs the 30 workflows emitted by renderTemplateFiles
evidence: Running initWorkflowPack({skipInstall:true}) into a temp dir emits 30 workflows but only 17 ui/*.tsx files. UI_WORKFLOWS (workflow-pack.js:1651-1669) lists exactly 17 keys, and gateway.ts mounts those same 17. The following installed workflows have NO .smithers/ui/<id>.tsx: backpressure-plan, context-doctor, context-engineer, create-skill, create-workflow, eval-author, extract-skill, monitor, monitor-smithers, report-slideshow, route-task, smithering, triage-run. All 13 carry real smithers-display-name/description metadata (e.g. 'Monitor', 'Smithering', 'Context Engineer') and are full canonical workflows, not scratch.
recommendation: Author a custom UI (WORKFLOW_UI_SOURCES entry + UI_WORKFLOWS + gateway mount) for each of the 13, prioritizing the operator/flagship ones (monitor, monitor-smithers, smithering, context-engineer, triage-run). Add a generation-time assertion that every emitted workflow id has a matching ui/<id>.tsx (or an explicit allowlist of intentionally UI-less workflows) so the gap can't silently grow.
vnote: Facts confirmed by running initWorkflowPack into /tmp: exactly 30 workflows are emitted (renderWorkflows + GENERATED_SEEDED_FILES) and 17 ui/*.tsx files. UI_WORKFLOWS at apps/cli/src/workflow-pack.js:1651-1668 lists 17 keys; gateway.ts emits exactly those 17 mountWorkflow() calls. The 13 missing-UI workflows match the finding precisely (backpressure-plan, context-doctor, context-engineer, create-skill, create-workflow, eval-author, extract-skill, monitor, monitor-smithers, report-slideshow, route-task, smithering, triage-run), and all 13 carry real `// smithers-display-name:` + `// smithers-source: seeded` metadata. HOWEVER the P1 'violates a bar' framing is overstated: (1) no test or generation-time assertion enforces 'every workflow has a UI' — workflow-ui-all.e2e.test.js (descriptor count 16) only tests workflows that already HAVE a UI and never asserts coverage of all 30; (2) the product explicitly supports UI-less workflows as a first-class, graceful state — apps/cli/src/index.js:2859 ('a workflow with no UI just...') and :5837-5838 return a clean NO_UI result, and the studio falls back to the generic tree/inspector (docs/changelogs/0.22.0.mdx:56); (3) several of the 13 produce their own HTML deliverable (monitor.tsx writes a .html report at line 363-364; report-slideshow.tsx generates a self-contained HTML slideshow) so a React gateway UI is redundant, and the rest are one-shot meta/authoring workflows (create-skill, eval-author, context-doctor, etc.) where the generic inspector is adequate. Real coverage gap worth a tracked allowlist/assertion, but P2 not P1, and the blanket 'author a UI for each of the 13' recommendation does not fit the HTML-report and one-shot meta workflows.

title: Workflow-to-UI binding is implicit filename convention with no missing-UI signal
evidence: A workflow .tsx never references its UI. Wiring happens in two places that derive the path from the id: `smithers up`/gateway auto-mount at index.js:1990 does `const uiEntry = resolve(workspace, '.smithers','ui', ${discovered.id}.tsx); if (existsSync(uiEntry)) gateway.register(id, wf, {ui:{entry:uiEntry,...}}); else gateway.register(id, wf)` — when the UI file is absent it silently registers with no UI and no log. The generated gateway.ts independently hardcodes a UI_WORKFLOWS list and warns only on import failure, not on a workflow that has no UI. The UI file additionally hardcodes its own `const WORKFLOW_KEY = "<id>"` (e.g. ui/implement.tsx:11) that must match the filename. So correctness depends on filename, UI_WORKFLOWS, gateway mount, and the in-file WORKFLOW_KEY all agreeing, with nothing enforcing it.
recommendation: Document the ui/<id>.tsx convention in docs/workflows/overview.mdx and the workflow-authoring docs, and emit an info log from the auto-mount path when a workflow has no UI so authors notice. Consider a single source of truth (derive WORKFLOW_KEY from the filename, or assert UI_WORKFLOWS == set(ui/*.tsx)).

title: docs/workflows/overview.mdx 'Default Workflows' table lists only 16 of 30 installed workflows
evidence: The overview 'Workflows' table links 16 ids (implement, research-plan-implement, mission, kanban, review, plan, research, debug, improve-test-coverage, ticket-create, tickets-create, grill-me, feature-enum, audit, ralph, workflow-skill). init actually installs 30. The 14 omitted from the landing page are: backpressure-plan, context-doctor, context-engineer, create-skill, create-workflow, eval-author, extract-skill, monitor, monitor-smithers, report-slideshow, route-task, smithering, triage-run, vcs. Each has a real docs/workflows/<id>.mdx, so the pages exist but are undiscoverable from the canonical index.
recommendation: Add the 14 missing workflows to the overview.mdx table (grouped if needed). Since all 30 have doc files, this is purely a discoverability/index gap.
vnote: Core facts confirmed and slightly understated. The overview.mdx table (lines 18-35) links exactly 16 ids; init emits 30; the 14 omitted match the finding exactly (backpressure-plan, context-doctor, context-engineer, create-skill, create-workflow, eval-author, extract-skill, monitor, monitor-smithers, report-slideshow, route-task, smithering, triage-run, vcs) and each has a real docs/workflows/<id>.mdx. The gap is actually worse than stated: docs/workflows/catalog.mdx (the other index page) also mentions only 17 of 30 and omits 13 of the same workflows. HOWEVER the 'undiscoverable from the canonical index' severity is mitigated: 29 of 30 workflow pages ARE listed in the docs.json left-nav sidebar (the primary site navigation), so users can reach them. This is a real index-completeness/discoverability gap on the landing tables, but P2 not P1 since the pages are navigable. (One genuine orphan surfaced separately — see new findings.)

title: docs/workflows/monitor.mdx is orphaned — not in docs.json sidebar nav, not linked anywhere
evidence: docs.json's workflows nav lists 29 slugs including monitor-smithers but NOT bare 'workflows/monitor' (grep -c '"workflows/monitor"' docs/docs.json == 0). monitor.mdx (7.3KB, the largest workflow doc) is referenced only by docs.json's monitor-smithers entry and itself — no other mdx links it. It is the one doc file present on disk but absent from the published sidebar, making the largest workflow doc unreachable.
recommendation: Add 'workflows/monitor' to the docs/docs.json workflows nav group so the page ships.

title: kanban UI ships and is mounted but is not functionally covered by the all-UI e2e
evidence: workflow-ui-descriptors.json contains 16 entries, but the shipped pack has 17 mounted UIs. The missing one is kanban: gateway.ts mounts kanban and ui/kanban.tsx ships, yet workflow-ui-all.e2e.test.js (which boots the gateway and asserts each descriptor's UI loads/serves) never visits /workflows/kanban. The screenshot helper _capture-workflow-ui-screenshots.test.js explicitly works around this at line 118 ('Also capture kanban (its own UI, not in descriptors)') but only takes a screenshot — it does not assert boot/output. So kanban's UI has no functional e2e assertion.
recommendation: Add a kanban descriptor (boot-level, verifyOutput false) to workflow-ui-descriptors.json so the all-UI e2e asserts it loads, matching the other 16 mounted UIs.

title: docs/workflows/catalog.mdx pack tables are stale and inconsistent ('install all three' / 4 packs / 16 of 30 workflows)
evidence: Line 6 says 'Choose the pack ... or install all three' but the table (lines 12-15) defines FOUR packs (Build, Plan, Quality, Meta). The four packs collectively reference only 16 workflow ids, while init ships 30 from a single .smithers pack (init has no per-pack selection — there is one canonical pack). The 'install all three' wording and the pack partitioning do not reflect the actual single-pack, 30-workflow install.
recommendation: Reword to reflect the single canonical .smithers pack, fix the 'all three' count, and either drop the pack partitioning or extend it to cover all 30 installed workflows.

title: No test guards UI_WORKFLOWS / gateway-mounts / ui-files / e2e-descriptors against drift
evidence: init.e2e.test.js asserts a handful of individual files exist (e.g. .smithers/ui/kanban.tsx at line 204) but nothing asserts the set equality: that every UI_WORKFLOWS key emits a ui/<key>.tsx, that gateway.ts mounts exactly the UI files present, or that the e2e descriptor set matches the mounted UIs. The kanban-missing-from-descriptors and the 17-mounts-vs-16-descriptors mismatches above are exactly the drift such a test would catch.
recommendation: Add a unit/e2e invariant: set(UI_WORKFLOWS keys) == set(ui/*.tsx basenames) == set(gateway.ts mountWorkflow keys) == set(e2e descriptor keys, allowing kanban as known special-case), so any future UI addition/removal keeps all four surfaces in lockstep.

title: docs/workflows/monitor.mdx is an orphan page — linked from no index and absent from docs.json nav
evidence: docs/workflows/monitor.mdx exists and documents the installed `monitor` workflow, but it is the ONLY one of the 30 workflow pages absent from the docs.json navigation (29/30 are present; grep 'workflows/monitor' in docs.json returns only the 'workflows/monitor-smithers' line at 140). It is also missing from both index pages (overview.mdx table and catalog.mdx). So unlike the other 13 UI-less workflows whose pages are still reachable via the nav sidebar, monitor.mdx is reachable from nowhere in the published docs — a true orphan page that will not render in the site nav.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: workflow-ui-all.e2e.test.js docstring says 'ALL 15 UIs' but the harness covers 16 descriptors
evidence: The test docstring claims 'ALL 15 UIs: build + boot + mount in the browser', but workflow-ui-descriptors.json contains 16 entries and the gateway mounts 17 UIs total (kanban tested via its own helper). The companion changelog (docs/changelogs/0.22.0.mdx:112) already says 'all 16 UIs', so the in-test count is stale. Minor doc-accuracy drift inside the test comment.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Declarative TanStack-DB sync hooks have no real-browser e2e; no shipped UI exercises them
file: packages/gateway-react/src/sync/useSyncQuery.ts, useSyncMutation.ts, useSyncSubscription.ts, useGatewayQuery.ts, useGatewayMutation.ts, useGatewayRunStream.ts, useGatewayRunTree.ts, useGatewayConnectionStatus.ts
evidence: These are the hooks the maintainer's bar targets ('using TanStack DB properly... with e2e tests'). Grepping all in-scope reference UIs (.smithers/ui/*.tsx) shows ZERO usages of useSyncQuery/useSyncMutation/useSyncSubscription/useGatewayQuery/useGatewayMutation/useGatewayRunStream/useGatewayRunTree/useGatewayConnectionStatus — every shipped UI (vcs.tsx, e2e-probe.tsx, grill-me.tsx, etc.) uses only useGatewayRun/useGatewayRunEvents/useGatewayNodeOutput/useGatewayRuns/useGatewayActions. The only real consumers of the declarative sync hooks are in apps/smithers (out of scope). So the real-browser e2e (apps/cli/tests/workflow-ui-all.e2e.test.js), which builds+boots every .smithers/ui bundle, never touches the declarative sync layer. Their only coverage is the happy-dom reconciler test (packages/gateway-react/tests/sync/sync.test.ts) — good, but not a real browser, and it uses a fake SyncTransport.
recommendation: Ship at least one .smithers/ui reference UI that drives useGatewayRunTree + useGatewayRunStream + useGatewayConnectionStatus (e.g. a live run-graph/inspector UI), and add it to workflow-ui-descriptors.json so the real-browser e2e exercises the TanStack-DB live-query path against a real gateway + real run.
vnote: Verified independently. `grep -rn` for useSyncQuery/useSyncMutation/useSyncSubscription/useGatewayQuery/useGatewayMutation/useGatewayRunStream/useGatewayRunTree/useGatewayConnectionStatus across .smithers/ui/*.tsx returns ZERO real usages. A hook-usage histogram of .smithers/ui shows only useGatewayNodeOutput (85), useGatewayRunEvents (40), useGatewayRuns (36), useGatewayActions (36), useGatewayRun (14), useGatewayApprovals (4) -- none of the declarative sync hooks. The only repo-wide consumers are apps/smithers (out of scope: GatewayRunInspector.tsx, GatewayWorkflowsSection.tsx, RemoteModePanel.tsx, plus app-local src/sync copies) and the 3 .smithers/workflows/*.tsx matches, which I opened and confirmed are plain string mentions inside workflow prompt text, not React usage. The real-browser e2e (apps/cli/tests/workflow-ui-all.e2e.test.js) boots .smithers/gateway.ts directly (line 150 spawns `run .smithers/gateway.ts`), launches headless Chromium (CHROMIUM gate, line 59), and fetches `/workflows/<key>?runId=<id>` directly (line 91) -- it never invokes `smithers ui` and never touches a declarative sync hook. Their only coverage is packages/gateway-react/tests/sync/sync.test.ts, which its own header comment confirms runs under happy-dom (not a real browser) with the sole seam being a fake SyncTransport. Minor: the auditor's 'every shipped UI uses only ...' list omitted useGatewayApprovals (4 uses), but that does not affect the conclusion. P1 test-gap stands.

title: `smithers ui` CLI command (resolution, autostart, error envelopes) is completely untested
evidence: Grepping apps/cli/tests and packages/*/tests for any invocation of the `ui` subcommand or its error codes (GATEWAY_UNREACHABLE, NO_UI, WORKFLOW_UNRESOLVED, NO_RUNS, UI_OPEN_FAILED) returns nothing. The real-browser e2e boots .smithers/gateway.ts directly and fetches /workflows/<key> URLs — it never goes through `smithers ui`. So the run→workflow→uiPath resolution, --no-open URL printing, latest-run fallback, --workflow shortcut, autostart-when-unreachable branch, and all five fail() envelopes have zero coverage. autoStartGateway spawns `smithers gateway` and polls /health 60× — also untested.
recommendation: Add a real-backend CLI test: start a Gateway with a UI-mounted workflow + a completed run, then assert `smithers ui <runId> --no-open --gateway <url>` prints the correct /workflows/<key>?runId=<id> URL and returns {opened:false,url,runId,workflow}; assert NO_UI for a workflow without a UI and NO_RUNS / GATEWAY_UNREACHABLE on the error paths.
vnote: Behavior is confirmed untested, but 'completely untested' is slightly overstated. The ui command source at apps/cli/src/index.js:5758-5848 matches the description exactly (GATEWAY_UNREACHABLE at 5785, WORKFLOW_UNRESOLVED at 5834, NO_UI/NO_RUNS in the try block, UI_OPEN_FAILED at 5846, autoStartGateway at 5782; autoStartGateway defined 1940-1963 spawns `smithers gateway` and polls /health 60x). Grepping apps/cli/tests + packages/*/tests + .smithers/tests for GATEWAY_UNREACHABLE/WORKFLOW_UNRESOLVED/UI_OPEN_FAILED/autoStartGateway/NO_UI/NO_RUNS returns nothing, and there is no runSmithers(["ui", ...]) anywhere. So the run->workflow->uiPath resolution, --no-open URL printing, latest-run fallback, --workflow shortcut, autostart branch, and all five fail() envelopes have zero behavioral coverage. The one nuance: apps/cli/tests/cli-help.test.js:3 ('help surface advertises the ui command') DOES assert the ui command appears in --help output, so the command is not literally 0% touched -- only its behavior is. Severity remains P1 for the behavioral test-gap.

title: Default operator console re-implements the whole wire protocol instead of using the published SDK
evidence: defaultOperatorUiClient is a 1432-line self-contained vanilla function serialized to the browser via `.toString()` (DEFAULT_OPERATOR_UI_CLIENT_JS = `(${defaultOperatorUiClient.toString()})();`). It has NO imports and hand-rolls rpc() (line 477), websocketUrl(), openGatewaySocket()/rpcSocket() WebSocket framing+reconnect (lines 508-606), and streamDevTools/streamRunEvents subscriptions — exactly the surface SmithersGatewayClient already provides and that the project ships as a published SDK. The gateway's own flagship UI does not dogfood its own SDK, doubling the protocol surface that can drift (e.g. resilient reconnect/backoff logic now lives in two places).
recommendation: Either build the operator console as a real gateway-react bundle (the same Bun.build path used for workflow UIs) so it consumes SmithersGatewayClient + the sync hooks, or extract the shared wire logic so both paths share one implementation. At minimum, document why it is intentionally dependency-free if that constraint is real.

title: Default operator console has no behavioral/browser test — only string-grep assertions
evidence: The operator-console tests assert the served asset text CONTAINS substrings like rpc("listWorkflows", `new WebSocket`, sessionStorage, and that `new Function(asset)` doesn't throw — i.e. it parses. There is no test that the console actually renders, lists runs, or drives an approval against a live gateway in a browser. Given it is 1432 lines of bespoke logic (reconnect, devtools tree, run chronicle), grep-for-substring is a weak guarantee that it works.
recommendation: Add the /console route to the real-browser e2e harness (or a dedicated Chromium test) that boots a Gateway with a seeded run and asserts the console renders the run and can submit an approval.

title: Pure run-status mapper `runStatusFromFrame` branches are not directly unit-tested
evidence: gatewayCollectionDefs.test.ts only covers the `nodes` def. runStatusFromFrame has distinct branches: run.completed with state failed/cancelled→failed, otherwise state??'ok'; run.started/run.resumed→running; run.paused→waiting; else undefined. Only the run.completed→ok branch is indirectly hit (via gateway-react sync.test.ts). The running/waiting/failed-from-lifecycle and the eventRows seq-guard (returns [] when seq is not a number) branches are untested. These are pure functions — cheap to test exhaustively and high-value since they drive the run badge color.
recommendation: Add direct unit tests for runStatusFromFrame across all event kinds and terminal states, and for eventRows dropping frames without a numeric seq.

title: refetch closures reference `params` but omit it from the dependency array
evidence: Each refetch is `useCallback(async () => { await registry.invalidate(gatewayKeys.X(params...)); }, [registry, collection])` — the closure reads `params`/`params.filter` but the dep array lists `collection` instead. It is correct in practice only because `collection` is memoized per params-fingerprint (a params change yields a new collection, recreating the closure), but the invariant is indirect and would trip react-hooks/exhaustive-deps; a future refactor that decouples collection identity from params would silently capture stale params.
recommendation: Either add the params fingerprint to the dep array, or compute the key once with useMemo and depend on the memoized key, to make the dependency explicit.

title: Doc claims a 'generation counter' stale-fence for useGatewayExtensionStream that does not exist in code
evidence: The hook's JSDoc says 'Stale frames are fenced: a re-render that changes (namespace, key, params) bumps a generation counter; frames from the prior subscription that arrive late are discarded.' The implementation uses no generation counter — it relies entirely on a per-effect AbortController and `abort.signal.aborted` checks. Functionally the abort path is fine, but the documented mechanism is inaccurate. Separately, the custom-workflow-ui.mdx guide presents useGatewayRun/useGatewayRunEvents/useGatewayNodeOutput as all backed by useGatewayRpc's clear-on-input-change model, but in reality useGatewayRun/useGatewayRunEvents (and useGatewayRuns/Approvals/Workflows/RunTree) are backed by the TanStack-DB sync collections via useLiveQuery; only useGatewayRpc/useGatewayNodeOutput/extension hooks use the generation-fence model.
recommendation: Update the JSDoc to describe the actual abort-based fencing, and clarify in custom-workflow-ui.mdx which hooks are collection-backed (stale-free via collection swap on key change) versus generation-fenced.

title: Extension-stream reconnect backoff timer ignores the abort signal
evidence: The reconnect wait is `await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, gatewayBackoffDelay(attempt, options.backoff)))` with no signal wiring. If the component unmounts (or params change) during a long backoff delay, the timer keeps running until it fires; the loop then re-checks abort and exits without setState, so there is no incorrect render, but the dangling timer delays teardown by up to the backoff cap. The sibling SmithersGatewayClient.sleepWithSignal and createGatewayCollection.sleep both correctly cancel on abort — this hook is the outlier.
recommendation: Replace the bare setTimeout with a signal-aware sleep (mirror sleepWithSignal / the createGatewayCollection.sleep helper) so abort resolves the wait immediately.
vnote: Verified at packages/gateway-react/src/useGatewayExtensionStream.ts:94 -- the reconnect wait is a bare `await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, gatewayBackoffDelay(attempt, options.backoff)))` with no signal wiring. The auditor's own caveat is accurate: after the timer fires, line 89 re-checks abort and returns without setState, so there is no incorrect render -- only a dangling timer delaying teardown by up to the backoff cap (hence correctly P2). Confirmed the siblings are signal-aware: gateway-client/src/SmithersGatewayClient.ts:121 sleepWithSignal adds an 'abort' listener that clearTimeout+resolves (lines 127-136), and createGatewayCollections.ts:131-138 registers an abort listener that resolves the wait immediately. This hook is the outlier. The hook is a public exported member of gateway-react (src/index.ts:15) with tests in extension-hooks.test.ts that cover backoff-key stability and the ring buffer, but neither test exercises unmount-during-backoff, so the bug is real, shippable, and untested. P2 confirmed.

title: HermesAgent has zero tests anywhere in the repo
evidence: grep for HermesAgent/HERMES_BASE_URL across packages/, apps/cli, e2e, workflows, docs returns only src/index.js, HermesAgent.js, HermesAgentOptions.ts, index.d.ts and packages/smithers re-exports — never a test file. HermesAgent adds real behavior over OpenAIAgent: it throws SmithersError(AGENT_CONFIG_INVALID) when baseURL/HERMES_BASE_URL is missing (HermesAgent.js:32-38) and forces nativeStructuredOutput=false by default. Neither the baseURL-required validation nor the structured-output default is exercised. sdk-agents.test.js tests AnthropicAgent and OpenAIAgent but never HermesAgent.
recommendation: Add a hermes-support.test.js: (1) assert constructing HermesAgent() with no baseURL and no HERMES_BASE_URL throws AGENT_CONFIG_INVALID; (2) assert it resolves baseURL from HERMES_BASE_URL; (3) assert supportsNativeStructuredOutput defaults to false (and can be enabled). Use MockLanguageModelV3 as the other SDK agents do — no real Hermes server needed for the validation/config paths.
vnote: Verified: grep for HermesAgent/HERMES_BASE_URL across packages/, apps/cli, e2e, workflows, docs returns only HermesAgent.js, HermesAgentOptions.ts, index.js/index.d.ts re-exports, and packages/smithers re-export — never a test file (grep of packages/agents/tests/ for HermesAgent exits 1, no match). HermesAgent.js:32-38 throws SmithersError('AGENT_CONFIG_INVALID') when baseURL/HERMES_BASE_URL is empty/undefined, and line 29 defaults nativeStructuredOutput=false — both real behaviors over OpenAIAgent, neither exercised. sdk-agents.test.js (lines 46-244) tests only AnthropicAgent and OpenAIAgent. P1 is appropriate: a shipping, publicly re-exported adapter with config-validation logic and zero coverage.

title: GeminiAgent has no buildCommand/interpreter behavior test despite being a fully-wired shipping adapter
evidence: Gemini's only test references are incidental: capability-registry.test.js:87 constructs `new GeminiAgent()` solely to check `.capabilities`, and agent-config-dir.test.js:153 tests only configDir/apiKey env mapping. There is no test of its 200-line `createOutputInterpreter` (init/MESSAGE/TOOL_USE/TOOL_RESULT/ERROR/RESULT JSONL handling, delta accumulation at lines 100-110, onExit fallback at 193-207) nor of `buildCommand` (json-vs-stream-json default selection at 219-220, resume at 249, allowedTools-empty-string edge at 238-245, jsonReminder injection at 264-266). Yet GeminiAgent is actively used: apps/cli/src/ask.js:363, apps/cli/src/index.js:6209, agent-detection.js maps gemini/gemini-api -> GeminiAgent, and .smithers/agents.ts references it. The @deprecated tag (GeminiAgent.js:42) is JSDoc-only with no runtime warning, and Antigravity (the suggested replacement) does have antigravity-support.test.js.
recommendation: Add a gemini-support.test.js mirroring forge-support/pi-support: a fake `gemini` binary that emits the JSONL event stream to assert interpreter events (started/action/completed, delta accumulation), plus direct buildCommand assertions for output-format selection, resume->--resume, and the allowedTools=[] empty-flag edge case. If Gemini is truly being retired, gate removal behind a deprecation timeline rather than shipping it untested.
vnote: Verified the test references: capability-registry.test.js:87 only constructs new GeminiAgent() to read .capabilities; agent-config-dir.test.js:154-179 runs generate() against a fake gemini binary but asserts ONLY GEMINI_DIR/GEMINI_API_KEY env mapping — no interpreter event assertions, no buildCommand flag assertions. GeminiAgent.js has a ~150-line createOutputInterpreter (init/MESSAGE/TOOL_USE/TOOL_RESULT/ERROR/RESULT, delta accumulation at 104-110, onExit fallback at 193-207) and buildCommand (json-vs-stream-json default at 219-220, resume->--resume at 249, allowedTools=[] empty-string edge at 238-245, jsonReminder at 264-266) — none asserted. Confirmed actively used: apps/cli/src/ask.js:363, apps/cli/src/index.js:6209-6210, agent-detection.js:162/507/511, .smithers/agents.ts:18, .smithers/workflows/studio-parity-swarm.tsx:165. @deprecated is JSDoc-only (line 42, no console.warn). The suggested replacement Antigravity DOES have buildCommand/resume coverage (antigravity-support.test.js:18,57-67). Minor imprecision in evidence: the config-dir test does run generate() end-to-end, but it asserts nothing about the interpreter/buildCommand branches, so the gap stands. P1 appropriate.

title: AmpAgent cannot resume a session — it is the only CLI adapter that never wires resumeSession into buildCommand
evidence: grep 'resumeSession' across all CLI adapters: Amp=0 references, every other CLI adapter (Antigravity, ClaudeCode, Codex, Forge, Gemini, Kimi, OpenCode, Pi, Vibe) =3+. Amp's interpreter tracks and emits `resume: sessionId` from the result event (AmpAgent.js:84-92, 166-169) and from onExit (188), but buildCommand (196-240) never reads `params.options?.resumeSession`, and AmpAgentOptions.ts has no threadId/resume/continueSession field. buildCommand also unconditionally appends `--archive` (line 221), so every headless generate starts and archives a fresh thread. The manifest is honest about it: cliAgentSurfaceManifest.js:406-410 notes 'headless generate starts a managed thread', but this still means resume/continuation is impossible for Amp via the standard adapter path that all other engines support.
recommendation: Either (a) implement Amp resume: add `threadId`/`resumeSession` handling that emits `amp threads continue <id>` (per the manifest's documented subcommand) and gate `--archive` so resumed threads aren't archived, plus add an amp-support resume test; or (b) explicitly document Amp as resume-unsupported in the capability registry (currently it advertises no resume-specific capability) so callers don't expect the emitted `resume` id to be usable.
vnote: Verified per-adapter resumeSession counts: Amp=0; Antigravity/ClaudeCode/Forge/Gemini/Kimi/OpenCode/Vibe=3, Codex=14, Pi=4. AmpAgent.buildCommand (196-240) never reads params.options?.resumeSession and unconditionally appends '--archive' (line 221). AmpAgentOptions.ts has no threadId/resume/continueSession field, and BaseCliAgentOptions.ts has no inherited resume field (grep found none). The interpreter DOES emit resume: sessionId (lines 87, 168, 188) and amp-support.test.js even asserts the emitted resume id (lines 133, 229) plus '--archive' in args (line 54) — so the emitted resume id is real but unusable via the standard adapter path. Manifest cliAgentSurfaceManifest.js:409 documents 'Native hijack uses `amp threads continue`; headless generate starts a managed thread.' Genuine missing feature; P1 appropriate.

title: Capability-registry factory exports are inconsistent: 4 of 10 re-exported from index, and those 4 are consumed nowhere
evidence: All 10 CLI adapters export a createXCapabilityRegistry function from their .js, but only 4 are re-exported from index.js: createAmpCapabilityRegistry, createAntigravityCapabilityRegistry, createForgeCapabilityRegistry, createVibeCapabilityRegistry (lines 48,49,56,58). The other 6 (Gemini, OpenCode, Claude, Codex, Kimi, Pi) are not re-exported. The sole internal consumer, cli-capabilities/getCliAgentCapabilityReport.js:2-11, imports ALL 10 directly from each adapter .js — never via index. A whole-repo grep shows the 4 index-level re-exports are imported nowhere outside packages/agents/src (no app, no other package, no test). So the public surface is both arbitrary (why these 4?) and effectively dead.
recommendation: Pick one policy: either re-export all 10 (if these are meant to be public for custom registry composition) or none (drop the 4 from index.js and index.d.ts). Given no external consumer exists, removing the 4 is the lower-risk cleanup; add a test asserting the export set if you keep any.
vnote: Verified all 10 adapters export createXCapabilityRegistry from their .js. index.js re-exports exactly 4: createAmpCapabilityRegistry (48), createAntigravityCapabilityRegistry (49), createForgeCapabilityRegistry (56), createVibeCapabilityRegistry (58). The other 6 (ClaudeCode, Codex, Gemini, Kimi, OpenCode, Pi) are not re-exported. Sole consumer getCliAgentCapabilityReport.js:2-11 imports all 10 DIRECTLY from each adapter .js, never via index. Whole-repo grep for the 4 re-exported names shows usages only in: index.js (the re-export), index.d.ts (type decls), each adapter's own .js (internal construction), and getCliAgentCapabilityReport.js (direct-from-adapter import) — no app, no test, no other package, and the smithers umbrella package re-exports only hashCapabilityRegistry (packages/smithers/src/index.js:170), not these factories. docs reference only the AgentCapabilityRegistry type, not the factories. So the 4 index re-exports are arbitrary and effectively dead. P2 appropriate.

title: OpenCodeAgent is the only adapter with a hand-maintained .ts declaration file instead of the standard XAgentOptions.ts pattern
evidence: 13 adapters follow the convention of a .js impl plus a dedicated XAgentOptions.ts type file (AmpAgentOptions.ts, CodexAgentOptions.ts, etc.). OpenCode is the only one with NO OpenCodeAgentOptions.ts; instead OpenCodeAgent.ts hand-declares the options type, the full `declare class OpenCodeAgent`, and `export declare function createOpenCodeCapabilityRegistry` (lines 5-43) that must be manually kept in sync with OpenCodeAgent.js. This is what the task flagged as 'OpenCodeAgent.ts AND OpenCodeAgent.js both exist'. It is NOT dead (index.js:24 imports OpenCodeAgentOptions from the .ts typedef; the .js is the runtime impl), but it is a structural outlier with manual drift risk — e.g. its buildCommand return type is hand-typed (lines 30-42) rather than inferred.
recommendation: Normalize to the repo convention: extract the options to OpenCodeAgentOptions.ts and let the .js impl carry JSDoc types like every other adapter, deleting the hand-written class/function declarations in OpenCodeAgent.ts. This removes the only adapter-level type-declaration duplicate and the associated sync burden.

title: Manifest emittedFlags are validated for internal consistency but never against real buildCommand output
evidence: CLI_AGENT_SURFACE_MANIFEST hand-lists emittedFlags per engine (e.g. Amp lines 386-400, Gemini, etc.) and the doctor report claims these are the flags each adapter emits. cli-capabilities.test.js only checks the manifest against itself (ids match registry ids:19-20, binary matches:23, unsupportedFlags not in emittedFlags:35-37) and a couple of hardcoded Antigravity flag assertions (42-55). No test instantiates an adapter, calls buildCommand, and asserts the produced args ⊆ manifest.emittedFlags. So if an adapter's buildCommand adds/renames a flag (e.g. Amp's --no-jetbrains, Vibe's --trust/--output), the manifest can silently drift and the doctor report would mislead users.
recommendation: Add a table-driven test that, for each manifest entry, constructs the adapter with options that exercise its mappings, calls buildCommand, and asserts every emitted '--flag' is present in manifest.emittedFlags (and that emittedFlags has no entries the adapter can never produce). This turns the manifest into a verified contract rather than documentation.

title: Real-CLI e2e exists for only 2 of 10 CLI engines (OpenCode, Vibe); the other 8 are proven only via fake-binary subprocess tests
evidence: Only opencode-e2e.test.js and vibe-agent-e2e.test.js gate on a real installed CLI (`which opencode`/`which vibe` + --help flag probing, skipped otherwise) and send a real prompt to the actual binary. Claude/Codex/Pi/Kimi/Forge are tested via fake `node` shim binaries placed on PATH (e.g. claude-support.test.js makeFakeClaude at line 10, pi-support makeFakePi) — excellent for argument/interpreter contract testing and genuinely subprocess-based (no in-process mocks), but they assert the adapter's framing of a stubbed stream, not that the real CLI's current output shape still matches the interpreter. Antigravity is weakest: antigravity-support.test.js only asserts buildCommand args (no subprocess at all, line 18 returns the spec directly). So real-CLI output-shape regressions (a CLI changing its JSONL event names) would be caught for OpenCode/Vibe but not for the other 8.
recommendation: Add `which`-gated real-CLI e2e (matching the opencode/vibe pattern, auto-skipped in CI per the project's agent-free CI constraint) for at least the highest-traffic engines (Claude, Codex), and at minimum add a fake-binary subprocess interpreter test for Antigravity so it reaches parity with the other CLI adapters.

title: 22 of 30 fault cases fabricate their own SQL schema and reimplement the feature in-test — they validate a mock of the contract, not the product
evidence: e2e/faults/case12-rewind-reverts-vcs.test.ts defines its own buildDb() with hand-rolled `CREATE TABLE _smithers_frames/_smithers_time_travel_audit/...` and a local `rewind()` function (lines 160-221) that issues its own DELETE/UPDATE SQL, then asserts the local function works — it never calls the real packages/time-travel jumpToFrame/rewindAudit. Same pattern in case03 (submitApprovalDecision/supervisorTakeover), case14 (full fake gateway: startGateway/handleRpc/checkAuth, lines 196-485), case17 (computeSignature/isValidSignature/startWebhookServer), case24 (classifyReplaySafety/emitReplayUnsafeApproval), case26, case27 (12 in-test functions, 0 real imports). Tally: REAL=2 FABRICATED=22 EMPTY=6 across e2e/faults/case*.ts. These tests pass independently of whether the real engine/gateway/time-travel code is correct or even present.
recommendation: Rewrite the fabricated cases to use the real APIs the way case25 and the package-level e2e tests already do: createSmithers/SmithersDb + ensureSmithersTables for storage, real Gateway from @smithers-orchestrator/server/gateway over the wire, real jumpToFrame/replayFromCheckpoint/captureSnapshot for time-travel. Where a real engine harness genuinely doesn't exist, delete the fabricated body rather than leave a green test that asserts a reimplementation.
vnote: Confirmed by reading all 30 e2e/faults/case*.test.ts. case12 hand-rolls buildDb() with CREATE TABLE _smithers_frames/_smithers_time_travel_audit/... and a local rewind() (lines 160-221) it then asserts against — zero real imports. case03 has local supervisorTakeover (146) + submitApprovalDecision (181) + buildDb (43); case14 has checkAuth (172), handleRpc (196), startGateway (382), only imports bun:sqlite/ws; case17 has computeSignature (114), isValidSignature (118), startWebhookServer (150); case24 has classifyReplaySafety (189) + emitReplayUnsafeApproval (213); case26 has buildDb (59) + reviewDiff (218); case27 has 0 real imports. Precise tally from grepping for real @smithers-orchestrator/smithers-orchestrator imports: 2 fully-real (case23 calls real executeSandbox, case25 boots real Gateway), 4 hybrid (case01/02/08 call real deriveRunState, case24 real getDefinedToolMetadata — but each still hand-rolls its own CREATE TABLE schema and reimplements surrounding logic), 18 fully fabricated (0 real imports), 6 empty stubs. The finding's 'FABRICATED=22' lumps the 4 hybrids in with the 18; those 4 do exercise one real function against a fabricated DB schema, so 'reimplements the feature' is slightly overstated for them — but they still validate against fabricated data, so the core claim and P0 hold.

title: case14 'gateway RPC roundtrip' and case17 'webhook bad signature' skip the real path on an obsolete/false rationale that case25 disproves
evidence: case14 line 853-861 and case17 line 446-451 both .skip the real-engine sub-test claiming 'booting the real Gateway from packages/server inside the e2e package fails on effect / @smithers-orchestrator/devtools resolution because workspace symlinks bypass e2e flat node_modules'. But case25-approval-scope-denial.test.ts:11 imports `{ Gateway } from "@smithers-orchestrator/server/gateway"`, calls `new Gateway(...)` and `gateway.listen({ port: 0 })` (lines 168-187), and its real-gateway tests run (not skipped) in the same e2e package. So the real gateway DOES boot; case14/17 instead hand-roll an entire fake WebSocketServer/HTTP gateway and assert against it.
recommendation: Delete the fake gateway/webhook harnesses in case14/case17 and drive the real Gateway (and real handleWebhook/computeWebhookSignature from packages/server) over the wire exactly as case25 does. Remove the false skip comments.
vnote: Confirmed and empirically verified. case14:854-861 and case17:447-450 both claim 'booting the real Gateway from packages/server inside the e2e package fails on effect / @smithers-orchestrator/devtools resolution because workspace symlinks bypass e2e flat node_modules.' I RAN `bun test faults/case25-approval-scope-denial.test.ts`: it imports Gateway from @smithers-orchestrator/server/gateway (line 11), calls new Gateway(...) + gateway.listen({port:0}) (168-187), and passed 5 tests (1 skip) — the real Gateway boots inside the e2e package, fully disproving the skip rationale. The real handleWebhook (gateway.js:2069) and computeWebhookSignature (gateway.js:1196) exist, so case17 has a real target it reimplements instead. P0 holds.

title: Nightly soak CI gate (120-min budget) runs effectively one fabricated-transport test; cases 29 and 30 are permanently skipped
evidence: .github/workflows/faults-nightly.yml runs `pnpm --filter @smithers-orchestrator/e2e test:soak` with SMITHERS_E2E_SOAK=1 and a 120-min timeout. Under that flag only case28 actually executes (case28:212 test.skipIf(!SOAK_ENABLED)), and case28 streams over a hand-rolled `WebSocketServer` subscriber (case28:4,86-88) against a fabricated `_smithers_events` table rather than the real gateway streamRunEvents. case29-soak-cron-2h (line 7 test.skip, no body) and case30-soak-jjhub (line 7 test.skip, no body) never run even with the flag set. The nightly soak therefore validates no real long-lived runtime.
recommendation: Point case28's live stream at the real gateway streamRunEvents/subscriber path (the real streaming code is exercised by packages/server/tests/streamDevTools.soak.test.ts). Either implement case29/case30 against the real scheduler and jjhub runtime or remove the empty stubs and the soak CI job until they exist, so the nightly gate isn't green-on-nothing.
vnote: Confirmed. .github/workflows/faults-nightly.yml: 120-min timeout, SMITHERS_E2E_SOAK=1, runs `pnpm --filter @smithers-orchestrator/e2e test:soak`. case28:212 test.skipIf(!SOAK_ENABLED) is the only soak test that runs under the flag; it imports ws WebSocketServer (line 4), builds its own _smithers_events table via buildDb (46-59), and runs a hand-rolled startSubscriberServer (86-88) — not the real streamDevToolsRoute. case29:7 and case30:7 are test.skip with empty bodies, never run even with the flag. The real streaming path (packages/server/src/gatewayRoutes/streamDevTools.js streamDevToolsRoute) and a real soak test (packages/server/tests/streamDevTools.soak.test.ts) both exist. P1 holds.

title: Six fault cases are empty skip-only stubs — entire feature areas have zero fault/e2e coverage
evidence: case19-auth-persistence-suspend-resume.test.ts, case20-browser-automation-reference-runtime.test.ts, case21-file-vcs-pointer-integrity.test.ts, case22-secret-injection-no-leak.test.ts are each a single `test.skip(... blocked on jjhub/0002)` with no body. case02-kill-sandbox-engine-alive.test.ts:189 skips the actual dual-heartbeat sandbox-vs-engine distinction (the named purpose of the case), leaving only engine-heartbeat assertions. case29/case30 (above) are also empty. Secret redaction (case22) and VCS pointer integrity (case21) are safety-critical and have no coverage anywhere in the repo.
recommendation: Track these as explicit coverage gaps, not as 'tests'. For secret redaction and VCS pointer integrity, add real e2e even if narrower than the jjhub-runtime ideal (e.g. assert redaction on the real log stream / real vcs adapter). Skip-only stubs inflate the apparent case count (30) versus the real count (2 real + a few hybrids).
vnote: Confirmed. case19,20,21,22 are each ~12 lines: a single test.skip('...blocked on jjhub/0002') with an empty body. case29,30 (~14 lines) are likewise empty test.skip. case02:189 test.skip leaves the dual-heartbeat sandbox-vs-engine distinction (the file's named purpose, 'kill-sandbox-engine-alive') unimplemented, keeping only engine-heartbeat assertions (case02 does test the REAL deriveRunState for engine heartbeats, so the case isn't entirely empty). Secret redaction (case22) and VCS pointer integrity (case21) are skip-only with no coverage elsewhere. The parenthetical 'real count (2 real + a few hybrids)' is a fair summary; my categorization is 2 real + 4 hybrid + 18 fabricated + 6 empty. P1 holds.

title: Major features have NO real happy-path e2e: crons, OpenAPI tools, memory, eval/optimize, sandbox suspend/resume
evidence: Surveyed all *.e2e.test.* across packages and apps/cli. Real e2e exists for: durable resume (engine/resume-no-time-travel, resume-with-time-travel), time-travel/rewind/fork (time-travel/timetravel, retry-task; engine/fork-session), snapshots (engine/durability-snapshots), approvals (case25, ask-human), signals (cli-signals), gateway RPC/cron-fire (gateway-edge fires a cron at line 54). But there is no real e2e for: `smithers eval`/`smithers optimize`, OpenAPI->tools generation, cross-run memory facts, full cron scheduler loop end-to-end (only a single fire in gateway-edge; case29 cron soak is skipped), or sandbox suspend/resume + auth persistence (case19/case21/case22 all skip-only). The `smithers ui` runtime has only apps/cli/tests/workflow-ui-all.e2e.test.js.
recommendation: Add real-backend happy-path e2e for eval/optimize (run a tiny JSONL suite end-to-end and assert the regression report), OpenAPI tool generation (generate from a fixture spec and assert callable tools), memory (write+query a fact across two runs), and a real multi-fire cron loop. These are user-facing CLI features shipping today with no e2e.
vnote: Mostly REFUTED. The finding's survey only globbed '*.e2e.test.*' and missed files named 'e2e.test.js' and '*-suite.test.js'. Real e2e DOES exist for: (1) smithers eval — apps/cli/tests/eval-suite.test.js:183 'runs cases, checks outputs, and writes a report' invokes the real CLI via runSmithers (spawnSync of the bun CLI binary), runs a real JSONL suite end-to-end, and asserts the written regression report (lines 219-223) — exactly what the finding says is missing; (2) smithers optimize — apps/cli/tests/optimize-suite.test.js:207 'proves a GEPA prompt artifact improves eval results and can be reused' via real runSmithers; (3) OpenAPI tool generation — packages/openapi/tests/e2e.test.js exercises real createOpenApiTools/createOpenApiTool from a fixture spec and asserts callable tools (it mocks globalThis.fetch for HTTP execution, so it is not a strict no-mock e2e, but the tool-generation feature IS covered); (4) memory cross-run facts — packages/memory/tests/e2e.test.js:55 'multiple runs accumulate facts' uses real createMemoryStore + ensureSmithersTables across runs. VALID residual gaps: the CLI cron scheduler loop (apps/cli/src/scheduler.js) has no e2e — only a single real cron fire at packages/server/tests/gateway-edge.e2e.test.jsx:54 via real gateway.processDueCrons(); and sandbox suspend/resume has no e2e (but that runtime does not exist yet, blocked on jjhub/0002, so it is a missing feature not a coverage gap). Net: the headline overstates absence; only ~2 of 5 named features genuinely lack happy-path e2e. Downgraded P1->P2.

title: workflow-ui-all e2e depends on a retired POC (apps/smithers-studio-2) for its Chromium binary
evidence: apps/cli/tests/workflow-ui-all.e2e.test.js:43 resolves Chromium via `apps/smithers-studio-2/node_modules/playwright/index.js` and line 59 `const workflowUiTest = CHROMIUM ? test : test.skip`. Per CLAUDE.md, apps/smithers-studio-2 is a retired POC that is 'not in the stack'. The only real-browser e2e for the shipped `smithers ui` workflow-UI system is gated behind a browser sourced from a retired app, so in CI (browser-free per project memory) it skips entirely, and locally it depends on a POC's install.
recommendation: Move the Playwright/Chromium dependency the workflow-ui e2e needs into the e2e or apps/cli package itself (or a shared test-deps package) so it doesn't reach into a retired POC. Keep the clean skip-when-absent behavior (legit CI pattern), but ensure the binary source is a supported part of the stack.

title: flake-log.md is empty, so the documented promotion gate (0 flakes / 100 CI runs) cannot have been applied — yet fabricated cases were 'promoted' to per-PR
evidence: e2e/README.md and e2e/flake-log.md both state a case is only promoted from nightly-only to the per-PR subset after 0 flakes per 100 consecutive CI runs, recorded in flake-log.md. flake-log.md has an empty table (header only, line 9-11). Meanwhile faults.yml runs the entire e2e/faults dir per-PR (test:faults = `bun test faults`). Either the promotion process is not real, or it was bypassed; the empty log makes the stated gating non-auditable. (Note the cases are mostly fabricated, so they cannot flake on real behavior anyway.)
recommendation: Either enforce and populate the promotion log, or drop the nightly-vs-per-PR promotion narrative from README/flake-log since per-PR already runs the whole dir. Align the documented gate with what CI actually does.

title: case08 inspector and case24 replay-safety are hybrids: real predicate called against fabricated storage
evidence: case08-inspector-never-idle.test.ts:3 imports the real deriveRunState and asserts via it (lines 277,330,366,388), but feeds it rows from a hand-rolled `CREATE TABLE _smithers_runs` (line 62) rather than real persisted state — so the run-state derivation logic is exercised but not the real read path. case24-replay-unsafe-approval.test.ts:3 calls the real getDefinedToolMetadata predicate but wraps it in an in-test classifyReplaySafety/emitReplayUnsafeApproval reimplementation against fabricated tables; case24:443 honestly discloses the real engine emits only a soft warning, no ReplayUnsafeApproval row, so the asserted contract does not exist in product yet.
recommendation: For case08, derive run state from a real SmithersDb-persisted run (as case23/case25 do). For case24, either wait for the engine to implement the ReplayUnsafeApproval contract and test the real path, or scope the test down to just the real getDefinedToolMetadata predicate and stop asserting a non-existent engine behavior.

title: Reconnect-afterSeq / ws-drop / webhook behaviors are fabricated in e2e/faults but exist as real (non-e2e) tests elsewhere — duplicate-but-fake instead of promoting the real ones
evidence: case09-reconnect-afterseq, case15-ws-drop-reconnect, case17-webhook-bad-signature all hand-roll WS servers / webhook handlers with fabricated _smithers_events tables. The real behaviors are covered by real tests in packages/server/tests (gateway-v1-contract.test.jsx, streamDevTools.test.tsx/.soak.test.ts, gateway.test.jsx) and packages/gateway/tests/rpc-contract.test.ts. The fault suite reimplements rather than reuses these real paths, and latency budget reconnectAfterSeqMaxMs (e2e/budgets/latency.json) is enforced only against the fake transport (case09), not the real reconnect path.
recommendation: Replace the fabricated WS/webhook harnesses with the real gateway streamRunEvents/handleWebhook, and enforce the reconnectAfterSeqMaxMs budget against the real reconnect, so the budget actually guards a regression in product code.

title: e2e package.json omits the smithers-orchestrator dependency that case25 imports, so the real-gateway e2e relies on hoisting
evidence: e2e/package.json dependencies list @smithers-orchestrator/db, /driver, /engine, /gateway, /sandbox, /scheduler, /server but NOT 'smithers-orchestrator'. Yet e2e/faults/case25-approval-scope-denial.test.ts:7 imports `{ createSmithers } from "smithers-orchestrator"`. It resolves today via workspace hoisting (the test passes), but the missing explicit dependency makes the only fully-real gateway e2e fragile to hoisting/install changes — and undercuts the case14/case17 'flat node_modules' excuse since case25 already depends on transitive resolution working.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: OpenAPI 'e2e' test mocks globalThis.fetch, so it is not a strict no-mock e2e
evidence: packages/openapi/tests/e2e.test.js:9-18 replaces globalThis.fetch with bun:test mock(()=>Promise.resolve(new Response(...))) in beforeEach and asserts against mockFetch.mock.calls. The tool factory (createOpenApiTools/createOpenApiTool) is real, but the HTTP roundtrip the tool performs is fabricated — under the repo's 'real backends only, no mocks' e2e bar this is a unit/integration test mislabeled e2e. A real version would hit a local fixture HTTP server.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: eval-writer shows faithfulnessScorer()/relevancyScorer() called with no judge argument
evidence: The skill's scorers example calls `faithfulnessScorer()` and `relevancyScorer()` with zero args. But packages/scorers/src/faithfulnessScorer.js (`export function faithfulnessScorer(judge)`) and relevancyScorer.js (`export function relevancyScorer(judge)`) both REQUIRE a `judge` AgentLike argument — they pass it straight into llmJudge({ judge }). Called with no arg the judge is undefined. The only docs example (docs/how-it-works.mdx:266) only ever shows `schemaAdherenceScorer()` (which legitimately takes no args), never the judge-based ones argument-free.
recommendation: Show these scorers with a judge agent, e.g. `faithfulnessScorer(judge)` / `relevancyScorer(judge)` where judge is an agent from agents.ts, matching the real signature.
