vnote: Verified in source. packages/scorers/src/faithfulnessScorer.js declares `export function faithfulnessScorer(judge)` and relevancyScorer.js declares `export function relevancyScorer(judge)`; both pass `judge` straight into `llmJudge({ ..., judge, ... })`. skills/eval-writer/SKILL.md calls them with zero args at lines 81 (`faithfulnessScorer()`) and 82 (`relevancyScorer()`). With no arg, llmJudge.js destructures `judge` as undefined and `judge.generate(...)` throws at runtime. schemaAdherenceScorer() correctly takes no args (createScorer-based). docs/how-it-works.mdx:266 only ever shows schemaAdherenceScorer() arg-free, never the judge scorers. Minor: cited line range '78-88' is slightly off (actual calls are lines 81-82), but file path and substance are exactly correct. Real agents exist in .smithers/agents.ts (agents.smart / providers.claude) that could serve as the judge. P1 is appropriate for an AI-facing init-pack skill that produces runtime-throwing code if followed verbatim.

title: eval-writer's llmJudge config shape is entirely wrong (model/prompt fields do not exist)
evidence: The skill writes `llmJudge({ model: claude, prompt: "Rate completeness 0-1" })`. The real LlmJudgeConfig (packages/scorers/src/LlmJudgeConfig.ts) has NO `model` or `prompt` fields; it requires `id`, `name`, `description`, `judge` (an AgentLike, not `model`), `instructions` (string), and `promptTemplate` (a function `(input)=>string`, not a `prompt` string). The llmJudge example in llmJudge.js uses `judge: new AnthropicAgent(...), instructions: ..., promptTemplate: ({output}) => ...`. The skill's object would fail type-checking and at runtime.
recommendation: Replace with the actual config shape: `llmJudge({ id, name, description, judge, instructions, promptTemplate })`, or point at the canonical example in docs rather than inventing a `{ model, prompt }` shape.
vnote: Verified against packages/scorers/src/LlmJudgeConfig.ts: the type is { id, name, description, judge: AgentLike, instructions: string, promptTemplate: (input)=>string }. There is NO `model` field and NO `prompt` field. skills/eval-writer/SKILL.md:83 writes `llmJudge({ model: claude, prompt: "Rate completeness 0-1" })`. In llmJudge.js the destructure yields id/name/description/judge/instructions undefined and promptTemplate undefined; calling `promptTemplate(input)` throws. Also `claude` is an undefined identifier in the snippet (agents.ts exports `agents`/`providers`, not a bare `claude`). The canonical correct shape is in docs/recipes.mdx:279-287 (`llmJudge({ id, name, description, judge, instructions, promptTemplate })`). Cited line '83-84' is right (the object spans 83). Confirmed P1.

title: report-maker documents report-slideshow with `runId` input, but the workflow requires `targetRunId`
evidence: The skill's copy-paste command is `workflow run report-slideshow --input '{"runId":"<run-id>"}'`. But .smithers/workflows/report-slideshow.tsx defines its input as `targetRunId` (required string) with an explicit comment: "Named targetRunId (not runId): the engine reserves input.runId for the run's own id". The engine special-cases input.runId (packages/engine/src/engine.js:5395, 5966). So the documented command passes the run id under a name the workflow ignores and omits the required targetRunId — it would fail or report on the wrong/no run.
recommendation: Change the example to `--input '{"targetRunId":"<run-id>"}'` to match the workflow's actual input schema.
vnote: Verified. .smithers/workflows/report-slideshow.tsx:13-25 defines inputSchema with required `targetRunId` (z.string(), no default) and an explicit comment that the engine reserves input.runId for the run's own id; the workflow reads ctx.input.targetRunId (line 93). skills/report-maker/SKILL.md:67 documents `--input '{"runId":"<run-id>"}'`. Two failure modes confirmed: (1) engine.js:5395-5397 throws SmithersError INVALID_INPUT 'Input runId does not match provided runId' when input.runId != the generated run id; (2) even if it matched, validateInput (engine.js:5398-5404) rejects the missing required targetRunId. The official docs (docs/workflows/report-slideshow.mdx:13-17, 33) correctly use targetRunId and explicitly warn 'The input is targetRunId, not runId', so the skill directly contradicts the canonical docs. Confirmed P1.

title: report-maker makes inaccurate claims about shared slideshow renderer and monitor-smithers
evidence: Skill says report-slideshow "reuses the `capture:slideshow` renderer" and "The context-engineer flagship calls the same component as its report step, and monitor-smithers can attach one to its periodic digest." Reality: report-slideshow.tsx uses its own `capture` + `render` Tasks (no `capture:slideshow` component); context-engineer.tsx's report is just `<Task id="report" agent={agents.smart}>` with an mdx prompt (line 301), not a shared component; monitor-smithers.tsx only emits a `digest` string (no slideshow) — the HTML-reporting workflow is the separate `monitor` workflow, and it renders via monitor-report.mdx, not report-slideshow.
recommendation: Drop or correct the 'same component'/'capture:slideshow renderer' claims, and reference `monitor` (not monitor-smithers) for HTML reporting, or remove the attribution.

title: context-engineer cites a node id `grill-until-clear` that does not exist
evidence: The skill lists "Grill — only to reduce risk (`grill-until-clear`, the `<GrillMe>` component)". Every other node id it cites matches the workflow exactly (classify-script, inventory-context, build-backpressure, approve-contract, execute:loop, report — all verified in context-engineer.tsx). But the grill node is rendered by `<GrillMe idPrefix="context-engineer">`, whose Task id is `context-engineer:grill` (components/GrillMe.tsx:40 `id={`${idPrefix}:grill`}`). `grill-until-clear` is not a real node id anywhere.
recommendation: Use the real node id `context-engineer:grill` (or drop the parenthetical id and keep just the component name) so an agent grepping for the node finds it.

title: smithers SKILL.md uses singular `smithers agent add|list|remove`; the actual command is `agents` (plural)
evidence: SKILL.md says "Manage accounts with `smithers agent add|list|remove`". The registered CLI command is `agents` (apps/cli/src/index.js:2330 `name: "agents"`), with no singular `agent` alias; init-command.js and index.js:2422 both prompt 'Add one with `smithers agents add`', and the installed CLI skill is `smithers-agents`. The singular form would not resolve. (Note: this inaccuracy also exists in the generated docs bundle via agent-detection.js comments, so the root fix is in docs source.)
recommendation: Change to `smithers agents add|list|remove` in the SKILL.md (and fix the docs/source comments so the regenerated bundle agrees).

title: smithers SKILL.md lists `<LoopUntilScored>` as a box-shipped built-in, but it is a seeded local-pack component
evidence: Line 219 groups `<LoopUntilScored>` with built-ins that 'ship in the box' (CheckSuite, DecisionTable, Poller, Runbook, TryCatchFinally, ContinueAsNew) — all of which exist in packages/components/src/components/. LoopUntilScored does NOT exist there; it lives only as .smithers/components/LoopUntilScored.tsx (seeded user pack), and docs/components/loop-until-scored.mdx imports it from `~/components/LoopUntilScored` and calls it 'a thin wrapper around <Loop>'. The same SKILL.md (line 268) separately and correctly lists it under the local `components/` pack — an internal contradiction. An agent grepping packages/components/ for it (as the SKILL.md's own line 565 advises) will not find it.
recommendation: Remove <LoopUntilScored> from the box-shipped built-in list (line 219) and keep it only in the local-pack components description, or annotate it as a seeded-pack component.

title: skills/smithers/llms-full.txt is up to date with the generated docs bundle (positive finding)
evidence: skills/smithers/llms-full.txt (md5 d2f7d40e...), docs/llms-full.txt, and apps/cli/docs/llms-full.txt are byte-identical (440,989 bytes each). Running the full pipeline `bun scripts/generate-llms.ts && bun scripts/optimize-llms-full.ts` produced zero git diff on all three, confirming the bundled skill copy is fresh against the MDX source and the check-llms.mjs CI gate would pass. generate-llms.ts mirrors the bundle into SKILL_DIR by design (line 289-293).
recommendation: No action needed; documenting that the bundle freshness mechanism works as intended. (Caveat: the `agents` vs `agent` text inaccuracy noted above is baked into the source MDX/comments, so it rides into the bundle too.)

title: eval-writer's scorer sampling config shape is wrong (kind/ratio instead of type/rate)
evidence: skills/eval-writer/SKILL.md writes `sampling: { kind: "ratio", ratio: 0.1 }` at line 84 and repeats `{ kind: "ratio", ratio: 0.1 }` in prose at line 92. The real SamplingConfig type (packages/scorers/src/types.ts:53-56) is `{ type: "all" } | { type: "ratio"; rate: number } | { type: "none" }` — the discriminant key is `type` (not `kind`) and the numeric field is `rate` (not `ratio`). The canonical recipe docs/recipes.mdx:288 correctly use `sampling: { type: "ratio", rate: 0.1 }`. The skill's shape fails type-checking and at runtime the sampler would not see a recognized `type`, so the documented 10% sampling would not be applied as intended. This is the third bug in the same scorers code block as the two confirmed findings.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Memory processors TokenLimiter and Summarizer are documented features but ship as no-op placeholders
evidence: Both processors are publicly exported from smithers-orchestrator/memory (packages/memory/src/index.js:29-30) and documented in docs/concepts/memory.mdx:70-86 as 'keep history under token budget / trim history to budget' (TokenLimiter(4000)) and 'compress old messages with an LLM' (Summarizer(myAgent)). The implementations do neither: TokenLimiter computes charBudget = maxTokens*4 then only `Effect.logInfo(...)` and returns; its own comment says 'this is a no-op placeholder that documents the intended behaviour'. Summarizer ignores its agent entirely and logs 'configured — operates at thread level', comment: 'This is a structural placeholder.' By contrast TtlGarbageCollector (same dir) actually calls store.deleteExpiredFactsEffect(). The test packages/memory/tests/processors.test.js only asserts `.name` and 'process does not throw' for these two (vs a real deletion assertion for the GC), confirming there is nothing functional to test.
recommendation: Either implement thread-scoped trimming/summarization (the store API the GC uses suggests the plumbing exists), or remove the exports and the docs/concepts/memory.mdx claims until implemented. Shipping a documented `TokenLimiter`/`Summarizer` that silently does nothing is worse than not shipping it.
vnote: Verified TokenLimiter.js:15-22 computes charBudget then only Effect.logInfo and returns (comment: 'no-op placeholder that documents the intended behaviour'). Summarizer.js:13-19 ignores its _agent param entirely and logs 'configured — operates at thread level' (comment: 'This is a structural placeholder.'). By contrast TtlGarbageCollector.js:14 calls store.deleteExpiredFactsEffect() for real. Both are exported from packages/memory/src/index.js:29-30 AND re-exported from the top-level smithers-orchestrator package (packages/smithers/src/index.js:233). docs/concepts/memory.mdx:73-74,84-85 documents them as functional ('keep history under token budget'/'trim history to budget', TokenLimiter(4000); 'compress old messages with an LLM', Summarizer(myAgent)). tests/processors.test.js:40-71 only asserts .name and 'process does not throw' for these two, vs a real deletion assertion (lines 16-30) for the GC. Grep found NO functional wiring anywhere. P1 is appropriate: documented, top-level public APIs that silently do nothing.

title: `smithers gui` launches a native app (com.smithers.SmithersGUI) that is not built or shipped from this repo
evidence: The `gui` command runs `open -b com.smithers.SmithersGUI <dir>` (default bundleId com.smithers.SmithersGUI). No app in this repo registers/builds that bundle id: `grep -rln 'com.smithers'` across apps/ matches only apps/review and apps/cli itself; there is no .app/.dmg artifact and no plist. Per CLAUDE.md the native Smithers app (apps/smithers) packaging 'is not built yet' and ~/gui is a retired POC. The `open -b` spawn is detached with errors swallowed (proc.on('error', () => {})), so for an end user without the (non-existent) app installed it prints 'Opening … in Smithers GUI…' and silently does nothing. The command is documented in docs/cli/overview.mdx as a real verb.
recommendation: Either hide/remove `smithers gui` until the native app ships, or make it degrade loudly (detect the bundle is absent and point the user at `smithers ui`, which is the working browser-based equivalent). At minimum document that it requires a separately-installed app.
vnote: Verified apps/cli/src/index.js:5721-5748: gui spawns `open -b com.smithers.SmithersGUI <dir>` (default bundleId com.smithers.SmithersGUI:5727) detached with errors swallowed (proc.on('error', () => {}):5741) after printing 'Opening … in Smithers GUI…'. Grep for SmithersGUI across the whole repo finds only: the CLI command, docs (overview.mdx:324, llms bundles, changelog 0.16.3.mdx:24), a retired-POC spec (.smithers/specs/live-run-devtools-ui.md targeting Swift/SwiftUI), and a smithers-studio-2 log path. No app in apps/ builds that bundle id (apps/ = cli, observability, review, smithers, smithers-demo, smithers-studio-2, smithers-tui-demo); no .app/.dmg/.plist artifact; no build script. ADDITIONAL EVIDENCE strengthening the finding: rewriteGuiShortcutArgv (index.js:5923-5938, invoked at 6320) rewrites `smithers .`, `smithers ..`, `smithers /abs/dir`, `smithers ./dir` into the `gui` command — and the 0.16.3 changelog promotes this as the `code .` convention — so every user who types `smithers .` hits a silently-no-op dead command. P1 appropriate.

title: Default semantic MCP surface omits the flagship time-travel / durability controls, forcing agents to the CLI
evidence: The README positions `smithers mcp add` as the primary way coding agents drive Smithers, and the default tool surface is 'semantic'. That surface exposes exactly 14 tools: list_workflows, run_workflow, list_runs, get_run, watch_run, explain_run, list_pending_approvals, resolve_approval, ask_human, get_node_detail, revert_attempt, list_artifacts, get_chat_transcript, get_run_events. Smithers' marquee selling points — reversibility (rewind/fork/replay/timeline/restore/snapshots), signal delivery to runs waiting on <Signal>/<WaitForEvent>, and cancel/down/retry-task — have CLI commands and installed skills (smithers-fork, smithers-rewind, smithers-signal, etc.) but NO semantic MCP tool. The smithers SKILL.md confirms the workaround is to shell out: line 233 'Time travel: ... smithers timeline | fork | replay | rewind | diff | timetravel | retry-task'; line 303 'Resolve it with smithers approve / deny / signal'. An MCP-only agent literally cannot perform the reversibility features the product is sold on.
recommendation: Add semantic MCP tools for the time-travel set (at least fork, rewind, replay, timeline) plus deliver_signal, cancel_run, and retry_task — or default new MCP registrations to the 'both' surface so the raw CLI-derived tools are available alongside the curated ones.
vnote: Verified apps/cli/src/mcp/semantic-tools.js defines exactly 14 tools (grep of 'name:': list_workflows, run_workflow, list_runs, get_run, watch_run, explain_run, list_pending_approvals, resolve_approval, ask_human, get_node_detail, revert_attempt, list_artifacts, get_chat_transcript, get_run_events). NONE for fork/rewind/replay/timeline/snapshots/restore/deliver_signal/cancel/down/retry_task. Default surface is 'semantic': argv-utils.js:28 (let surface = 'semantic') for --mcp mode, and the `ask` command also defaults toolSurface to 'semantic' (index.js:5282). A 'both' surface exists (registerRawToolsOnMcpServer at index.js:6363) but is not the default. README.md:53-58 positions `mcp add` as the primary driver ('register the MCP server into every coding agent'). skills/smithers/SKILL.md:233 confirms the workaround is to shell out ('Time travel: smithers timeline | fork | replay | rewind | diff | timetravel | retry-task') and :303 ('Resolve it with smithers approve / deny / signal'). An MCP-only agent cannot perform the reversibility/signal features the product is sold on. P1 appropriate. (Note: revert_attempt IS exposed semantically, a single time-travel-adjacent tool, but it does not cover fork/rewind/replay/timeline/signal/cancel/retry.)

title: Several documented remote sandbox targets (gVisor, Daytona, Cloudflare) have no shipped or example provider
evidence: README.md and docs/index.mdx advertise sandbox targets gVisor, Kubernetes, Freestyle, Daytona, and Cloudflare. The shipped sandbox package (packages/sandbox/src/) only defines the SandboxProvider interface + generic execute, and SandboxRuntime is limited to 'bubblewrap' | 'docker' | 'codeplane' (packages/components/src/components/SandboxRuntime.ts). The `provider` prop is pluggable (unknown), and only two of the five advertised targets have any concrete artifact: Freestyle (examples/freestyle/provider.ts + docs/examples/freestyle-sandbox-provider.mdx) and Kubernetes (examples/kubernetes/). A repo-wide search finds zero gVisor, Daytona, or Cloudflare provider implementation or example. Daytona/Cloudflare are explicitly linked as 'managed remote sandboxes' in the README table.
recommendation: Provide at least an example provider (like the Freestyle one) for each advertised target, or soften the docs to state these are integration points the user must implement against the SandboxProvider interface rather than shipped/exampled providers.
vnote: Verified README.md:172-174 and docs/index.mdx:240-241 advertise gVisor, Kubernetes, Freestyle, Daytona, Cloudflare as remote sandbox targets. packages/sandbox/src/ ships only the SandboxProvider.ts interface + generic execute.js/bundle.js/transport.js. packages/components/src/components/SandboxRuntime.ts:1 limits SandboxRuntime to 'bubblewrap'|'docker'|'codeplane'. examples/ contains concrete artifacts only for Freestyle (examples/freestyle/provider.ts + docs/examples/freestyle-sandbox-provider.mdx) and Kubernetes (examples/kubernetes/ with Dockerfile + k8s/ manifests). Repo-wide grep for gvisor/daytona/cloudflare provider implementations or examples found ZERO — only doc/README/changelog prose mentions (e.g. examples/defending-code/README.md:149,174 references gVisor only in prose). dstack example exists but is not one of the advertised five. P2 appropriate.

title: `smithers openapi` advertises 'Generate AI SDK tools' but only has a `list` (preview) subcommand — no generate verb
evidence: The openapi CLI group description is 'Generate AI SDK tools from OpenAPI specs.' but its only subcommand is `list` ('Preview tools that would be generated from an OpenAPI spec.'), which prints operationIds and returns them. There is no `generate` / `emit` subcommand that actually writes or materializes tools; generation only exists at the library level (@smithers-orchestrator/openapi/tool-factory). A user reading `smithers openapi --help` is led to expect generation from the CLI and only gets a preview.
recommendation: Either add a `smithers openapi generate` subcommand that emits the tool definitions/code, or change the group description to reflect that the CLI only previews and generation is programmatic.
vnote: Verified apps/cli/src/index.js:2486-2511: openapiCli description is 'Generate AI SDK tools from OpenAPI specs.' but its only subcommand is `list` ('Preview tools that would be generated from an OpenAPI spec.') which prints operationIds via listOperations and returns them. Grep confirms openapiCli has only one .command('list') registration and is registered to the root CLI at index.js:5900. There is no generate/emit verb; actual generation lives only at library level (@smithers-orchestrator/openapi/tool-factory, imported at 2494). Severity P2/medium appropriate — a docs/expectation mismatch, not a broken capability.

title: README promotes the deprecated `<Ralph>` component as a first-class primitive with example code
evidence: README.md's Components table lists `<Ralph>` ('Loop until a condition is met') as a primitive and shows a worked `<Ralph until={...} maxIterations={5}>…</Ralph>` example. But docs/components/loop.mdx:50 states 'Ralph is still exported as a deprecated alias of Loop for older workflows. New code should import and render Loop.' There is no docs/components/ralph.mdx page (only loop.mdx, review-loop.mdx, loop-until-scored.mdx). The README thus steers new users toward a deprecated component.
recommendation: Replace the `<Ralph>` row and example in README.md with `<Loop>` to match the canonical docs, or annotate Ralph as a deprecated alias.

title: `smithers memory` and `smithers cron` CLI groups are partial vs their underlying store/adapter capabilities
evidence: `memory` is described as 'View and query cross-run memory facts' but only exposes `list` — the memory store supports setFact/deleteExpiredFacts and namespaces, yet there is no CLI to set, clear, delete, or enumerate namespaces. `cron` is described as 'Manage and run background schedule triggers' with start/add/list/rm, but there is no enable/disable toggle (despite the cron row carrying an `enabled` flag and listCrons(false) filtering) and no 'run now' / manual trigger, so a registered schedule can only be removed, not paused.
recommendation: Add `memory delete`/`memory clear` (and optionally `memory set`/`memory namespaces`) and `cron enable`/`cron disable` (plus an optional `cron run <id>` to fire on demand) to bring the CLI surface in line with the documented 'manage' framing.
vnote: Verified apps/cli/src/index.js:2230-2328. memory group ('View and query cross-run memory facts') exposes only `list`; the store (packages/memory/src/store/MemoryStore.ts) supports setFact (line 13), deleteFact (line 19), deleteExpiredFacts (line 29), threads, and messages — none surfaced in the CLI for writes/deletes/clear. cron group ('Manage and run background schedule triggers') has start/add/list/rm but NO enable/disable toggle despite the _smithers_cron table carrying an `enabled` flag (adapter.js:2521) and listCrons(enabledOnly) filtering on it (adapter.js:2527-2529); `add` always sets enabled:true (index.js:2287), and `rm` (deleteCron) is the only mutation, so a schedule can be removed but not paused. There is also no 'run now'. Minor correction to the original evidence: the store has no listNamespaces API, so 'enumerate namespaces' is not a missing CLI surface so much as a missing store API; the set/delete/clear gaps are real. P2 appropriate.

title: `smithers .` / `smithers <dir>` shortcut silently no-ops because it routes to the unbuilt `gui` command
evidence: apps/cli/src/index.js:5923-5938 (rewriteGuiShortcutArgv, invoked at 6320) rewrites a bare path positional (`.`, `..`, an absolute dir, or `./`/`../` dir) into the `gui` subcommand, which then runs `open -b com.smithers.SmithersGUI` with errors swallowed (proc.on('error', () => {}) at 5741). The 0.16.3 changelog (docs/changelogs/0.16.3.mdx:23-27) promotes this as the VS Code/Cursor `code .` convention. Since no app in this repo builds com.smithers.SmithersGUI (verified by grep), every user who types `smithers .` gets a printed 'Opening … in Smithers GUI…' and nothing happens. This is a documented entry-point shortcut that is dead for all users, not just the explicit `smithers gui` verb.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Circular dependency between @smithers-orchestrator/agents and @smithers-orchestrator/observability ships to npm
evidence: agents/index.js -> BaseCliAgent/index.js -> BaseCliAgent.js eagerly imports `@smithers-orchestrator/observability/logging` and `/metrics`; meanwhile apps/observability/src/_traceEventNormalizers.js imports `extractTextFromJsonValue`/`normalizeTokenUsage` from `@smithers-orchestrator/agents/BaseCliAgent`. A full import-graph scan of all 32 packages + apps found this as the ONLY 2-cycle. Both packages are non-private and published. The cycle is asymmetric/partly lazy (observability/index.js does NOT re-export normalizeStructuredEvent, so observability's barrel does not eagerly close the loop), which limits init-order risk, but it is still a true published-package cycle.
recommendation: Move the two shared pure helpers (extractTextFromJsonValue, normalizeTokenUsage) into a low-level leaf package (e.g. tool-context or a new shared-text package) that both agents and observability depend on, eliminating the cycle. Then add cycle detection to scripts/check-dependency-boundaries.mjs so it cannot regrow.

title: observability is a foundational library but lives in apps/ (package masquerading as an app)
evidence: @smithers-orchestrator/observability is depended on by ~12 packages (agents, db, driver, components, memory, openapi, sandbox, scorers, server, time-travel, vcs, smithers) and is publicly re-exported as `smithers-orchestrator/observability` (root package.json exports `./observability -> ./apps/observability/src/index.js`). It is non-private with a `files` field and is published to npm by scripts/publish.mjs, yet it sits under apps/ not packages/. This is the direct cause of packages/ libraries depending on an apps/ member and contributes to the agents cycle confusion.
recommendation: Relocate apps/observability to packages/observability (it is a library, not an app). The Docker/grafana/prometheus assets it ships can stay co-located. Keep the published name unchanged.

title: dependency-boundary check scans ZERO files for the e2e workspace (directWorkspaceDirs entry is effectively dead)
evidence: directWorkspaceDirs=["e2e"] adds the e2e package to the scan list, but filesForPackage uses `roots = pkg.dir === "." ? ["scripts"] : ["src"]`. The e2e workspace has NO src/ directory — its tests live in e2e/faults/, e2e/budgets/, e2e/exports/, e2e/harness/. So the checker collects 0 source files for e2e and validates nothing there. The whole point of adding e2e to directWorkspaceDirs is silently defeated; undeclared imports in the e2e suite would not be caught.
recommendation: Make filesForPackage scan the actual source roots for e2e (e.g. faults/budgets/exports/harness, or fall back to the package root excluding ignoredDirs) so the e2e suite's imports are actually validated.
vnote: Reproduced exactly. scripts/check-dependency-boundaries.mjs:14 adds e2e via directWorkspaceDirs, and line 101 sets roots = pkg.dir === '.' ? ['scripts'] : ['src']. The e2e workspace has NO src/ directory (verified: test -d e2e/src returns DOES NOT EXIST); its 46 source files live in e2e/faults/, e2e/budgets/, e2e/exports/, e2e/harness/. Simulating collectSourceFiles(join('e2e','src')) yields exactly 0 files, so e2e is validated against nothing. Concrete impact: the checker (run in CI via package.json:74 'test' script and 'check:deps') reports 'passed for 41 workspace package(s)' while three undeclared imports go undetected — e2e/exports/programmatic-api.test.ts imports 'smithers-orchestrator' (the packages/smithers workspace pkg), and e2e/faults/case25-approval-scope-denial.test.ts imports 'react', 'zod', and 'smithers-orchestrator', none of which appear in e2e/package.json's dependencies/devDependencies. These are precisely the undeclared imports the checker exists to catch, so the directWorkspaceDirs:['e2e'] entry is silently defeated. Severity P1 confirmed: a CI guard silently passing on real violations it was designed to detect.

title: .smithers workflow pack (a shipping target) is excluded from the dependency-boundary check and imports undeclared react/effect
evidence: .smithers is a declared root workspace member (root package.json workspaces) and a documented shipping target (the `smithers init` pack), but workspaceRoots=[packages,apps] and directWorkspaceDirs=[e2e] omit it. .smithers source imports `react` (.smithers/ui/*.tsx, ~14 files), `react-dom`, and `effect` (.smithers/workflows/demo.tsx:1327) yet .smithers/package.json declares only @smithers-orchestrator/scorers, smithers-orchestrator, and zod — react/effect/react-dom are NOT declared. For an end user who runs `smithers init`, these bare peer deps rely on monorepo root hoisting and would be undeclared in the shipped pack.
recommendation: Add .smithers to directWorkspaceDirs in the boundary checker (and teach filesForPackage to scan workflows/ui/components/skills), then declare react/react-dom/effect in .smithers/package.json (or document them as expected host-provided peers).

title: Subpath exports point their `types` condition at the barrel index.d.ts, which does not contain the subpath's symbols
evidence: Every subpath in agents/package.json maps `types` to ./src/index.d.ts (the barrel) except ./mcp/createMcpToolset. But the barrel index.d.ts does NOT export extractTextFromJsonValue or normalizeTokenUsage (verified: grep finds them only in src/BaseCliAgent/index.js; not in src/index.d.ts nor src/index.js). The build (tsup --dts-only, entry index only) emits a single barrel d.ts and no per-subpath d.ts. In-repo this is masked because tsconfig `paths`/bundler resolution falls through to the .js source (apps/observability typecheck passes), but an external consumer of the published package importing `@smithers-orchestrator/agents/BaseCliAgent` would get types resolved from a barrel that lacks those symbols. The same single-barrel `types` pattern is used by tool-context, usage, accounts, control-plane (`./*` -> index.d.ts).
recommendation: Emit per-subpath declaration files (multiple tsup entries) and point each subpath's `types` at its own .d.ts, or ensure the barrel index.d.ts re-exports everything reachable via the subpaths.

title: Three workspace packages (accounts, usage, tool-context) are missing from the root tsconfig paths map
evidence: Every other internal @smithers-orchestrator/* package has an explicit paths entry, but @smithers-orchestrator/accounts, @smithers-orchestrator/usage, and @smithers-orchestrator/tool-context have none. tool-context is a runtime dependency of engine (engine.js:29 runWithToolContext) AND is re-exported into the public surface (packages/smithers/src/tools/context.js:10), and the root tsconfig `include` covers packages/smithers/src/** — so the root typecheck must resolve tool-context, relying purely on node_modules/exports fallback rather than the explicit paths the rest of the repo uses. It works today but is an inconsistency that breaks the stated tsconfig convention.
recommendation: Add paths entries for @smithers-orchestrator/accounts, /usage, and /tool-context (and their /* subpaths) to match every other package.

title: smithers <-> cli package cycle exists (bin delegates dynamically; cli imports smithers statically)
evidence: packages/smithers/src/bin/smithers.js does `await import("@smithers-orchestrator/cli")`, and apps/cli statically imports `smithers-orchestrator` in 6 files (resolve-root.js, agent-detection.js, workflow-pack.js, find-db.js, workflowUiSources.js, workflows.js). Both declare the other as a dependency. The dynamic import at the bin entrypoint deliberately breaks the static cycle, which is an acceptable pattern, but it is still a package-level cyclic dependency that the boundary checker has no way to flag as intentional vs accidental (no cycle detection at all).
recommendation: Document the lazy-bin-delegation cycle and, if cycle detection is added, allowlist this specific dynamic-import edge.

title: Root package.json exports map is a dev-only alias that diverges structurally from the actually-published exports
evidence: The root (private `smithers-monorepo`) exports map enumerates 17 subpaths pointing at packages/*/src and apps/observability/src. The actually-published package smithers-orchestrator (packages/smithers) exposes only 9 explicit subpaths plus a `./*` wildcard backed by re-export shims (server.js, serve.js, gateway.js, observability.js, memory.js, openapi.js, scorers.js, dom/renderer.js all exist and re-export the underlying package). The two maps reach the same code by different mechanisms, so the root map is purely a development convenience (its apps/observability/src paths would be meaningless to any external consumer). Maintaining two diverging surface definitions is a drift hazard.
recommendation: Document that the root exports map is dev-only, or generate it from the published smithers package exports so the two cannot drift.

title: Published smithers-orchestrator ./* wildcard publicly exposes internal helper files
evidence: The `./*` wildcard makes every top-level file in packages/smithers/src importable as a public subpath, including internal helpers create.js, examples-entry.js, and findSmithersAnchorDir.js (18 files total). These are not intended public API but are reachable as smithers-orchestrator/create, smithers-orchestrator/examples-entry, etc.
recommendation: Either enumerate the intended public subpaths explicitly (drop the broad wildcard) or move internal-only helpers into a subdirectory not matched by `./*`.

title: No automated guard that every documented public subpath export resolves
evidence: The only export-surface test checks ~8 named functions on the main `smithers-orchestrator` barrel. Because publishing uses the `./*` wildcard with hand-written re-export shims (server.js, serve.js, gateway.js, observability.js, memory.js, openapi.js, scorers.js, dom/renderer.js, control-plane.js, sandbox.js, mdx-plugin.js), a missing or broken shim would only fail at a downstream consumer's import time, not in CI. There is no test that imports each public subpath and asserts it loads.
recommendation: Add an e2e test that imports every public subpath (server, serve, gateway, observability, scorers, memory, openapi, sandbox, mdx-plugin, dom/renderer, control-plane, tools, jsx-runtime) and asserts a non-empty module, guarding the wildcard-backed surface.

title: checkUsageLimit ignores `period` — a 'daily' limit sums all-time usage (quota math is window-blind)
evidence: src/index.js:852-893. The limit row is keyed by period (daily/monthly, PK at line 133) but the usage SUM (lines 869-883) windows only on observed_at_ms >= sinceMs AND <= untilMs, where sinceMs defaults to 0 and untilMs to Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (lines 867-868). `period` is read into a variable (line 857) used ONLY to select the limit row, never to bound the time range. Verified by probe: a period:'daily' limitQuantity:3 with two 2-unit events 60 days apart returned {usedQuantity:4, exceeded:true} — i.e. it counted usage from two months ago against a *daily* quota. A 'daily' and a 'monthly' limit with identical metric/unit produce identical usedQuantity for the same call.
recommendation: Either (a) derive [sinceMs,untilMs] from `period` (daily = start-of-day, monthly = start-of-month relative to now/untilMs) so the quota window matches the declared period, or (b) if period is intentionally opaque and the caller MUST pass sinceMs/untilMs, document that loudly in index.d.ts + control-plane.mdx and add a test proving daily vs monthly require different caller windows. Today the field is a silent footgun.
vnote: CONFIRMED at src/index.js:852-893 by reading + runtime probe. The limit row PK includes period (schema line 133); checkUsageLimit reads period (line 857) ONLY to select the limit row (WHERE ... period = ?, lines 861-863). The usage SUM (lines 869-883) windows on observed_at_ms BETWEEN sinceMs (default 0, line 867) and untilMs (default Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER, line 868). period never bounds the time range. Probe: period:'daily' limitQuantity:3 with two 2-unit events 60 days apart returned {usedQuantity:4, exceeded:true} — it counted 60-day-old usage against a *daily* quota. An identical 'daily' and 'monthly' limit returned identical usedQuantity:4 (daily.usedQuantity===monthly.usedQuantity: true). Aggravating: the docs example (docs/deployment/control-plane.mdx:112-119) and the only test (tests/control-plane.test.js:238-252) both pass period:'daily'/'monthly' WITHOUT sinceMs/untilMs, so the public docs actively present period as if it bounds the window. control-plane.mdx never mentions sinceMs/untilMs and never explains period does not constrain the window. index.d.ts:145 lists period, sinceMs, untilMs as independent optionals with no JSDoc on their relationship. P1 stands: real quota-bypass / false-positive footgun in a billing/limits path.

title: Duplicate slug / UNIQUE-constraint conflicts leak raw SQLite Error instead of a typed SmithersError
evidence: src/index.js:501-504 (createOrg INSERT), 538-541 (createTeam), 592-595 (createProject). The package wraps all validation failures in SmithersError('INVALID_INPUT', ...) (e.g. nonEmptyString/slug/optionalId) and gives friendly errors for missing projects (assertProjectExists, line 478). But the most common conflict — inserting a duplicate org/team/project slug — bubbles the raw bun:sqlite Error. Probe output: `ctor name: Error | code: SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_UNIQUE | msg: UNIQUE constraint failed: _smithers_cp_orgs.slug`. Callers cannot distinguish a 409-conflict from an internal error without string-matching the message.
recommendation: Catch SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_* in the create* methods and rethrow as SmithersError with a stable code (e.g. 'CONFLICT' / 'ALREADY_EXISTS') so the hosted boundary can map it to HTTP 409. Add tests asserting the thrown error type for duplicate slug.

title: recordUsage / recordAuditEvent do not validate project existence — asymmetric with setUsageLimit/putSecretRef, leaks raw FK error
evidence: setUsageLimit (src/index.js:814-816) and putSecretRef (src/index.js:902-904) call assertProjectExists() to raise a friendly 'Control-plane project not found' SmithersError. recordUsage (src/index.js:753-783) and recordAuditEvent (src/index.js:979-1009) do NOT — they rely on the FK constraint. Probe with projectId='ghost' (org exists, project does not) produced a raw `FOREIGN KEY constraint failed` Error rather than a typed SmithersError. Inconsistent caller experience for the same logical failure.
recommendation: Either call assertProjectExists in recordUsage/recordAuditEvent for parity, or document that these methods surface raw FK errors. Prefer consistency: every write that takes a projectId should fail the same way for a missing project.

title: Unguarded JSON.parse on DB-stored metadata_json — exportOrgAudit/getOrg/etc. throw a raw SyntaxError on corrupt rows
evidence: src/index.js:284-290 parseJsonObject does `JSON.parse(value)` with no try/catch. All read mappers (projectRow, identityProviderRow, usageRow, auditRow) call it on metadata_json read from the DB. Writes always JSON.stringify a validated object, so first-party rows are safe, but any out-of-band/legacy/corrupt row makes reads crash. Probe: inserting metadata_json='not json' directly then calling exportOrgAudit threw `JSON Parse error: Unexpected identifier "not"` (a raw error, not SmithersError). For an audit-export path used in compliance/incident response, a single bad row aborting the whole export is a poor failure mode.
recommendation: Wrap JSON.parse in try/catch inside parseJsonObject and fall back to {} (matching the function's existing 'return {} on non-object' contract), or rethrow as a SmithersError tagged with the row id. Add a test seeding a malformed metadata_json row.
vnote: CONFIRMED at src/index.js:288 by reading + runtime probe. parseJsonObject does a bare JSON.parse(value) with no try/catch; it is the only JSON.parse in the file. It is called by every read mapper: projectRow (line 358), identityProviderRow (390), and the usage/audit mappers (410, 461). Writes always JSON.stringify a jsonObject()-validated value so first-party rows are safe, but any out-of-band/legacy/corrupt row crashes reads. Probe: setting metadata_json='not json' directly then calling exportOrgAudit threw a raw SyntaxError ('JSON Parse error: Unexpected identifier "not"'), NOT a SmithersError (is SmithersError? false). For a compliance/incident-response audit-export path, one bad row aborting the whole export is a poor failure mode and inconsistent with the package's SmithersError contract. No test seeds a malformed metadata_json row (grep for malformed/corrupt/SyntaxError in tests = 0 hits). P2 is appropriate: requires a corrupt/out-of-band row to trigger, but the read mappers offer no resilience and the contract is violated.

title: Secret-value test assertion is a no-op — does not actually prove the no-plaintext invariant
evidence: tests/control-plane.test.js:183 asserts `expect(JSON.stringify(rawRows)).not.toContain("super-secret-value")`, but the string 'super-secret-value' is never passed to putSecretRef anywhere in the test (the ref values are ARNs). The assertion passes vacuously and would pass even if the schema DID store secrets. The real guarantee (verified manually via probe) is structural: the _smithers_cp_secret_refs schema (src/index.js:140-153) has no secret_value/value column, so putSecretRef physically cannot persist a plaintext secret even if extra fields are passed in the input object.
recommendation: Replace the vacuous assertion with a structural one: PRAGMA table_info and assert columns do NOT include any value-bearing field, and/or call putSecretRef with an extra {secretValue:'x'} property and assert no row column contains it. This documents the invariant the way a reviewer expects.

title: Docs imply period-windowed quotas but checkUsageLimit is window-blind
evidence: docs/deployment/control-plane.mdx:84-121 shows setUsageLimit({period:'monthly', limitQuantity:10_000_000}) then checkUsageLimit({period:'monthly'}) returning {usedQuantity, remainingQuantity, exceeded}, with the comment 'Check whether the project is within its quota.' A reader reasonably infers a 'monthly' limit checks monthly usage. In reality (see the period bug) the sum is all-time unless the caller passes sinceMs/untilMs, which the docs never mention for checkUsageLimit. The d.ts (index.d.ts:145) exposes sinceMs/untilMs but with no explanation of their relationship to period.
recommendation: Once the period bug is decided (window from period, or caller-supplied window), update control-plane.mdx to state explicitly how the usage window is determined and that period alone does NOT bound the sum (current behavior).

title: No test exercises UNIQUE-conflict or constraint-error branches; coverage is line-only
evidence: tests/control-plane.test.js uses toThrow() three times (lines 225, 255, 272) and asserts message text twice (255 'project not found', 353 'org not found'). None inserts a duplicate slug, duplicate (org,team) PK, or triggers a UNIQUE violation, and none asserts the error TYPE/code for any failure. The 'foreign keys prevent orphan projects' test (line 262) only checks createProject for a missing org throws generically. Reported 100% line coverage (confirmed) masks that the constraint-failure / conflict code paths and their (raw vs typed) error behavior are untested — exactly the branches most likely to regress.
recommendation: Add tests for: duplicate org slug, duplicate team slug within an org, duplicate project slug, addTeamMember/addProjectTeam upsert (ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE) actually updating role, and recordUsage with a non-existent projectId. Assert the concrete error type each raises so the contract is pinned.

title: checkUsageLimit/setUsageLimit `period` field is entirely non-functional — no validation, no enumeration, no behavioral effect beyond row-keying
evidence: src/index.js:857 nonEmptyString('period', input.period ?? 'monthly') accepts ANY non-empty string. There is no enum check (e.g. daily|weekly|monthly) and no code anywhere translates a period into a time window (grep: period appears only in schema PK/index and as a WHERE selector at lines 842, 861). Combined with the window-blindness in the confirmed P1, the field is dead semantics: a caller can setUsageLimit period:'banana' and checkUsageLimit period:'banana' and get an answer that sums all-time usage. The field exists purely to allow multiple limit rows per (org,project,metric,unit) but carries no quota meaning. This is a latent maintenance/correctness trap distinct from the P1 (the P1 is the wrong-answer bug; this is that the abstraction itself is hollow and unvalidated).
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: 10 live runtime RPC methods are absent from the canonical GATEWAY_RPC_DEFINITIONS contract (real drift, opposite of the prompt's premise)
evidence: packages/server/src/gateway.js dispatches these methods via `case` branches: health (3800), workflows.list (3814), approvals.list (4422), runs.diff (4404), frames.list (3902), frames.get (3915), attempts.list (3933), attempts.get (3949), getDevToolsSnapshot (3998), runs.rerun (4533). None appear in GATEWAY_RPC_DEFINITIONS (19 methods, lines 397-707), none are in GATEWAY_RPC_LEGACY_METHOD_ALIASES (lines 380-395), so they have no request/response schema, no versioned error list, no docs/rpc page, and no /v1/rpc/* path in openapi.yaml. They are reachable only because the server resolves their scope through the special-cases in getRequiredScopeForGatewayMethod (lines 725-748) and falls back to 'run:read'. Programmatically confirmed: filtering server-dispatched methods against listGatewayRpcMethods()+aliases yields exactly these 10 orphans. workflows.list/approvals.list are even legacy spellings of listWorkflows/listApprovals but are NOT registered as aliases.
recommendation: Either promote these into GATEWAY_RPC_DEFINITIONS (schema + errors + docs page + OpenAPI path) so the contract is complete, or, for the ones that are intentionally internal, register the legacy spellings (workflows.list, approvals.list) in GATEWAY_RPC_LEGACY_METHOD_ALIASES and explicitly document the rest as out-of-contract. The canonical contract claiming to be the single source of truth while the runtime serves 10 uncontracted methods is the actual documented-drift this audit was looking for.
vnote: Reproduced exactly. GATEWAY_RPC_DEFINITIONS (rpc/index.ts:397-707) has 19 methods; GATEWAY_RPC_LEGACY_METHOD_ALIASES (380-395) has 14 aliases. The routeRequest switch in packages/server/src/gateway.js (3794-4642) dispatches 42 distinct method labels; filtering against canonical+aliases yields exactly these 10 orphans: health, workflows.list, approvals.list, runs.diff, frames.list, frames.get, attempts.list, attempts.get, getDevToolsSnapshot, runs.rerun. None appear in openapi.yaml (only the 19 /v1/rpc/* paths exist; grep for the orphan names returns 0 paths) and none have a docs/rpc/*.mdx page (exactly 19 pages, matching the 19 definitions). check-docs.mjs:911 gates the 19-count for docs but does not cover the orphans. Confirmed the orphans are NOT dead server code: getDevToolsSnapshot is consumed by production clients packages/pi-plugin/src/runtime/DevToolsClient.ts:401 and packages/gateway-client/src/sync/gatewayCollectionDefs.ts:118; frames.list/frames.get/attempts.list/attempts.get/runs.diff/approvals.list are exercised in packages/server/tests/gateway.test.jsx (lines 460-535). Also confirmed workflows.list and approvals.list are legacy spellings of listWorkflows/listApprovals that are NOT in the alias map (runs.list IS aliased, but these two are not). P1 is justified: the package's own description claims to be the source of 'Stable Smithers Gateway RPC contracts' while the runtime serves 10 uncontracted methods.

title: OpenAPI drift guard (check:openapi) is never run on PRs — only in the ungated `build` script
evidence: check:openapi (`bun run scripts/generate-openapi.ts --check`) appears only inside the gateway `build` script (line 31: `bun run check:openapi && tsc ...`). The gateway `test` script is just `bun test tests` (line 32-ish), and .github/workflows/ci.yml runs `pnpm check:effect`, `pnpm check:deps`, `pnpm -r typecheck`, `.smithers typecheck`, and `pnpm test` — it never runs `pnpm -r build`. Root `pnpm test` = `... && pnpm -r test`, which invokes each package's `test`, not `build`. Therefore if a contributor changes GATEWAY_RPC_DEFINITIONS without regenerating openapi.yaml, CI stays green; the stale OpenAPI only surfaces at publish time. (Note: check-docs.mjs DOES gate the docs/rpc pages and the 19-count at scripts/check-docs.mjs:911, so docs drift is caught — but the committed openapi.yaml is not.)
recommendation: Add `check:openapi` (and the build's `tsc --noEmit`, or rely on the existing typecheck job) to the CI gate — e.g. add `pnpm --filter @smithers-orchestrator/gateway check:openapi` to the test or typecheck workflow job, or move it into the gateway `test` script so `pnpm -r test` picks it up.
vnote: The premise is false. The finding's evidence only examined .github/workflows/ci.yml, but .github/workflows/faults.yml is titled 'Faults (per-PR)' and triggers `on: pull_request` (lines 3-4). Its step at faults.yml:33 runs `pnpm -r build`, which runs the gateway `build` script `bun run check:openapi && tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit` (gateway/package.json:31). I confirmed the gateway is in the workspace (pnpm-workspace.yaml: packages/*) and is the only package whose build script invokes check:openapi. generate-openapi.ts:255-261 exits 1 when the committed openapi.yaml is stale, so a PR that changes GATEWAY_RPC_DEFINITIONS without regenerating openapi.yaml would fail the faults job (build step runs before test:faults). faults-nightly.yml:34 also runs `pnpm -r build`. The drift guard therefore DOES run on every PR. (Minor caveat: check:openapi is bundled into `pnpm -r build` for all 30+ packages rather than a dedicated narrow step, and whether faults.yml is a required branch-protection check could not be verified from the repo — but the workflow does run on PR and would go red, refuting the claim that the guard 'never runs on PRs'.)

title: rpc-contract.test.ts example validation is schema self-validation, not a round-trip; near-tautological for opaque response schemas
evidence: The 'every exampleRequest/exampleResponse validates against its own schema' test validates each example against the SAME schema that produced it. For the ~9 methods whose responseSchema is `objectSchema({}, [], '...', true)` (additionalProperties:true, no properties, no required) — rewindRun (responseSchema {type:object, properties:{}, required:[], additionalProperties:true}), submitSignal, getNodeOutput, getNodeDiff, cronList, cronCreate, listApprovals, getRun's nested blocked/unhealthy — ANY object passes, so the check is a no-op for those payloads. There is no test asserting the contract schemas match the actual runtime responses in packages/server/src/gateway.js, nor that they match the exported TS *Request/*Response types in the same file. The 'guards the guard' test (lines 200-219) only proves the validator rejects additionalProperties:false violations using cancelRun, which is one of the few closed schemas.
recommendation: Add a round-trip test that asserts the JsonSchema definitions agree with the exported TS types (e.g. a type-level conformance check that LaunchRunResponse satisfies the launchRun responseSchema shape), and/or an integration test that drives the real server gateway and validates actual responses against the contract schemas. At minimum, tighten the opaque response schemas (rewindRun, cronDelete-style) to declare their known properties so the example check stops being a tautology.

title: `approve` special-case in getRequiredScopeForGatewayMethod maps a method the runtime never dispatches (vestigial)
evidence: getRequiredScopeForGatewayMethod returns 'approval:submit' for method === 'approve' (lines 744-746). But packages/server/src/gateway.js has no `case "approve"` — it dispatches `submitApproval` and the legacy alias `approvals.decide` (gateway.js:1948-1949, 4440-4441). 'approve' is not in GATEWAY_RPC_DEFINITIONS, not in GATEWAY_RPC_LEGACY_METHOD_ALIASES, and not a granted-scope name handled here (legacy scope 'approve' is handled separately in scopes.ts legacyAccessImplies). The server resolves scope by dispatched method name (gateway.js:879), and no client can dispatch a bare 'approve' RPC. The branch is covered by tests (rpc-contract.test.ts:168, 242) but those tests pin a behavior with no production consumer.
recommendation: Confirm no external client dispatches a literal 'approve' RPC method; if none, remove the `approve` special-case (and its two test assertions) or add a code comment explaining the intended consumer. Leaving a scope mapping for a non-existent method invites a future contributor to assume 'approve' is a real RPC.
vnote: Confirmed. No `case "approve"` exists in packages/server/src (verified by grep); a client sending {method:'approve'} would pass through the auth pre-check at gateway.js:3249 (which computes scope approval:submit via getRequiredScopeForGatewayMethod), then hit routeRequest's default branch (gateway.js:4639-4640) returning METHOD_NOT_FOUND. So no real `approve` RPC ever executes through the gateway. The only `Rpc.make("approve")` is in packages/engine/src/effect/rpc-schema.js:81, a separate Effect-based control-plane RPC group (SmithersRpcGroup) that does NOT import @smithers-orchestrator/gateway and does not consume getRequiredScopeForGatewayMethod (verified: no gateway import in packages/engine/src). The real consumer of the `approve` *granted scope* is legacyAccessImplies in scopes.ts:57-58, which is independent of the line 744-746 method branch. The two test assertions (rpc-contract.test.ts:168, 242) pin behavior with no production consumer. Medium confidence and P2 dead-code are appropriate.

title: JsonSchema type declares anyOf/format/default/maximum fields that no schema sets and the test validator cannot check
evidence: The JsonSchema type (lines 9-25) declares `anyOf`, `format`, `default`, and `maximum`. Grep across rpc/index.ts shows zero schema values set any of these (`anyOf:` 0, `format:` 0, `default:` 0, `maximum:` 0 occurrences as object keys). The test validator (rpc-contract.test.ts) handles type/properties/required/additionalProperties/items/enum/const/nullable/minimum/oneOf but NOT anyOf/format/default/maximum (no references). So if a contributor later adds `maximum` or `anyOf` to a schema, the contract test would silently ignore the constraint, giving false confidence. generate-openapi.ts also does not special-case them (it serializes the raw object, which is fine, but the validator gap remains).
recommendation: Either drop the unused anyOf/format/default/maximum members from JsonSchema (smaller, honest API surface), or, if they're intended for future use, extend the test validator to enforce them so adding such a constraint is actually verified against examples.
vnote: Confirmed with word-boundary greps: anyOf (line 24), format (14), default (17), maximum (16) appear ONLY in the JsonSchema type declaration in rpc/index.ts and are never set as a key in any schema value. (Note: `minimum` IS used via integerSchema at line 273 — the original 'minimum' grep miss was a shorthand-property artifact, but the four cited fields are genuinely unused.) The test validator validateAgainstSchema (rpc-contract.test.ts:26-103) handles null/nullable, const, enum, oneOf, type (union+integer), minimum, properties/required/additionalProperties, items — and its own docstring (lines 18-24) explicitly enumerates this vocabulary, omitting anyOf/format/default/maximum. So adding such a constraint later would be silently ignored by the example-validation tests. generate-openapi.ts serializes schemas via a generic toYaml walker (lines 72,85,216) so it would faithfully emit them, but the validator gap is real. High confidence and P2 dead-code are justified.

title: generate-openapi.ts has zero test coverage despite emitting the published contract artifact and shipping in `files`
evidence: No test references generate-openapi.ts, openapi.yaml, or buildOpenApiDocument (grep of packages/gateway/tests returns nothing). The custom toYaml serializer (lines 204-251) hand-rolls YAML scalar quoting, key escaping, array/object indentation, and multiline-string handling with no unit tests; correctness was only verifiable by parsing the generated openapi.yaml at audit time (it does parse, and the GatewayScope multiline description round-trips). The generated openapi.yaml is listed in package.json `files` (it's published to consumers). A regression in the serializer (e.g. a key containing a colon, or an example value with YAML-special chars) would only be caught by the ungated check:openapi (see the CI-gating finding).
recommendation: Add a unit test that calls buildOpenApiDocument()/toYaml() and asserts the output parses with a real YAML parser and contains one path per definition with the expected operationId, scope, and error responses; assert it equals the committed openapi.yaml. This also closes the CI gap if wired into the gateway `test` script.

title: TS *Request/*Response types and JsonSchema definitions are maintained in parallel with no agreement test
evidence: The file declares hand-written TS types (LaunchRunRequest at 93, LaunchRunResponse at 102, ... StreamDevToolsRequest at 234) separately from the JsonSchema requestSchema/responseSchema embedded in GATEWAY_RPC_DEFINITIONS (398-707). All exported types are consumed externally (verified: each used 2-13 times across packages/apps), so none are dead — but coverage is uneven (rewindRun has RewindRunRequest but no RewindRunResponse; getRun/submitSignal/cron*/getNodeOutput/getNodeDiff have no Response type) and nothing asserts a type matches its schema. A field renamed in the TS type but not the schema (or vice versa) would compile and pass all 12 tests.
recommendation: Add a compile-time conformance check (e.g. `const _check: SubmitApprovalResponse = exampleFromSchema` patterns, or a type that asserts each *Response is assignable to the schema's inferred shape) so the two representations cannot silently diverge. Alternatively, derive one from the other (infer TS types from the JsonSchema) to make a single source of truth.

title: Misleading test comment claims a non-existent 'canonical approve method'
evidence: rpc-contract.test.ts:241 comment reads 'And the canonical "approve" method resolves to approval:submit.' followed by expect(getRequiredScopeForGatewayMethod("approve")).toBe("approval:submit"). There is no canonical `approve` method: it is absent from GATEWAY_RPC_DEFINITIONS (rpc/index.ts:397-707), absent from the GatewayRpcMethod union (rpc/index.ts:72-91), and the server's routeRequest has no `case "approve"` (returns METHOD_NOT_FOUND via the default branch at gateway.js:4639-4640). The comment cements the false impression that `approve` is a real RPC, compounding the vestigial-special-case problem and making future cleanup riskier.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: objectSchema(additionalProperties) supports a sub-schema type per JsonSchema, but no definition ever uses it and the OpenAPI generator/validator paths for it are untested
evidence: objectSchema accepts `additionalProperties: boolean | JsonSchema` (line 280) and the JsonSchema type permits a sub-schema (line 22). Every call site passes either the default `false` or `true` (grep of rpc/index.ts shows no object-valued additionalProperties argument). The test validator DOES have a branch for object-valued additionalProperties (rpc-contract.test.ts:90-92), but because no schema exercises it, that branch is never executed by the example-validation test — the same false-confidence pattern flagged for anyOf/maximum, but here the validator code is present yet dead. Either add a schema that uses a sub-schema additionalProperties (and an example proving the branch fires) or document that only boolean additionalProperties is in use.
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: 31-line test hits 100% lines but misses ~8 branches/boundary cases the ~100% bar requires
evidence: getToolIdempotencyKey has branches that share a source line with covered code, so Bun's line-coverage reports 100% while the branches go untested. Uncovered: (a) empty-string idempotencyKey falling through to derivation (line 36 condition's false-via-length branch), (b) non-string idempotencyKey, (c) the `iteration ?? 0` default at line 42 — the test ALWAYS passes iteration (0 and 3), never omits it, (d) the missing-runId side of `!ctx.runId || !ctx.nodeId` at line 39 (test only covers missing nodeId via {runId:'r'}), (e) the default-parameter `ctx = getToolContext()` path at line 32 — getToolIdempotencyKey() is only ever called outside a run scope (returns null), never inside one. nextToolSeq is only tested from seq=0 (line 27 ctx={}), never with a pre-existing seq exercising the `?? 0` else. runWithToolContext is never tested for sync return value, for propagating a thrown error while still restoring context, or for nested-scope restore. I wrote throwaway tests for every one of these against the real module and all 9 pass, confirming the code is correct — but the suite does not assert them, which is below the stated ~100%-incl-error/boundary bar.
recommendation: Add cases: getToolIdempotencyKey with idempotencyKey:'' and a non-string value (assert fall-through to smithers:r:n:0), with runId+nodeId but no iteration (assert :0 default), with {nodeId:'n'} only (missing runId -> null), and called inside runWithToolContext (default-param ambient read). Add nextToolSeq with {seq:41}->42. Add runWithToolContext returns fn's value, propagates a thrown error AND leaves getToolContext() undefined afterward, and a nested-scope test (inner shadows, outer restored).
vnote:
CONFIRMED at P1. Reproduced independently. `bun test --coverage` reports 100% Funcs/100% Lines for both src/index.js and src/toolContext.js (Bun's table reports only Funcs/Lines, no branch metric; no coverageThreshold in bunfig.toml), yet the cited branches share source lines with covered code so they go unasserted. Verified each untested branch in toolContext.js by writing throwaway tests against the real module — all 9 passed, confirming code is correct but unasserted:
(a) empty-string idempotencyKey (line 36 `.length > 0` false branch) — existing test only passes `{idempotencyKey:'custom'}` (non-empty). UNTESTED.
(b) non-string idempotencyKey (line 36 `typeof === 'string'` false branch) — never passed. UNTESTED.
(c) `iteration ?? 0` default at line 42 — existing test always passes `iteration:3`; the only other path `{runId:'r'}` returns null early at line 39, never reaching line 42. UNTESTED.
(d) `!ctx.runId` side of line 39 OR — existing test only uses `{runId:'r'}` (exercises `!ctx.nodeId`); `{nodeId:'n'}` only is never tested. UNTESTED.
(e) default-param `ctx = getToolContext()` at line 32 resolving to a real ambient ctx — existing test only calls bare `getToolIdempotencyKey()` OUTSIDE a scope (returns null at line 33). UNTESTED inside a run scope.
nextToolSeq `seq ?? 0` else — existing test starts from fresh `{}` (seq from 0); `{seq:41}->42` never tested. UNTESTED.
runWithToolContext: error-propagation-with-restore and nested-scope restore are UNTESTED anywhere.

MINOR ADJUSTMENT to one sub-claim: the finding says runWithToolContext is 'never tested for sync return value.' Within packages/tool-context's own suite that is true, but cross-package packages/smithers/tests/define-tool-durability.test.js:14-15 passes a sync arrow `() => tool.execute(...)` and asserts the returned value, so the sync-return passthrough has incidental cross-package coverage. Also note packages/smithers/tests/tools-unit.test.js:113-130 duplicates the EXACT same idempotency/seq assertions (same inputs) so it adds zero coverage for the gaps above — the branch gaps are real everywhere, not just in this file. Overall finding substance (meaningful branch/boundary cases unasserted despite the stated ~100%-incl-error/boundary bar) is accurate; P1 holds because 6 distinct untested branches plus 2 untested control-flow behaviors (error propagation, nested restore) fall below the explicit bar, even though the code itself is correct so there is no latent bug.

title: Public type surface is Record<string,any> — direct importers of the package get zero type safety
evidence: All four functions are typed with Record<string, any>: runWithToolContext(ctx: Record<string,any>, ...), getToolContext(): Record<string,any>|undefined, getToolIdempotencyKey(ctx?: Record<string,any>), nextToolSeq(ctx: Record<string,any>). The implementation reads specific fields (ctx.idempotencyKey, ctx.runId, ctx.nodeId, ctx.iteration, ctx.seq in toolContext.js:36-50) but no minimal interface is exported (grep for 'interface'/'type' in src returns nothing but the AsyncLocalStorage<Record<string,any>> JSDoc). smithers re-declares these in packages/smithers/src/tools.d.ts:56-64 with the strong ToolContext type, so consumers going through smithers are safe; anyone importing '@smithers-orchestrator/tool-context' directly gets `any` for every field. The package cannot import smithers' ToolContext (it references SmithersDb/SmithersEvent and would re-introduce the cycle), but it could still export a minimal structural interface { runId?: string; nodeId?: string; iteration?: number; idempotencyKey?: string|null; seq?: number; [k:string]: unknown } and accept that, giving real autocomplete/safety without any cyclic import.
recommendation: Export a minimal ToolContextShape interface from the package and type the functions against it instead of Record<string,any>. smithers' richer ToolContext would extend/be assignable to it, so the smithers re-declaration in tools.d.ts can stay (or be dropped). Keeps the cycle broken while restoring type safety for direct importers.

title: package.json ./* subpath export maps every subpath's types to index.d.ts (latent mis-mapping, currently unused)
evidence: The exports map has a wildcard entry: "./*": { "types": "./src/index.d.ts", "import": "./src/*.js", "default": "./src/*.js" }. The `import` correctly resolves per-subpath (e.g. ./toolContext -> src/toolContext.js) but `types` is hard-pinned to index.d.ts for ALL subpaths, so a subpath import would get JS for the subpath but types for the root. It happens to be harmless today because (a) index.d.ts already contains everything and (b) grep across packages/ and apps/cli shows NOTHING imports a tool-context subpath — only the root '.' entry is used. So this is dead export config with a latent correctness bug if a real subpath module is ever added.
recommendation: Either drop the ./* subpath block (nothing uses it — the root '.' export is sufficient) or fix its types to "./src/*.d.ts" so subpath types track subpath JS. Removing it is simplest given zero consumers.

title: examples/ tree (108 workflows) is in NO CI gate — typecheck:examples script exists but is never invoked
evidence: package.json:67 defines `"typecheck:examples": "tsc -p examples/tsconfig.json --noEmit"`, but `grep -rn typecheck:examples` over the whole repo returns ONLY that definition line — it appears in no workflow, script, or hook. .github/workflows/ci.yml runs only `pnpm check:effect`, `pnpm check:deps`, `pnpm -r typecheck`, `pnpm --filter ./.smithers typecheck` (lines 28-34) and `pnpm test` (line 59). examples/ has no package.json and is not in pnpm-workspace.yaml (packages: packages/*, apps/*, e2e, .smithers), so `pnpm -r typecheck`/`pnpm -r test` skip it. I verified by running `pnpm typecheck:examples` manually: exit 0, 0 errors — so the examples currently compile, but nothing enforces it, so any future API rename or component removal will silently rot all 108 examples that ship in the init pack and docs.
recommendation: Add `- run: pnpm typecheck:examples` to the typecheck job in ci.yml (right after the existing typecheck steps). It already passes today, so wiring it in is a zero-cost guard that turns silent example rot into a red build.
vnote: Core claim CONFIRMED: typecheck:examples is NOT in the PR/push CI gate. .github/workflows/ci.yml (verified full file) runs only pnpm check:effect, check:deps, pnpm -r typecheck, pnpm --filter ./.smithers typecheck (lines 28-34) and pnpm test (line 59). examples/ has no package.json (verified) and is not in pnpm-workspace.yaml (packages: packages/*, apps/*, e2e, .smithers — verified), so pnpm -r typecheck skips it. pnpm test runs check-docs.mjs which references examples/tsconfig.json ONLY as a readFileSync string-content assertion (lines 791,810-811,822 — substring checks for runState path mappings) and never shells out to tsc (grep for 'tsc' in check-docs.mjs returns zero spawn/exec). I reproduced exit-0 manually. HOWEVER the evidence's stronger claim is REFUTED: typecheck:examples is NOT 'never invoked' — it IS invoked in scripts/publish.mjs:168-169 ('pnpm typecheck:examples', gated by !SKIP_CHECKS in the `pnpm release` flow). So examples ARE typechecked before any npm publish, not silently rotting forever — rot is caught at release time, just not per-PR. The grep claim 'appears in no workflow, script, or hook' is false (it appears in publish.mjs AND is name-referenced in check-docs.mjs:153). Severity lowered P1->P2: the gap is a missing per-PR guard, but a real publish-time gate already exists. File count: examples has 111 .jsx/.tsx files (close to the cited 108).

title: Neither 'examples smoke test' actually exercises the examples/ tree — both only scan docs/**
evidence: packages/gateway-react/tests/docsExamplesCompile.test.ts reads only `docs/examples/workflow-ui-react.mdx` (line 70) and `docs/examples/workflow-ui-vanilla.mdx` (line 83) and tsc-compiles their first code block — it never touches repo-root examples/. apps/cli/tests/docs-examples-smoke.test.js globs `docs/**/*.{mdx,md}` (line 155/180) for embedded workflow snippets and runs `smithers graph` on them — again never the examples/ directory. I confirmed no test in packages/, e2e/, or apps/cli/tests/ loads any examples/*.jsx file (e.g. fan-out-fan-in, gastown, ralph-loop, _example-kit). So the ~108 shipped example workflows have zero runtime/render verification — 'nobody has verified they actually render/run' is literally true.
recommendation: Add a real smoke test that globs examples/**/*.{jsx,tsx} and runs `smithers graph <file>` (render-only, no agent execution, like docs-examples-smoke does) on each, seeding a fake agent per the CI-is-agent-free constraint. At minimum, typecheck them in CI (see prior finding). This is the only way to catch the model-ID and API drift below before users hit it.
vnote: CONFIRMED. packages/gateway-react/tests/docsExamplesCompile.test.ts reads only docs/examples/workflow-ui-react.mdx (line 70) and docs/examples/workflow-ui-vanilla.mdx (line 83), extracts the first code block, tsc-compiles it — never touches repo-root examples/. apps/cli/tests/docs-examples-smoke.test.js globs Bun.Glob('docs/**/*.{mdx,md}') at lines 155 and 180 (verified exact), filters for embedded 'export default smithers' workflow snippets — never the examples/ directory. Grep across packages/*/tests, apps/cli/tests, e2e for references to root examples/ returns only those two docs-scoped files (plus an unrelated demo-mcp-server.js fixture). No test loads fan-out-fan-in, ralph-loop, gastown, _example-kit, or any examples/*.jsx. So the shipped examples have zero runtime/render verification, exactly as claimed. (They ARE typechecked at publish time per finding 1, but that is type-only, not render/run — the finding's 'zero runtime/render verification' wording stands.) P1 appropriate.

title: Pervasive stale model ID: deprecated claude-sonnet-4-20250514 in 93 of 97 top-level example workflows (274 occurrences)
evidence: `grep -rln claude-sonnet-4-20250514 examples/` = 93 files; `grep -rhoE` counts 274 occurrences. Per the Anthropic catalog (claude-api skill, shared/models.md), `claude-sonnet-4-20250514` is Claude Sonnet 4 (May 2025), Deprecated and retiring 2026-06-15, replacement `claude-sonnet-4-6`. Examples set it via both `anthropic("claude-sonnet-4-20250514")` (e.g. waterfall.jsx:51-67, pr-lifecycle.jsx:61-73, memory-support-agent.jsx) and bare strings (fan-out-fan-in.jsx:41/46/51, mcp-integration/workflow.tsx:35, bun-port-smithers/components/agents.ts:135). These are the canonical, copy-pasted starting templates the init pack's `create-workflow` provision step tells agents to copy from (apps/cli/src/seeded-workflow-pack.generated.js: 'Browse examples/ ... the fastest correct starting point'), so the stale ID propagates into every newly authored workflow.
recommendation: Bulk-update examples to current IDs (claude-sonnet-4-6 / claude-opus-4-8). Because today's date is 2026-06-16, the May-2025 snapshot has already passed its 2026-06-15 retirement — these examples now reference a model that 404s at runtime, not merely a deprecated one.
vnote: CONFIRMED exactly. grep -rln = 93 files; grep -rhoE count = 274 occurrences (verified). Cited lines accurate: examples/fan-out-fan-in.jsx:41/46/51 (bare string), pr-lifecycle.jsx:61/67/73 and waterfall.jsx:51/57/62/67 (anthropic() wrapper), memory-support-agent.jsx:58/66/74/81 (anthropic() wrapper), mcp-integration/workflow.tsx:35 (AnthropicAgent bare string), bun-port-smithers/components/agents.ts:135 (env fallback). Distribution: 89 files use anthropic('claude-sonnet-4-20250514'), 3 use bare model: string. Per the authoritative claude-api skill (shared/models.md + shared/model-migration.md): claude-sonnet-4-20250514 = Claude Sonnet 4 (claude-sonnet-4-0), DEPRECATED, replacement claude-sonnet-4-6. The migration guide's Deprecated table lists retirement June 15, 2026; today's date verified as 2026-06-16 (one day past retirement) — so this references a model at/past retirement, supporting the runtime-404 concern. Minor nuance: shared/models.md's Deprecated table lists this model's retirement as 'TBD' while the migration guide says 'June 15, 2026' (an internal doc inconsistency), but both agree it is Deprecated with replacement claude-sonnet-4-6, so the finding is correct regardless. Propagation claim CONFIRMED: the seeded provision prompt (apps/cli/src/seeded-workflow-pack.generated.js, create-workflow-provision.mdx) literally says 'Browse examples/ ... they are the fastest correct starting point' and instructs agents to copy 1-3 example candidates as templates. P1 appropriate.

title: Hallucinated / nonexistent model ID 'claude-sonnet-4-7' in ralph-loop.jsx
evidence: examples/ralph-loop.jsx:9 — `new ClaudeCodeAgent({ model: "claude-sonnet-4-7", ... })`. There is no Claude Sonnet 4.7 in the Anthropic catalog (shared/models.md lists Sonnet 4.6 and 4.5 as active; the 4.7 line is Opus-only — claude-opus-4-7). `grep -rn claude-sonnet-4-7` over examples/, packages/, docs/ returns ONLY this one line, confirming it is a typo/hallucination, not a real ID used elsewhere. It typechecks because ClaudeCodeAgentOptions.ts:47 declares `model?: string` (open string, no union of valid IDs), so tsc accepts any string; at runtime `claude --model claude-sonnet-4-7` (ClaudeCodeAgent.js:413 passes it straight to the CLI `--model` flag) would 404/error. ralph-loop is one of the headline 'starter' patterns.
recommendation: Change to `claude-sonnet-4-6` (or `claude-opus-4-8`). Separately, consider narrowing the `model` option type or adding a runtime model-ID validation/allowlist so hallucinated IDs are caught by tsc or at construction rather than failing only when the agent actually runs.
vnote: CONFIRMED. examples/ralph-loop.jsx:9 has `model: "claude-sonnet-4-7"` inside new ClaudeCodeAgent({...}) (verified). grep for claude-sonnet-4-7 across examples/, packages/, docs/ returns ONLY this one line — confirming it is a one-off typo/hallucination, not a real ID used elsewhere. Per the authoritative claude-api skill catalog, there is NO Claude Sonnet 4.7: the Sonnet line has 4.6 (active) and 4.5 (active); 4.7 exists only on the Opus line (claude-opus-4-7). It typechecks because packages/agents/src/ClaudeCodeAgentOptions.ts:47 declares `model?: string` (open string, no union — verified), so tsc accepts any string. At runtime packages/agents/src/ClaudeCodeAgent.js:413 does pushFlag(args, '--model', this.opts.model ?? this.model) — passing it straight to the claude CLI --model flag (verified), where a nonexistent model 404s/errors. P1 appropriate.

title: examples/tsconfig.json points smithers-orchestrator at src/*.js source, not the published package — typecheck:examples does not validate against shipped types
evidence: examples/tsconfig.json remaps `smithers-orchestrator` -> `../packages/smithers/src/examples-entry.js` and every `@smithers-orchestrator/*` -> the in-repo `src/*.js`/`src/*/index.js`, with `allowJs: true`. This is the right call for a monorepo, but it means the examples are typechecked against raw source, not the built `.d.ts` / published export map that real `bunx smithers-orchestrator` users (and the init pack) consume. A subpath that is missing from the package.json `exports` map but present as a source file would still resolve here and pass, masking a real consumer-facing break.
recommendation: Acceptable as the primary gate, but consider an additional smoke that resolves examples against the actual published export surface (the docs-examples-smoke symlink approach in apps/cli/tests already does this for docs snippets and could be extended to examples/).

title: Directory-based example projects use raw bare model strings, compounding the staleness blast radius
evidence: Beyond the 97 top-level workflows, examples/ ships 7 sub-projects (bun-port-smithers, defending-code, dstack, freestyle, kubernetes, mcp-integration, swe-evo). defending-code/workflow.jsx:35 and defending-code/README.md:135 default to `claude-sonnet-4-5` (active legacy, but not current); bun-port-smithers/components/agents.ts:135 and mcp-integration/workflow.tsx:35 use the now-retired `claude-sonnet-4-20250514`; swe-evo README/RESULTS reference `gpt-5.5`/`gpt-5.4` (non-Anthropic Codex models — out of scope for model-catalog validation but worth a doc pass for consistency). These directory examples are even less likely to be covered: docs/-only smoke tests never see them, and several are full projects with their own package boundaries.
recommendation: Roll these into the same model-ID refresh and ensure the examples typecheck/smoke gate (above) walks subdirectories, not just top-level *.jsx.

title: Doc inconsistency: shared/models.md lists Sonnet 4 retirement as 'TBD' while model-migration.md says 'June 15, 2026' (note: claude-api is an installed skill, not the smithers repo)
evidence: In the bundled claude-api skill, shared/models.md Deprecated table shows `claude-sonnet-4-20250514` (claude-sonnet-4-0) Retires=TBD, but shared/model-migration.md Deprecated Models table shows the same model Retires=June 15, 2026. Same for claude-opus-4-20250514. This is an inconsistency within the skill docs, NOT the smithers repo under audit — flagging only because it surfaced while verifying the model-ID findings; out of scope for the smithers examples audit but relevant context for the severity of finding 3 (the precise retirement date that makes the examples 404 'today' comes only from the migration guide, not the catalog).
recommendation: (verifier-discovered)

title: Flagship package smithers-orchestrator has no `test` script — its unit tests never run in CI
evidence: packages/smithers/package.json scripts = {typecheck, build} only — NO `test` key. Yet packages/smithers/tests/ contains tools-unit.test.js, create-unit.test.js, define-tool-durability.test.js, exit-listener-leak-unit.test.js (plus src/__type-tests__/task-fork-jsx.test.tsx).
